> **Why "V"?** Because the development stages **descend** on the left, the testing stages **ascend** on the right, and they meet at the base (coding). The shape of the model is the letter **V**.

### 🔍 Intuitive Analogy — "The House-Building V"
- **Foundation Design (left top)** → **Occupancy Test (right top)** — does the house serve families?
- **Architectural Plans** → **System Test** — do all rooms work together?
- **Detailed Door/Window Designs** → **Integration Test** — do doors and walls fit?
- **Bricklaying (bottom)** → **Unit Test** — is each brick solid?

---

## 2.3 Independent Verification & Validation (IV&V)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Independent Verification & Validation (IV&V)** is the V&V performed by an **organization that is technically, managerially, and financially independent** of the development organization.

### Why Independence Matters

| Independence Level | Description | Defect-Detection Power |
|---|---|---|
| **Low** | Developer tests own code | Poor (self-blindness) |
| **Medium** | Peer developer tests | Better |
| **High** | Internal test team | Good |
| **Highest** | External / third-party (IV&V) | **Excellent** |

### Real-World IV&V Domains
- **Avionics** (DO-178C mandates IV&V)
- **Medical devices** (FDA requires it)
- **Nuclear reactor control systems**
- **Defence & space systems**

---

## 2.4 Quality Concepts — Definitions

### 2.4.1 Quality
> [!NOTE]
> **Quality** is the degree to which a software product meets **specified requirements** (verification view) **AND** satisfies **customer/user needs** (validation view).

**Two industrial definitions you MUST memorise for KTU:**

1. **Crosby's Definition (1968):** *"Quality is conformance to requirements."*
2. **Juran's Definition:** *"Quality is fitness for use."*

### 2.4.2 Quality Control (QC)
> *"Quality Control* is the operational technique and activity used to **fulfil requirements of quality**."* — It is **product-oriented** and focuses on **defect identification**.

### 2.4.3 Quality Assurance (QA)
> *"Quality Assurance* is the **planned systematic pattern of all actions necessary to provide adequate confidence** that the product conforms to established technical requirements."* — It is **process-oriented** and focuses on **defect prevention**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** **A**ssurance **P**revents (process); **C**ontrol **D**etects (product). A-P & C-D.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following formulae appear frequently in KTU Module 1 and Module 2 numerical questions.

### Table 2.1 — Software Quality Metrics Formulas

| # | Metric | Formula | Notation | Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \frac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Size}}$ | Size in KLOC or FP | per KLOC / per FP |
| 2 | **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $DRE = \frac{D_f}{D_f + D_u} \times 100$ | $D_f$ = defects found before release, $D_u$ = defects found after release | **% (0–100)** |
| 3 | **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** | $MTTF = \frac{\sum t_i}{N}$ | Total operating time / total failures | Hours |
| 4 | **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \frac{\sum \text{repair time}}{N}$ | Total repair time / total repairs | Hours |
| 5 | **Availability (A)** | $A = \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} \times 100$ | MTTF + MTTR = MTBF | **% (0–100)** |
| 6 | **Test Effectiveness** | $TE = \frac{\text{Tests that found a bug}}{\text{Total tests executed}} \times 100$ | — | **%** |
| 7 | **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | $CoQ = C_{prev} + C_{app} + C_{intf} + C_{extf}$ | Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure | Currency |
| 8 | **Code Coverage** | $CC = \frac{\text{Statements Executed}}{\text{Total Statements}} \times 100$ | Branch, statement, path coverage | **%** |
| 9 | **Requirement Stability Index (RSI)** | $RSI = \frac{R_s}{R_s + R_c} \times 100$ | $R_s$ = requirements stable, $R_c$ = requirements changed | **%** |
| 10 | **Reliability Growth Function (simplified Musa)** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Failure rate $\lambda$ constant | Probability |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Exam-Caution:** When writing **absolute value** or **magnitude** in KTU answer sheets, never use the pipe symbol inside a markdown table. Instead, use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ (as in $\vert MTTF \vert$ if needed). Although handwritten answers have no such restriction, this matters in the digital answer format.

---

## 2.6 McCall's Quality Model (1977) — The 11 Factors

McCall's model is **the most asked topic** in KTU Module 1 questions worth 7–14 marks. It organises software quality into **three perspectives** mapped against **11 quality factors** and **23 quality criteria**.

### The Three Perspectives (Axes)

1. **Product Operation** — How does the product run?
2. **Product Revision** — How easy is it to maintain?
3. **Product Transition** — Can it adapt to new environments?

### Table 2.2 — McCall's 11 Quality Factors

| Perspective | Quality Factor | Meaning | Sample Criterion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Product Operation | **Correctness** | Extent to which program satisfies spec | Traceability |
| Product Operation | **Reliability** | Ability to perform without failure | Mean time to failure |
| Product Operation | **Efficiency** | Resources used (CPU, memory) | Execution efficiency |
| Product Operation | **Integrity** | Access control and security | Access control |
| Product Operation | **Usability** | Ease of learning and use | Operability |
| Product Revision | **Maintainability** | Ease of fixing bugs | Modifiability |
| Product Revision | **Flexibility** | Ease of changing for new environment | Adaptability |
| Product Revision | **Testability** | Ease of testing | Test instrumentation |
| Product Transition | **Portability** | Ease of moving to new platform | Hardware independence |
| Product Transition | **Reusability** | Use of components in other apps | Code generality |
| Product Transition | **Interoperability** | Interface with other systems | Communication commonality |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "CORIUS-MFR-PRI":**
> **CO**rrectness, **R**eliability, **I**ntegrity, **U**sability, **E**fficiency (Operation) — **M**aintainability, **F**lexibility, **T**estability (Revision) — **P**ortability, **R**eusability, **I**nteroperability (Transition).
> 
> Mnemonic: **C**ats **R**est **I**n **U**nderground **S**tations, **M**ainly **F**or **T**ravel — **P**assengers **R**ide **I**n.

---

## 2.7 Cost of Quality (CoQ) — Detailed Decomposition

CoQ is a KTU Module 1 favourite. It captures **all costs incurred to ensure quality**, including failures.

$$\begin{aligned}
CoQ_{total} &= C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\_failure} + C_{external\_failure}
\end{aligned}$$

| Cost Type | Category | Examples | Stage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **$C_{prev}$** Prevention | Conformance | Training, process improvement, tool acquisition | Before defects occur |
| **$C_{app}$** Appraisal | Conformance | Inspections, testing, reviews | During development |
| **$C_{intf}$** Internal Failure | Non-Conformance | Rework, bug-fixing during dev, retest | Before release |
| **$C_{extf}$** External Failure | Non-Conformance | Warranty claims, patches, customer support, lawsuits | After release |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of 1-10-100 (Boehm):** A defect caught at the **requirements stage** costs **1 unit**. Caught at **design** = 10 units. Caught at **post-release** = **100 units**. This is why **prevention** is the cheapest investment in SQA.

---

## 2.8 Static vs Dynamic V&V — The Authoritative Distinction

| Dimension | Static V&V | Dynamic V&V |
|---|---|---|
| **Execution** | Does NOT execute the code | MUST execute the code |
| **When** | Early in life cycle | Later in life cycle |
| **Cost per defect** | Low | Higher |
| **Examples** | Reviews, inspections, walkthroughs, static analysis | Unit, integration, system, acceptance testing |
| **Tools** | Lint, SonarQube, ESLint, CodeSonar | JUnit, Selenium, Postman, JMeter |
| **Scope** | Documentation, design, code structure | Functional & non-functional behaviour |
| **KTU Tag** | "Verification-heavy" | "Validation-heavy" |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Defect Density & DRE Calculation (Worked Example)

### Problem Statement
A software module of size **20 KLOC** was developed. During testing:
- **45 defects** were found and fixed before release.
- After release, the customer reported **5 more defects** within 3 months.

Calculate:
1. Defect Density (DD)
2. Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)
3. Comment on quality.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**

$$Size = 20 \text{ KLOC}, \quad D_f = 45, \quad D_u = 5$$

**Step 1 — Calculate Defect Density:**

$$\begin{aligned}
DD &= \frac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Size (KLOC)}} \\
  &= \frac{D_f + D_u}{20} \\
  &= \frac{45 + 5}{20} \\
  &= \frac{50}{20} \\
  &= 2.5 \text{ defects per KLOC}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** Industry average DD ranges from **1 to 5 defects/KLOC** for a moderately complex system. A DD of 2.5 is **acceptable**.

**Step 2 — Calculate Defect Removal Efficiency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
DRE &= \frac{D_f}{D_f + D_u} \times 100 \\
    &= \frac{45}{45 + 5} \times 100 \\
    &= \frac{45}{50} \times 100 \\
    &= 0.9 \times 100 \\
    &= 90\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** A DRE of **90%** is considered **good** in commercial software. For safety-critical systems, the target is **≥ 99%**.

**Step 3 — Quality Commentary:**

> [!NOTE]
> The team successfully caught **90% of all defects** before release. The remaining 5% (1 in 20) leaked to the customer. For safety-critical systems, this is unacceptable. For commercial business apps, this is reasonable.

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Cost of Quality Decomposition (Worked Example)

### Problem Statement
A company spent on a software project:
- Prevention: **₹1,00,000**
- Appraisal: **₹1,50,000**
- Internal Failure: **₹3,00,000**
- External Failure: **₹5,00,000**

Calculate:
1. Total Cost of Quality
2. Conformance vs Non-Conformance Cost
3. Recommend an improvement strategy.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Total CoQ:**

$$\begin{aligned}
CoQ_{total} &= C_{prev} + C_{app} + C_{intf} + C_{extf} \\
            &= 1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 \\
            &= 10{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Conformance Cost (Prevention + Appraisal):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{conf} &= C_{prev} + C_{app} \\
         &= 1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 \\
         &= 2{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Non-Conformance Cost (Internal + External Failure):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{nonconf} &= C_{intf} + C_{extf} \\
            &= 3{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 \\
            &= 8{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Ratio Analysis:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Ratio} &= \frac{C_{conf}}{C_{nonconf}} \\
             &= \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \\
             &= 0.3125
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The company is spending **less than 1/3** on prevention/appraisal compared to failure costs. The **optimal** software industry benchmark is **equal** or **higher** spend on conformance.

**Step 5 — Recommendation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategy:** **Double the prevention budget.** Invest in:
> - Formal training (₹50,000)
> - Code review tools (₹30,000)
> - Process audits (₹20,000)
>
> Expected outcome: External failure cost drops from ₹5,00,000 to ~₹1,50,000 — **net savings of ₹2,70,000**, a typical **3-to-1 ROI** on prevention investment.

---

## 3.3 Problem 3 — Availability Computation

### Problem Statement
A server logs the following for 1000 hours of operation:
- **Total Uptime (working hours)** = 950 hours
- **Total Downtime** = 50 hours
- During downtime, **5 failures** occurred with repair times: 8h, 12h, 10h, 15h, 5h.

Calculate MTTF, MTTR, MTBF, and Availability.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):**

$$\begin{aligned}
MTTF &= \frac{\text{Total Uptime}}{\text{Number of Failures}} \\
     &= \frac{950}{5} \\
     &= 190 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):**

$$\begin{aligned}
MTTR &= \frac{\text{Total Repair Time}}{\text{Number of Repairs}} \\
     &= \frac{8 + 12 + 10 + 15 + 5}{5} \\
     &= \frac{50}{5} \\
     &= 10 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):**

$$\begin{aligned}
MTBF &= MTTF + MTTR \\
     &= 190 + 10 \\
     &= 200 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Availability:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{MTTF}{MTBF} \times 100 \\
  &= \frac{190}{200} \times 100 \\
  &= 0.95 \times 100 \\
  &= 95\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** A 95% availability = **"Three Nines less" (99.9%)** for enterprise systems. For mission-critical systems (e.g., medical), the target is **99.999% ("Five Nines")**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — V&V vs QA vs QC vs Testing

This matrix is **the KTU exam table** that board examiners look for. It is mandatory for any 14-mark answer.

| Dimension | Verification | Validation | Quality Assurance (QA) | Quality Control (QC) | Software Testing |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Spec conformance | User need satisfaction | Process correctness | Product correctness | Defect identification |
| **Focus** | Right product built correctly | Right product built | Right process followed | Right product outcomes | Defect discovery |
| **Question** | "Build right?" | "Right product?" | "Right process?" | "Right outcome?" | "Any defects?" |
| **Type** | Static | Dynamic | Managerial | Technical | Technical |
| **When** | Early life cycle | Late life cycle | Throughout | Throughout | Throughout |
| **Example** | Code inspection | System testing | Process audit | Test execution | Running JUnit tests |
| **Cost impact** | Cheap per defect | Expensive per defect | Investment in process | Operational expense | Operational expense |
| **Independence** | Often internal | Often internal/external | External auditor | Internal team | Internal team |

---

## 3.5 McCall's Quality Criterion Mapping — Analytical View

The 11 factors are measured by 23 quality **criteria**. The mapping is a high-yield KTU topic.

| Quality Factor | Quality Criteria (Sample) |
|---|---|
| **Correctness** | Traceability, Completeness, Consistency |
| **Reliability** | Accuracy, Error tolerance, Consistency |
| **Efficiency** | Execution efficiency, Storage efficiency |
| **Integrity** | Access control, Access audit |
| **Usability** | Operability, Training, Communicativeness |
| **Maintainability** | Modifiability, Conciseness, Self-descriptiveness |
| **Flexibility** | Adaptability, Generality, Expandability |
| **Testability** | Instrumentation, Self-contained-ness, Simplicity |
| **Portability** | Hardware independence, Software independence |
| **Reusability** | Code generality, Modularity, Documentation independence |
| **Interoperability** | Communication commonality, Data commonality |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Explain McCall's model with diagram," always draw the **transition criterion–factor matrix** AND briefly explain **two factors with their criteria** to score full 7 marks in the sub-part.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Boehm's V-Model of Software Development

The V-Model is the most frequently drawn diagram in KTU Module 1. The following mermaid block renders a **block-level functional architecture flow** that captures the exact mapping between development and validation phases.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA1["Requirements Specification"] --> nodeB1["Acceptance Testing"]
    nodeA2["System HLD Design"] --> nodeB2["System Testing"]
    nodeA3["Detailed Design LLD"] --> nodeB3["Integration Testing"]
    nodeA4["Coding Implementation"] --> nodeB4["Unit Testing"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC1["Customer Acceptance"]
    nodeA1 --- nodeA2
    nodeA2 --- nodeA3
    nodeA3 --- nodeA4
    nodeB4 --- nodeB3
    nodeB3 --- nodeB2
    nodeB2 --- nodeB1
    nodeA4 --- nodeB4
    style nodeA1 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333
    style nodeA2 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333
    style nodeA3 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333
    style nodeA4 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333
    style nodeB1 fill:#b0e0ff,stroke:#333
    style nodeB2 fill:#b0e0ff,stroke:#333
    style nodeB3 fill:#b0e0ff,stroke:#333
    style nodeB4 fill:#b0e0ff,stroke:#333
    style nodeC1 fill:#c2f0c2,stroke:#333
```

**Legend:**
- 🟡 Yellow boxes = **Verification phases** (document reviews)
- 🔵 Blue boxes = **Validation phases** (test execution)
- 🟢 Green box = **Final acceptance** (operational)

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — V&V Master Workflow (Process Map)

This flowchart maps the **complete V&V decision flow** from project initiation to release. It is a high-yield KTU diagram for 7-mark sub-questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode1["Project Initiated"] --> nodeS1["Define Quality Standards Plan"]
    nodeS1 --> nodeS2["Requirements Review Verification"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3["Design Review Verification"]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4["Code Inspection Walkthrough"]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5{"Static Analysis Pass?"}
    nodeS5 -- No --> nodeS4
    nodeS5 -- Yes --> nodeV1["Unit Testing Validation"]
    nodeV1 --> nodeV2["Integration Testing"]
    nodeV2 --> nodeV3["System Testing"]
    nodeV3 --> nodeV4["Acceptance Testing"]
    nodeV4 --> nodeD1{"Customer Signs Off?"}
    nodeD1 -- No --> nodeV3
    nodeD1 -- Yes --> nodeRel["Software Released"]
    nodeRel --> nodeMaint["Maintenance V and V"]
    style startNode1 fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#000
    style nodeS1 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333
    style nodeS2 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333
    style nodeS3 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333
    style nodeS4 fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333
    style nodeV1 fill:#b0e0ff,stroke:#333
    style nodeV2 fill:#b0e0ff,stroke:#333
    style nodeV3 fill:#b0e0ff,stroke:#333
    style nodeV4 fill:#b0e0ff,stroke:#333
    style nodeRel fill:#c2f0c2,stroke:#333
    style nodeMaint fill:#f0c2f0,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Left-side (yellow):** All static V&V activities (verification).
- **Right-side (blue):** All dynamic V&V activities (validation).
- **Diamond shapes:** Decision gates — verification gates (yellow) and validation gates (blue).
- **Final release (green):** Accepted product.

---

## 4.3 Diagram C — McCall's Quality Model Hierarchy

This tree-style diagram captures the **three perspectives → 11 factors → criteria** relationship.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootQ["Software Quality"] --> nodeOp["Product Operation"]
    rootQ --> nodeRev["Product Revision"]
    rootQ --> nodeTrn["Product Transition"]
    nodeOp --> facC["Correctness"]
    nodeOp --> facR["Reliability"]
    nodeOp --> facE["Efficiency"]
    nodeOp --> facI["Integrity"]
    nodeOp --> facU["Usability"]
    nodeRev --> facM["Maintainability"]
    nodeRev --> facF["Flexibility"]
    nodeRev --> facT["Testability"]
    nodeTrn --> facP["Portability"]
    nodeTrn --> facRe["Reusability"]
    nodeTrn --> facInt["Interoperability"]
    facC --> critC1["Traceability"]
    facC --> critC2["Completeness"]
    facR --> critR1["Accuracy"]
    facR --> critR2["Mean Time To Failure"]
    facE --> critE1["Execution Efficiency"]
    facI --> critI1["Access Control"]
    facU --> critU1["Operability"]
    facM --> critM1["Modifiability"]
    facF --> critF1["Adaptability"]
    facT --> critT1["Simplicity"]
    facP --> critP1["Hardware Independence"]
    facRe --> critRe1["Code Generality"]
    facInt --> critInt1["Communication Commonality"]
    style rootQ fill:#222,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style nodeOp fill:#ffe082,stroke:#333
    style nodeRev fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#333
    style nodeTrn fill:#90caf9,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- 🟨 Yellow = Operation perspective (5 factors)
- 🟩 Green = Revision perspective (3 factors)
- 🟦 Blue = Transition perspective (3 factors)
- Each factor maps to measurable **criteria** (e.g., Correctness → Traceability).

---

## 4.4 Diagram D — Cost of Quality Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootC["Cost of Quality COQ"] --> nodeConf["Conformance Costs"]
    rootC --> nodeNonConf["Non-Conformance Costs"]
    nodeConf --> nodePrev["Prevention Costs"]
    nodeConf --> nodeApp["Appraisal Costs"]
    nodePrev --> costPrev1["Training"]
    nodePrev --> costPrev2["Process Improvement"]
    nodePrev --> costPrev3["Tool Acquisition"]
    nodeApp --> costApp1["Inspections"]
    nodeApp --> costApp2["Testing"]
    nodeApp --> costApp3["Reviews"]
    nodeNonConf --> nodeInt["Internal Failure"]
    nodeNonConf --> nodeExt["External Failure"]
    nodeInt --> costInt1["Rework"]
    nodeInt --> costInt2["Retest"]
    nodeInt --> costInt3["Bug Fixes"]
    nodeExt --> costExt1["Warranty Claims"]
    nodeExt --> costExt2["Patches"]
    nodeExt --> costExt3["Customer Support"]
    style rootC fill:#222,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style nodeConf fill:#c2f0c2,stroke:#333
    style nodeNonConf fill:#ffabab,stroke:#333
    style nodePrev fill:#d0f0d0,stroke:#333
    style nodeApp fill:#d0f0d0,stroke:#333
    style nodeInt fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#333
    style nodeExt fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#333
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- 🟢 Green = Conformance (good) costs — invest MORE here.
- 🔴 Red = Non-conformance (bad) costs — invest LESS here.
- **Optimal strategy:** Shift budget from red to green. This is the *quality economics* principle.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> Differentiate between **Verification and Validation** in software quality assurance. State Boehm's definition for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| Boehm's Question | "Are we building the product **right**?" | "Are we building the **right** product?" |
| Focus | Specifications | User needs |
| Nature | Static (mostly) | Dynamic |
| Examples | Reviews, inspections, walkthroughs | Unit, integration, system, acceptance testing |

Verification checks **conformance to design**; validation checks **fitness for use**.

*(Examiner's note: A 2-row table with Boeing's quote = 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
> What is **Software Quality Assurance (SQA)**? List any four activities performed under SQA.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
SQA is a systematic, planned set of coordinated activities ensuring software work products and processes conform to requirements, plans, and standards. Four activities:

1. **Technical Reviews** — verifying documents (SRS, design, code).
2. **Software Testing** — executing programs to find defects.
3. **Process & Product Standards** — enforcing IEEE/ISO standards.
4. **Process Measurement & Metrics** — defect density, DRE, MTTF.
5. **Test Coverage Analysis** — measuring code coverage.

*(Any 4 of the above = 3 marks.)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question 1 (A) — Verification & Validation Deep Dive
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, University Question]**
> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concepts of **Verification and Validation** in software quality assurance. State Boehm's definitions and give **two examples** of activities for each.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Draw and explain **Boehm's V-Model** of software development. How does it demonstrate the relationship between development and testing phases?

### ✅ Model Answer — Question 1(A)

#### (a) Verification & Validation Concepts [7 marks]

**Verification** is the process of evaluating a software system or component to determine whether the products of a given development phase satisfy the conditions imposed at the start of that phase. As per **Barry W. Boehm**, *"Verification: Are we building the product right?"* It focuses on **specification conformance** and is largely a **static activity**.

**Two verification examples:**
1. **Code Inspection (Fagan Inspection):** A structured, group-based review where code is read line-by-line to find defects without execution.
2. **Requirements Review:** Checking the SRS document for completeness, consistency, unambiguity, and traceability.

**Validation** is the process of evaluating a software system during or at the end of the development process to determine whether it satisfies the specified user requirements. Boehm's definition: *"Validation: Are we building the right product?"* It focuses on **user needs and fitness for use** and is largely a **dynamic activity**.

**Two validation examples:**
1. **System Testing:** Executing the complete integrated system to verify it meets functional and non-functional requirements.
2. **Acceptance Testing (UAT):** End-users run the system in a real or simulated environment to confirm fitness for business use.

The two are complementary: a system can be verified but not validated, or vice versa. Both are required for high-quality software.

#### (b) Boehm's V-Model [7 marks]

The **V-Model** is a software development life cycle model in which **development phases (descending left arm)** are directly mapped to corresponding **testing phases (ascending right arm)**. The shape forms the letter **"V"**.

| Development Phase (Left Arm) | Corresponding Test (Right Arm) |
|---|---|
| User Requirements | Acceptance Testing |
| System Requirements / HLD | System Testing |
| Detailed Design / LLD | Integration Testing |
| Implementation / Coding | Unit Testing |

**Explanation:**
- The **left arm** represents **decomposition** of requirements into design and code. Each phase is a **verification** checkpoint.
- The **right arm** represents **integration and testing** — code is built up from units to subsystems to the full system, validating against the original user needs.
- The **base of V** = coding; everything above is built on code correctness.
- The **V-Model** explicitly enforces that **for every development activity, a corresponding test exists**, ensuring that verification and validation are **planned and traceable** from day one.
- This model is an **extension of the waterfall model** with testing mapped alongside development.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students draw a **waterfall model** instead of a V-Model. The defining feature of the V-Model is the **right arm — testing phases parallel to development**. A V-shape **without the testing arm** scores ZERO for the diagram.

---

### Question 1 (B) — Quality, QA, QC & McCall's Model
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 1]**
> **(a) [7 marks]** Define **Software Quality, Quality Assurance, and Quality Control**. Differentiate between Quality Assurance and Quality Control. State the **two industrial definitions of quality** (Crosby and Juran).
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Explain **McCall's Quality Model** in detail. List the **three perspectives, 11 quality factors, and any 5 quality criteria**.

### ✅ Model Answer — Question 1(B)

#### (a) Software Quality, QA, QC [7 marks]

**Software Quality:** The degree to which a software product meets specified requirements (conformance) AND satisfies customer/user needs (fitness for use).

**Two industrial definitions (must memorise for KTU):**
- **Philip Crosby:** *"Quality is conformance to requirements."*
- **Joseph Juran:** *"Quality is fitness for use."*

**Quality Assurance (QA):** A planned, systematic pattern of all actions necessary to provide adequate confidence that the product conforms to established technical requirements. **QA is process-oriented** and focuses on **defect prevention**.

**Quality Control (QC):** The operational techniques and activities used to fulfil quality requirements. **QC is product-oriented** and focuses on **defect identification**.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Quality Assurance (QA) | Quality Control (QC) |
|---|---|---|
| Orientation | Process | Product |
| Focus | Prevention | Detection |
| Stage | Throughout SDLC | Mostly testing phase |
| Tools | Audits, standards, process improvement | Test cases, reviews, inspections |
| Example | CMMI audit, ISO 9001 certification | Running test cases, defect logging |

#### (b) McCall's Quality Model [7 marks]

McCall's model (1977) organises software quality into **three perspectives** measured by **11 quality factors**, which are in turn evaluated by **23 quality criteria**.

**Three Perspectives:**
1. **Product Operation** — How does the product run? (Performance, reliability during use)
2. **Product Revision** — How easy is it to maintain and change?
3. **Product Transition** — Can it adapt to new environments?

**11 Quality Factors:**

| Perspective | Factors |
|---|---|
| **Product Operation** | Correctness, Reliability, Efficiency, Integrity, Usability |
| **Product Revision** | Maintainability, Flexibility, Testability |
| **Product Transition** | Portability, Reusability, Interoperability |

**5 Quality Criteria (sample from the 23):**
1. **Traceability** — ability to trace each requirement to design/code.
2. **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** — reliability measure.
3. **Execution Efficiency** — CPU/memory utilisation.
4. **Modifiability** — ease of change.
5. **Hardware Independence** — portability criterion.

The **transition criterion–factor matrix** is the standard way to depict McCall's model. Each factor is rated on a scale (e.g., 0–10) for each quality criterion, and a weighted average gives the **overall quality index**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only the 11 factors but **forget the 3 perspectives**. The perspectives are the **structural backbone** of the model — omitting them loses 2 marks.

---

## 🎯 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings — Top Pitfalls for This Topic

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Losing Marks in Module 1:**
> 1. **Confusing V&V with Testing:** Many students equate "V&V" with "Software Testing." V&V is the broader umbrella; testing is one activity within validation.
> 2. **Skipping the Boehm Quote:** A full-mark answer **always** mentions *"Are we building the product right?"* and *"Are we building the right product?"* — verbatim. Without these, expect a 1–2 mark deduction.
> 3. **Mixing up QA vs QC:** Writing *"QA detects defects"* or *"QC prevents defects"* is an instant 1-mark cut.
> 4. **Forgetting the V-shape right arm:** A V-Model drawn without the testing arm is a waterfall model. Zero marks for the diagram.
> 5. **McCall's Model — listing factors only:** The examiner expects the **3 perspectives** + 11 factors + sample criteria. Listing factors alone is partial credit only.
> 6. **No numerical example in CoQ answer:** If the question mentions "Cost of Quality," always include a **worked numerical example** with ₹ amounts. 3 extra marks guaranteed.
> 7. **Static vs Dynamic confusion:** Verification ≠ static analysis only. **Reviews, inspections, walkthroughs** are also verification. Listing only "static analysis" loses marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — V&V in SQA — The 12-Point Revision Sheet**

1. ✅ **SQA** is a **process-oriented, umbrella activity** spanning the entire SDLC.
2. ✅ **Verification** = "Building the product **right**" (Boehm) = static, spec-focused.
3. ✅ **Validation** = "Building the **right** product" (Boehm) = dynamic, user-focused.
4. ✅ **V&V are complementary, not substitutes.** Both are required.
5. ✅ The **V-Model** maps each development phase to a corresponding test phase (4 pairs).
6. ✅ **IV&V** = third-party V&V, used in safety-critical domains (avionics, medical, nuclear).
7. ✅ **Quality (Crosby)** = conformance to requirements; **Quality (Juran)** = fitness for use.
8. ✅ **QA** = prevention (process); **QC** = detection (product). A-P & C-D mnemonic.
9. ✅ **McCall's Model** = 3 perspectives × 11 factors × 23 criteria. Mnemonic: **CORIUS-MFR-PRI**.
10. ✅ **Defect Density** = Defects/Size (KLOC). **DRE** = $D_f / (D_f + D_u) \times 100$.
11. ✅ **CoQ** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure. Optimal = more on prevention.
12. ✅ **Rule of 1-10-100:** Requirements defect = 1 unit cost; design = 10; post-release = 100. Prevention pays off.

> **Best-of-Luck for KTU Board Exams — Master the Boehm Quote and the V-Model, and you already secure 4–6 marks in every SQA answer.** 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Black Box Testing - Requirements-Based Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT747 - SOFTWARE TESTING AND QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Black Box Testing - Requirements-Based Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Black Box Testing — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Black Box Testing** (also called *specification-based testing*, *behavioral testing*, or *functional testing*) is a software testing methodology in which the internal structure, design, implementation, and internal paths of the system under test (SUT) are **NOT** known to the tester. The tester interacts with the system purely through its **inputs** and observes the corresponding **outputs**, comparing actual behavior against the specification document.

**Key Academic Vocabulary (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
- **SUT** — System Under Test
- **IUT** — Implementation Under Test
- **Test Oracle** — The authoritative source (specification) used to determine whether the observed output is correct
- **Equivalence Partitioning** — Input-domain partitioning technique
- **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)** — Edge-value testing technique

---

## 1.2 Requirements-Based Testing — The KTU 2024 Focus Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Requirements-Based Testing** is a *systematic, traceable* sub-category of Black Box Testing where every single **test case is derived directly, explicitly, and unambiguously from a documented requirement**. Each requirement maps to **one or more test cases**, and each test case traces back to **one or more requirements**. This bidirectional mapping is enforced via a **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme PECNT747 syllabus, Requirements-Based Testing is treated as a **verification activity** — answering the engineering question:

$$ \text{Verification} = \text{``Are we building the product RIGHT, as specified?''} $$

as opposed to Validation which asks: *“Are we building the RIGHT product?”*

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

### Analogy 1 — The Vending Machine 🧃

Imagine you walk up to a sealed **vending machine**. You cannot see the motors, springs, coin validators, or wiring inside (the "**black box**"). You only have access to:
- The **coin slot** (input)
- The **selection buttons** (input)
- The **dispensing tray** (output)

You test it by inserting different coin combinations, pressing different buttons, and checking whether the correct item (or the correct change) comes out. You judge success **only against the user manual/label** on the machine (the "**requirements document**").

If the manual says “₹20 → Coke”, and you insert ₹20 and press Coke but get Fanta — that is a **failure**, even if the machine appears to be functioning electrically perfectly. This judgment is exactly how **Requirements-Based Testing** works.

### Analogy 2 — Driving Test Against the Rule Book 🚗

A driving test examiner has **no interest** in how the engine combustion cycle works (internal code). They check the driver **only against the documented traffic rules** (requirements). Every rule (requirement) → one or more driving maneuvers (test cases). The examiner maintains a checklist (RTM) to verify *every* rule was demonstrated.

> [!TIP]
> **The Two Pillars of Requirements-Based Testing:**
> 1. **Specification-Driven** — Test cases come ONLY from the specification (no hidden assumptions).
> 2. **Traceability-Driven** — Every test case can be traced back to the requirement it verifies, and every requirement has at least one test case verifying it.

---

## 1.4 Place in the Black Box Testing Family

```
                    BLACK BOX TESTING
                          │
   ┌──────────┬───────────┼────────────┬──────────────┐
   │          │           │            │              │
   ▼          ▼           ▼            ▼              ▼
Require-   Equivalence  Boundary    Decision       State-Based
ments-     Partitioning  Value      Table          Testing
Based                   Analysis    Testing
Testing
   │
   ├── Functional Requirements Testing
   ├── Non-Functional Requirements Testing
   └── Domain Requirements Testing
```

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Phase Operational Workflow of Requirements-Based Testing

### **Phase 1 — Requirements Acquisition & Review**
- Gather all approved requirements documents (SRS — Software Requirements Specification per IEEE 830).
- Identify ambiguities, contradictions, incompleteness.
- Resolve with stakeholders **before** test design.

### **Phase 2 — Requirement Categorization**
Requirements are classified into four KTU-recognized categories:

| # | Category | Description | Example |
|---|----------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **Functional Requirements (FR)** | What the system does | "User can reset password via email link" |
| 2 | **Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)** | How well the system performs | "Password reset email must arrive within 30 seconds" |
| 3 | **Domain Requirements (DR)** | Business rules of the application domain | "Loan eligibility: age ≥ 21 and income ≥ ₹25,000" |
| 4 | **Interface Requirements (IR)** | Interaction with external systems | "API must accept JSON payload over HTTPS port 443" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** Examiners frequently ask students to *differentiate between Functional and Non-Functional requirements* with examples. Always give at least one example for each.

### **Phase 3 — Test Condition Identification**
For each requirement, identify one or more **test conditions** (also called *testable conditions*). A test condition is a specific aspect of the requirement that can be independently verified.

**Example transformation:**
- **Requirement R1:** "The login form shall accept usernames between 6 and 20 alphanumeric characters."
- **Derived Test Conditions:**
    - TC1.1 → Username of length 6 (lower boundary)
    - TC1.2 → Username of length 20 (upper boundary)
    - TC1.3 → Username of length 13 (interior)
    - TC1.4 → Username with special characters (negative)

### **Phase 4 — Test Case Specification**
Each test condition expands into a fully formed test case with:
- **Test Case ID** (e.g., TC_R1_01)
- **Requirement Reference** (e.g., REQ-SRS-001)
- **Pre-conditions**
- **Inputs** (with valid + invalid data)
- **Test Steps**
- **Expected Result** (from spec)
- **Actual Result** (filled at execution)
- **Pass/Fail Criteria**
- **Post-conditions**

### **Phase 5 — Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) Construction**
Establish **bidirectional traceability**.

### **Phase 6 — Execution & Coverage Analysis**
Run test cases, log defects, compute coverage metrics.

---

## 2.2 Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) — Formal Structure

The **RTM** is a table (often implemented in tools like Jira, Azure DevOps, IBM DOORS) that records the mapping between requirements, test cases, test execution, and defects.

A typical RTM column set:

| Req ID | Req Description | Priority | Test Case ID | Test Description | Exec Status | Defect ID | Test Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|

**Two Cardinal Directional Traces:**
- **Forward Trace:** Requirement → Test Case → Defect
- **Backward Trace:** Defect → Test Case → Requirement

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Coverage Metrics

| # | Metric | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Requirements Coverage (RC)** | $\text{RC} = \dfrac{\text{Number of requirements with at least 1 test case}}{\text{Total number of approved requirements}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed in **%**. Target in industry: **100%** for safety-critical. |
| 2 | **Test Case Coverage (TCC)** | $\text{TCC} = \dfrac{\text{Number of executed test cases}}{\text{Total number of planned test cases}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed in **%**. |
| 3 | **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $\text{DRE} = \dfrac{\text{Defects found before release}}{\text{Defects found before release} + \text{Defects found after release}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed in **%**. Higher is better. |
| 4 | **Requirement Volatility (RV)** | $\text{RV} = \dfrac{\text{Number of changed requirements}}{\text{Total number of original requirements}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed in **%**. Indicates spec instability. |
| 5 | **Test Pass Rate (TPR)** | $\text{TPR} = \dfrac{\text{Number of passed test cases}}{\text{Total number of executed test cases}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed in **%**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute value notation rule:** When writing these formulas in your answer sheet, **never use the vertical bar `|` symbol directly inside a markdown table row** — it breaks the markdown parser. Use LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` instead. Example: write $\vert A \vert$ not $\vert A \vert$ in raw markdown.

---

## 2.4 Why Requirements-Based Testing? — Engineering Utility

1. **Coverage Completeness:** Forces complete verification of every documented requirement.
2. **Ambiguity Detection:** Reveals missing, ambiguous, or contradictory requirements early.
3. **Scope Control:** Prevents *scope creep* by anchoring tests to the original specification.
4. **Defect Cost Reduction:** Finding requirement ambiguities in the test-design phase is up to **100× cheaper** than finding them post-deployment.
5. **Audit & Compliance:** Industries like medical devices (FDA), aviation (DO-178C), and automotive (ISO 26262) **legally require** bidirectional traceability.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Deriving Test Cases From a Requirement

### Given Requirement (From a Banking ATM SRS)

> **REQ-SRS-014 (Functional Requirement):**
> *"The ATM shall allow a customer to withdraw cash in multiples of ₹100, with a minimum withdrawal amount of ₹100 and a maximum withdrawal amount of ₹10,000 per transaction. If the user is a Senior Citizen (age ≥ 60), the maximum limit is ₹25,000 per transaction."*

**Pre-conditions:** Valid ATM card inserted, PIN verified, sufficient account balance.

---

### Step 1 — Decompose the Requirement Into Test Conditions

Decomposition logic in plain text:

- The wording "multiples of ₹100" implies the **modular condition** $W \equiv 0 \pmod{100}$, where $W$ is the withdrawal amount.
- The wording "minimum ₹100" is a **lower boundary condition**.
- The wording "maximum ₹10,000" is an **upper boundary condition**.
- The wording "Senior Citizen → max ₹25,000" introduces a **conditional boundary**.

Hence we obtain five logically distinct test conditions:
1. Lower boundary value testing
2. Upper boundary value testing (non-senior)
3. Conditional upper boundary testing (senior citizen)
4. Modular/invalid increment testing
5. Equivalence partition (interior valid value)

---

### Step 2 — Construct the Test Case Set

| TC ID | Requirement | Profile | Input W (₹) | Expected Output | Boundary Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| TC_R14_01 | REQ-SRS-014 | Regular | 100 | Dispense ₹100; balance debited; receipt printed | Lower boundary |
| TC_R14_02 | REQ-SRS-014 | Regular | 10000 | Dispense ₹10,000; balance debited; receipt printed | Upper boundary (regular) |
| TC_R14_03 | REQ-SRS-014 | Regular | 999 | Reject transaction with message "Amount must be multiple of ₹100" | Just-below modular |
| TC_R14_04 | REQ-SRS-014 | Regular | 10001 | Reject transaction with message "Exceeds maximum limit" | Just-above upper boundary |
| TC_R14_05 | REQ-SRS-014 | Senior | 25000 | Dispense ₹25,000; balance debited; receipt printed | Conditional upper boundary |
| TC_R14_06 | REQ-SRS-014 | Senior | 25001 | Reject transaction with message "Exceeds senior maximum" | Just-above conditional upper |
| TC_R14_07 | REQ-SRS-014 | Regular | 5500 | Dispense ₹5,500; balance debited; receipt printed | Interior equivalence |
| TC_R14_08 | REQ-SRS-014 | Senior | 12345 | Reject — not multiple of ₹100 | Modular violation |

---

### Step 3 — Build the Requirements Traceability Matrix (Forward Trace)

| Requirement | Priority | Test Cases Mapped | Executed? | Status | Defects Raised |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| REQ-SRS-014 | High | TC_R14_01, TC_R14_02, TC_R14_03, TC_R14_04, TC_R14_05, TC_R14_06, TC_R14_07, TC_R14_08 | All 8 | 7 Pass / 1 Fail | DEF-2031 |

---

### Step 4 — Compute the Requirements Coverage Metric

We have the following values:

$$ \text{Total Approved Requirements in SRS} = 14 $$

$$ \text{Requirements with at least 1 test case} = 14 $$

We now substitute these values into the requirements coverage formula. The substitution step is shown explicitly below so the underlying arithmetic is unambiguous.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RC} &= \frac{\text{Number of requirements with at least 1 test case}}{\text{Total number of approved requirements}} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
         &= \frac{14}{14} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
         &= 1 \times 100\% \\[6pt]
         &= 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Every single approved requirement has at least one test case verifying it. This satisfies the industry gold standard for **safety-critical systems**.

---

### Step 5 — Compute the Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)

Suppose during the **requirements-based testing phase**, the team raised **18 defects**. Post-release customer reports in the first quarter identified **2 additional defects** that had escaped testing.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DRE} &= \frac{\text{Defects found before release}}{\text{Defects found before release} + \text{Defects found after release}} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
          &= \frac{18}{18 + 2} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
          &= \frac{18}{20} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
          &= 0.90 \times 100\% \\[6pt]
          &= 90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A **DRE of 90%** is considered acceptable for commercial software. For mission-critical aerospace systems, KTU-aligned industry benchmark requires DRE $\geq 99\%$.

---

### Step 6 — Backward Trace (From Defect to Root-Caused Requirement)

Now suppose a defect **DEF-2031** was raised during the execution of test case **TC_R14_03** (input ₹999 was unexpectedly accepted and dispensed ₹900 due to a programmer truncating the last two digits — a classic integer-truncation bug).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Backward Trace: } \text{DEF-2031} &\longrightarrow \text{TC\_R14\_03} \longrightarrow \text{REQ-SRS-014} \longrightarrow \text{SRS Section 3.4 (Functional)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This single-line backward trace proves (for audit purposes) that the defect has a **clear causal anchor in a documented requirement**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — When To Use Requirements-Based Testing

| Scenario | Is RBT Suitable? | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Well-documented SRS exists | ✅ Highly Suitable | Clear basis for test derivation |
| Requirements frequently changing | ⚠️ Conditionally Suitable | RTM must be continuously regenerated — high overhead |
| Legacy system with no spec | ❌ Not Suitable | No authoritative source for test oracle |
| Agile / User-story-based project | ✅ Suitable | Map user stories as "requirements" |
| Safety-critical (medical, aviation) | ✅ Mandatory | Regulatory requirement |
| Exploratory bug-hunt | ❌ Not Suitable | Use exploratory testing instead |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]**
**Q:** Define *Requirements-Based Testing*. Mention any two of its key characteristics.

**Model Answer:**
Requirements-Based Testing is a black-box testing technique in which test cases are designed **directly from the documented requirements specification** (SRS). Each test case is traceable to one or more requirements, and each requirement is verified by at least one test case.

**Two key characteristics (any two):**
1. **Specification-Driven:** Test design depends solely on the specification, not on internal code knowledge.
2. **Bidirectional Traceability:** A Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) links requirements → test cases → defects and vice versa.
3. **Completeness Verification:** It exposes missing, ambiguous, or contradictory requirements before test execution.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**
**Q:** Differentiate between *Functional Requirements* and *Non-Functional Requirements*. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Functional Requirements | Non-Functional Requirements |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Specify *what* the system must do (features, behavior) | Specify *how well* the system performs the function (quality attributes) |
| **Verifiable via** | Input-output behavior testing | Performance, usability, reliability, scalability metrics |
| **Example** | "The system shall allow users to reset their password using a registered email." | "The password reset email shall be delivered within 30 seconds of request." |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **process of Requirements-Based Testing**. Discuss the role of the Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the following requirement from the SRS of an **Online Bookstore**:
> *"A registered customer can place an order for a maximum of **5 different books** in a single transaction. The order amount must be **between ₹200 and ₹5,000**. A flat **10% discount** is applied if the order amount exceeds ₹1,000. The system shall reject orders whose delivery PIN code is **not serviceable**."*

Derive at least **8 test cases** using Requirements-Based Testing technique and build the RTM. Compute the **Requirements Coverage** assuming the SRS has 12 requirements and all 12 have been mapped to test cases. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer for (a):

**Requirements-Based Testing Process (Diagram):**

```
Approved SRS
      ↓
Requirement Categorization (FR / NFR / DR / IR)
      ↓
Test Condition Identification
      ↓
Test Case Specification
      ↓
Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)
      ↓
Test Case Review
      ↓
Test Execution
      ↓
Coverage & Defect Analysis
      ↓
Test Closure Report
```

**Role of the RTM:**

The **Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM)** is a structured document (usually a table) that records the relationship between **requirements, test cases, test execution status, and defects**. It serves four essential roles:

1. **Completeness Verification** — Ensures every requirement has at least one corresponding test case.
2. **Forward Traceability** — From each requirement to its mapped test cases and any defects raised.
3. **Backward Traceability** — From each defect back to the requirement that caused it (root-cause analysis).
4. **Change Impact Analysis** — When a requirement changes, RTM instantly identifies all impacted test cases.
5. **Audit Compliance** — Mandatory evidence for regulated industries (FDA, DO-178C, ISO 26262).

---

### Model Answer for (b):

**Derived Test Cases:**

| TC ID | Requirement | Input Scenario | Expected Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| TC_OB_01 | REQ-OB-01 | 5 distinct books, total = ₹200 | Order accepted, no discount |
| TC_OB_02 | REQ-OB-01 | 6 distinct books, total = ₹600 | Order rejected — exceeds 5 book limit |
| TC_OB_03 | REQ-OB-02 | 3 books, total = ₹199 | Order rejected — below minimum amount |
| TC_OB_04 | REQ-OB-02 | 4 books, total = ₹5,000 | Order accepted, no discount |
| TC_OB_05 | REQ-OB-02 | 3 books, total = ₹5,001 | Order rejected — exceeds maximum amount |
| TC_OB_06 | REQ-OB-03 | 3 books, total = ₹1,500 | Order accepted, 10% discount applied (₹150 off) |
| TC_OB_07 | REQ-OB-03 | 3 books, total = ₹1,000 | Order accepted, no discount (boundary) |
| TC_OB_08 | REQ-OB-04 | Delivery PIN = 682001 (serviceable) | Order accepted |
| TC_OB_09 | REQ-OB-04 | Delivery PIN = 999999 (non-serviceable) | Order rejected |

**Requirements Traceability Matrix:**

| Req ID | Req Description | Priority | Test Case IDs | Execution Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| REQ-OB-01 | Max 5 books per order | High | TC_OB_01, TC_OB_02 | Passed |
| REQ-OB-02 | Order amount ₹200–₹5,000 | High | TC_OB_03, TC_OB_04, TC_OB_05 | Passed |
| REQ-OB-03 | 10% discount above ₹1,000 | Medium | TC_OB_06, TC_OB_07 | Passed |
| REQ-OB-04 | Reject non-serviceable PIN | High | TC_OB_08, TC_OB_09 | Passed |

**Requirements Coverage Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RC} &= \frac{\text{Number of requirements with at least 1 test case}}{\text{Total number of approved requirements}} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
         &= \frac{12}{12} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
         &= 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Requirements Coverage = **100%** — every approved requirement has been mapped to at least one test case.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** What are the **advantages and limitations** of Requirements-Based Testing? Compare it with exploratory testing. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A login module of an e-commerce site has the following requirement:
> *"User passwords must be **8–16 characters** long, contain at least **one uppercase letter**, **one digit**, and **one special character** from the set `@#$%&*`. The system must lock the account after **3 consecutive failed attempts**."*

Using Requirements-Based Testing, derive at least **8 test cases** (covering valid passwords, invalid passwords, and account-locking behavior). Also construct a brief RTM and compute **Defect Removal Efficiency** if 25 defects were found pre-release and 3 defects were found post-release. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer for (a):

**Advantages of Requirements-Based Testing:**

1. **Complete coverage verification** — RTM guarantees no requirement is skipped.
2. **Detects specification defects early** — ambiguous or missing requirements surface during test design.
3. **Supports audit & regulatory compliance** — required in safety-critical domains.
4. **Improves scope management** — prevents scope creep.
5. **Enables impact analysis** — when a requirement changes, RTM identifies affected test cases.

**Limitations of Requirements-Based Testing:**

1. **Requires well-documented, stable requirements** — fails on legacy or agile-light projects.
2. **Cannot find defects not covered by any requirement** (i.e., unspecified behavior).
3. **Time-consuming for large specifications** — RTM maintenance overhead is high.
4. **Tester never explores alternate usage paths** — limited creativity.
5. **Spec errors propagate** — if the SRS is wrong, the tests are wrong.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Requirements-Based Testing | Exploratory Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Pre-written specification | Tester skill, charters, scenarios |
| **Test Design** | Done before execution | Done in parallel with execution |
| **Documentation** | Heavy (test cases + RTM) | Light (session notes only) |
| **Coverage** | Measurable, traceable | Subjective |
| **Defect Detection** | Misses non-spec bugs | Excellent for non-obvious bugs |
| **Best Used For** | Compliance, regression | New features, time-boxed charters |

---

### Model Answer for (b):

**Derived Test Cases for the Login Module:**

| TC ID | Password Input | Expected Result | Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| TC_LP_01 | "Pass@123" (8 chars, upper, digit, special) | Login successful | REQ-LP-01 |
| TC_LP_02 | "P@1234567890ab1" (16 chars, all rules met) | Login successful | REQ-LP-01 |
| TC_LP_03 | "Pass@12" (only 7 chars) | Reject — too short | REQ-LP-01 |
| TC_LP_04 | "password@123" (no uppercase) | Reject — missing uppercase | REQ-LP-01 |
| TC_LP_05 | "PASSWORD@123" (no digit) | Reject — missing digit | REQ-LP-01 |
| TC_LP_06 | "Pass1234" (no special char) | Reject — missing special character | REQ-LP-01 |
| TC_LP_07 | "Pass@#$%@123" (special char outside set) | Reject — special char not allowed | REQ-LP-01 |
| TC_LP_08 | 1st wrong attempt | Login failed, counter = 1 | REQ-LP-02 |
| TC_LP_09 | 2nd wrong attempt | Login failed, counter = 2 | REQ-LP-02 |
| TC_LP_10 | 3rd wrong attempt | Account locked, alert sent | REQ-LP-02 |
| TC_LP_11 | 4th attempt with correct password | Still locked — counter does not reset on lock | REQ-LP-02 |

**Mini-RTM:**

| Req ID | Description | TC IDs |
|---|---|---|
| REQ-LP-01 | Password validation rules | TC_LP_01 through TC_LP_07 |
| REQ-LP-02 | Account locking after 3 failures | TC_LP_08, TC_LP_09, TC_LP_10, TC_LP_11 |

**Defect Removal Efficiency Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DRE} &= \frac{\text{Defects found before release}}{\text{Defects found before release} + \text{Defects found after release}} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
          &= \frac{25}{25 + 3} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
          &= \frac{25}{28} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
          &= 0.8929 \times 100\% \\[6pt]
          &\approx 89.29\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A **DRE of ≈ 89.29%** is acceptable for commercial software but should be raised to ≥ 99% for safety-critical systems (aviation, medical).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Requirements-Based Testing Answers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Verification with Validation** — Verification = "building the product right" (matches spec). Validation = "building the right product" (meets user need). Examiners deduct marks if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Skipping the RTM** — Even if you derive beautiful test cases, omitting the RTM table costs 2–3 marks. **Always include the RTM.**
> 3. **Forgetting boundary values** — When a requirement mentions a range (e.g., "8–16 characters"), the boundaries 8 and 16 **must** each have a test case.
> 4. **Wrong formula notation** — Writing percentages as decimals (e.g., `0.90` instead of `90%`) loses a mark.
> 5. **Mixing up Forward and Backward Traceability** — Forward = Req → Test → Defect. Backward = Defect → Test → Req.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** — When deriving test cases, state any assumptions (e.g., "assuming user is registered").
> 7. **Numerical errors in DRE/RC** — Always show the **substitution step** explicitly; do not jump to the final answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> ✅ Requirements-Based Testing = Black Box testing where test cases are derived **directly from the SRS**.
>
> ✅ It is a **Verification** activity (not Validation).
>
> ✅ The four requirement categories = **FR, NFR, DR, IR**.
>
> ✅ The five lifecycle phases = Categorize → Identify Test Conditions → Specify Test Cases → Build RTM → Execute & Measure.
>
> ✅ RTM provides **Forward Trace** (Req → Test → Defect) and **Backward Trace** (Defect → Test → Req).
>
> ✅ Key metric formulas (must memorize):
> $$\text{RC} = \frac{\text{Reqs with TC}}{\text{Total Reqs}} \times 100\%$$
> $$\text{DRE} = \frac{\text{Pre-release defects}}{\text{Pre + Post release defects}} \times 100\%$$
> $$\text{TPR} = \frac{\text{Passed TCs}}{\text{Executed TCs}} \times 100\%$$
> $$\text{RV} = \frac{\text{Changed Reqs}}{\text{Original Reqs}} \times 100\%$$
>
> ✅ **Use `\vert` or `\mid`** in LaTeX, never a raw `|` symbol, when writing absolute-value expressions inside markdown tables.
>
> ✅ Always derive **boundary value test cases** when the requirement specifies a range.
>
> ✅ **Bidirectional traceability** is mandatory for safety-critical systems (DO-178C, ISO 26262, FDA).
>
> ✅ RBT is **specification-driven**; it cannot find defects arising from unspecified behavior.
>
> ✅ Industry gold-standard **Requirements Coverage = 100%** for safety-critical, ≥ 95% for commercial.
>
> ✅ **DRE ≥ 99%** is the benchmark for mission-critical systems; ~85–90% is acceptable for commercial.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boundary Value Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT747 - SOFTWARE TESTING AND QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boundary Value Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)** is a systematic **black-box test case design technique** in which test cases are designed to exercise the **boundary conditions** of an input domain partition. It is based on the empirical observation in software engineering that **errors are most frequently encountered at the edges (extremes) of an input range rather than in the central (nominal) region**.

Formally, if a program's input variable $x$ is constrained to lie within a closed range $[a, b]$, then Boundary Value Analysis targets the following ordered set of critical values:

$$\{a-1,\; a,\; a+\Delta,\; \text{nominal},\; b-\Delta,\; b,\; b+1\}$$

where $\Delta$ denotes the **smallest valid increment** above the lower bound, and the term "nominal" denotes any valid interior value. Test cases are constructed by varying **one variable at a time to its boundary extreme**, while keeping all other variables at their nominal values. This is known as the **Single Fault Assumption (SFA)** in KTU parlance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tollbooth" Intuition

Imagine you are driving a truck whose **height must lie strictly between 2.0 m and 3.5 m** to safely pass under a tollbooth. A structural engineer would never test the truck only at the comfortable "middle" height (e.g., 2.7 m). Instead, they would:

- Drive through at exactly **2.0 m** (lower boundary — will it clip?).
- Drive through at **2.0001 m** (just inside the boundary — clearance margin).
- Drive through at exactly **3.5 m** (upper boundary — will it hit?).
- Drive through at **3.5001 m** (just outside — should crash/be rejected).

This is precisely the philosophy of BVA in software: **the boundaries are where the contract between the program and the real world is enforced** — off-by-one errors, fence-post errors, and rounding bugs all congregate here. Testing the "middle" is like checking only the middle of a bridge span.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** BVA is positioned as the *companion technique* to **Equivalence Partitioning (EP)** in Module 2. While EP gives you the *partitions*, BVA drills into the *edges of those partitions*. A complete Module 2 answer will almost always demonstrate the EP-to-BVA bridge.

## 1.3 Vocabulary of Boundary Values

The following five canonical values are defined for every ordered input variable (KTU Board Convention):

| Notation | Symbolic Name | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $x_{min}$ | **Min** | The smallest valid value on the boundary. |
| $x_{min+}$ | **Min Plus** | The smallest valid value *just above* the lower boundary. |
| $x_{nom}$ | **Nominal** | Any typical interior value (often the midpoint). |
| $x_{max-}$ | **Max Minus** | The largest valid value *just below* the upper boundary. |
| $x_{max}$ | **Max** | The largest valid value on the boundary. |

> [!TIP]
> A common student pitfall is to assume "nominal" is a specific fixed value such as the midpoint. In KTU valuations, **nominal is any valid in-range value**; the choice does not affect the count of BVA test cases.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of BVA — Step-by-Step

BVA proceeds through four structured logical phases, mirroring the KTU textbook sequence:

1. **Partition Identification:** Identify the input domain partitions using Equivalence Partitioning. Each valid partition yields a continuous range $[x_{min}, x_{max}]$.
2. **Boundary Enumeration:** For each input variable, mark the five canonical points: $x_{min}$, $x_{min+}$, $x_{nom}$, $x_{max-}$, $x_{max}$.
3. **Single Fault Assumption (SFA) Application:** Construct test cases such that **exactly one variable is set to a boundary extreme**, and *all other variables remain at their nominal values*. This reduces the test matrix from exponential to linear growth.
4. **Optional Off-Boundary Testing (Robust BVA):** Extend the matrix by including values **just outside** the valid domain ($x_{min-1}$ and $x_{max+1}$) to verify the program gracefully rejects invalid input.

## 2.2 Why BVA Works — The Cognitive "Why"

> [!NOTE]
> **Empirical Basis:** Beizer's classic software testing literature documents that boundary errors account for a disproportionately large share of latent defects. Programmers frequently write loop conditions as `<=` instead of `<`, or off-by-one in array indexing — these defects *only* surface when the input sits precisely on or adjacent to the boundary.

- **The "Why" of SFA:** Combinatorial testing of *all* boundary combinations yields $5^n$ test cases, which is infeasible for $n \geq 5$. The Single Fault Assumption trades *strict fault-detection completeness* for **practical scalability**.
- **The "Why" of Nominal Coverage:** A nominal value is included to ensure the program behaves correctly for ordinary inputs, not only at the edges — a common student oversight.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Test Case Count Cheat Sheet

For a function with **$n$ input variables**, each variable being an ordered (range-bounded) variable, the **KTU 2024 Board** recognizes exactly four standard counts:

| BVA Variant | Formula | Logical Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Standard BVA** (Single Fault) | $4n + 1$ | $n$ Min + $n$ Min+ + $n$ Max- + $n$ Max + $1$ Nominal |
| **Robust BVA** | $6n + 1$ | Standard + $n$ Min- + $n$ Max+ (off-boundary) |
| **Worst-Case BVA** (no SFA) | $5^n$ | All combinations of {Min, Min+, Nom, Max-, Max} |
| **Robust Worst-Case BVA** | $7^n$ | All combinations of {Min-, Min, Min+, Nom, Max-, Max, Max+} |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Always state the formula *before* substituting. Students lose marks by writing "15 test cases" without showing "$4 \times 3 + 1$". **Always show the derivation.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems Validation:** Sensor input ranges (e.g., temperature -40°C to 125°C) are tested at boundaries to verify ADC saturation behaviour.
- **Aerospace Control Software:** Flight envelope boundaries (Mach number, angle of attack) are tested via BVA to prevent catastrophic out-of-envelope transitions.
- **Banking & Financial Systems:** Interest rate boundaries, withdrawal limits, and tax slabs are validated using BVA principles to prevent regulatory exposure.
- **Network Protocol Stack Engineering:** MTU sizes, packet TTL values, and port ranges (0–65535) all demand BVA-based boundary testing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Standard BVA Count: $4n + 1$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **single most asked question** in KTU Module 2 for BVA. Master it.

**Given:** A program has $n$ independent input variables, each with a valid domain $[a_i, b_i]$, where $a_i < b_i$.

**Step 1 — Enumerate boundary points per variable.**
For any single ordered variable $x_i$, BVA prescribes exactly five canonical checkpoints:

$$\{x_{i,min},\; x_{i,min+},\; x_{i,nom},\; x_{i,max-},\; x_{i,max}\}$$

However, the *nominal* point is shared across all variables in the SFA strategy. We therefore segregate nominal from boundary extremes.

**Step 2 — Apply the Single Fault Assumption (SFA).**
Under SFA, *exactly one* variable is perturbed to a boundary extreme; the remaining $n-1$ variables are held at their nominal values. This yields the following test case construction rule:

- For each variable $x_i$ ($i = 1, 2, \dots, n$):
  - Test 1: $x_i = x_{i,min}$, all other $x_j = x_{j,nom}$.
  - Test 2: $x_i = x_{i,min+}$, all other $x_j = x_{j,nom}$.
  - Test 3: $x_i = x_{i,max-}$, all other $x_j = x_{j,nom}$.
  - Test 4: $x_i = x_{i,max}$, all other $x_j = x_{j,nom}$.

This produces exactly **4 test cases per variable**.

**Step 3 — Add the global nominal test case.**
One additional test case in which *all* variables are simultaneously at their nominal value is included to verify the non-boundary baseline behaviour:

$$\text{Total} = \underbrace{(4 \text{ tests} \times n \text{ variables})}_{\text{per-variable SFA}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{all-nominal}}$$

$$\boxed{\;N_{\text{standard}} = 4n + 1\;}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Dec Pattern):** A function `discount(price, qty)` accepts `price ∈ [100, 5000]` and `qty ∈ [1, 50]`. Compute the Standard BVA test case count and list the test matrix.

**Step 1 — Identify variable count.**
$n = 2$ (variables: `price`, `qty`).

**Step 2 — Apply formula.**

$$N_{\text{standard}} = 4n + 1 = 4(2) + 1 = 9 \text{ test cases}$$

**Step 3 — Enumerate the canonical boundary points.**

For `price ∈ [100, 5000]`:

$$x_{1,min} = 100, \quad x_{1,min+} = 101, \quad x_{1,nom} = 2550, \quad x_{1,max-} = 4999, \quad x_{1,max} = 5000$$

For `qty ∈ [1, 50]`:

$$x_{2,min} = 1, \quad x_{2,min+} = 2, \quad x_{2,nom} = 25, \quad x_{2,max-} = 49, \quad x_{2,max} = 50$$

**Step 4 — Construct the SFA test matrix.**

| Test ID | price | qty | Variant Logic |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| T1 | 100 | 25 | price = min, qty = nominal |
| T2 | 101 | 25 | price = min+, qty = nominal |
| T3 | 4999 | 25 | price = max-, qty = nominal |
| T4 | 5000 | 25 | price = max, qty = nominal |
| T5 | 2550 | 1 | price = nominal, qty = min |
| T6 | 2550 | 2 | price = nominal, qty = min+ |
| T7 | 2550 | 49 | price = nominal, qty = max- |
| T8 | 2550 | 50 | price = nominal, qty = max |
| T9 | 2550 | 25 | all-nominal baseline |

**Verification:** Row count = 9 ✓ Formula match ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of BVA Variants

| Property | Standard BVA | Robust BVA | Worst-Case BVA | Robust Worst-Case |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Count Formula** | $4n + 1$ | $6n + 1$ | $5^n$ | $7^n$ |
| **Includes Invalid Inputs?** | No | Yes (Min-, Max+) | No | Yes |
| **Single Fault Assumption?** | Yes | Yes | No (all combos) | No (all combos) |
| **Cost for $n=4$** | 17 | 25 | 625 | 2,401 |
| **Suitable for $n \geq 5$?** | Yes | Yes | No (infeasible) | No (infeasible) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The exponential growth of worst-case variants reflects the **combinatorial explosion** — a phenomenon students must comment on in KTU 14-mark answers to demonstrate depth.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Robust BVA Count

For the same `discount(price, qty)` problem with $n = 2$:

$$N_{\text{robust}} = 6n + 1 = 6(2) + 1 = 13 \text{ test cases}$$

The additional four cases (relative to standard BVA) are:

- `price = 99` (Min-), `qty = 25` — invalid lower breach.
- `price = 5001` (Max+), `qty = 25` — invalid upper breach.
- `price = 2550`, `qty = 0` (Min-) — invalid lower breach.
- `price = 2550`, `qty = 51` (Max+) — invalid upper breach.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BVA Methodology Flow — Block-Level Architecture

The following block diagram captures the full operational pipeline of Boundary Value Analysis as required by Module 2 of the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT SPECIFICATION] --> B[APPLY EQUIVALENCE PARTITIONING]
    B --> C[IDENTIFY VALID RANGES PER VARIABLE]
    C --> D[ENUMERATE FIVE CANONICAL BOUNDARY POINTS]
    D --> E1[MIN]
    D --> E2[MIN PLUS]
    D --> E3[NOMINAL]
    D --> E4[MAX MINUS]
    D --> E5[MAX]
    E1 --> F[APPLY SINGLE FAULT ASSUMPTION]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G{CHOOSE VARIANT}
    G -->|STANDARD BVA| H1[COUNT EQUALS 4N PLUS 1]
    G -->|ROBUST BVA| H2[ADD MIN MINUS AND MAX PLUS]
    G -->|WORST CASE BVA| H3[FULL COMBINATORIAL ENUMERATION]
    H1 --> I[CONSTRUCT TEST MATRIX]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[EXECUTE TEST CASES]
    J --> K[REPORT DEFECTS AT BOUNDARIES]

    style A fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2c5282,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style C fill:#2c5282,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style D fill:#2b6cb0,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style F fill:#3182ce,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style G fill:#d69e2e,stroke:#fff,color:#000
    style H1 fill:#38a169,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style H2 fill:#38a169,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style H3 fill:#e53e3e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style I fill:#805ad5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style J fill:#d53f8c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style K fill:#1a202c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Boundary Mapping Diagram — One-Dimensional Visual

The following block schematic illustrates the spatial arrangement of the five canonical boundary points along a single valid range $[a, b]$. This is the **classic KTU Board diagram** that students must reproduce.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[INVALID ZONE X LESS THAN A] --> P2[A MIN BOUNDARY]
    P2 --> P3[A PLUS 1 MIN PLUS]
    P3 --> P4[VALID INTERIOR NOMINAL]
    P4 --> P5[B MINUS 1 MAX MINUS]
    P5 --> P6[B MAX BOUNDARY]
    P6 --> P7[INVALID ZONE X GREATER THAN B]

    style P1 fill:#fed7d7,stroke:#c53030,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#fbd38d,stroke:#c05621,color:#000
    style P3 fill:#c6f6d5,stroke:#22543d,color:#000
    style P4 fill:#bee3f8,stroke:#2a4365,color:#000
    style P5 fill:#c6f6d5,stroke:#22543d,color:#000
    style P6 fill:#fbd38d,stroke:#c05621,color:#000
    style P7 fill:#fed7d7,stroke:#c53030,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Decoding for Examiners:** The diagram uses the KTU board convention — **red = invalid zones** (tested only in Robust BVA), **orange = boundary extremes** (always tested), **green = adjacent valid points** (always tested), **blue = nominal interior** (single shared test in SFA).

## 4.3 Decision Tree — BVA Variant Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[HAS THE INPUT DOMAIN BEEN PARTITIONED] -->|NO| R1[PERFORM EQUIVALENCE PARTITIONING FIRST]
    Q1 -->|YES| Q2[IS THE PROGRAM TOLERANT TO INVALID INPUT]
    Q2 -->|YES| Q3[DO YOU NEED TO TEST INVALID INPUT BOUNDARIES]
    Q2 -->|NO| Q4[IS COMBINATORIAL TESTING FEASIBLE]
    Q3 -->|YES| S1[USE ROBUST BVA COUNT 6N PLUS 1]
    Q3 -->|NO| S2[USE STANDARD BVA COUNT 4N PLUS 1]
    Q4 -->|YES| S3[USE WORST CASE BVA COUNT 5 POWER N]
    Q4 -->|NO| S4[USE STANDARD BVA COUNT 4N PLUS 1]

    style Q1 fill:#2c5282,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style Q2 fill:#2c5282,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style Q3 fill:#2c5282,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style Q4 fill:#2c5282,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style S1 fill:#38a169,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style S2 fill:#38a169,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style S3 fill:#e53e3e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style S4 fill:#38a169,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style R1 fill:#d69e2e,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define Boundary Value Analysis. Why is it considered more effective than random input testing?

**[Model Answer — KTU Board Key Pattern]**

> Boundary Value Analysis (BVA) is a **black-box test case design technique** that focuses on identifying and testing the values at the boundaries of input equivalence partitions. It operates on the **empirical observation that programmers introduce most defects near the edges of input ranges**, such as off-by-one errors in loop conditions or boundary comparisons.
>
> BVA is more effective than random testing because it provides a **systematic, deterministic, and complete coverage rule** of the boundary region. Each valid range $[a, b]$ is tested at exactly the points $\{a, a+1, \text{nominal}, b-1, b\}$, ensuring that the most defect-prone code paths are exercised without the redundancy of random sampling. It is also **economical** — using the Single Fault Assumption, only $4n + 1$ test cases are required for $n$ variables, as opposed to exhaustive enumeration.

### Question A2: List the four standard BVA variants and state the test case count formula for each.

**[Model Answer]**

> 1. **Standard BVA (Single Fault Assumption):** $4n + 1$ test cases.
> 2. **Robust BVA:** $6n + 1$ test cases (includes invalid Min- and Max+).
> 3. **Worst-Case BVA:** $5^n$ test cases (no SFA — all boundary combinations).
> 4. **Robust Worst-Case BVA:** $7^n$ test cases (no SFA + invalid boundary combinations).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam Style

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**
> A software module `calculate_loan(age, salary, loan_amount)` accepts:
> - `age ∈ [21, 60]` (integer years)
> - `salary ∈ [15000, 200000]` (rupees per month)
> - `loan_amount ∈ [50000, 2000000]` (rupees)
>
> **Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Identify the input equivalence partitions and apply **Standard Boundary Value Analysis** to derive the complete test case matrix.
>
> **Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare Standard BVA with **Robust BVA** and **Worst-Case BVA**. Recommend the most suitable variant for this module and justify.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify variables and partitions.**
$n = 3$ variables. All inputs are ordered (range-bounded) integers, hence BVA applies directly to each.

**Step 2 — Compute Standard BVA count.**

$$N_{\text{standard}} = 4n + 1 = 4(3) + 1 = 13 \text{ test cases}$$

**Step 3 — Enumerate canonical boundary points.**

| Variable | Min | Min+ | Nominal | Max- | Max |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| age | 21 | 22 | 40 | 59 | 60 |
| salary | 15000 | 15001 | 100000 | 199999 | 200000 |
| loan_amount | 50000 | 50001 | 1000000 | 1999999 | 2000000 |

**Step 4 — Build the Standard BVA Test Matrix (SFA).**

| ID | age | salary | loan_amount | Perturbed Variable |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| T1 | 21 | 100000 | 1000000 | age = Min |
| T2 | 22 | 100000 | 1000000 | age = Min+ |
| T3 | 59 | 100000 | 1000000 | age = Max- |
| T4 | 60 | 100000 | 1000000 | age = Max |
| T5 | 40 | 15000 | 1000000 | salary = Min |
| T6 | 40 | 15001 | 1000000 | salary = Min+ |
| T7 | 40 | 199999 | 1000000 | salary = Max- |
| T8 | 40 | 200000 | 1000000 | salary = Max |
| T9 | 40 | 100000 | 50000 | loan_amount = Min |
| T10 | 40 | 100000 | 50001 | loan_amount = Min+ |
| T11 | 40 | 100000 | 1999999 | loan_amount = Max- |
| T12 | 40 | 100000 | 2000000 | loan_amount = Max |
| T13 | 40 | 100000 | 1000000 | all-nominal baseline |

**Verification:** 12 SFA + 1 nominal = 13 test cases ✓

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Standard BVA | Robust BVA | Worst-Case BVA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Test case count for $n=3$ | 13 | 19 | 125 |
| Invalid input coverage | No | Yes (Min-, Max+) | No |
| Single Fault Assumption | Yes | Yes | No |
| Boundary completeness | Partial (SFA) | Partial (SFA) | Total (5³) |
| Practical feasibility for $n=3$ | High | High | Moderate |

**Recommendation:** **Robust BVA** is the most suitable variant for the `calculate_loan` module.

**Justification:**

1. **Domain Criticality:** Loan processing is a financial domain where invalid inputs (e.g., negative salaries, age 18) must be rejected gracefully without system crashes. Robust BVA provides dedicated test cases for Min- and Max+ breaches.
2. **Complementing SFA with Boundary Breach Detection:** While standard BVA verifies valid boundaries, robust BVA additionally exercises the rejection-handling code path — a frequent source of unhandled-exception defects.
