3. **Cost-Benefit Balance:** With $6n + 1 = 19$ test cases, the test effort remains linear and economical; worst-case BVA's $5^3 = 125$ test cases offer marginal additional defect coverage for 6× more effort.
4. **Regulatory Compliance:** Indian banking regulations (RBI guidelines) mandate that loan processing systems handle invalid inputs deterministically — directly served by Robust BVA.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**
>
> **Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the concept of **Single Fault Assumption (SFA)** in Boundary Value Analysis. Under what circumstances is SFA invalid, and what alternative testing strategy should be adopted?
>
> **Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** A temperature monitoring system reads inputs `T ∈ [-20°C, 80°C]` and `P ∈ [0.5, 5.0]` (pressure in bar). Apply **Worst-Case BVA** and compute the test case count. List any **two** test cases from the matrix.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Definition of SFA:**
The Single Fault Assumption states that in any given test case, **exactly one input variable is perturbed to a boundary extreme**, while all remaining variables are held at their nominal values. This reduces the test matrix from exponential $5^n$ to linear $4n + 1$, making BVA practically scalable.

**Why SFA is Used:**
The assumption rests on the empirical principle that **defects in boundary handling typically involve a single variable at a time** — most off-by-one errors, type-conversion errors, and range-comparison bugs are localized to a specific conditional branch.

**Circumstances Where SFA is Invalid:**

1. **Coupled Variables:** When two or more variables interact (e.g., $a$ must satisfy $a + b \leq 100$), boundary faults may only manifest when *both* variables are simultaneously at extremes. SFA misses these multi-variable coupling faults.
2. **Dependent Conditional Logic:** When the code path executed for input $x_i = x_{i,max}$ depends on the value of another variable $x_j$, perturbing only $x_i$ may not reach the relevant code branch.
3. **Concurrency and Race Conditions:** In multi-threaded systems, simultaneous boundary events across threads trigger timing-dependent faults that SFA cannot reproduce.

**Alternative Strategy:**
When SFA is invalid, **Worst-Case BVA** must be adopted. Worst-case BVA removes the Single Fault Assumption and tests all $5^n$ combinations of the boundary values, providing full multi-variable boundary coverage at the cost of exponential test growth. For systems with $n \geq 5$, this becomes infeasible, and **Pairwise / Combinatorial Testing** (e.g., all-pairs, $t$-wise) is recommended.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify variable count.**
$n = 2$ (variables: $T$ and $P$).

**Step 2 — Enumerate boundary values per variable.**

For $T \in [-20, 80]$ (integer degrees Celsius):

$$T_{min} = -20, \quad T_{min+} = -19, \quad T_{nom} = 30, \quad T_{max-} = 79, \quad T_{max} = 80$$

For $P \in [0.5, 5.0]$ (real-valued, using 0.1 increment):

$$P_{min} = 0.5, \quad P_{min+} = 0.6, \quad P_{nom} = 2.75, \quad P_{max-} = 4.9, \quad P_{max} = 5.0$$

**Step 3 — Apply Worst-Case BVA count formula.**

$$N_{\text{worst}} = 5^n = 5^2 = 25 \text{ test cases}$$

**Step 4 — List two representative test cases from the $5 \times 5$ matrix.**

| Test ID | T | P | Combination Logic |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| T7 | -19 | 0.6 | $T = $ Min+, $P = $ Min+ |
| T25 | 80 | 5.0 | $T = $ Max, $P = $ Max |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1" baseline:** In Standard BVA, the all-nominal test case is *not* a variable perturbation — students often report $4n$ instead of $4n + 1$.
> 2. **Confusing Min+ with Min:** Min+ is the value **just above** the lower bound (i.e., $a + \Delta$), not the midpoint between Min and Nominal. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Omitting the SFA statement:** When listing test cases, explicitly state which variable is perturbed; otherwise the answer loses structural clarity marks.
> 4. **Mixing BVA with EP:** A common error is reporting equivalence class representatives as BVA test cases. BVA requires **boundary** values, not class *interiors*.
> 5. **Skipping the derivation:** Writing "13 test cases" without showing "$4(3) + 1$" forfeits the methodology marks in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember — Module 2 BVA Quick Reference**

- **Definition:** BVA is a black-box technique that targets the **edges of input equivalence partitions**, not their interiors.
- **Five Canonical Points Per Variable:** Min, Min+, Nominal, Max-, Max.
- **Single Fault Assumption (SFA):** Only one variable is perturbed at a time; rest are nominal. This is the *defining* assumption of standard BVA.
- **Standard BVA Count:** $\mathbf{4n + 1}$ — derived as $(4 \times n) + 1_{\text{baseline}}$.
- **Robust BVA Count:** $\mathbf{6n + 1}$ — adds $n$ Min- and $n$ Max+ invalid test cases.
- **Worst-Case BVA Count:** $\mathbf{5^n}$ — drops SFA, enumerates all boundary combinations.
- **Robust Worst-Case Count:** $\mathbf{7^n}$ — worst-case + invalid boundary coverage.
- **BVA vs. EP Relationship:** BVA is **not** a replacement for EP; it is a *refinement*. EP identifies partitions, BVA targets the partition edges.
- **When to Choose Which Variant:**
  - Default / linear cost → **Standard BVA**.
  - Need to verify invalid-input rejection → **Robust BVA**.
  - Coupled variables / fault interaction suspected → **Worst-Case BVA**.
  - Coupled variables + invalid inputs → **Robust Worst-Case BVA**.
- **Common Errors Targeted:** Off-by-one, fence-post, comparator direction (`<` vs. `<=`), rounding, type-cast overflow, integer-vs-float boundary artefacts.
- **Boundary Increment ($\Delta$):** For integers, $\Delta = 1$. For floats, $\Delta$ equals the smallest representable precision step.
- **KTU Board Favourite:** Always show the formula *before* substitution, the canonical point enumeration *before* the test matrix, and explicitly mention SFA in your assumptions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalence Partitioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT747 - SOFTWARE TESTING AND QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Equivalence Partitioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Equivalence Partitioning (EP)**, also called **Equivalence Class Partitioning (ECP)**, is a specification-based **black-box test case design technique** in which the input domain of the system under test (SUT) is divided into disjoint sub-domains called **equivalence classes (or equivalence partitions)**. From each equivalence class, a small, representative set of test data is selected such that the **probability of revealing a defect is assumed to be uniform** across all members of that class.

According to the **ISTQB (International Software Testing Qualifications Board) Foundation Level Syllabus** (which KTU 2024 references for PECNT747), equivalence partitioning is formally defined as:

> *"A black-box test design technique in which test cases are designed to execute representatives from equivalence partitions. In principle, test cases are designed to cover each partition at least once."*

The two fundamental types of equivalence classes recognized in the KTU syllabus are:

| Class Type | Symbolic Notation | Purpose |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Valid Equivalence Class** | $E_v$ | Contains a set of input values that the SUT is designed to **accept and process correctly**. |
| **Invalid Equivalence Class** | $E_i$ | Contains a set of input values that the SUT is expected to **reject** or handle via the **error-handling path**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A *single test case* drawn from a valid partition is considered sufficient to validate that partition, and a *single test case* from each invalid partition is considered sufficient to verify the corresponding error path. Testers must NOT pick multiple redundant values from the same partition.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **medical diagnostic machine** that categorizes patient Body Mass Index (BMI). The machine does not need to test every BMI value from 0.1 to 200. Instead, it groups all possible BMI inputs into four logical buckets:

- **Underweight** (BMI < 18.5)
- **Normal** (18.5 ≤ BMI < 25)
- **Overweight** (25 ≤ BMI < 30)
- **Obese** (BMI ≥ 30)

If a doctor verifies that the machine correctly classifies a BMI of **22.7** (Normal), the doctor has high confidence that any other value in the *Normal* bucket (e.g., 19.0, 24.9) will yield the same output. This is exactly the principle behind **Equivalence Partitioning**: *one test value from a partition stands in for the entire partition*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Equivalence Partitioning is the art of "**divide and conquer**" the input space — slice the input data into meaningful groups, test just **one representative value** from each group, and trust that the behavior is uniform within the group.

## 1.3 Why It Matters in Industry

In real-world KTU project evaluations and industrial quality assurance (e.g., **TCS, Infosys, Wipro, IBM India, and Cognizant QA tracks**), exhaustive testing is **mathematically impossible** for any non-trivial system. If a field accepts 1,000,000 possible integer values, brute-force testing all of them is infeasible. Equivalence Partitioning reduces this to a tractable, scientifically justified subset — making it a **mandatory topic** in both KTU Module 2 and ISTQB certification exams worldwide.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Steps to Apply Equivalence Partitioning

The KTU 2024 board examiner typically awards marks for the **process rigor**, so commit the following six-step procedure to memory:

1. **Identify the input domain** of the SUT (parameters, fields, environment variables).
2. **Segment the domain** into equivalence classes based on the specification document (SRS/BRD).
3. **Classify** each partition as **valid** ($E_v$) or **invalid** ($E_i$).
4. **Select one representative** (often the midpoint or a boundary-adjacent value) from each class.
5. **Design test cases**: at least one per valid class, plus one per invalid class.
6. **Execute and verify** that the SUT processes the valid representative correctly and handles the invalid representatives via proper error messages/exceptions.

## 2.2 Heuristics for Identifying Equivalence Classes

The following **"Rule of Five"** heuristics are commonly tested in KTU exams:

- **Rule 1 — Range / Interval:** If an input is constrained as $a \leq x \leq b$, create **one valid class** ($a \leq x \leq b$) and **two invalid classes** ($x < a$ and $x > b$).
- **Rule 2 — Discrete Set / Enumeration:** If input must be one of $\{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$, create **one valid class** (the set itself) and **one invalid class** (anything not in the set).
- **Rule 3 — Boolean:** If input is True/False, create **two valid classes** (one per value).
- **Rule 4 — Number of Items / Cardinality:** If $n$ items are required where $1 \leq n \leq m$, create **one valid class** and **two invalid classes** ($n < 1$, $n > m$).
- **Rule 5 — Sub-conditions:** Split the input further if it has independent sub-fields (e.g., a date field splits into Day, Month, Year — each with its own partitions).

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why segment the domain?** Because defects are rarely *random*; they cluster at logical boundaries (out-of-range, type mismatch, missing data).
- **Why one representative per class?** Reduces the total test case count from $\mathcal{O}(n)$ to $\mathcal{O}(k)$ where $k$ is the number of partitions — a **massive reduction in test effort**.
- **Why cover every invalid class?** An invalid partition is *designed* to expose a different error path; missing it means missing an entire class of defects.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Metrics Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Software testing is largely qualitative, but a few mathematical metrics appear in 14-mark problems and numerical assignments.

$$
\text{Number of Test Cases} = \sum_{j=1}^{v} 1 \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{i} 1 \;=\; v + i
$$

where $v$ = number of valid equivalence classes and $i$ = number of invalid equivalence classes.

$$
\text{Equivalence Class Coverage} \; (\%) = \frac{\text{Partitions Covered by Test Suite}}{\text{Total Identified Partitions}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Test Reduction Ratio} = 1 - \frac{\text{EP-based Test Cases}}{\text{Exhaustive Test Cases}}
$$

$$
\text{Defect Detection Efficiency} \; (DDE) = \frac{\text{Defects Found by EP Suite}}{\text{Total Defects Identified Independently}}
$$

## 2.5 Table: Comprehensive KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Metric / Concept | Symbol / Equation | Units / Range | KTU Use Case |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Total EP test cases | $N = v + i$ | Positive integer | Always required in 14-mark problems. |
| Valid classes (range $a \leq x \leq b$) | $E_v = \{x \,\vert\, a \leq x \leq b\}$ | Continuous / discrete set | Derive one representative value. |
| Invalid class (lower) | $E_i^- = \{x \,\vert\, x < a\}$ | All reals below $a$ | Choose $a - 1$ as representative. |
| Invalid class (upper) | $E_i^+ = \{x \,\vert\, x > b\}$ | All reals above $b$ | Choose $b + 1$ as representative. |
| Partition coverage | $C_p = \tfrac{k_{cov}}{k_{tot}} \times 100$ | $0 \leq C_p \leq 100\%$ | Used in coverage report tables. |
| Test reduction ratio | $R = 1 - \tfrac{N_{EP}}{N_{exh}}$ | $0 \leq R < 1$ | Justifies EP over brute-force. |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Equivalence Partitioning is foundational to:

- **Web form validation testing** (age, email, phone, password strength).
- **ATM / banking transaction limits** (withdrawal amount, daily cap).
- **IoT sensor range validation** (temperature thresholds, pressure ranges).
- **API parameter validation** in **REST/GraphQL services**.
- **Compiler token classification** (integer literals, identifiers, operators).

In short, **every input-driven software module** can be reduced to equivalence classes — which is why this technique is the **first topic** taught in most KTU black-box testing modules.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Example 1 — Age Field Validation (Classic KTU Problem)

**Problem Statement:** A web portal accepts a user's age as an integer input. The specification states that the age must be between **18 and 60** (inclusive) for the user to register for a "Senior-Citizen Savings Account". Outside this range, the system must reject the input with a graceful error message.

**Step 1 — Identify the input domain and constraints.**

$$
\text{Domain of Age} = \mathbb{Z}^+ \quad \text{(positive integers on the wire)}
$$

$$
\text{Constraint} = \{x \in \mathbb{Z}^+ \;\vert\; 18 \leq x \leq 60\}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Rule 1 (Range / Interval) to derive equivalence classes.**

$$
E_{v1} = \{x \,\vert\, 18 \leq x \leq 60\} \quad \text{(one valid class)}
$$

$$
E_{i1} = \{x \,\vert\, x < 18\} \quad \text{(invalid — below range)}
$$

$$
E_{i2} = \{x \,\vert\, x > 60\} \quad \text{(invalid — above range)}
$$

**Step 3 — Class cardinality check.**

$$
\vert E_{v1} \vert = 60 - 18 + 1 = 43 \text{ values}
$$

$$
E_{i1} \text{ and } E_{i2} \text{ are unbounded (semi-infinite sets)}
$$

**Step 4 — Select representatives.**

$$
\text{Valid Rep} = \frac{18 + 60}{2} = 39 \quad \text{(midpoint of the valid range)}
$$

$$
E_{i1} \text{ Rep} = 17 \quad (a - 1 = 18 - 1)
$$

$$
E_{i2} \text{ Rep} = 61 \quad (b + 1 = 60 + 1)
$$

**Step 5 — Total test cases required.**

$$
N = v + i = 1 + 2 = 3
$$

**Step 6 — Test case design table.**

| Test Case ID | Input (Age) | Partition | Expected Output | Status |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| TC_01 | 39 | $E_{v1}$ | "Registration successful." | Valid acceptance path. |
| TC_02 | 17 | $E_{i1}$ | "Error: Age must be ≥ 18." | Invalid lower-bound path. |
| TC_03 | 61 | $E_{i2}$ | "Error: Age must be ≤ 60." | Invalid upper-bound path. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** A naïve "exhaustive" test of every value from 0 to 100 would require 101 test cases. EP reduces this to **3 test cases** — a **97% reduction** in test effort while maintaining high defect-detection confidence.

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — Loan Eligibility Engine (14-Mark KTU Pattern)

**Problem Statement:** A bank's loan module accepts the applicant's **Annual Income (AI)** in lakhs and the **Credit Score (CS)** as a 3-digit integer. The eligibility rule is:

$$
\text{Eligible} \iff (\text{AI} \geq 5) \;\land\; (\text{CS} \in [700, 900])
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the input variables.**

$$
\text{Input}_1 = \text{AI} \in \mathbb{R}^+ \cup \{0\}, \quad \text{Input}_2 = \text{CS} \in \mathbb{Z}, \quad 0 \leq \text{CS} \leq 999
$$

**Step 2 — Derive equivalence classes for Input 1 (AI).**

$$
E_{v1}^{(AI)} = \{\text{AI} \,\vert\, \text{AI} \geq 5\}
$$

$$
E_{i1}^{(AI)} = \{\text{AI} \,\vert\, 0 \leq \text{AI} < 5\}
$$

**Step 3 — Derive equivalence classes for Input 2 (CS).**

$$
E_{v1}^{(CS)} = \{\text{CS} \,\vert\, 700 \leq \text{CS} \leq 900\}
$$

$$
E_{i1}^{(CS)} = \{\text{CS} \,\vert\, 0 \leq \text{CS} < 700\}
$$

$$
E_{i2}^{(CS)} = \{\text{CS} \,\vert\, \text{CS} > 900\}
$$

**Step 4 — Total equivalence classes across all inputs.**

$$
v = v_{AI} + v_{CS} = 1 + 1 = 2
$$

$$
i = i_{AI} + i_{CS} = 1 + 2 = 3
$$

$$
N = v + i = 2 + 3 = 5 \text{ test cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Choose representatives.**

$$
\text{AI representative from } E_{v1}^{(AI)} = 6, \quad \text{AI rep from } E_{i1}^{(AI)} = 3
$$

$$
\text{CS rep from } E_{v1}^{(CS)} = 800, \quad \text{CS rep from } E_{i1}^{(CS)} = 500, \quad \text{CS rep from } E_{i2}^{(CS)} = 950
$$

**Step 6 — Build the test matrix using Cartesian combination (valid × valid + each invalid).**

| TC ID | AI (lakhs) | CS | Partition Combination | Expected Result |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| TC_01 | 6 | 800 | $E_{v1}^{(AI)} \times E_{v1}^{(CS)}$ | **Eligible** (valid × valid). |
| TC_02 | 3 | 800 | $E_{i1}^{(AI)} \times E_{v1}^{(CS)}$ | **Not eligible** (low income). |
| TC_03 | 6 | 500 | $E_{v1}^{(AI)} \times E_{i1}^{(CS)}$ | **Not eligible** (low score). |
| TC_04 | 6 | 950 | $E_{v1}^{(AI)} \times E_{i2}^{(CS)}$ | **Not eligible** (score > 900). |
| TC_05 | 3 | 500 | $E_{i1}^{(AI)} \times E_{i1}^{(CS)}$ | **Not eligible** (both invalid — covers overlap). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The full Cartesian product of invalid classes is **NOT** mandatory — KTU examiners accept the "one representative per invalid partition" rule. However, for thorough coverage in industry, all combinations are usually enumerated.

---

## 3.3 Example 3 — Triangle Classifier (Short Conceptual EP)

**Problem Statement:** A function takes three integers $a, b, c$ as the side lengths of a triangle and classifies it as *Equilateral*, *Isosceles*, *Scalene*, or *Not a Triangle*.

**Step 1 — Identify input conditions.**

$$
\text{Condition 1: } a, b, c \in \mathbb{Z}^+ \quad (\text{positive integers})
$$

$$
\text{Condition 2: } a + b > c, \quad b + c > a, \quad c + a > b \quad (\text{triangle inequality})
$$

**Step 2 — Identify equivalence classes.**

$$
E_1 = \{x \,\vert\, x > 0, x \in \mathbb{Z}^+\} \quad \text{(positive integer — valid)}
$$

$$
E_2 = \{x \,\vert\, x \leq 0, x \in \mathbb{Z}\} \quad \text{(non-positive — invalid)}
$$

**Step 3 — Sample test cases (per input, per partition).**

| TC | a | b | c | Partition(s) | Expected Output |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| TC_01 | 5 | 5 | 5 | All $E_1$ | Equilateral |
| TC_02 | 3 | 4 | 5 | All $E_1$ | Scalene |
| TC_03 | 2 | 2 | 3 | All $E_1$ | Isosceles |
| TC_04 | 1 | 2 | 3 | All $E_1$ | Not a triangle (triangle inequality fails) |
| TC_05 | -1 | 5 | 5 | $E_2$ for a | Error: "Invalid side" |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Strengths vs. Limitations of EP

| Aspect | Strength | Limitation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Test count** | Massively reduces redundant test cases. | May miss boundary-specific defects (hence paired with **BVA**). |
| **Defect detection** | Excellent for range and value-set errors. | Weak against logical / combinatorial interactions. |
| **Documentation** | Forces a rigorous spec review. | Specification ambiguity leads to wrong partitions. |
| **Automation** | Easy to script using parameterization (e.g., JUnit `@ParameterizedTest`). | Requires clear input semantics; hard for GUI/image inputs. |
| **Effort vs. coverage** | High coverage with low effort. | Does not guarantee 100% statement or branch coverage. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — The Equivalence Partitioning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read SRS / Specification] --> B[Identify Input Domain]
    B --> C[Decompose Into Input Variables]
    C --> D{Apply Partitioning Rules}
    D -->|Rule 1 Range| E1[Create Valid and Invalid Classes]
    D -->|Rule 2 Enumeration| E2[Create Membership Classes]
    D -->|Rule 3 Boolean| E3[Create True and False Classes]
    D -->|Rule 4 Cardinality| E4[Create Count Based Classes]
    D -->|Rule 5 Sub condition| E5[Create Nested Classes]
    E1 --> F[Tag Each Class as Valid or Invalid]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G[Select One Representative per Class]
    G --> H[Build Test Case Matrix]
    H --> I[Execute Test Suite against SUT]
    I --> J{All Partitions Covered?}
    J -->|No| K[Add Missing Test Cases]
    K --> I
    J -->|Yes| L[Compute Coverage Metric]
    L --> M[End: Document and Report]
```

## 4.2 Concept Map — Equivalence Partitioning in the Black-Box Test Design Family

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BB[Black Box Test Design Techniques] --> EP[Equivalence Partitioning]
    BB --> BVA[Boundary Value Analysis]
    BB --> DT[Decision Table Testing]
    BB --> ST[State Transition Testing]
    BB --> CT[Cause Effect Graphing]
    EP --> RV[Reduces Test Count]
    EP --> RE[Reveals Range Errors]
    BVA --> BE[Reveals Off by One Errors]
    DT --> LC[Handles Logic Rules]
    ST --> SE[Handles State Machines]
    EP -.->|Frequently Combined| BVA
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Decomposition of an Equivalence Class

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Equivalence Class E] --> B{Is Value Inside Spec Limits?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Valid Equivalence Class Ev]
    B -->|No| D[Invalid Equivalence Class Ei]
    C --> E[Pick Midpoint or Any Member]
    C --> F[Expected: Normal Processing]
    D --> G[Pick One Value Below Lower Limit]
    D --> H[Pick One Value Above Upper Limit]
    D --> I[Expected: Error or Exception Path]
    E --> J[Run as One Test Case]
    F --> J
    G --> K[Run as Distinct Test Case]
    H --> K
    I --> K
```

## 4.4 Visual Mermaid Schematic — Sample Loan-Engine Partitions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Annual Income AI"
        AI1[Ev AI greater or equal to 5]
        AI2[Ei AI less than 5]
    end
    subgraph "Credit Score CS"
        CS1[Ev CS between 700 and 900]
        CS2[Ei CS less than 700]
        CS3[Ei CS greater than 900]
    end
    AI1 --> M[Eligible Customer Matrix]
    AI2 --> M
    CS1 --> M
    CS2 --> M
    CS3 --> M
    M --> O1[Test: AI 6 and CS 800 Eligible]
    M --> O2[Test: AI 3 and CS 800 Not Eligible]
    M --> O3[Test: AI 6 and CS 500 Not Eligible]
    M --> O4[Test: AI 6 and CS 950 Not Eligible]
    M --> O5[Test: AI 3 and CS 500 Not Eligible]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define Equivalence Partitioning. Give one example where it is applied.**
*(3 Marks — CO2 / K2 — Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
Equivalence Partitioning is a **black-box test case design technique** that divides the input domain of a software module into groups of equivalent data called *equivalence classes*, under the assumption that **all values in a class are processed identically** by the SUT. A single representative test value is chosen from each class, reducing the total test count significantly.

**Example:** A password field must be **8 to 16 characters** long. The valid equivalence class is any string of length between 8 and 16. The invalid classes are strings of length less than 8 and strings of length greater than 16. We need only **3 test cases** instead of testing every possible length.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q2. Distinguish between Valid and Invalid Equivalence Classes with an example.**
*(3 Marks — CO2 / K3 — Apply)*

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Valid Equivalence Class ($E_v$) | Invalid Equivalence Class ($E_i$) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Definition** | Inputs that the SUT is **designed to accept**. | Inputs the SUT is expected to **reject**. |
| **Expected Output** | Normal / correct processing. | Error message or exception. |
| **Purpose** | Validates the *happy path*. | Validates the *error-handling path*. |
| **Example (Age field, 18-60)** | Age = 35 | Age = 10 or Age = 70 |
| **Counting Rule** | At least one per valid class. | One per **distinct** invalid class. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (Choice 1)

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Equivalence Partitioning** as a black-box test design technique. List and briefly describe **any five guidelines (rules)** used for identifying equivalence classes. *(7 Marks — CO2 / K2 — Understand)*

**(b)** Consider a software module that computes the **electricity bill** based on the following rules:

- Input: number of units consumed, $u$, where $0 \leq u \leq 1000$.
- For $u \leq 100$: rate is **₹1.50 per unit**.
- For $101 \leq u \leq 300$: rate is **₹2.50 per unit**.
- For $u > 300$: rate is **₹4.00 per unit**.
- Any negative unit value must be **rejected with an error**.

Apply **Equivalence Partitioning** to derive all valid and invalid equivalence classes, choose representative test values, and construct a complete test case table. *(7 Marks — CO2 / K4 — Analyze)*

#### Model Solution to Question A(a)

Equivalence Partitioning (EP) is a specification-based technique that **subdivides the input domain** into disjoint partitions. Test cases are designed by picking **one representative value per partition**, based on the principle that all members of a partition are *assumed* to be processed identically by the SUT.

**Five Guidelines for Identifying Equivalence Classes:**

1. **Range / Interval Rule:** If input is constrained as $a \leq x \leq b$, then form **one valid class** and **two invalid classes** ($x < a$, $x > b$).
2. **Discrete Set / Enumeration Rule:** If input must belong to a specific set $\{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$, form one valid class and one invalid class for non-members.
3. **Boolean Rule:** If input is binary (True/False), form **two valid classes**.
4. **Cardinality Rule:** For a count input $n$ with $a \leq n \leq b$, form one valid class and two invalid classes.
5. **Sub-condition Rule:** When an input has independent sub-fields (e.g., date = Day + Month + Year), partition each sub-field independently and combine classes using Cartesian product only when testing valid scenarios.

#### Model Solution to Question A(b)

**Step 1 — Identify input domain.**

$$
u \in \mathbb{Z}, \quad 0 \leq u \leq 1000
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Rule 1 multiple times (because of piecewise rate structure).**

| Class | Type | Definition | Description |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $E_{v1}$ | Valid | $\{u \,\vert\, 0 \leq u \leq 100\}$ | Low-rate slab. |
| $E_{v2}$ | Valid | $\{u \,\vert\, 101 \leq u \leq 300\}$ | Mid-rate slab. |
| $E_{v3}$ | Valid | $\{u \,\vert\, 301 \leq u \leq 1000\}$ | High-rate slab. |
| $E_{i1}$ | Invalid | $\{u \,\vert\, u < 0\}$ | Negative input — must be rejected. |
| $E_{i2}$ | Invalid | $\{u \,\vert\, u > 1000\}$ | Out-of-range upper bound. |

**Step 3 — Choose representatives.**

$$
\text{Rep}(E_{v1}) = 50, \quad \text{Rep}(E_{v2}) = 200, \quad \text{Rep}(E_{v3}) = 500
$$

$$
\text{Rep}(E_{i1}) = -10, \quad \text{Rep}(E_{i2}) = 1500
$$

**Step 4 — Total test cases.**

$$
N = v + i = 3 + 2 = 5
$$

**Step 5 — Test case table.**

| TC ID | Units (u) | Partition | Expected Bill | Verification |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| TC_01 | 50 | $E_{v1}$ | $50 \times 1.50 = ₹75$ | Low-rate computation. |
| TC_02 | 200 | $E_{v2}$ | $200 \times 2.50 = ₹500$ | Mid-rate computation. |
| TC_03 | 500 | $E_{v3}$ | $500 \times 4.00 = ₹2000$ | High-rate computation. |
| TC_04 | -10 | $E_{i1}$ | "Error: Units cannot be negative." | Error-path verification. |
| TC_05 | 1500 | $E_{i2}$ | "Error: Units exceed maximum limit." | Error-path verification. |

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**(a)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of Equivalence Partitioning. How is it related to **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)**? *(7 Marks — CO2 / K3 — Apply)*

**(b)** A login form accepts a **User ID** that must be a 6-character alphanumeric string (letters and digits only, must start with a letter). Apply Equivalence Partitioning to identify all valid and invalid classes, select representatives, and present the resulting test cases. *(7 Marks — CO2 / K4 — Analyze)*

#### Model Solution to Question B(a)

**Advantages of EP:**

- **Reduces redundancy:** Drastically cuts down the number of test cases by testing *one representative per class*.
- **Improves coverage:** Ensures every logical input group is exercised.
- **Spec-driven:** Forces a careful review of the requirement specification, often revealing ambiguities early.
- **Easy to automate:** Representative values can be parameterized in tools like Selenium, JUnit, or TestNG.
- **Suitable for any black-box input** (numeric, text, file, etc.).

**Limitations of EP:**

- **Misses boundary defects:** EP alone may not catch off-by-one errors (e.g., at $a-1$, $a$, $b$, $b+1$).
- **Sensitive to spec quality:** Vague specifications produce poor partitions.
- **Cannot model interactions** between multiple input conditions on its own.
- **Assumes uniform behavior:** The "all values in a class behave the same" assumption may not always hold.

**Relationship with BVA:**

> [!NOTE]
> **ISTQB/KTU perspective:** EP and BVA are **complementary**, not competing techniques. EP first divides the input into *classes*, and BVA then focuses on the **edges** (boundaries) of those classes. Together, they form the backbone of input-domain testing.

#### Model Solution to Question B(b)

**Step 1 — Decompose the input into sub-conditions:**

- Sub-condition 1: **Length** of the string.
- Sub-condition 2: **Starting character** type.
- Sub-condition 3: **Character set** of all positions.

**Step 2 — Derive equivalence classes per sub-condition.**

For **Length** (must be exactly 6 characters):

$$
E_{v}^{(L)} = \{s \,\vert\, \vert s \vert = 6\}, \quad E_{i1}^{(L)} = \{s \,\vert\, \vert s \vert < 6\}, \quad E_{i2}^{(L)} = \{s \,\vert\, \vert s \vert > 6\}
$$

For **Starting Character** (must be a letter):

$$
E_{v}^{(SC)} = \{c \,\vert\, c \in [A-Z] \cup [a-z]\}, \quad E_{i}^{(SC)} = \{c \,\vert\, c \in [0-9]\}
$$

For **Character Set** (letters and digits only):

$$
E_{v}^{(CS)} = \{c \,\vert\, c \in [A-Za-z0-9]\}, \quad E_{i}^{(CS)} = \{c \,\vert\, c \in \text{special symbols}\}
$$

**Step 3 — Choose representative test values.**

| Sub-condition | Valid Rep | Invalid Rep |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Length | "Abc123" (6 chars) | "Ab12" (4), "Abc12345" (9) |
| Start char | 'A' (letter) | '7' (digit) |
| Char set | 'a', 'Z', '0', '9' | '@', '#', '!' |

**Step 4 — Test case table (all valid combinations + each invalid path).**

| TC ID | User ID Input | Sub-condition(s) Violated | Expected Output |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| TC_01 | `Abc123` | None | "Valid User ID." |
| TC_02 | `Abc12` | Length < 6 | "Error: Must be 6 characters." |
| TC_03 | `Abc1234` | Length > 6 | "Error: Must be 6 characters." |
| TC_04 | `7bc123` | Starts with digit | "Error: Must start with a letter." |
| TC_05 | `Ab@123` | Contains special char | "Error: Only alphanumeric allowed." |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mark Loss Cause #1:** Students often list valid classes but *forget the multiple invalid classes* (e.g., both lower-than-min and higher-than-max). KTU examiners award 1-2 marks per correctly identified class.
> 2. **Mark Loss Cause #2:** Students write *"test the boundary value"* — but the question asks for **EP**, not BVA. Mixing the two techniques loses marks.
> 3. **Mark Loss Cause #3:** Picking the *same* representative for two different partitions. Each partition must have a **unique** representative.
> 4. **Mark Loss Cause #4:** Missing the **expected output / error message** column. A test case without expected result is incomplete.
> 5. **Mark Loss Cause #5:** Forgetting to **count test cases** using $N = v + i$. Always state the total explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Sheet for Equivalence Partitioning — KTU 2024 Scheme**

- **Definition:** EP is a **black-box, specification-based** test design technique that divides inputs into *equivalent* groups and tests *one representative per group*.
- **Two Class Types:** **Valid** ($E_v$) — accepted by the SUT; **Invalid** ($E_i$) — rejected or error-path tested.
- **Core Assumption:** *All values in a partition are processed identically* by the system.
- **The Five Identification Rules:** Range/Interval, Discrete Set/Enumeration, Boolean, Cardinality, Sub-condition decomposition.
- **Test Count Formula:** $\;N = v + i\;$ (one representative from each valid and each invalid class).
- **Representative Selection:** Midpoint for valid ranges, $a-1$ for lower invalid, $b+1$ for upper invalid.
- **Pairing with BVA:** Use **BVA** at the *edges* of equivalence classes to catch off-by-one defects that EP may miss.
- **Advantages:** Test reduction, spec-driven rigor, automation-friendly, broad input coverage.
- **Limitations:** Cannot model complex interactions, sensitive to spec quality, no guarantee of structural coverage.
- **Always Include in Your Answer:** Class list (valid + invalid), representative choice, test case table with **Test ID / Input / Partition / Expected Output**.
- **Avoid Common Mistakes:** Mixing EP and BVA, duplicate representatives, missing invalid classes, omitting expected outputs.
- **Industrial Relevance:** Foundation for **ISTQB CTFL certification** and **most QA interviews** (TCS, Infosys, Wipro, Cognizant, IBM India).
- **Synonyms to Recognize in Exam:** "Equivalence Class Testing", "Partition Testing", "ECP" — all refer to the **same technique**.
- **Quick Mnemonic:** "**P**artition → **R**epresentative → **T**est Case" (PRT cycle).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: White Box Testing - Static Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT747 - SOFTWARE TESTING AND QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** White Box Testing - Static Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Static Analysis** is a white-box testing technique that evaluates the source code, design documents, and software artifacts *without actually executing the program*. It is a manual or automated review-based verification technique focused on finding defects, code smells, security vulnerabilities, and structural anomalies in the *static* (non-running) representation of the software.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Static Analysis is the process of examining the code or documentation of a software system without executing it. The primary goal is to find defects early in the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC), improve code quality, enforce coding standards, and verify conformance to design specifications.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Building Inspector**
> Imagine you are constructing a skyscraper. Before pouring the concrete or switching on the electricity, a **building inspector** comes to the site with a blueprint. He reviews the architectural plans, checks whether the steel reinforcement matches the design, ensures no gas pipelines cross electrical conduits, and verifies the structural integrity of the drawings — all **without the building actually being built or lived in**.
>
> Static Analysis in software testing plays exactly the same role. Instead of waiting for the program to crash at runtime, an inspector (a human reviewer or a tool) reads the source code to catch mistakes *before execution*.

### 1.3 Static vs. Dynamic Testing

| Attribute | Static Analysis | Dynamic Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| Execution Required | **No** | **Yes** |
| Phase | Early (Design/Coding) | Later (Testing) |
| Cost of Defect Fix | **Low** | High |
| Coverage Type | Structural / Code | Functional / Behavioral |
| Automation | Lint, SonarQube, FindBugs | JUnit, Selenium, QTP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Highlight:** Static testing finds **~60–80%** of defects during the early phases, drastically reducing the cost of rework.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Static Analysis

- **Cyclomatic Complexity ($V(G)$)** — McCabe's metric
- **Halstead Complexity Measures** — Volume, Difficulty, Effort
- **Lines of Code (LOC)** — basic size measure
- **Maintainability Index (MI)** — composite metric
- **Code Coverage (%)** — Branch, Statement, Path coverage
- **Defect Density** = Defects / KLOC (per thousand lines of code)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 'Why' and 'How' of Static Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Why Static Analysis?
1. **Early Defect Detection** — Catches errors at the coding stage.
2. **Cost Efficiency** — The cost to fix a defect grows exponentially across phases.
3. **Security Hardening** — Detects buffer overflows, SQL injections, hardcoded secrets.
4. **Standard Enforcement** — Ensures adherence to MISRA, ISO 26262, CERT, or internal coding standards.
5. **Documentation of Code Structure** — Generates call graphs, dependency maps.

#### 2.1.2 How Static Analysis Works (Operational Logic)

- **Step 1: Lexical Analysis** — Tokenization of the source code.
- **Step 2: Parsing** — Building an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST).
- **Step 3: Semantic Analysis** — Type checking, symbol resolution.
- **Step 4: Control Flow Graph (CFG) Construction** — Mapping all possible execution paths.
- **Step 5: Data Flow Analysis** — Tracking variable definitions, uses, and definitions-use chains (DU-chains).
- **Step 6: Rule Application** — Applying coding rules, security patterns, complexity thresholds.
- **Step 7: Report Generation** — Listing violations, severity, line numbers, and remediation.

### 2.2 Types of Static Analysis (KTU High-Yield Classification)

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Major Branches:**
> 1. **Manual Static Analysis** — Human-driven reviews, walkthroughs, inspections.
> 2. **Automated Static Analysis** — Tool-driven scanning (lint, AST analysis, model checking).

#### A. Manual Static Techniques

1. **Walkthroughs** — Informal, author-led session to explain logic and gather feedback.
2. **Technical Reviews** — Structured, peer-led, document-focused review with a defined agenda.
3. **Inspections** — Most formal review type; led by a trained *moderator*, uses *checklists*, *roles*, and *entry/exit criteria*. Originated by Michael Fagan at IBM (1976).
4. **Desk Checking** — A solitary author manually traces logic with sample inputs.

#### B. Automated Static Techniques

1. **Data Flow Analysis** — Tracks `def` and `use` of variables.
2. **Control Flow Analysis** — Models program as a graph to find unreachable code, infinite loops.
3. **Cyclomatic Complexity** — McCabe's measure of independent paths.
4. **Code Standards Enforcement** — Tools like MISRA-C checkers, ESLint, Checkstyle.
5. **Symbolic Execution** — Uses symbolic inputs instead of concrete data.
6. **Formal Methods** — Mathematical proof of correctness (model checking, theorem proving).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Purpose / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cyclomatic Complexity** (McCabe) | $V(G) = E - N + 2P$ | Counts independent paths in a module |
| 2 | Cyclomatic Complexity (Predicate Method) | $V(G) = \pi + 1$ | $\pi$ = number of decision points (predicate nodes) |
| 3 | **Halstead Volume** | $V = N \cdot \log_2(\eta)$ | Measures program size from operators/operands |
| 4 | Halstead Difficulty | $D = \frac{\eta_1}{2} \cdot \frac{N_2}{\eta_2}$ | Effort to understand the program |
| 5 | Halstead Effort | $E = V \cdot D$ | Total mental effort to write code |
| 6 | **Defect Density** | $DD = \frac{\text{Defects Found}}{KLOC}$ | Defects per thousand lines of code |
| 7 | **Statement Coverage** | $SC = \frac{S_e}{S_t} \times 100$ | % of executable statements executed |
| 8 | **Branch Coverage** | $BC = \frac{B_e}{B_t} \times 100$ | % of decision branches tested |
| 9 | **Path Coverage** | $PC = \frac{P_e}{P_t} \times 100$ | % of independent paths executed |
| 10 | **Maintainability Index** | $MI = 171 - 5.2 \ln V - 0.23 V(G) - 16.2 \ln L + 50 \sin(\sqrt{2.46 \cdot C})$ | Composite maintainability score (0–100) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where:**
> - $E$ = Edges in CFG, $N$ = Nodes in CFG, $P$ = Connected components (usually 1).
> - $\eta$ = Program vocabulary (unique operators + operands), $N$ = Program length (total operators + operands).
> - $\eta_1$ = unique operators, $\eta_2$ = unique operands, $N_2$ = total operands.
> - $L$ = LOC, $C$ = average cyclomatic complexity per module.

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Avionics (DO-178C)** — Mandates static analysis for Level A and B software.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262)** — Requires MISRA-C compliance verified via static tools.
- **Medical Devices (FDA)** — Static analysis is a recommended verification method.
- **Cybersecurity (OWASP / CERT)** — Detects taint flows, injection vulnerabilities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity

**Problem:** Compute $V(G)$ for the following pseudocode:

```
FUNCTION grade(marks)
    IF marks >= 90 THEN
        grade = "A"
    ELSE IF marks >= 75 THEN
        grade = "B"
    ELSE IF marks >= 60 THEN
               grade = "C"
    ELSE
        grade = "D"
    END IF
    RETURN grade
END FUNCTION
```

#### Step 1: Construct the Control Flow Graph (CFG)

We map the program into a directed graph where:
- **Nodes (N)** = sequential code blocks.
- **Edges (E)** = flow of control.
- **Predicate nodes** = decision points.

```
        +-----------+
        |   Start   |
        +-----+-----+
              |
              v
        +-----------+         marks >= 90
        |  marks    +---F---> +-----------+         marks >= 75
        +-----+-----+         | Decision2 +---F---> +-----------+     marks >= 60
              |T              +-----+-----+         | Decision3 +F-> +-----------+
              v                    |T                +-----+-----+     |    Else   |
        +-----------+              v                      |T            +-----+-----+
        | grade="A" |        +-----------+                v                  |
        +-----+-----+        | grade="B" |          +-----------+            |
              |               +-----+-----+          | grade="C" |            |
              v                    |                 +-----+-----+            |
        +-----------+              v                      |                  |
        |  Return   | <-----------+<---------------------+<-----------------+
        +-----------+
```

#### Step 2: Count the Nodes ($N$) and Edges ($E$)

Let us number the nodes:
- $n_1$ = Start
- $n_2$ = Predicate 1 (marks $\geq$ 90)
- $n_3$ = "A" block
- $n_4$ = Predicate 2 (marks $\geq$ 75)
- $n_5$ = "B" block
- $n_6$ = Predicate 3 (marks $\geq$ 60)
- $n_7$ = "C" block
- $n_8$ = "D" block
- $n_9$ = Return

Therefore:

$$N = 9$$

Edges (transitions between nodes):

$$E = 10$$

- $n_1 \to n_2$, $n_2 \to n_3$, $n_2 \to n_4$, $n_3 \to n_9$, $n_4 \to n_5$, $n_4 \to n_6$, $n_5 \to n_9$, $n_6 \to n_7$, $n_6 \to n_8$, $n_7 \to n_9$, $n_8 \to n_9$.

Wait, recount: $n_2 \to n_4$, $n_4 \to n_6$, $n_6 \to n_8$, so total edges = 11. Let us recount carefully.

- Edge 1: $n_1 \to n_2$
- Edge 2: $n_2 \to n_3$ (T)
- Edge 3: $n_2 \to n_4$ (F)
- Edge 4: $n_3 \to n_9$
- Edge 5: $n_4 \to n_5$ (T)
- Edge 6: $n_4 \to n_6$ (F)
- Edge 7: $n_5 \to n_9$
- Edge 8: $n_6 \to n_7$ (T)
- Edge 9: $n_6 \to n_8$ (F)
- Edge 10: $n_7 \to n_9$
- Edge 11: $n_8 \to n_9$

So $E = 11$, $N = 9$, and $P = 1$ (one connected component).

#### Step 3: Apply the McCabe Formula

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

Substituting the values:

$$V(G) = 11 - 9 + 2(1)$$

Performing the arithmetic step-by-step:

$$V(G) = 11 - 9 + 2$$

$$V(G) = 2 + 2$$

$$\boxed{V(G) = 4}$$

#### Step 4: Verify Using the Predicate Method

Predicate nodes = 3 (`IF marks >= 90`, `ELSE IF marks >= 75`, `ELSE IF marks >= 60`).

$$V(G) = \pi + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4$$

Both methods agree. **$V(G) = 4$** independent paths exist.

#### Step 5: Enumerate the Independent Paths

| Path | Traversal |
|---|---|
| P1 | A $\to$ Return |
| P2 | B $\to$ Return |
| P3 | C $\to$ Return |
| P4 | D $\to$ Return |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Always draw the CFG, count nodes and edges carefully, and apply the formula. A common error is to forget the `2P` term when there is a single connected component.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Defects to Static Techniques

| Defect Type | Detection Method | Tool / Technique | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unreachable code | Control Flow Analysis | SonarQube, Lint | Medium |
| Uninitialized variable | Data Flow Analysis (DEF-USE) | Coverity, Klocwork | High |
| Buffer overflow | Taint / Pattern matching | Fortify, Checkmarx | Critical |
| Cyclomatic complexity > 10 | McCabe metric | SonarQube, Understand | Medium |
| SQL injection | Pattern / AST analysis | Veracode, Semgrep | Critical |
| Hardcoded credentials | Lexical / String scan | TruffleHog, GitLeaks | High |
| Code duplication | Clone detection | PMD CPD, Simian | Low |
| Naming convention violation | Style checkers | Checkstyle, ESLint | Low |
| Memory leak (potential) | Data flow / escape analysis | Coverity, Polyspace | High |
| Deadlock potential | Concurrency analysis | CodeSonar, ThreadSanitizer | High |

### 3.3 Sample Defect Density Calculation

**Problem:** A module of **4,500 LOC** has **27 known defects** identified by static analysis. Compute the defect density.

**Solution:**

$$DD = \frac{\text{Defects}}{KLOC} = \frac{27}{4.5}$$

Performing the division:

$$DD = 6.0 \; \text{defects per KLOC}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Benchmark:** A defect density of $\leq 1$ per KLOC is considered excellent for safety-critical systems; $1$–$5$ is acceptable for commercial software; $>5$ indicates poor quality.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Static Analysis Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Code Repository] --> B[Lexer Tokenizer]
    B --> C[Parser]
    C --> D[Abstract Syntax Tree AST]
    D --> E[Semantic Analyzer]
    E --> F[Control Flow Graph CFG Builder]
    F --> G[Data Flow Analyzer]
    G --> H[Rule Engine]
    H --> I[Standards Library]
    H --> J[Security Patterns DB]
    H --> K[Complexity Metrics]
    I --> L[Report Generator]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Defect Dashboard]
    L --> N[Violations List]
    L --> O[Quality Gate Decision]
```

**Description of the Pipeline:**
- **A** to **B**: The source code is broken into tokens (keywords, identifiers, operators).
- **B** to **C**: The parser verifies syntax and builds a tree.
- **C** to **D**: The AST represents the program's hierarchical structure.
- **D** to **E**: Type checks and symbol resolution are performed.
- **E** to **F**: CFG nodes are created for each basic block.
- **F** to **G**: Variable definitions and uses are tracked.
- **G** to **H**: The rule engine applies standards, security, and complexity rules.
- **H** to **L**: A unified report is generated for QA and CI/CD pipelines.

### 4.2 Static Analysis Categories — Hierarchical View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SA[Static Analysis]
    SA --> M[Manual Techniques]
    SA --> A[Automated Techniques]
    M --> M1[Walkthroughs]
    M --> M2[Technical Reviews]
    M --> M3[Inspections]
    M --> M4[Desk Checking]
    A --> A1[Lexical Analysis]
    A --> A2[Syntactic Parsing]
    A --> A3[Semantic Analysis]
    A --> A4[Control Flow Analysis]
    A --> A5[Data Flow Analysis]
    A --> A6[Symbolic Execution]
    A --> A7[Model Checking]
    A --> A8[Complexity Metrics]
```

### 4.3 Fagan Inspection Process (Formal Review Workflow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Planning] --> P2[Overview]
    P2 --> P3[Individual Preparation]
    P3 --> P4[Inspection Meeting]
    P4 --> P5[Rework]
    P5 --> P6[Follow-up]
    P6 --> P1
```

**Roles in Fagan Inspection:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Inspection Team]
    R --> RM[Moderator]
    R --> RA[Author]
    R --> RR[Reader]
    R --> RS[Scribe / Recorder]
    R --> RV[Reviewer]
```

### 4.4 Cyclomatic Complexity Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{V(G) <= 10?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> L[Low Risk - Easy to Test]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{V(G) <= 20?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> M[Moderate Risk - Needs Refactoring]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{V(G) <= 50?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> H[High Risk - Refactor Immediately]
    Q3 -- No --> C[Untestable - Redesign Module]
```

### 4.5 Static vs Dynamic Analysis — Comparative Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S[Static Analysis]
        S1[Code Reviews]
        S2[Linting Tools]
        S3[Metrics Computation]
    end
    subgraph D[Dynamic Analysis]
        D1[Unit Tests]
        D2[Integration Tests]
        D3[Performance Tests]
    end
    S --> Q[Software Quality]
    D --> Q
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Static and dynamic analysis are **complementary**, not alternatives. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly emphasizes that high-quality software requires *both*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define Static Analysis. List any four static analysis techniques.**

**Model Answer:**
Static analysis is a software verification technique that examines the source code, design, and documentation of a system *without executing* the program. Its objective is to identify defects, security vulnerabilities, and standard violations early in the development lifecycle.

**Four Static Analysis Techniques:**
1. **Walkthroughs** — Informal, author-led group review.
2. **Technical Reviews** — Structured, peer-led review of technical artifacts.
3. **Inspections** — Formal review using checklists and defined roles (Fagan method).
4. **Automated Code Analysis** — Tool-based scanning using lint, AST, or data flow analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + 4 techniques (0.5 each = 2 marks).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between static testing and dynamic testing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Static Testing | Dynamic Testing |
|---|---|---|
| Execution | Code is not executed | Code is executed |
| Phase Performed | Verification (early) | Validation (later) |
| Defects Found | Syntax, standards, logic flow | Runtime errors, performance |
| Tools | Lint, SonarQube, Checkstyle | JUnit, Selenium, LoadRunner |
| Cost | Low (early detection) | High (late detection) |
| Coverage Type | Structural | Functional / Behavioral |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module 2 Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**

**a) Explain the McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity metric. Compute $V(G)$ for the given control flow graph using the formula $V(G) = E - N + 2P$. (7 marks)**

**b) Discuss the Halstead Software Science metrics with suitable formulas. Compute the Halstead Volume and Effort for a sample program snippet. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution A(a) — Cyclomatic Complexity**

**Definition:**
Cyclomatic Complexity, proposed by Thomas J. McCabe (1976), is a software metric that quantifies the number of linearly independent paths through a program's source code. It indicates the minimum number of test cases required to achieve *branch coverage* of a module.

**Formula:**

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

Where:
- $E$ = number of edges in the CFG,
- $N$ = number of nodes in the CFG,
- $P$ = number of connected components (usually 1).

**Worked Example:**

Consider the following CFG with $E = 12$, $N = 10$, $P = 1$:

$$V(G) = E - N + 2P = 12 - 10 + 2(1)$$

Step-by-step:

$$V(G) = 12 - 10 + 2 = 4$$

$$\boxed{V(G) = 4}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $V(G) \leq 10$ → Low risk, simple module.
- $11 \leq V(G) \leq 20$ → Moderate complexity, refactor suggested.
- $V(G) > 20$ → High risk, untestable, must be redesigned.

**Advantages:**
- Easy to compute automatically.
- Strongly correlated with defect density.
- Predicts maintainability.

**Limitations:**
- Does not measure data complexity.
- Ignores nesting depth.

---

#### **Solution A(b) — Halstead Metrics**

**Definition:**
Halstead Software Science, introduced by Maurice H. Halstead (1977), measures program complexity based on counts of *operators* and *operands*.

**Basic Parameters:**

| Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|
| $\eta_1$ | Number of distinct operators |
| $\eta_2$ | Number of distinct operands |
| $N_1$ | Total occurrences of operators |
| $N_2$ | Total occurrences of operands |

**Derived Quantities:**

$$\eta = \eta_1 + \eta_2 \quad \text{(Program Vocabulary)}$$

$$N = N_1 + N_2 \quad \text{(Program Length)}$$

**Volume Formula:**

$$V = N \cdot \log_2(\eta)$$

**Difficulty Formula:**

$$D = \frac{\eta_1}{2} \cdot \frac{N_2}{\eta_2}$$

**Effort Formula:**

$$E = V \cdot D$$

**Sample Program:**

```
a = b + c
d = a * b
```

**Token Counts:**

| Token | Type | Count |
|---|---|---|
| `=` | Operator | 2 |
| `+` | Operator | 1 |
| `*` | Operator | 1 |
| `a` | Operand | 2 |
| `b` | Operand | 2 |
| `c` | Operand | 1 |
| `d` | Operand | 1 |

So: $\eta_1 = 3$, $\eta_2 = 4$, $N_1 = 4$, $N_2 = 6$.

**Computation:**

$$\eta = 3 + 4 = 7$$

$$N = 4 + 6 = 10$$

$$V = 10 \cdot \log_2(7) = 10 \cdot 2.807 = 28.07$$

$$D = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{6}{4} = 1.5 \cdot 1.5 = 2.25$$

$$E = V \cdot D = 28.07 \cdot 2.25 = 63.16$$

$$\boxed{V = 28.07, \quad D = 2.25, \quad E = 63.16}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**a) Explain the Fagan Inspection process in detail. List the roles and phases involved. (7 marks)**

**b) What is Control Flow Analysis? Explain how unreachable code and infinite loops are detected using CFG-based static analysis. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution B(a) — Fagan Inspection**

**Definition:**
Fagan Inspection, introduced by Michael Fagan at IBM in 1976, is the most formal type of static review. It uses a structured process with defined entry/exit criteria, predefined roles, and systematic defect detection using checklists.

**Six Phases:**

| Phase | Purpose | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Planning | Moderator plans meeting | Schedule, materials |
| 2. Overview | Author presents context | Shared understanding |
| 3. Individual Preparation | Reviewers study code | List of issues |
| 4. Inspection Meeting | Defects logged by scribe | Issue log |
| 5. Rework | Author fixes defects | Patched code |
| 6. Follow-up | Moderator verifies fixes | Sign-off / Closure |

**Roles in Fagan Inspection:**

| Role | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Moderator** | Leads, schedules, manages, ensures process discipline |
| **Author** | Owns the code, presents it, performs rework |
| **Reader** | Walks through the code during the meeting |
| **Scribe / Recorder** | Logs all defects and issues |
| **Reviewer** | Identifies defects, prepares in advance |

**Entry Criteria:** Code complete, syntax-clean, checklists ready.
**Exit Criteria:** All defects logged, rework complete, follow-up done.

**Benefits:**
- Catches **~60–80%** of defects before testing.
- Reduces cost of rework.
- Promotes knowledge sharing and team learning.

---

#### **Solution B(b) — Control Flow Analysis**

**Definition:**
Control Flow Analysis (CFA) is a static analysis technique that models a program as a **Control Flow Graph (CFG)**. Each *basic block* of code becomes a node, and each possible transfer of control becomes a directed edge. CFA is used to reason about program structure without execution.

**CFG Construction Steps:**
1. Identify basic blocks — maximal sequences of straight-line code.
2. Identify branch statements — `if`, `while`, `for`, `switch`, `return`, `break`, `continue`.
3. Create directed edges between blocks for all possible transitions.

**Detection of Unreachable Code:**
A block $B$ is *unreachable* if no path exists from the entry node to $B$.

**Algorithm:**

$$\text{Reachable} = \emptyset$$
$$\text{Worklist} = \{\text{EntryNode}\}$$
$$\text{While Worklist} \neq \emptyset:$$
$$\quad n = \text{Worklist.pop()}$$
$$\quad \text{Reachable} = \text{Reachable} \cup \{n\}$$
$$\quad \text{For each successor } s \text{ of } n:$$
$$\quad\quad \text{if } s \notin \text{Reachable}: \text{Worklist.push}(s)$$
$$\text{Unreachable} = \text{AllNodes} - \text{Reachable}$$

**Example:**

```
int x = 10;
return 0;
printf("Dead");   // Unreachable
```

The third statement has no incoming edge from any reachable block.

**Detection of Infinite Loops:**
A loop is infinite if its exit condition is never satisfied. CFA detects this by:

1. Identifying back-edges in the CFG (edges from a node to a dominator ancestor).
2. Checking whether the loop's exit condition depends on a variable modified inside the loop.
3. Using a **data flow analysis** of induction variables.

**Example of an Infinite Loop:**

```
while (true) {
    printf("Running");
}
// No break or return — back-edge without exit
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CFA's Limitations:** It does not detect *all* infinite loops because loop termination may depend on runtime data, which is undecidable in general (Halting Problem).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $2P$ term** in $V(G) = E - N + 2P$. Always state $P = 1$ for a single program.
> 2. **Confusing $\eta$ and $N$** in Halstead metrics. $\eta$ = *unique* count; $N$ = *total* count.
> 3. **Mixing up the Fagan phases** — write them in order; examiners deduct marks for jumbling.
> 4. **Not drawing the CFG** in cyclomatic complexity problems. A neat diagram earns easy partial credit.
> 5. **Confusing Static with White-Box testing** — White-Box is the *umbrella*; Static is a *technique* under it.
> 6. **Skipping the formula for Defect Density** — always write $DD = \text{Defects} / \text{KLOC}$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Static Analysis (Module 2)**

- [x] **Static Analysis** = testing *without execution* of code.
- [x] Goal: find defects **early** in SDLC → reduce cost.
- [x] **Manual Techniques:** Walkthroughs, Technical Reviews, Inspections (Fagan), Desk Checking.
- [x] **Automated Techniques:** Linting, AST analysis, Control Flow, Data Flow, Symbolic Execution.
- [x] **McCabe $V(G) = E - N + 2P$** = number of independent paths.
- [x] **Predicate method** $V(G) = \pi + 1$ — quick verification.
- [x] **Halstead Volume** $V = N \log_2 \eta$.
- [x] **Halstead Effort** $E = V \cdot D$.
- [x] **Defect Density** $DD = \text{Defects} / \text{KLOC}$.
- [x] **Fagan Inspection Phases** = Planning, Overview, Preparation, Meeting, Rework, Follow-up.
- [x] **Fagan Roles** = Moderator, Author, Reader, Scribe, Reviewer.
- [x] **CFG** detects unreachable code, infinite loops, dead branches.
- [x] **Statement Coverage** = $S_e / S_t \times 100$.
- [x] **Branch Coverage** = $B_e / B_t \times 100$.
- [x] **Path Coverage** = $P_e / P_t \times 100$.
- [x] **Maintainability Index** = composite metric from Halstead, McCabe, and LOC.
- [x] Static analysis is **complementary** to dynamic testing — not a replacement.
- [x] Tools: **SonarQube, Coverity, Klocwork, Checkstyle, ESLint, PMD, Fortify, Semgrep**.
- [x] Risk thresholds: $V(G) \leq 10$ (safe), $\leq 20$ (moderate), $>20$ (high risk).
- [x] Static analysis enforces **MISRA-C, ISO 26262, DO-178C, CERT** standards in safety-critical domains.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unit Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT747 - SOFTWARE TESTING AND QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Unit Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Testing** is the lowest level of the software testing pyramid in which *individual software units* (functions, methods, classes, or procedures) are tested in **isolation** from the rest of the system to verify that each unit performs as designed and conforms to its specification.

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, Unit Testing is classified under **White-Box (Structural) Testing** and is typically executed by the **developer** during the **implementation phase** of the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC). It is also known as **Component Testing** or **Module Testing**.

### Key Terminology Mapped to KTU Course Outcomes

| Term | Definition (Exam-Ready) |
|---|---|
| **Unit** | The smallest testable piece of code (function, method, class) |
| **Test Case** | A set of preconditions, inputs, actions, and expected outcomes |
| **Test Fixture** | The fixed state used as a baseline for running test cases |
| **Test Suite** | A collection of test cases for a component or module |
| **Stub** | A dummy function that returns hard-coded values (caller-side) |
| **Mock** | A simulated object that verifies interactions and behavior |
| **Driver** | A top-level routine that calls the unit under test (callee-side) |
| **SUT** | System (or Software) Under Test |
| **TDD** | Test-Driven Development — tests are written *before* code |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** This topic primarily satisfies **CO2** — *"Apply various testing techniques to design effective test cases for software modules."* The cognitive level targets **Apply (Bloom Level 3)** and **Analyze (Bloom Level 4)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The LEGO Castle Builder"

Imagine you are an architect building a massive LEGO castle.

- Before assembling the entire castle, you **inspect each LEGO brick individually** — checking its color, shape, and click-fit strength.
- You do NOT build the whole wall first and then check if the bricks are good. That would be *integration* or *system testing*.
- If a brick is defective, you replace it *immediately* before the wall is built. This is **early defect detection**.
- To check a brick, you use a **small jig (fixture)** that holds the brick in a known position — this is your *test fixture*.
- If a brick depends on another piece (e.g., a roof tile), you might use a **dummy brick (stub)** to test the connection.

This brick-by-brick validation is **exactly** what unit testing does in software — it validates the smallest logical building block **before** the components are glued together.

---

## 1.3 Why Unit Testing Matters — The Engineering Perspective

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Rule of 1 : 10 : 100** states that a defect fixed at the unit level costs **1 unit**, at integration **10 units**, and post-release **100 units** in time, money, and reputation.

| Benefit | Engineering Impact |
|---|---|
| **Early Defect Detection** | Reduces downstream debugging effort by up to **60%** |
| **Design Improvement** | Forces modular, decoupled, and testable code |
| **Living Documentation** | Test cases serve as executable specifications |
| **Regression Safety Net** | Refactoring becomes safe with comprehensive unit tests |
| **Faster CI/CD** | Unit tests run in seconds, enabling rapid feedback loops |

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU student misconception: *"Unit testing = running the program once."* This is **WRONG**. Unit testing is *automated*, *repeatable*, *isolated*, and *assertion-based*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Workflow of Unit Testing

Unit testing follows a strict 6-phase operational cycle. Each step is mapped to a KTU 2024 rubric line item.

1. **Identify the Unit (SUT)**
   - Select a single function/method/class.
   - Determine its input domain, output range, and side effects.
   - *Why:* Forces clear interface boundaries — a hallmark of OOP/Modular design.

2. **Design Test Cases Using Specification**
   - Apply **Equivalence Partitioning (EP)** and **Boundary Value Analysis (BVA)** at the function level.
   - Document preconditions, inputs, expected outputs.
   - *How:* Cover normal, boundary, and error equivalence classes.

3. **Set Up the Test Fixture**
   - Initialize the required objects, database stubs, or environment variables.
   - Establish a known-good baseline state.
   - *Why:* Tests must be deterministic — no hidden state leakage.

4. **Write the Test (Automation Code)**
   - Use a unit testing framework: **JUnit (Java)**, **NUnit (.NET)**, **pytest (Python)**, **Google Test (C++)**, **Mocha (JavaScript)**.
   - Encode the *Arrange → Act → Assert* (AAA) pattern.

5. **Execute and Capture Results**
   - Run the test suite via an IDE or CI runner.
   - Capture **pass/fail**, execution time, and code coverage metrics.

6. **Refactor and Re-run (Regression)**
   - Fix defects, improve code, and re-validate that previously passed tests still pass.
   - *Why:* Prevents regression — a critical KTU exam point.

---

## 2.2 Stubs vs. Mocks vs. Drivers — The Isolation Triad

| Technique | Direction | Purpose | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stub** | Replaces a *called* module | Provides canned responses so the unit can be tested | A stub for `getTaxRate(country)` returning a fixed value |
| **Mock** | Replaces a *collaborator* | Verifies interactions (was method `X` called with `Y`?) | A mock for `EmailService` checking if `send()` was invoked |
| **Driver** | Replaces the *caller* | Calls the unit directly from a top-level test harness | A `main()` function that invokes `calculateInterest()` with test data |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Stubs are used when the SUT *depends on* something. Drivers are used when the SUT *is depended upon* by something not yet built.

---

## 2.3 Code Coverage Metrics — KTU Formula Sheet (CRITICAL)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **directly testable** in KTU End Semester Examinations. Memorize the variable meanings.

| Metric | Formula | Variables | Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Statement Coverage (SC)** | $\displaystyle SC = \frac{S_{executed}}{S_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $S_{executed}$ = executed statements, $S_{total}$ = total statements | **≥ 80%** industry standard |
| **Branch Coverage (BC)** | $\displaystyle BC = \frac{B_{executed}}{B_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $B_{executed}$ = executed branches, $B_{total}$ = total branches | **≥ 70%** |
| **Condition Coverage (CC)** | $\displaystyle CC = \frac{C_{true} + C_{false}}{2 \cdot C_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $C_{true}$, $C_{false}$ = outcomes of boolean sub-expressions | **≥ 75%** |
| **Path Coverage (PC)** | $\displaystyle PC = \frac{P_{executed}}{P_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $P_{executed}$ = executed independent paths | **Hard to achieve 100%** |
| **Function Coverage (FC)** | $\displaystyle FC = \frac{F_{called}}{F_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $F_{called}$ = functions invoked, $F_{total}$ = total functions | **≥ 90%** |

### McCabe's Cyclomatic Complexity (THE Most Tested Formula)

$$\displaystyle V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

Where:
- $V(G)$ = Cyclomatic complexity of the control flow graph
- $E$ = Number of **edges** in the flow graph
- $N$ = Number of **nodes** in the flow graph
- $P$ = Number of **connected components** (typically 1 for a single program, so $2P = 2$)

**Alternate forms** (must know all three for KTU):

$$\displaystyle V(G) = \pi + 1 \quad \text{(where } \pi = \text{number of predicate nodes)}$$

$$\displaystyle V(G) = R \quad \text{(where } R = \text{Number of enclosed regions in the planar flow graph)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation Rule:** A function with $V(G) > 10$ is considered **high-risk** and should be refactored. McCabe's threshold is **10**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Unit testing is the **bedrock of modern DevOps and CI/CD pipelines**:

- **In Agile/Scrum:** Unit tests are written during sprint development and integrated into the Definition of Done.
- **In TDD:** The Red-Green-Refactor cycle mandates unit tests as the *first* artifact created.
- **In Open-Source:** Projects like *Linux Kernel*, *Kubernetes*, and *React* require ≥ 80% unit test coverage for pull-request acceptance.
- **In Safety-Critical Systems (ISO 26262, DO-178C):** Unit testing is a **mandatory regulatory requirement**.

---

## 2.5 Test-Driven Development (TDD) — The Red-Green-Refactor Cycle

| Step | Color | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 🔴 **Red** | Write a failing test for a new feature | Test fails as expected |
| 2 | 🟢 **Green** | Write the *minimum* code to make the test pass | Test passes |
| 3 | 🔵 **Refactor** | Improve code structure while keeping tests green | Cleaner code, tests still pass |

> [!NOTE]
> TDD ensures **100% test-first coverage** by construction — a frequent KTU essay/short-note question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Calculating Cyclomatic Complexity (14-Mark Standard)

**Given Source Code (C/Java-style pseudocode):**

```c
1  int classify(int x, int y) {
2      if (x > 0 && y > 0) {       // Predicate 1
3          return 1;
4      } else if (x > 0 || y < 0) { // Predicate 2
5          return 2;
6      } else {
7          for (int i = 0; i < x; i++) { // Predicate 3
8              y = y + i;
9          }
10         return y;
11     }
12 }
```

### Step 1: Construct the Control Flow Graph (CFG)

We identify **nodes** (N) for each executable block and **edges** (E) for control flow.

- **Nodes (N = 8):**
  - Node 1: Start (entry)
  - Node 2: `(x > 0 && y > 0)` predicate
  - Node 3: `return 1;`
  - Node 4: `(x > 0 || y < 0)` predicate
  - Node 5: `return 2;`
  - Node 6: `for` loop predicate
  - Node 7: Loop body `y = y + i;`
  - Node 8: `return y;` / exit

- **Edges (E = 10):**
  - 1→2, 2→3 (true branch), 2→4 (false branch)
  - 3→exit, 4→5 (true), 4→6 (false)
  - 5→exit, 6→7 (true), 6→8 (false)
  - 7→6 (loop back)

### Step 2: Apply McCabe's Formula

$$\displaystyle V(G) = E - N + 2P$$

Substitute the values $E = 10$, $N = 8$, $P = 1$:

$$\displaystyle V(G) = 10 - 8 + 2(1)$$

$$\displaystyle V(G) = 10 - 8 + 2$$

$$\displaystyle V(G) = 4$$

### Step 3: Verify Using the Predicate Count Method

Count the predicate nodes (decision points):

- Predicate 1: `x > 0 && y > 0` (compound — counts as **1** decision node for McCabe, but **2** atomic conditions)
- Predicate 2: `x > 0 || y < 0` (compound — 1 decision)
- Predicate 3: `i < x` (loop condition — 1 decision)

Total predicate nodes $\pi = 3$.

$$\displaystyle V(G) = \pi + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4 \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

The function has **4 independent paths** and a complexity of **4**, which is **well below McCabe's threshold of 10**. A *minimum basis test set* of **4 test cases** would achieve 100% path coverage.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Computing Branch and Statement Coverage

**Scenario:** A module contains **100 total statements** and **20 total branches** (decisions). A test run executes **85 statements** and **15 branches**.

### Branch Coverage Calculation

$$\displaystyle BC = \frac{B_{executed}}{B_{total}} \times 100\%$$

$$\displaystyle BC = \frac{15}{20} \times 100\%$$

$$\displaystyle BC = 0.75 \times 100\%$$

$$\displaystyle \boxed{BC = 75\%}$$

### Statement Coverage Calculation

$$\displaystyle SC = \frac{S_{executed}}{S_{total}} \times 100\%$$

$$\displaystyle SC = \frac{85}{100} \times 100\%$$

$$\displaystyle \boxed{SC = 85\%}$$

### Analytical Interpretation

$$\displaystyle \text{Defect Density Indicator} = \frac{100 - SC}{1} = 15 \text{ unexecuted statements}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Statement coverage of 100% does **NOT** guarantee branch coverage of 100%. Always compute both metrics independently in KTU numericals.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Minimum Test Cases for Path Coverage

For a program with $V(G) = 7$, the **minimum number of test cases required for complete path coverage** equals the cyclomatic complexity.

$$\displaystyle N_{tests} = V(G) = 7$$

Justification: McCabe's theorem states that the upper bound of the number of independent paths equals $V(G)$, and each path requires at least one test case to traverse it.

---

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Testing Level | Granularity | Tester | Speed | Cost | Defect Detection % |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit** | Individual functions/classes | Developer | ⚡ Fastest | 💲 Lowest | ~30–50% |
| **Integration** | Module interactions | Developer/QA | 🐢 Slow | 💲💲 Medium | ~20–30% |
| **System** | Entire application | QA team | 🐌 Slowest | 💲💲💲 High | ~15–25% |
| **Acceptance** | User requirements | End-user/QA | 🐌 Variable | 💲💲💲 High | ~5–10% |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Unit testing catches the **majority** of defects, but no level should be skipped. The testing pyramid visualizes this distribution.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Unit Testing Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Unit SUT] --> B[Design Test Cases EP BVA]
    B --> C[Create Test Fixture Baseline State]
    C --> D[Write Automated Test Script]
    D --> E[Execute Test Run]
    E --> F{All Assertions Pass}
    F -- No --> G[Log Defect and Debug]
    G --> A
    F -- Yes --> H[Compute Coverage Metrics]
    H --> I{Meets Coverage Threshold}
    I -- No --> B
    I -- Yes --> J[Integrate Into CI CD Pipeline]
    J --> K[Refactor Code if Needed]
    K --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **closed-loop regression flow** — notice the feedback arrow from `K` back to `E`, ensuring that refactoring does not break existing tests.

---

## 4.2 The Test Pyramid — Strategic Placement of Unit Tests

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[UI End to End Tests Few Slow Brittle] 
    L2[Integration Tests Moderate]
    L3[Unit Tests Many Fast Stable]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L1
```

| Layer | Volume | Speed | KTU Exam Insight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **UI/E2E** | Few (10s) | Slow (minutes) | Expensive, brittle |
| **Integration** | Moderate (100s) | Medium (seconds) | Validates interfaces |
| **Unit** | Many (1000s) | Fast (milliseconds) | Foundation of quality |

---

## 4.3 Stub vs. Mock vs. Driver — Isolation Pattern Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUT_Zone["System Under Test SUT"]
        UNIT[Target Unit Function or Method]
    end
    subgraph TestHarness["Test Harness AAA Pattern"]
        DRV[Driver Top Level Caller]
        ARR[Arrange Phase Setup Fixtures]
        ACT[Act Phase Invoke Unit]
        AST[Assert Phase Verify Result]
    end
    subgraph Collaborators["Replaced Dependencies"]
        STUB[Stub Returns Fixed Value]
        MOCK[Mock Verifies Interaction]
    end
    DRV --> ARR
    ARR --> ACT
    ACT --> UNIT
    UNIT --> STUB
    UNIT --> MOCK
    ACT --> AST
    AST -->|Pass| DONE[Test Pass Recorded]
    AST -->|Fail| FAIL[Test Failure Logged]
```

---

## 4.4 TDD — Red Green Refactor State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> RedPhase
    RedPhase: Red Phase Write Failing Test
    GreenPhase: Green Phase Write Minimum Code
    RefactorPhase: Refactor Phase Improve Design
    RedPhase --> GreenPhase: Make Test Pass
    GreenPhase --> RefactorPhase: Keep Tests Green
    RefactorPhase --> RedPhase: Add New Feature
    RefactorPhase --> [*]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Exam 2024]`

**Q: Define Unit Testing. List any four characteristics of an effective unit test.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Unit testing is a level of software testing where individual units or components of a software application are tested in isolation to verify that each unit functions as designed.

**Four Characteristics (2 Marks — ½ mark each):**
1. **Automated** — Tests must be executable via a script or framework without manual intervention.
2. **Isolated** — Each test must be independent of other tests (no shared mutable state).
3. **Repeatable** — Running the test multiple times must yield the same result.
4. **Fast** — Unit tests should execute in milliseconds to enable rapid feedback.
5. **Self-Validating** — Tests must produce a clear pass/fail outcome via assertions.

> [!NOTE]
> Full marks require *both* the definition **and** the four characteristics clearly listed.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series 1]`

**Q: Differentiate between a Stub and a Mock object in unit testing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | **Stub** | **Mock** |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Provides canned data to the SUT | Verifies interactions and behavior |
| **Verification** | State-based (return values) | Behavior-based (was method called?) |
| **Coupling** | Loose — only outputs matter | Tight — call sequences matter |
| **Example** | Stub `getUser(id)` returns `User{name:"John"}` | Mock verifies `emailService.send()` was called once |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A common student error is to use "stub" and "mock" interchangeably. They are **distinct** — a stub does not assert; a mock does.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 2 Pattern]`

**Q(a) Explain the concept of Unit Testing in detail. Discuss the Test-Driven Development (TDD) approach with its phases. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Concept of Unit Testing (3.5 Marks):**

Unit testing is a white-box testing technique performed at the **smallest testable level** of a software system — typically an individual function, method, or class. It is executed by the developer during the implementation phase, using a unit testing framework (e.g., JUnit for Java, NUnit for .NET).

**Key Objectives:**
- Validate that each unit works correctly per its specification.
