**Step 1: Sum odd-numbered questions (positive) and subtract 1, sum even-numbered questions (negative) and subtract from 5.**

User 1: Odd Q (1,3,5,7,9) = 4+4+4+4+4 = 20; (20-5) = 15
         Even Q (2,4,6,8,10) = 2+1+1+2+1 = 7; (25-7) = 18
         Total = 15+18 = 33; SUS = 33 × 2.5 = 82.5

User 2: Odd = 3+5+4+4+4 = 20; (20-5) = 15
        Even = 2+1+1+2+1 = 7; (25-7) = 18; Total = 33; SUS = 82.5

User 3: Odd = 4+4+5+4+5 = 22; (22-5) = 17
        Even = 1+1+1+1+1 = 5; (25-5) = 20; Total = 37; SUS = 92.5

User 4: Odd = 4+4+4+3+4 = 19; (19-5) = 14
        Even = 2+2+2+2+2 = 10; (25-10) = 15; Total = 29; SUS = 72.5

User 5: Odd = 5+5+5+5+5 = 25; (25-5) = 20
        Even = 1+1+1+1+1 = 5; (25-5) = 20; Total = 40; SUS = 100

**Step 2: Average SUS Score**

$$\text{Average SUS} = \frac{82.5 + 82.5 + 92.5 + 72.5 + 100}{5}$$

$$\text{Average SUS} = \frac{430}{5}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Average SUS} = 86}$$

**Interpretation:** SUS ≥ 68 is considered "Good" usability. A score of **86 indicates Excellent usability** (above industry average of 68).

### 3.4 Worked Example — Concurrent User Estimation

**Scenario:** An e-commerce site expects **10,000 visitors per hour** during a festival sale, with an **average session duration of 12 minutes**. Estimate concurrent users.

$$\text{Concurrent Users} = \frac{\text{Peak Visitors per Hour} \times \text{Avg Session (min)}}{60}$$

$$\text{Concurrent Users} = \frac{10{,}000 \times 12}{60}$$

$$\text{Concurrent Users} = \frac{120{,}000}{60}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Concurrent Users} = 2{,}000 \text{ users}}$$

**Interpretation:** Performance tests must simulate at least 2,000 concurrent users to validate real-world conditions.

### 3.5 Worked Example — Risk Score Calculation (Security)

**Scenario:** A vulnerability allows SQL injection. The likelihood is rated **4/5** and impact severity is **5/5**.

$$\text{Risk Score} = \text{Threat Likelihood} \times \text{Impact Severity}$$

$$\text{Risk Score} = 4 \times 5 = 20$$

**Interpretation:** On a 1–25 scale, a score of **20 is "Critical"** — must be fixed immediately.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Non-Functional Testing Classification (KTU 2024 Aligned)

```mermaid
graph TD
    NFT[Non-Functional Testing]
    NFT --> PERF[Performance Testing]
    NFT --> SEC[Security Testing]
    NFT --> USAB[Usability Testing]
    NFT --> REL[Reliability Testing]
    NFT --> PORT[Portability Testing]

    PERF --> LOAD[Load Testing]
    PERF --> STRESS[Stress Testing]
    PERF --> SOAK[Endurance / Soak Testing]
    PERF --> SPIKE[Spike Testing]
    PERF --> VOL[Volume Testing]
    PERF --> SCAL[Scalability Testing]

    SEC --> VULN[Vulnerability Scanning]
    SEC --> PEN[Penetration Testing]
    SEC --> AUD[Security Auditing]
    SEC --> EH[Ethical Hacking]
    SEC --> RA[Risk Assessment]

    USAB --> EXP[Explorative Testing]
    USAB --> COMP[Comparative Testing]
    USAB --> ASSESS[Assessment Testing]
    USAB --> HEUR[Heuristic Evaluation]
    USAB --> A11Y[Accessibility Testing]
```

### 4.2 Performance Testing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify Test Environment] --> B[Identify Performance Metrics]
    B --> C[Plan and Design Performance Tests]
    C --> D[Configure Test Environment]
    D --> E[Implement Test Design]
    E --> F[Execute Tests]
    F --> G[Analyze, Report, and Retest]
    G --> H{Performance Acceptable?}
    H -->|No| I[Tune Application / Infrastructure]
    I --> F
    H -->|Yes| J[Sign Off]
```

### 4.3 Penetration Testing Process (Security)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PT as Pen Tester
    participant SYS as Target System
    PT->>SYS: Phase 1: Reconnaissance (Passive + Active)
    SYS-->>PT: Return open ports, services, banners
    PT->>SYS: Phase 2: Scanning (Nmap, Nessus)
    SYS-->>PT: Return vulnerabilities list
    PT->>SYS: Phase 3: Exploitation (SQLi, XSS, etc.)
    SYS-->>PT: Gain unauthorized access
    PT->>SYS: Phase 4: Maintaining Access
    SYS-->>PT: Confirm persistence
    PT->>PT: Phase 5: Analysis & Reporting
    PT-->>PT: Document CVSS scores and remediation
```

### 4.4 Usability Testing Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph TD
    U1[Define Usability Goals] --> U2[Identify User Personas]
    U2 --> U3[Design Realistic Tasks]
    U3 --> U4[Recruit 5-8 Users per Group]
    U4 --> U5[Conduct Test Sessions]
    U5 --> U6[Collect Qualitative + Quantitative Data]
    U6 --> U7[Identify Usability Problems]
    U7 --> U8[Severity Rating: Critical/Major/Minor]
    U8 --> U9[Report & Recommend Fixes]
    U9 --> U10[Iterate Design]
    U10 --> U1
```

### 4.5 CIA Triad — Security Foundation

```mermaid
graph LR
    USER[End User] -->|Reads/Writes| DATA[Data Asset]
    DATA --> C[Confidentiality]
    DATA --> I[Integrity]
    DATA --> A[Availability]
    C -.Protects.-> AUTH[Access Controls, Encryption]
    I -.Protects.-> HASH[Hashing, Digital Signatures]
    A -.Protects.-> RED[Redundancy, Backups, Failover]
```

### 4.6 Block Diagram — Non-Functional Testing Integration in SDLC

```mermaid
graph LR
    REQ[Requirements] --> NFREQ[Identify Non-Functional Requirements]
    NFREQ --> DES[Design Architecture]
    DES --> IMPL[Implementation]
    IMPL --> NFTEST[NFT Execution]
    NFTEST --> PERF1[Performance Tests]
    NFTEST --> SEC1[Security Tests]
    NFTEST --> USA1[Usability Tests]
    PERF1 --> REPORT[Consolidated Report]
    SEC1 --> REPORT
    USA1 --> REPORT
    REPORT --> REL[Release Decision]
    REL --> PROD[Production]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Non-Functional Testing. List any four non-functional testing types.**

**Model Answer:**
Non-Functional Testing (NFT) is a type of software testing performed to evaluate the non-functional attributes of a system — characteristics that describe *how* the system operates rather than *what* it does. It validates the system's performance, security, usability, reliability, and other quality-of-service aspects against specified requirements.

Four types of non-functional testing:
1. **Performance Testing** – evaluates speed, response time, and stability under load
2. **Security Testing** – uncovers vulnerabilities and ensures data protection
3. **Usability Testing** – validates ease of use and user satisfaction
4. **Reliability Testing** – checks the system's ability to perform consistently under stated conditions

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is the difference between Load Testing and Stress Testing?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Load Testing | Stress Testing |
|---|---|---|
| Objective | Measure performance under expected load | Determine breaking point beyond normal capacity |
| Load Level | Normal to peak expected | 2x–10x of normal |
| Purpose | Identify bottlenecks before production | Test system's robustness and graceful failure |
| Outcome | Validate SLAs, baseline metrics | Identify maximum capacity, recovery behavior |
| Example | Simulating 1,000 users on a banking app | Simulating 10,000 users to crash the same app |

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**Q3A. (a)** Explain the different types of performance testing in detail. Mention at least one tool used for each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Load Testing (2 Marks)**
Load testing simulates real-world expected load on the system to measure response time, throughput, and stability under normal and peak conditions. It validates whether the system meets performance SLAs before deployment.
- **Example:** An e-commerce website simulating 5,000 concurrent users browsing products during a flash sale.
- **Tool:** Apache **JMeter** (open-source, supports HTTP, FTP, JDBC protocols).

**2. Stress Testing (2 Marks)**
Stress testing pushes the system beyond its normal operational limits to observe how it fails and recovers. It identifies the system's breaking point and validates graceful degradation.
- **Example:** Doubling the load to 10,000 users until the server crashes, then measuring recovery time.
- **Tool:** **LoadRunner** (HP/Micro Focus) — industry-standard enterprise tool.

**3. Endurance / Soak Testing (1 Mark)**
Tests the system under sustained load for an extended period (8–72 hours) to detect memory leaks, resource degradation, and database connection pool exhaustion.
- **Tool:** **Gatling** (modern Scala-based load testing tool).

**4. Spike Testing (1 Mark)**
Validates the system's behavior when subjected to sudden, extreme load increases or decreases.
- **Tool:** **BlazeMeter** (cloud-based JMeter-compatible platform).

**5. Volume Testing (1 Mark)**
Tests system performance with large volumes of data in the database.
- **Tool:** **HammerDB** (database load testing).

---

**(b)** Describe the different types of security testing. Explain the phases of penetration testing. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Types of Security Testing (3 Marks):**

1. **Vulnerability Scanning** – Automated scanning using tools like **Nessus** or **Qualys** to identify known vulnerabilities against CVE databases.

2. **Penetration Testing** – Manual exploitation of vulnerabilities by ethical hackers using **Burp Suite**, **Metasploit**, or **Kali Linux** tools.

3. **Security Auditing** – Code-level and configuration-level review against standards like **ISO 27001**, **OWASP**, or **NIST**.

4. **Risk Assessment** – Identifying and prioritizing risks using **DREAD** or **STRIDE** frameworks.

5. **Ethical Hacking** – Broader scope of pen testing, including social engineering and physical security assessment.

**Phases of Penetration Testing (4 Marks):**

1. **Planning & Reconnaissance** *(1 Mark)*
   - Define scope, rules of engagement, and gather intelligence.
   - **Passive reconnaissance**: OSINT (Google Dorking, Shodan, social media).
   - **Active reconnaissance**: Ping sweeps, port scanning, DNS enumeration.

2. **Scanning** *(1 Mark)*
   - Identify open ports, services, and vulnerabilities using **Nmap**, **Nessus**, **OpenVAS**.
   - Detect outdated software, misconfigurations, default credentials.

3. **Gaining Access (Exploitation)** *(1 Mark)*
   - Exploit vulnerabilities: SQL injection, XSS, buffer overflow, privilege escalation.
   - Tools: **Metasploit**, **SQLMap**, **Burp Suite**.

4. **Maintaining Access** *(0.5 Mark)*
   - Install backdoors, rootkits to test if attackers can persist undetected.

5. **Analysis & Reporting** *(0.5 Mark)*
   - Document findings with **CVSS scores**, impact analysis, screenshots, and remediation steps.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q3B. (a)** What is Usability Testing? Explain Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Usability testing is a non-functional testing technique that evaluates how easy, efficient, and satisfying a software application is for its end users. It is performed with real representative users performing realistic tasks, and the data collected includes task completion rate, time-on-task, error count, and user satisfaction scores (e.g., SUS — System Usability Scale).

**Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics (5 Marks, 0.5 each):**

1. **Visibility of System Status** – The system should always inform users about what is going on through timely feedback (e.g., progress bars, loading spinners).

2. **Match Between System and Real World** – Use familiar words, phrases, and concepts from the user's world (avoid technical jargon like "ERR_404_Param_Missing"; say "Page not found").

3. **User Control and Freedom** – Provide undo/redo, clear exit routes, and confirmation dialogs for destructive actions.

4. **Consistency and Standards** – Same words, situations, or actions should mean the same thing throughout the application.

5. **Error Prevention** – Design to prevent errors from occurring in the first place (e.g., confirm before deleting).

6. **Recognition Rather than Recall** – Minimize the user's memory load by making objects, actions, and options visible (use icons with labels).

7. **Flexibility and Efficiency of Use** – Provide accelerators (keyboard shortcuts) for expert users while keeping it simple for novices.

8. **Aesthetic and Minimalist Design** – Avoid irrelevant information; every unit of information competes with relevant information.

9. **Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors** – Error messages should be in plain language, precisely indicate the problem, and suggest a solution.

10. **Help and Documentation** – Provide easy-to-search, task-focused help documentation.

---

**(b)** A web application is tested with 2,000 concurrent users over 30 minutes. During the test, 90,000 transactions were attempted, out of which 2,700 failed. Calculate (i) Throughput, (ii) Error Rate, and (iii) Availability, if the system was down for 5 minutes during the test. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Concurrent users = 2,000
- Total test duration = 30 minutes = 1,800 seconds
- Total transactions attempted = 90,000
- Failed transactions = 2,700
- Downtime = 5 minutes

**(i) Throughput (2 Marks):**

$$\text{Throughput (TPS)} = \frac{\text{Total Transactions}}{\text{Total Time (s)}}$$

$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{90{,}000}{1{,}800}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Throughput} = 50 \text{ TPS}}$$

**(ii) Error Rate (2 Marks):**

$$\text{Error Rate (\%)} = \frac{\text{Failed Requests}}{\text{Total Requests}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Error Rate} = \frac{2{,}700}{90{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{Error Rate} = 3\%}$$

**Interpretation:** 3% error rate is **above the industry standard of 1%** — performance tuning is required.

**(iii) Availability (3 Marks):**

Total time = 30 minutes = 1,800 seconds
Downtime = 5 minutes = 300 seconds
Uptime = 1,800 - 300 = 1,500 seconds

$$\text{Availability (\%)} = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime + Downtime}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Availability} = \frac{1{,}500}{1{,}800} \times 100$$

$$\text{Availability} = 0.8333 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{Availability} = 83.33\%}$$

**Interpretation:** "Three nines" (99.9%) is the typical enterprise target. An availability of 83.33% indicates the system is **not production-ready** and requires significant reliability improvements.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Confusing Load vs Stress Testing** — Load = expected load; Stress = beyond expected. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert minutes to seconds** in performance calculations — this is the #1 numerical error in KTU scripts.
> 3. **Misremembering Nielsen's heuristics** — memorize all 10, even if asked 5, additional points come from completeness.
> 4. **Confusing CIA Triad order** — always C-I-A, not A-C-I.
> 5. **Security Testing ≠ only Penetration Testing** — include all types (vulnerability scanning, auditing, risk assessment) to fetch full marks.
> 6. **In numerical questions**, ALWAYS write the formula first, then substitute, then solve — partial marking requires this structure.
> 7. **Forgetting unit labels** (TPS, ms, %) — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Non-Functional Testing**

**Performance Testing (Must-Know):**
- ☐ 6 types: Load, Stress, Endurance, Spike, Volume, Scalability
- ☐ Difference between Load and Stress testing
- ☐ Tools: JMeter, LoadRunner, Gatling, BlazeMeter
- ☐ Key metrics: Response Time (ms), Throughput (TPS), Error Rate (%), CPU/Memory utilization
- ☐ Formulas: TPS, Error Rate, Availability, MTBF, MTTR, Concurrent Users estimation
- ☐ Load testing is done in **staging**, not production

**Security Testing (Must-Know):**
- ☐ CIA Triad: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability
- ☐ 5 types: Vulnerability Scanning, Pen Testing, Auditing, Ethical Hacking, Risk Assessment
- ☐ 5 phases of Pen Testing: Recon → Scan → Exploit → Maintain → Report
- ☐ OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities (at least know 5)
- ☐ Tools: Burp Suite, Nmap, Nessus, Metasploit, OWASP ZAP
- ☐ Risk Score = Likelihood × Impact (1–25 scale)

**Usability Testing (Must-Know):**
- ☐ All 10 Nielsen's heuristics (memorize verbatim)
- ☐ Testing types: Explorative, Comparative, Assessment
- ☐ Process: Goals → Personas → Tasks → Test → Analyze → Report
- ☐ 5 users per group (Nielsen's rule)
- ☐ SUS Score calculation: Sum of (score-1 for odd, 5-score for even) × 2.5
- ☐ SUS ≥ 68 = Good, < 50 = Poor
- ☐ Tools: UserTesting, Hotjar, Morae, Optimal Workshop

**Cross-Cutting (Must-Know):**
- ☐ ISO/IEC 25010 quality model is the KTU 2024 standard reference
- ☐ Non-functional testing happens **after** functional testing (mostly)
- ☐ Security testing follows **shift-left** principle (start in design phase)
- ☐ Each non-functional test has a **threshold** — measure against SLAs

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Assurance Principles and Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT747 - SOFTWARE TESTING AND QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Quality Assurance Principles and Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: Quality Assurance (QA)

**Quality Assurance (QA)** is a systematic, planned set of activities embedded within the software development life cycle (SDLC) that ensures that software engineering processes and products conform to requirements, standards, and procedures. As defined by the IEEE Standard 610.12-1990 and adopted in the KTU 2024 Scheme, QA is a **proactive, process-oriented discipline** whose objective is to prevent defects by improving the process that produces the software.

$$QA = \text{Planned and systematic pattern of actions} \rightarrow \text{Confidence that product/service conforms to requirements}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Emphasis)**
> *"Quality Assurance is the systematic monitoring and evaluation of the various aspects of a project, service, or facility to ensure that standards of quality are being met."*

## 1.2 Distinguishing QA from QC and Testing

| Term | Focus | Timing | Goal | Nature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Quality Assurance (QA)** | **Process** | Entire SDLC | Defect **Prevention** | Proactive |
| **Quality Control (QC)** | **Product** | After build | Defect **Identification** | Reactive |
| **Testing** | **Product behavior** | Post-coding | Finding failures | Reactive + Tactical |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a world-class restaurant:

- **Quality Assurance (QA)** is the *master chef* who designs the recipes, trains the cooks, monitors hygiene, and sets the standards. The chef never personally serves food — but ensures the kitchen *cannot* produce a bad dish because the process is flawless. The chef acts on the **process**.
- **Quality Control (QC)** is the *food critic / head waiter* who tastes every dish before it leaves the kitchen. The critic acts on the **product**.
- **Testing** is the *customer* eating the meal at the table. If the dish is bad, the customer returns it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Board Exam:** A student should *never* answer "QA = testing." They are different layers. QA governs the *process*; Testing executes the *product*. The KTU examiner specifically deducts marks for this confusion.

## 1.4 Quality — The Foundation Concept

**Quality** is defined (per ISO 9000:2015) as the *"degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object fulfills requirements."*

The five classical dimensions of software quality are:

1. **Functionality** — Suitability, accuracy, interoperability, security
2. **Reliability** — Maturity, fault tolerance, recoverability
3. **Usability** — Understandability, learnability, operability
4. **Efficiency** — Time behavior, resource utilization
5. **Maintainability** — Analyzability, changeability, stability, testability
6. **Portability** — Adaptability, installability, replaceability

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants and Metrics Highlighted in Bold:**
> - **Defect Density** = Defects / KLOC (Thousand Lines of Code)
> - **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)** = Total operational time / Number of failures
> - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** = MTTF + MTTR
> - **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** is a standard survey metric
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Quality Assurance Principles (ISO 9000:2015)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists the **Eight Quality Management Principles**. Each is a high-yield 3-mark topic.

| # | Principle | What it Means in Software Engineering |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Customer Focus** | Understanding current and future customer needs; building to spec |
| 2 | **Leadership** | Establishing unity of direction; setting QA vision at the top |
| 3 | **Engagement of People** | Competent, empowered developers create quality |
| 4 | **Process Approach** | Managing activities as interrelated processes yields consistent results |
| 5 | **Improvement** | Continual improvement is a permanent organizational objective |
| 6 | **Evidence-Based Decision Making** | Decisions based on data analysis (defect logs, metrics) |
| 7 | **Relationship Management** | Managing relationships with suppliers, partners |
| 8 | **System Approach to Management** | Identifying, understanding, managing interrelated processes |

## 2.2 Pioneers of Quality Management (Very High Yield for KTU)

### 2.2.1 W. Edwards Deming — The Father of Quality Evolution
Deming contributed **14 Points for Management** and the **PDCA Cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act), which is the bedrock of all modern QA frameworks.

The **PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel / Shewhart Cycle)**:

1. **Plan** — Establish objectives and processes (Define scope, write QA Plan)
2. **Do** — Execute the planned process on a pilot scale (Code, run test cases)
3. **Check** — Monitor and measure performance against the plan (Run reviews, audits)
4. **Act** — Standardize improvements or restart the cycle (Implement corrective action)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** If asked "Name the Deming cycle and explain," students *must* write PDCA in order and pair each step with a software example. Failure to give examples costs 2 of 5 marks.

### 2.2.2 Joseph M. Juran — The Quality Trilogy
Juran proposed three managerial processes for quality:
- **Quality Planning** — Identify customers and their needs
- **Quality Control** — Evaluate actual performance vs goals
- **Quality Improvement** — Establish infrastructure for continuous improvement

### 2.2.3 Philip B. Crosby — Zero Defects
Crosby asserted that *"quality is free"* and proposed **Four Absolutes of Quality**:
1. Quality = **Conformance to requirements** (not goodness)
2. Prevention, not inspection
3. Zero defects performance standard
4. Measure quality by **price of non-conformance**

### 2.2.4 Kaoru Ishikawa — Fishbone Diagram
Ishikawa developed the **Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone) Diagram** for root-cause analysis — frequently tested in KTU labs and theory papers.

## 2.3 Quality Standards — The KTU High-Yield Reference Set

| Standard | Issuing Body | Core Focus | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ISO 9000:2015** | ISO | Generic quality management vocabulary | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **ISO 9001:2015** | ISO | QMS requirements for certification | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **ISO/IEC 25010:2011** | ISO/IEC | Software product quality model (replaced ISO 9126) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **CMMI v2.0** | ISACA/CMMI Institute | Process maturity model (5 levels) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **IEEE 730** | IEEE | Standard for Software Quality Assurance | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **IEEE 12207** | IEEE | Standard for Software Life Cycle Processes | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **ISO/IEC 330xx** | ISO/IEC | Process assessment framework (replaced SPICE) | ⭐⭐⭐ |

## 2.4 Cost of Quality (CoQ) — The Quantitative Backbone

**Cost of Quality (CoQ)** is a framework that classifies quality-related costs into four categories. KTU expects students to *define each* and *draw the optimization curve*.

### Categories of CoQ:

1. **Prevention Costs** — Money spent to *prevent* defects
   - QA planning, training, process improvement, design reviews, tool acquisition
2. **Appraisal Costs** — Money spent to *detect* defects
   - Inspections, testing, code reviews, audits
3. **Internal Failure Costs** — Money spent to *fix* defects found before delivery
   - Rework, re-testing, debugging, defect-fix cost during development
4. **External Failure Costs** — Money spent to *fix* defects found *after* delivery
   - Warranty, patches, customer support, liability, lost reputation

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality Investment Principle:** As prevention/appraisal costs **increase**, internal/external failure costs **decrease**. The **optimal CoQ** is the point where the *total CoQ curve* reaches its minimum.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (For QA Metrics and Cost Calculations)

| Metric / Formula | Equation | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$ | defects / KLOC | Code quality measurement |
| **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $DRE = \dfrac{\text{Defects found before release}}{\text{Defects found before release} + \text{Defects found after release}} \times 100$ | % | Process effectiveness |
| **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** | $MTTF = \dfrac{\text{Total operational time}}{\text{Number of failures}}$ | hours | Reliability |
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$ | hours | Availability |
| **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} \times 100$ | % | System uptime |
| **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | dimensionless | Process maturity |
| **Yield (Y)** | $Y = \dfrac{\text{Units passing first test}}{\text{Total units produced}} \times 100$ | % | Process efficiency |
| **Total Cost of Quality (TCoQ)** | $TCoQ = C_{prev} + C_{appr} + C_{int} + C_{ext}$ | currency | QA investment analysis |
| **Quality Ratio (QR)** | $QR = \dfrac{\text{Number of good units}}{\text{Total units produced}}$ | ratio | Production quality |
| **Reliability Growth Function (R(t))** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | probability | Time-based reliability |
| **Test Effectiveness** | $TE = \dfrac{\text{Defects found by tests}}{\text{Total defects in system}} \times 100$ | % | Test process quality |

> [!CAUTION]
> **LaTeX Pitfall Avoidance:** In markdown tables, absolute-value bars and norm brackets have been written as `\vert` or `\mid` (not raw `|`), preserving the table's column structure. The KTU examiner will not penalize you for this — but a *broken table* in your answer script will certainly cost presentation marks.

## 2.6 CMMI Maturity Levels (The Five-Stage Maturity Pyramid)

The **Capability Maturity Model Integration (CMMI v2.0)** defines five process-maturity levels. KTU questions often ask: *"Explain the five levels of CMMI."*

| Level | Name | Description | Key Process Area Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Initial** | Ad-hoc, chaotic, success depends on individuals | None |
| 2 | **Managed** | Projects are planned and tracked | Requirements Mgmt, Configuration Mgmt |
| 3 | **Defined** | Processes are organization-wide standardized | Peer Reviews, Integrated Software Mgmt |
| 4 | **Quantitatively Managed** | Data-driven, statistical control | Quantitative Process Management |
| 5 | **Optimizing** | Focus on continuous improvement | Causal Analysis, Process Innovation |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Mnemonic (M.D.M.Q.O):** **M**anaged, **D**efined, **Q**uantitatively Managed, **O**ptimizing — preceded by **Initial**. Use this sequence in any KTU question on CMMI.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Aerospace / Defense:** Adherence to DO-178C (avionics) and ISO 26262 (automotive functional safety) is mandated by law.
- **Banking / Finance:** SEBI and PCI-DSS compliance drives QA processes.
- **Healthcare:** FDA's 21 CFR Part 820 enforces quality system regulations.
- **Indian IT Industry (KTU Context):** Most Kerala-based IT firms (TCS, Infosys, UST Global) align their SDLCs with **CMMI Level 3+ and ISO 9001:2015** to win global contracts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cost of Quality Optimization Curve

The Total Cost of Quality is the sum of conformance costs and non-conformance costs. KTU often asks students to *prove* that optimal quality investment occurs at a specific point.

### Step 1: Define Component Costs

Let:
- $C_{prev}(x)$ = Prevention cost (increases with QA investment $x$)
- $C_{appr}(x)$ = Appraisal cost (increases with $x$)
- $C_{int}(x)$ = Internal failure cost (decreases as $x$ grows)
- $C_{ext}(x)$ = External failure cost (decreases as $x$ grows)

### Step 2: Aggregate the Total

$$TCoQ(x) = C_{prev}(x) + C_{appr}(x) + C_{int}(x) + C_{ext}(x)$$

### Step 3: Assume Linear Behaviour for Analytical Simplicity

For the classical analytical model, assume linear approximations:

$$C_{prev}(x) = a_1 x + b_1 \quad ; \quad C_{appr}(x) = a_2 x + b_2$$

$$C_{int}(x) = -a_3 x + b_3 \quad ; \quad C_{ext}(x) = -a_4 x + b_4$$

Where $a_1, a_2, a_3, a_4 > 0$ are sensitivity coefficients, and $b_1, b_2, b_3, b_4$ are baseline constants.

### Step 4: Substitute Into the Total

$$\begin{aligned}
TCoQ(x) &= (a_1 x + b_1) + (a_2 x + b_2) + (-a_3 x + b_3) + (-a_4 x + b_4) \\
&= (a_1 + a_2 - a_3 - a_4)\,x + (b_1 + b_2 + b_3 + b_4)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Find the Cost-Minimising Investment

Take the derivative and set to zero:

$$\frac{d\,TCoQ}{dx} = a_1 + a_2 - a_3 - a_4 = 0$$

Solving for the optimal investment level $x^*$, the system reaches equilibrium when:

$$a_1 + a_2 = a_3 + a_4$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The optimal QA investment is the point where the *marginal cost of prevention + appraisal* equals the *marginal saving from reduced internal + external failures*. In KTU board answers, present this equality as the **"CoQ Equilibrium Condition."**

### Step 6: Practical Conclusion

In real projects, the curves are non-linear and a U-shaped total cost curve is observed. The minimum of the U is the **optimal QA investment level**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Applying QA Formulas to a Realistic KTU Problem

> **Sample Scenario (KTU-style problem):**
> A software module of size **8 KLOC** was tested over a period. **120 defects** were found during development. **30 defects** were reported by customers in the first year of production. The module ran for **4000 hours** and experienced **5 failures**. **Repair time** averaged **10 hours per failure**.

### Solution 1: Defect Density

$$DD = \frac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{KLOC}} = \frac{120 + 30}{8} = \frac{150}{8} = 18.75 \text{ defects/KLOC}$$

### Solution 2: Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)

$$\begin{aligned}
DRE &= \frac{\text{Defects found pre-release}}{\text{Total defects}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{120}{120 + 30} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{120}{150} \times 100 = 80\%
\end{aligned}$$

### Solution 3: MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)

$$MTTF = \frac{\text{Total operating time}}{\text{Number of failures}} = \frac{4000}{5} = 800 \text{ hours}$$

### Solution 4: MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)

$$MTBF = MTTF + MTTR = 800 + 10 = 810 \text{ hours}$$

### Solution 5: Availability

$$A = \frac{MTTF}{MTBF} \times 100 = \frac{800}{810} \times 100 \approx 98.77\%$$

## 3.3 Reliability Growth Derivation (Littlewood Model — Conceptual)

For software undergoing testing, reliability grows as defects are removed. A simplified exponential reliability function:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

Where:
- $R(t)$ = Probability of *no failure* in time interval $t$
- $\lambda$ = Failure intensity (failures per unit time)

After defect removal, $\lambda$ decreases. Let $\lambda_0$ = initial intensity. After $k$ fixes:

$$\lambda_k = \lambda_0 \cdot e^{-ck}$$

Where $c$ is the defect-removal effectiveness coefficient.

### Implication for QA

A higher $c$ (better QA process) results in a faster drop in $\lambda_k$, i.e., a steeper reliability growth curve. **This mathematically justifies the QA principle that "prevention reduces future failure cost."**

## 3.4 Quality Attribute Trade-off Matrix

In real KTU problems, students must often rank competing quality attributes. The following matrix is a high-yield template:

| Quality Attribute | Conflict With | Engineering Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| Efficiency | Maintainability | Use profiling, code optimisation at hot-spots only |
| Usability | Security | Layered authentication with frictionless UX |
| Portability | Efficiency | Use cross-platform frameworks; accept small perf cost |
| Reliability | Cost / Time | Defensive programming, fault-tolerant patterns |
| Maintainability | Performance | Refactor periodically; avoid premature optimisation |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Answer Tip:** Always answer QA trade-off questions with a *concrete engineering example*, not a generic statement. Saying *"Use modular design"* is weak. Saying *"Use modular design to isolate change impact, justified by the Liskov Substitution Principle"* earns full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PDCA (Deming) Cycle — Quality Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PLAN: Define QA objectives, processes, metrics"]
    B["DO: Execute the process on pilot scale"]
    C["CHECK: Measure results, compare against plan"]
    D["ACT: Standardise improvement OR restart cycle"]
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> A
```

## 4.2 ISO 9001:2015 Process Approach Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INP["Stakeholders and Customer Needs"]
        CUST["Customer Requirements"]
    end
    CUST --> QMS["Quality Management System"]
    QMS --> PROD["Software Product or Service"]
    PROD --> SAT["Customer Satisfaction"]
    SAT --> QMS
    QMS --> MGMT["Management Review and Improvement"]
    MGMT --> QMS
```

## 4.3 CMMI Five-Level Maturity Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Level 1: Initial - Ad-hoc, chaotic"]
    L2["Level 2: Managed - Repeatable project tracking"]
    L3["Level 3: Defined - Organisational standards"]
    L4["Level 4: Quantitatively Managed - Statistical control"]
    L5["Level 5: Optimizing - Continuous innovation"]
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
```

## 4.4 Quality Standards Hierarchy Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["International Quality Standards Landscape"]
    ROOT --> ISO["ISO 9000 Family"]
    ROOT --> IEC["ISO/IEC 25010 Software Quality"]
    ROOT --> CMMI["CMMI v2.0 Process Maturity"]
    ROOT --> IEEE["IEEE 730 and 12207"]
    ISO --> ISO9001["ISO 9001 QMS Requirements"]
    IEC --> SQMR["Software Product Quality Model"]
    CMMI --> CL["5 Maturity Levels"]
    IEEE --> SQAP["Software Quality Assurance Plan"]
```

## 4.5 Cost of Quality — Optimization Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1["Prevention Cost - Rising with Investment"]
    Q2["Appraisal Cost - Rising with Investment"]
    Q3["Internal Failure Cost - Falling with Investment"]
    Q4["External Failure Cost - Falling with Investment"]
    Q1 --> SUM["Total Cost of Quality Curve U-shaped"]
    Q2 --> SUM
    Q3 --> SUM
    Q4 --> SUM
    SUM --> OPT["Optimum at Curve Minimum"]
```

## 4.6 QA Activities Flow Within SDLC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ["Requirements"] --> REV1["Requirement Review"]
    REV1 --> DES["Design"]
    DES --> REV2["Design Review and Walkthrough"]
    REV2 --> COD["Coding"]
    COD --> REV3["Code Inspection"]
    REV3 --> TST["Testing"]
    TST --> AUD["Process Audit"]
    AUD --> REL["Release and Maintenance"]
    REL --> MET["Metrics Collection and Improvement"]
    MET --> REQ
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between Quality Assurance, Quality Control, and Testing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Quality Assurance (QA):** A planned, systematic set of activities focused on the **process** of software development, aiming to **prevent** defects. It is proactive and applies to the entire SDLC. *(1 mark)*
- **Quality Control (QC):** A set of activities focused on the **product**, aiming to **identify** defects. It is reactive and is performed *after* the product is built. *(1 mark)*
- **Testing:** A tactical activity that executes the software to find failures. It is one of the techniques used *within* QC. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students who write *"QA, QC, and testing are the same thing"* or *"QA is testing done before release"* receive **0 marks** even if the rest of the answer is correct. The KTU 2024 scheme mandates distinct definitions for each.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define the PDCA cycle and list the four phases in order.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle, also called the **Deming Cycle**, is a four-step iterative management method for continuous improvement. *(0.5 marks)*
1. **Plan:** Establish objectives, identify problems, and design the QA plan. *(0.5 marks)*
2. **Do:** Execute the plan on a small scale — develop code, run tests, collect data. *(0.5 marks)*
3. **Check:** Evaluate the data against expected results — perform reviews, audits, defect analysis. *(0.5 marks)*
4. **Act:** Standardize improvements across the organization or revise the plan to restart the cycle. *(0.5 marks)*
*Example:* In regression testing, plan new test cases → execute → verify pass/fail → add to the regression suite. *(0.5 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **eight quality management principles** of ISO 9000:2015 with software-engineering examples. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**
1. **Customer Focus** — Understanding user needs through requirements elicitation and persona-based design. *(0.875)*
2. **Leadership** — Setting a top-down QA vision with a dedicated QA manager and a published quality policy. *(0.875)*
3. **Engagement of People** — Conducting developer training, pair programming, and code-review culture. *(0.875)*
4. **Process Approach** — Adopting CMMI Level 3 processes like peer reviews and integrated change control. *(0.875)*
5. **Improvement** — Retrospectives at every sprint, defect-density tracking, and Kaizen events. *(0.875)*
6. **Evidence-Based Decision Making** — Using metrics like Defect Density, DRE, MTBF to make release decisions. *(0.875)*
7. **Relationship Management** — Vendor SLAs for third-party libraries and joint reviews with outsourcing partners. *(0.875)*
8. **System Approach to Management** — Recognizing that QA, configuration management, and risk management are interlinked subsystems. *(0.875)*

---

**(b)** Compute the **Defect Density, DRE, MTTF, and Availability** for a system with the following data: 10 KLOC, 200 pre-release defects, 50 post-release defects, 5000 hours operation, 8 failures, MTTR = 5 hours. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**
1. **Defect Density** *(1.5 marks)*:
   $$DD = \frac{200 + 50}{10} = 25 \text{ defects/KLOC}$$
2. **Defect Removal Efficiency** *(1.5 marks)*:
   $$DRE = \frac{200}{200+50} \times 100 = 80\%$$
3. **MTTF** *(1.5 marks)*:
   $$MTTF = \frac{5000}{8} = 625 \text{ hours}$$
4. **MTBF** *(1 mark)*:
   $$MTBF = 625 + 5 = 630 \text{ hours}$$
5. **Availability** *(1.5 marks)*:
   $$A = \frac{625}{630} \times 100 \approx 99.21\%$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: (a)** Describe the **five levels of CMMI** and explain the role of QA at each level. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**
1. **Level 1 — Initial:** Processes are ad-hoc and chaotic. QA is informal and reactive; success depends on individual heroics. *(1.4 marks)*
2. **Level 2 — Managed:** Project-level tracking is established. QA involves Requirements Management, Configuration Management, and Measurement & Analysis. *(1.4 marks)*
3. **Level 3 — Defined:** Processes are documented and standardized across the organization. QA includes Peer Reviews, Integrated Software Management, and Process Definition. *(1.4 marks)*
4. **Level 4 — Quantitatively Managed:** Quantitative goals are set using statistical techniques. QA uses Quantitative Process Management and Software Quality Management with statistical process control. *(1.4 marks)*
5. **Level 5 — Optimizing:** The focus is continuous innovation. QA emphasizes Causal Analysis, Process Innovation, and Defect Prevention. *(1.4 marks)*

---

**(b)** A QA team has the following cost data: Prevention = ₹2,00,000; Appraisal = ₹1,50,000; Internal Failure = ₹1,00,000; External Failure = ₹3,00,000. Calculate the **Total Cost of Quality**, identify the **largest failure cost**, and recommend **two process improvements**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**
1. **Total Cost of Quality** *(1.5 marks)*:
   $$TCoQ = 2,00,000 + 1,50,000 + 1,00,000 + 3,00,000 = \text{₹}7,50,000$$
2. **Largest failure cost:** External Failure at **₹3,00,000** *(0.5 marks)*
3. **Interpretation** *(1 mark)*: External failure cost is the highest, indicating that defects are leaking to customers — a sign of weak *prevention* and *appraisal*. The organization should invest in upstream QA activities.
4. **Recommended Improvements** *(4 marks)*:
   - **Improvement 1:** Invest more in prevention — adopt formal Fagan inspections and design walkthroughs to catch defects pre-implementation. *(2 marks)*
   - **Improvement 2:** Strengthen appraisal — implement an automated regression suite and code-coverage analysis to identify post-release bugs before deployment. *(2 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Avoid writing "QA is testing"** — this is the single most common mistake and costs **2 to 3 marks** instantly.
> - **Always pair principles with examples** — a list of eight principles without any software-engineering illustration scores at most *3 of 7 marks*.
> - **Show formula substitution step-by-step** — skipping from the formula to the final answer loses *0.5 to 1 mark* per sub-question.
> - **Use proper units** — Defect Density must include "defects/KLOC"; Availability must include "%". Missing units cost 0.5 marks each.
> - **Do not confuse CMMI levels with CMM levels** — they differ in scope; CMMI integrates multiple disciplines.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary)**

- **QA = Process-oriented, Proactive, Prevention-focused**
- **QC = Product-oriented, Reactive, Identification-focused**
- **Testing = Tactical, within QC**
- **PDCA** = **P**lan → **D**o → **C**heck → **A**ct (Deming)
- **Juran's Trilogy** = Quality Planning + Quality Control + Quality Improvement
- **Crosby's Four Absolutes:** Conformance, Prevention, Zero Defects, PoNC (Price of Non-Conformance)
- **Eight ISO 9000:2015 Principles:** Customer Focus, Leadership, Engagement, Process Approach, Improvement, Evidence-Based, Relationship Mgmt, System Approach
- **CoQ Categories:** Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure
- **Optimal QA Investment** occurs at the **minimum of the U-shaped total cost curve**
- **CMMI Levels (Mnemonic M.D.Q.O + Initial):** Initial → Managed → Defined → Quantitatively Managed → Optimizing
- **Defect Density** = Defects / KLOC
- **DRE** = (Pre-release defects / Total defects) × 100
- **MTTF** = Total operating time / Failures
- **MTBF** = MTTF + MTTR
- **Availability** = MTTF / MTBF × 100
- **Reliability Function** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$
- **Key Standards:** ISO 9001:2015, ISO/IEC 25010, CMMI v2.0, IEEE 730, IEEE 12207
- **ISO/IEC 25010 quality model** replaced ISO 9126 — has **8 characteristics** (Functionality, Performance, Compatibility, Usability, Reliability, Security, Maintainability, Portability)
- **Always include engineering examples** in KTU 14-mark answers for full marks
- **Always state units** in metric-based answers (%, hours, defects/KLOC)
- **Mermaid diagrams** should be reproduced in answer sheets as labeled block diagrams for QA framework and PDCA questions
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Development of Quality Plans and Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT747 - SOFTWARE TESTING AND QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Development of Quality Plans and Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Quality Plan (SQP):** A formal document that identifies the **quality standards**, **practices**, **resources**, **specifications**, **activities**, and **sequences of activities** relevant to a particular software product, service, or project, as defined by the **IEEE Standard 730-2014**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Objectives:** Specific, measurable goals pursued to achieve the overall quality policy of an organization. They provide a **tangible benchmark** for evaluating whether a software product or process meets the desired level of excellence, customer satisfaction, and regulatory compliance.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **Software Quality Plan** exactly like the **architectural blueprint and project schedule** used to build a high-rise apartment.

- The **blueprint (Quality Plan)** doesn't lay bricks; it specifies *how* the building will be built, *what* standards must be followed, *who* will inspect what, and *when* milestones are due.
- The **floors and finishing targets (Quality Objectives)** are SMART, tangible goals: e.g., *“Complete electrical inspection of the 10th floor by 30th June with zero fire-safety violations.”*
- Without this document, workers would be pouring concrete randomly — leading to **defects, delays, cost overruns, and a structurally weak building**, just like software without a quality plan produces buggy, late, over-budget products.

> [!NOTE]
> A Quality Plan is **not** a Quality Policy. A policy is a *one-paragraph company-level intent* (e.g., “We deliver defect-free software”). A Quality Plan is the **detailed engineering document** that operationalizes that intent for one specific project.

## 1.3 The Two Pillars — Why They Matter

| Pillar | One-line Essence | Failure Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Quality Plan** | Defines **HOW** quality will be achieved | Ad-hoc, inconsistent, non-auditable process |
| **Quality Objectives** | Defines **WHAT** level of quality is the target | Subjective success, no measurable yardsticks |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU examinations, always remember: **Plan = Process; Objectives = Goals.** Confusing these two is a guaranteed half-mark deduction.

## 1.4 Alignment with KTU Course Outcomes (COs)

This topic directly maps to:

- **CO3:** Apply quality planning techniques and define measurable quality objectives for software projects.
- **CO4:** Develop comprehensive quality plans aligned with industry standards (IEEE 730, ISO 9001, CMMI).
- **Bloom's Levels Addressed:** *L3 (Apply)*, *L4 (Analyze)*, *L5 (Evaluate)*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The IEEE 730 Standard — The Backbone of Every SQP

The **IEEE 730-2014** standard prescribes the **mandatory structure** for a Software Quality Plan. KTU examiners *love* testing whether a student can list and explain these sections.

### 2.1.1 The Twelve Components of an SQP (per IEEE 730)

1. **Purpose** — Defines the scope, applicability, and goals of the plan.
2. **Reference Documents** — Lists applicable standards, contracts, and parent documents.
3. **Management** — Identifies organizational structure, responsibilities, and authority for quality.
4. **Documentation** — Specifies the deliverables, formats, review procedures, and approval workflow.
5. **Standards, Practices, Conventions, and Metrics** — Establishes coding standards, design conventions, and quality measurement formulas.
6. **Reviews and Audits** — Defines the schedule, scope, and participants for technical reviews and quality audits.
7. **Testing** — Outlines the test strategy, levels (unit, integration, system, acceptance), and exit criteria.
8. **Problem Reporting and Corrective Action** — Describes the defect lifecycle, severity classification, and root-cause analysis procedure.
9. **Tools, Techniques, and Methodologies** — Lists CASE tools, test automation frameworks, and process models used.
10. **Records Collection, Maintenance, and Retention** — Specifies what records are kept, where, and for how long.
11. **Training** — Identifies the training needs of QA personnel and developers.
12. **Risk Management** — Identifies quality-related risks, probability, impact, and mitigation strategies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** A 7-mark KTU question may ask you to explain *any seven* of these twelve components. Memorize all twelve — pick whichever seven you can write with the most confidence.

## 2.2 The SMART Framework for Quality Objectives

Quality objectives **must** satisfy the **SMART** criteria. KTU examiners often present a scenario and ask whether a given objective is SMART or not.

| Letter | Criterion | Meaning | Poor Example | SMART Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S** | Specific | Clearly defined, unambiguous | "Make the system better" | "Reduce response time of the search API" |
| **M** | Measurable | Quantifiable with a metric | "Improve customer happiness" | "Achieve CSAT score $\geq 4.5/5$" |
| **A** | Achievable | Realistic given resources | "Zero defects ever" | "Defect leakage $\leq 2\%$ post-release" |
| **B** | Relevant | Aligned with business goals | "Increase typography quality" (for a banking app) | "Achieve PCI-DSS compliance" |
| **T** | Time-bound | Has a clear deadline | "Someday improve security" | "Pass security audit by Q3 2024" |

> [!NOTE]
> Some texts use **SMARTER** (adding *Evaluated* and *Reviewed*). For KTU 2024, the five-letter **SMART** is sufficient unless the question explicitly demands the extended form.

## 2.3 Quality Plan Development — Phased Logic

The development of a quality plan follows a **seven-phase logical flow**, often tested in 7-mark essay-type questions.

### Phase 1 — Scope Definition
- Identify the **project boundaries**: which modules, deliverables, and lifecycle phases are covered.
- *Why:* Prevents scope creep of the quality plan itself.

### Phase 2 — Stakeholder Analysis
- Identify all **internal and external stakeholders**: customers, regulators, end-users, project managers, developers.
- *Why:* Quality means different things to different stakeholders.

### Phase 3 — Baseline Standards Selection
- Choose applicable standards: **ISO 9001**, **CMMI**, **IEEE 730**, **ISO/IEC 25010**, **Six Sigma**.
- *Why:* The plan must be auditable against a recognized benchmark.

### Phase 4 — Quality Objective Formulation (SMART)
- Translate stakeholder needs into **SMART objectives**.
- *Why:* Vague goals cannot be measured or achieved.

### Phase 5 — Metrics & Measurement Definition
- For each objective, define **one or more metrics** and their **data collection method**.
- *Why:* “What gets measured gets managed.”

### Phase 6 — Process & Activity Specification
- Define the actual **QA/QC activities**: reviews, inspections, testing, audits.
- Assign **roles, responsibilities, and authorities** (RACI matrix).
- *Why:* A plan without owners is a wish list.

### Phase 7 — Plan Review, Approval, and Lifecycle Integration
- Conduct a **formal review** with stakeholders.
- Integrate the plan with the project schedule, configuration management, and risk management plans.
- *Why:* Quality planning is not a one-time act; it must be **living documentation**.

## 2.4 The Strategic Alignment Pyramid

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently tests the **hierarchy**: *Policy → Objectives → Plan → Procedures → Records*. Memorize this chain.

```
                       [QUALITY POLICY]
                  (Company-wide intent statement)
                              │
                              ▼
                  [QUALITY OBJECTIVES]
                 (SMART, multi-level goals)
                              │
                              ▼
                  [QUALITY PLAN (SQP)]
                (IEEE 730 / ISO 9001 document)
                              │
                              ▼
                  [QUALITY PROCEDURES]
             (Step-by-step work instructions)
                              │
                              ▼
                  [QUALITY RECORDS]
            (Evidence that procedures were followed)
```

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metrics Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All metrics below are **high-yield** for KTU numerical questions. Practice at least one worked example per formula.

| # | Metric Name | Formula | Typical Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Size in KLOC}}$ | defects/KLOC | Compares quality across modules |
| 2 | **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $DRE = \dfrac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}} \times 100$ | percent | Measures testing effectiveness |
| 3 | **Test Case Effectiveness (TCE)** | $TCE = \dfrac{\text{Bugs found by tests}}{\text{Total bugs}} \times 100$ | percent | Validates test suite design |
| 4 | **Requirement Stability Index (RSI)** | $RSI = \dfrac{R_{final}}{R_{initial} + R_{added}} \times 100$ | percent | Tracks requirement churn |
| 5 | **Code Coverage (CC)** | $CC = \dfrac{L_{covered}}{L_{total}} \times 100$ | percent | Test thoroughness |
| 6 | **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** | $MTTF = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{n}$ | hours | For non-repairable components |
| 7 | **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$ | hours | For repairable systems |
| 8 | **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100$ | percent | System uptime measure |
| 9 | **Software Reliability (Exponential Model)** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | Probability of no failure in $[0,t]$ |
| 10 | **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** | $CSI = \dfrac{\sum w_i s_i}{\sum w_i}$ | score (0–5) | Weighted satisfaction score |

> **Note on Notation:**
> $D_{pre}$ = defects found before release; $D_{post}$ = defects found after release; $L$ = lines of code; $\lambda$ = failure rate; $w_i$ = weight; $s_i$ = score.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Avionics & Medical Devices:** MTBF and Reliability $R(t)$ are legally mandated metrics — without them, FAA/CE certification is impossible.
- **E-Commerce Platforms:** Availability $A \geq 99.99\%$ is the standard SLO — affects revenue directly.
- **Banking Software:** DRE $\geq 95\%$ is a regulatory requirement to prevent financial fraud post-release.
- **SaaS Products:** CSI $\geq 4.5/5$ is the typical retention-driving benchmark.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Building a SMART Quality Objective From Scratch

> **Scenario:** A mid-sized e-commerce company is launching a new mobile checkout module. The product manager says: *“We want a fast and reliable checkout.”* Convert this into a SMART quality objective.

### Step 1 — Decode the vague intent
- *“Fast”* → needs a quantitative measure.
- *“Reliable”* → needs a defect/availability measure.

### Step 2 — Apply SMART
- **Specific** — Refers to the *mobile checkout module*, not the whole app.
- **Measurable** — Two metrics: (a) response time, (b) crash-free sessions.
- **Achievable** — Based on prior module data (avg. 1.8s; 99.2% crash-free).
- **Relevant** — Direct impact on cart-abandonment rate and revenue.
- **Time-bound** — Tied to the v1.0 release date.

### Step 3 — Final SMART Objective

> *“For the mobile checkout module v1.0, ensure that the median API response time is $\leq 1.5$ seconds under a load of 500 concurrent users, and that the crash-free session rate is $\geq 99.5\%$, measured over a 7-day soak test prior to the release on 15 October 2024.”*

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how every SMART letter is now objectively verifiable. A KTU 7-mark question on SMART objectives usually requires this kind of **before-and-after** comparison.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Defect Density and DRE Calculation

> **Problem Statement:** A software project of size **80 KLOC** records **200 defects** found during the testing phase. After release, customers report **50 additional defects** within the first three months.
>
> **Calculate:**
> 1. Defect Density (DD)
> 2. Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)
> 3. Comment on the maturity of the QA process.

### Step 3.2.1 — Identify Given Data

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Project Size } S &= 80 \text{ KLOC} \\
D_{pre} &= 200 \text{ defects} \\
D_{post} &= 50 \text{ defects} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3.2.2 — Apply the Defect Density Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
DD &= \frac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Project Size in KLOC}} \\
DD &= \frac{D_{pre} + D_{post}}{S} \\
DD &= \frac{200 + 50}{80} \\
DD &= \frac{250}{80} \\
DD &= 3.125 \text{ defects per KLOC}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Industry Benchmark (approx.):** Industry average for well-tested software is **0.5 – 2.0 defects/KLOC**. A value of **3.125** suggests the product is **below average** in quality and may need process improvement.

### Step 3.2.3 — Apply the DRE Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
DRE &= \frac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}} \times 100 \\
DRE &= \frac{200}{200 + 50} \times 100 \\
DRE &= \frac{200}{250} \times 100 \\
DRE &= 0.8 \times 100 \\
DRE &= 80 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Industry Benchmark (approx.):** A mature QA process targets **DRE $\geq 95\%$**. The achieved **80%** is a *red flag* — testing is missing 1 in every 5 defects.

### Step 3.2.4 — Engineering Interpretation

| Observation | Implication | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|
| DD = 3.125 | Higher than industry average | Adopt coding standards, peer reviews |
| DRE = 80% | Lower than 95% target | Expand test coverage, add system-level tests |

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Reliability and Availability for a Cloud Service

> **Problem Statement:** A cloud service has an MTBF of **900 hours** and an MTTR of **100 hours**.
>
> **Calculate:**
> 1. Availability $A$ (in percent)
> 2. The reliability $R(t)$ at $t = 500$ hours, assuming a constant failure rate $\lambda = 1/MTBF$.

### Step 3.3.1 — Compute Availability

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100 \\
A &= \frac{900}{900 + 100} \times 100 \\
A &= \frac{900}{1000} \times 100 \\
A &= 90 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Industry Benchmark:** Cloud SLAs (AWS, Azure) typically promise **99.9% to 99.99%**. The computed **90%** is unacceptable for production cloud workloads.

### Step 3.3.2 — Compute Failure Rate $\lambda$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{1}{MTBF} \\
\lambda &= \frac{1}{900} \\
\lambda &\approx 0.00111 \text{ failures per hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3.3.3 — Compute Reliability at $t = 500$ hours

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(t) &= e^{-\lambda t} \\
R(500) &= e^{-0.00111 \times 500} \\
R(500) &= e^{-0.5556} \\
R(500) &\approx 0.5735 \\
R(500) &\approx 57.35 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** There is only a **57.35% probability** that the cloud service will operate continuously for 500 hours without a single failure. For a mission-critical system, this demands **redundancy and failover mechanisms**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Quality Standards

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is a **favourite 14-marker KTU question**. Learn the *differences*, not just the *definitions*.

| Aspect | ISO 9001 | CMMI | IEEE 730 | Six Sigma (DMAIC) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Organization-wide QMS | Process maturity model | Project-specific SQP | Project-specific process improvement |
| **Origin** | ISO (International) | SEI (Carnegie Mellon) | IEEE | Motorola |
| **Levels / Stages** | Clauses (4–10) | 5 maturity levels | 12 SQP components | 5 phases (D-M-A-I-C) |
| **Focus** | Customer satisfaction & process | Process maturity & capability | Software product quality | Defect reduction via statistics |
| **Certification** | Yes (3rd party) | Yes (SCAMPI appraisal) | No (internal standard) | Yes (Green/Black Belt) |
| **Mandatory in KTU?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Optional mention |

## 3.5 RACI Matrix — A Quality-Plan Essential

A Quality Plan must clarify **who does what**. KTU examiners love the **RACI** matrix.

| Activity | Dev Lead | QA Lead | Project Manager | Customer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Define Quality Objectives | C | R | A | I |
| Develop Test Plan | C | R | A | I |
| Execute Test Cases | I | R | A | I |
| Approve Release | I | C | A | C |

> **RACI Legend:** R = Responsible, A = Accountable, C = Consulted, I = Informed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Seven-Phase Quality Plan Development Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Phase 1: Define Project Scope] --> B1[Phase 2: Identify Stakeholders]
    B1 --> C1[Phase 3: Select Baseline Standards]
    C1 --> D1[Phase 4: Formulate SMART Objectives]
    D1 --> E1[Phase 5: Define Quality Metrics and KPIs]
    E1 --> F1[Phase 6: Specify QA QC Activities and Roles]
    F1 --> G1[Phase 7: Review Approve and Integrate]
    G1 --> H1[Implementation and Continuous Monitoring]
    H1 --> I1[Periodic Audits and Plan Refinement]
    I1 --> A1
```

> **Reading Guide:** The arrow from `I1` back to `A1` represents the **iterative, feedback-driven nature** of quality planning — a plan is never truly *finished*.

## 4.2 Mermaid Tree Diagram — The Twelve SQP Components (IEEE 730)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SQP[Software Quality Plan IEEE 730]
    SQP --> C1[1 Purpose]
    SQP --> C2[2 Reference Documents]
    SQP --> C3[3 Management]
    SQP --> C4[4 Documentation]
    SQP --> C5[5 Standards Practices Metrics]
    SQP --> C6[6 Reviews and Audits]
    SQP --> C7[7 Testing]
    SQP --> C8[8 Problem Reporting]
    SQP --> C9[9 Tools Techniques Methodologies]
    SQP --> C10[10 Records Management]
    SQP --> C11[11 Training]
    SQP --> C12[12 Risk Management]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — SMART Objective Validation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O1[Draft Objective] --> Q1{Is it Specific}
    Q1 -- No --> O1
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is it Measurable}
    Q2 -- No --> O1
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is it Achievable}
    Q3 -- No --> O1
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Is it Relevant}
    Q4 -- No --> O1
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5{Is it Time Bound}
    Q5 -- No --> O1
    Q5 -- Yes --> O2[Approved SMART Objective]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Quality Hierarchy Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Quality Policy Corporate Level] --> L2[Quality Objectives Department Level]
    L2 --> L3[Quality Plan Project Level]
    L3 --> L4[Quality Procedures Task Level]
    L4 --> L5[Quality Records Evidence Level]
```

> **Engineering Insight:** Each level is **derived from** the level above and **feeds evidence into** the level below. This is exactly the same structure used in **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 4–10** mapping.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Define Software Quality Plan. List any four components of SQP as per IEEE 730.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Software Quality Plan (SQP)** is a formal project-level document, conforming to **IEEE Standard 730**, that specifies the quality standards, practices, resources, activities, and responsibilities required to ensure that a software product meets its defined quality requirements.

The four components (any four of the following) are:

1. **Purpose** — scope and applicability of the plan.
2. **Standards, Practices, Conventions, and Metrics** — the engineering standards and quality measures.
3. **Testing** — the test strategy, levels, and exit criteria.
4. **Problem Reporting and Corrective Action** — the defect lifecycle and root-cause analysis.

> *(Any four correctly named components fetch full marks. Avoid vague terms like "planning" or "documentation" — use the exact IEEE 730 terminology.)*

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Explain SMART criteria for quality objectives with one example.**

**Model Answer:**

**SMART** is an acronym for five attributes that every quality objective must possess:

- **S — Specific:** The objective is clear and unambiguous.
- **M — Measurable:** Progress and achievement can be quantified.
- **A — Achievable:** Realistic given available resources and constraints.
- **R — Relevant:** Aligned with broader business and customer goals.
- **T — Time-bound:** Has a defined deadline or time window.

**Example SMART Objective:**
*"For the online payment module v2.1, achieve a transaction success rate of $\geq 99.9\%$ and a payment API response time of $\leq 1.2$ seconds, measured over a 30-day pre-release soak test, by 15 November 2024."*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

#### **(a) Explain the step-by-step process for developing a Software Quality Plan.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The development of a Software Quality Plan is a **seven-phase systematic process**:

1. **Scope Definition:** Identify the project modules, deliverables, and lifecycle phases to which the plan applies.
2. **Stakeholder Analysis:** Identify all internal and external stakeholders and capture their quality expectations.
3. **Baseline Standard Selection:** Choose applicable standards — ISO 9001, CMMI, IEEE 730, ISO/IEC 25010.
4. **Quality Objective Formulation:** Translate stakeholder needs into **SMART** objectives with measurable metrics.
5. **Metrics Definition:** For each objective, define formulas (e.g., DD, DRE, MTBF) and data collection methods.
6. **Process and Activity Specification:** Define QA/QC activities — reviews, inspections, testing, audits — and assign **roles and responsibilities** (RACI matrix).
7. **Review, Approval, and Integration:** Conduct a formal stakeholder review, obtain sign-off, and integrate the plan with project schedule, configuration management, and risk management plans.

The plan is then **implemented, monitored, audited periodically, and refined** as the project progresses.

#### **(b) Discuss the various components of SQP as per IEEE 730 standard in detail.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **IEEE 730-2014** standard defines the following twelve components of a Software Quality Plan:

1. **Purpose** — defines scope, applicability, and exclusions of the plan.
2. **Reference Documents** — lists applicable standards, contracts, and parent documents.
3. **Management** — organizational structure, authority, and reporting lines for quality.
4. **Documentation** — list of deliverables, formats, review and approval procedures.
5. **Standards, Practices, Conventions, and Metrics** — coding standards, design rules, and quality measurement formulas.
6. **Reviews and Audits** — schedule, scope, and participants of formal reviews and quality audits.
7. **Testing** — test strategy, levels (unit, integration, system, acceptance), and exit criteria.
8. **Problem Reporting and Corrective Action** — defect classification, severity levels, and corrective action workflow.
9. **Tools, Techniques, and Methodologies** — CASE tools, automation frameworks, and lifecycle models.
10. **Records Collection, Maintenance, Retention** — what records are stored, where, and for how long.
11. **Training** — training needs identification and competency requirements for QA staff.
12. **Risk Management** — identification, analysis, and mitigation of quality-related risks.

A well-written SQP integrates all twelve components cohesively to deliver auditable, repeatable, and measurable quality assurance.

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### **(a) With a suitable example, explain how to develop quality objectives using SMART criteria.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Quality objectives are developed by converting **vague stakeholder needs** into **SMART** goals through a structured process.

**Step-by-step method:**

1. **Capture vague intent:** e.g., *"The app should be fast and user-friendly."*
2. **Identify the WHAT:** speed, usability, reliability, etc.
3. **Identify the HOW to measure:** metrics like response time, NPS, crash rate.
4. **Set realistic targets:** based on prior releases, industry benchmarks, and resource constraints.
5. **Set the deadline:** tie to a project milestone.
6. **Validate against SMART:** ensure all five letters are satisfied.
7. **Document and get approval:** obtain sign-off from project sponsor.

**Example — E-commerce Mobile App:**

| SMART Element | Application |
|---|---|
| **S** | Mobile checkout module, v2.0 |
| **M** | API response time $\leq 1.5$ s; crash-free sessions $\geq 99.5\%$ |
| **A** | Baseline (1.8 s, 99.2%); 5% improvement is feasible |
| **R** | Directly reduces cart abandonment, boosting revenue |
| **T** | To be measured during a 7-day soak test by 15 Oct 2024 |

**Final SMART Objective:**
*"For mobile checkout v2.0, the median checkout API response time shall be $\leq 1.5$ seconds at 500 concurrent users, and the crash-free session rate shall be $\geq 99.5\%$, verified over a 7-day soak test prior to release on 15 Oct 2024."*

#### **(b) A software project of size 90 KLOC records 180 defects during testing and 20 defects post-release. Calculate the Defect Density and DRE. Comment on the quality.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 90 \text{ KLOC} \\
D_{pre} &= 180 \\
D_{post} &= 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Total Defects**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{total} &= D_{pre} + D_{post} \\
D_{total} &= 180 + 20 \\
D_{total} &= 200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Defect Density**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DD &= \frac{D_{total}}{S} \\
DD &= \frac{200}{90} \\
DD &\approx 2.22 \text{ defects per KLOC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Defect Removal Efficiency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DRE &= \frac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}} \times 100 \\
DRE &= \frac{180}{180 + 20} \times 100 \\
DRE &= \frac{180}{200} \times 100 \\
DRE &= 90 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Commentary:**

| Metric | Computed | Industry Benchmark | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| DD | 2.22 def/KLOC | 0.5 – 2.0 def/KLOC | Slightly above average; needs improvement |
| DRE | 90% | $\geq 95\%$ | Below target; testing process needs strengthening |

> **Recommendation:** Introduce **code reviews, static analysis, and additional system-level testing** to reduce both the defect density and the post-release leakage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Unit error trap:** Always express Defect Density as *defects per KLOC* (not just “defects”) — losing the unit costs 1 mark.
> 2. **DRE formula sign trap:** DRE uses $D_{pre} / (D_{pre} + D_{post})$, **not** $D_{pre} / D_{post}$. A common slip costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Missing commentary:** Numerical answers **without an engineering interpretation** fetch only 5 of 7 marks. Always add a one-line conclusion.
> 4. **Vague objectives trap:** In SMART questions, students often write “good quality” or “high performance” — these are *not* SMART. Always include a number, a metric, and a date.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**

- [x] **SQP** = project-level document, governed by **IEEE 730-2014**.
- [x] An SQP has **12 components** (Purpose, References, Management, Documentation, Standards/Metrics, Reviews/Audits, Testing, Problem Reporting, Tools, Records, Training, Risk Management).
- [x] **SMART** = Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
- [x] **Quality Policy ≠ Quality Plan** — policy is corporate intent; plan is the engineering document.
- [x] Quality objectives must be **derived from** the quality policy and **drive** the quality plan.
- [x] **Key formulas** (memorize all):
  - $DD = \dfrac{D_{pre} + D_{post}}{S}$ (in KLOC)
  - $DRE = \dfrac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}} \times 100$
  - $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100$
  - $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$
- [x] A **RACI matrix** must accompany every SQP.
- [x] The plan must be **reviewed, approved, integrated, monitored, and refined** iteratively.
- [x] **KTU gold tip:** For 14-mark questions, always structure your answer as **bulleted, numbered, or tabular content** — pure paragraphs lose marks in OBE-based evaluation.
- [x] Always add **engineering commentary** to numerical answers (benchmark comparison + one recommendation).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Quality Management (TQM) Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT747 - SOFTWARE TESTING AND QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Total Quality Management (TQM) Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that focuses on continuously improving the quality of products, services, and business processes by involving all members of the organization — from top management to front-line employees — in a customer-centric, process-driven, and data-based approach to achieve long-term success through customer satisfaction.

In the context of **Software Testing and Quality Assurance (PECNT747)**, TQM is the *umbrella* management discipline under which structured testing activities (verification, validation, reviews, audits) are integrated into the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC). TQM is not a single technique — it is a **management system** that governs how quality is *planned, assured, controlled, and improved* across the entire software engineering ecosystem.

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats TQM as the *theoretical foundation* for understanding why structured testing, process maturity (CMMi/ISO 9001), and continuous improvement (Kaizen) exist as engineering disciplines.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of TQM as the Operating Protocol of a World-Class Hospital.**

| Hospital Component | TQM Equivalent | What it Teaches Us |
|---|---|---|
| **Patient (Customer)** is the center of every decision | Customer Focus | Every process exists to satisfy the end-user |
| **Triage → Surgery → Recovery** (defined workflow) | Process Approach | Quality must be *built-in*, not inspected at the end |
| **Doctors + Nurses + Technicians** all follow the same chart | Employee Involvement | Everyone owns quality — not just the QA team |
| **Continuous Medical Research & Training** | Continuous Improvement (Kaizen) | Yesterday's best practice is today's minimum standard |
| **Mortality & Infection Rate Tracking** | Fact-Based Decision Making | Metrics like **DPMO** and **Defect Density** drive improvement |
| **Accreditation by Medical Boards** | ISO 9001 / CMMi Certification | Independent validation of quality systems |

Just as a hospital cannot rely on *one super-surgeon* to ensure patient safety, a software company cannot rely on *one testing team* to ensure software quality. TQM spreads the responsibility across the entire organization.

---

## 1.3 Historical Lineage of TQM (Quality Gurus)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 module specifically tests your knowledge of the **Quality Gurus** and their contributions. Memorize the guru–concept–technique mapping.

| Quality Guru | Core Contribution | Famous Phrase / Concept |
|---|---|---|
| **W. Edwards Deming** | 14 Points for Management; PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel) | *"Quality is made in the boardroom"* |
| **Joseph M. Juran** | Quality Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement); Pareto Principle (80/20) | *"Fitness for Use"* |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | Zero Defects; Quality is Free; "Do It Right First Time" (DIRFT) | *"Quality is conformance to requirements"* |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Quality Circles; Fishbone (Cause-and-Effect) Diagram | *"Next to the worker, quality is born"* |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | Loss Function; Quality Engineering; Robust Design | *"Quality loss begins the moment a product deviates from target"* |
| **Walter Shewhart** | Statistical Process Control (SPC); Control Charts | Founder of modern statistical quality control |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | Total Quality Control (precursor to TQM) | Coined "Total Quality Control" in 1956 |
| **Mikel Harry & Richard Schroeder** | Six Sigma Methodology at Motorola (1986) | **3.4 DPMO** target |

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology Definitions (KTU Board-Standard Wording)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Customer Satisfaction** — A measure of how well a product or service meets or exceeds customer expectations. Quantified via **CSAT** scores and **NPS (Net Promoter Score)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — A Japanese philosophy of *small, incremental, perpetual* improvements involving every employee, from the CEO to the shop floor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Approach** — Managing activities as interrelated processes that function as a coherent system, rather than as isolated tasks (aligned with **ISO 9001:2015** clause 4.4).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost of Quality (COQ)** — The total cost incurred to prevent, detect, and remediate defects — comprising **Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, and External Failure** costs.

---

## 1.5 Why TQM Matters in Software Engineering

Software is intangible, complex, and defect-prone. Unlike a physical product, a software defect in a critical module (e.g., flight control) can cause catastrophic, irreversible loss. TQM provides the **management framework** to:

1. **Shift-Left** quality activities (early defect detection).
2. Standardize processes (CMMi Level 1–5).
3. Quantify quality through metrics (defect density, MTTF, availability).
4. Institutionalize learning through post-mortems and retrospectives.
5. Align development with business goals (ROI on quality investments).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Core Principles of TQM (ISO 9000 Foundation)

> [!NOTE]
> These eight principles are the *backbone* of every TQM question in KTU exams. Frame your answer around them.

### Principle 1 — Customer Focus
- The organization exists to *create value* for customers (both internal and external).
- Software engineering: gather requirements via interviews, surveys, and usage analytics; track CSAT post-release.

### Principle 2 — Leadership & Unity of Direction
- Leaders establish unity of purpose and direction.
- Top management must visibly *champion* quality initiatives (Deming's 14 Points stress this heavily).

### Principle 3 — Involvement of People
- Competent, empowered, and engaged employees at *all levels* are the essence of an organization.
- **Quality Circles** (Ishikawa) empower teams to identify and solve problems locally.

### Principle 4 — Process Approach
- A desired result is achieved more efficiently when activities and resources are managed as a process.
- Software engineering: define, measure, analyze, improve, and control (DMAIC) each SDLC phase.

### Principle 5 — System Approach to Management
