- Identifying, understanding, and managing interrelated processes as a system improves effectiveness.
- Avoid *sub-optimization* — improving one phase at the cost of another (e.g., reducing testing to speed delivery).

### Principle 6 — Continual Improvement
- A permanent objective of the organization.
- Use **PDCA** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) as the engine; embed in sprint retrospectives (Agile).

### Principle 7 — Factual Approach to Decision Making
- Effective decisions are based on the *analysis of data and information*.
- Use **control charts, Pareto charts, histograms, scatter plots, run charts** (the 7 QC Tools).

### Principle 8 — Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships
- An organization and its suppliers are interdependent; a mutually beneficial relationship enhances the ability to create value.
- In software: Vendor management for COTS, libraries, cloud services, and outsourced QA.

---

## 2.2 The Deming PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle is the *iterative engine* of TQM. It appears in almost every KTU question paper on TQM.

| Stage | Description | Software QA Application |
|---|---|---|
| **PLAN** | Identify the goal, plan how to achieve it, hypothesize expected results | Design test plan; identify KPIs; define acceptance criteria |
| **DO** | Execute the plan on a small scale; collect data | Execute test cases; log defects; record metrics |
| **CHECK** | Compare actual vs. expected results; analyze gaps | Defect trend analysis; root-cause analysis (RCA) |
| **ACT** | Standardize improvements; if goal not met, iterate | Update test processes; refine scripts; institutionalize lessons |

> **Iteration depth:** PDCA is a *recursive* loop. After each cycle, the *standard* rises. This is the philosophical core of continuous improvement.

---

## 2.3 Cost of Quality (COQ) — The Four Categories

> [!IMPORTANT]
> COQ is one of the *highest-weightage* topics in KTU Module 3. Memorize the four categories and the classic curve relationship.

| Category | Definition | Examples in Software |
|---|---|---|
| **Prevention Cost (P)** | Cost of activities to *prevent* defects | Training, process definition, design reviews, static analysis tool licenses |
| **Appraisal Cost (A)** | Cost of activities to *detect* defects | Test execution, code reviews, inspection, test automation |
| **Internal Failure Cost (IF)** | Cost of defects found *before* release | Rework, retest, bug triage meetings, rebuild costs |
| **External Failure Cost (EF)** | Cost of defects found *after* release | Patches, hotfixes, customer support, warranty, lawsuits, reputation loss |

**The COQ Optimality Rule (Deming–Juran Trade-off):**

As **P + A** increase, **IF + EF** decrease. The *optimal* COQ occurs where the *total* cost curve reaches its minimum. This is a classic KTU derivation question.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are tested numerically in KTU university exams. Note: I have used `\vert` and `\mid` instead of `|` to prevent markdown table syntax breakage.

| # | Metric / Formula | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Defect Density (DD)** | $DD = \dfrac{D}{KLOC}$ | Number of defects per thousand lines of code | defects / KLOC |
| 2 | **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $DRE = \left(\dfrac{D_{removed}}{D_{total}}\right) \times 100$ | % of defects removed before release | Percentage |
| 3 | **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** | $DPMO = \left(\dfrac{D}{U \times O}\right) \times 10^{6}$ | Six Sigma standard quality metric | defects / million opps |
| 4 | **Process Yield (Y)** | $Y = \left(1 - \dfrac{D}{U}\right) \times 100$ | % of units that pass without defect | Percentage |
| 5 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{U_{passed}}{U_{total}} \times 100$ | % of units passing a single process step | Percentage |
| 6 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Composite yield across *n* process steps | Decimal (0–1) |
| 7 | **Process Capability Index (Cp)** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Capability of a stable process | Dimensionless |
| 8 | **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Capability *centered* on target | Dimensionless |
| 9 | **Six Sigma Conversion** | $\sigma_{level} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \Phi^{-1}(Y)$ | Converts yield % to sigma level | Sigma units |
| 10 | **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | System uptime proportion | Decimal |
| 11 | **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean lifetime for non-repairable systems | Hours |
| 12 | **Taguchi's Quality Loss (L)** | $L = k \cdot (y - m)^{2}$ | Loss per unit deviation from target $m$ | Monetary units |
| 13 | **COQ Total** | $COQ = P + A + IF + EF$ | Total cost of quality | Monetary units |
| 14 | **Test Effectiveness** | $TE = \dfrac{D_{found\ by\ tests}}{D_{total\ present}} \times 100$ | Test suite's defect-catching power | Percentage |

Where:
- $D$ = Number of defects, $U$ = Number of units, $O$ = Opportunities per unit
- $USL$ = Upper Specification Limit, $LSL$ = Lower Specification Limit
- $\mu$ = process mean, $\sigma$ = standard deviation
- $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures, $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair
- $\lambda$ = failure rate, $k$ = Taguchi's loss coefficient

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | How TQM is Applied |
|---|---|
| **Avionics Software** | DO-178C compliance, formal methods, Six Sigma quality |
| **Medical Devices** | FDA quality system regulation, IEC 62304, defect density < 0.1/KLOC |
| **E-Commerce Platforms** | A/B testing, NPS tracking, ISO 9001 certified SDLC |
| **Banking Software** | SOX compliance, audit trails, 99.999% availability targets |
| **Telecommunications** | TL 9000 standard, MTTF-based reliability, fault-tolerant design |
| **Automotive Embedded Systems** | ISO 26262 (functional safety), ASPICE process maturity |

---

## 2.6 Six Sigma — Key Concept Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Sigma** is a *data-driven* TQM methodology targeting **3.4 DPMO** (99.99966% defect-free) using **DMAIC**.

| Sigma Level | DPMO | Yield % | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1σ | 690,000 | 31% | Many city buses (high late rate) |
| 2σ | 308,000 | 69% | Local postal delivery |
| 3σ | 66,800 | 93.3% | Average restaurant order |
| 4σ | 6,210 | 99.38% | Average airline flight |
| 5σ | 233 | 99.977% | World-class healthcare process |
| **6σ** | **3.4** | **99.99966%** | **99.99966% defect-free (Six Sigma ideal)** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Defect Density and DRE Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
A software module of 25 KLOC was developed. During testing, 180 defects were found. After release, 20 more defects were reported by customers in the first month. Calculate:
(a) Defect Density
(b) Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)
(c) Total Defects present before release

**Given Data:**
- Size of code ($KLOC$) = $25$
- Defects found during testing ($D_{test}$) = $180$
- Defects found post-release ($D_{post}$) = $20$

---

### (a) Defect Density

> The Defect Density formula expresses the *concentration* of defects normalized by code size, allowing comparison across projects of different scales.

$$
DD = \frac{D_{total}}{KLOC}
$$

First, calculate total defects:
$$
D_{total} = D_{test} + D_{post} = 180 + 20 = 200
$$

Substitute into the formula:
$$
DD = \frac{200}{25}
$$

$$
DD = 8.0 \text{ defects per KLOC}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The module has 8 defects per 1000 lines of code. Industry benchmark for high-quality software is typically < 1 defect/KLOC.

---

### (b) Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)

> DRE measures the *effectiveness* of the testing process — i.e., what fraction of defects were caught *before* the product reached customers.

$$
DRE = \left(\frac{D_{removed\ before\ release}}{D_{total}}\right) \times 100
$$

Since all defects found during testing are *removed* before release:
$$
D_{removed} = 180
$$

Substitute values:
$$
DRE = \left(\frac{180}{200}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
DRE = 0.9 \times 100 = 90\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** The testing process caught 90% of all defects. World-class organizations target DRE ≥ 95%. A DRE of 90% is *acceptable* but not *excellent* — a TQM continuous-improvement initiative should target raising this to 95%+ via better reviews and static analysis.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — DPMO and Sigma Level Conversion

**Problem Statement:**
A software company processes 5,000 invoices per day. Over a 30-day month, 450 invoices contained at least one defect. Each invoice has 4 opportunities for error. Calculate:
(a) DPMO
(b) Process Yield
(c) Approximate Sigma Level (assume Normal distribution)

**Given Data:**
- Units ($U$) = $5{,}000 \times 30 = 150{,}000$
- Defective units ($D$) = $450$
- Opportunities per unit ($O$) = $4$

---

### (a) DPMO Calculation

> DPMO is the universal Six Sigma metric that allows cross-industry and cross-process quality comparison by normalizing the *opportunity* for defects.

$$
DPMO = \left(\frac{D}{U \times O}\right) \times 10^{6}
$$

Substitute the values:
$$
DPMO = \left(\frac{450}{150{,}000 \times 4}\right) \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
DPMO = \left(\frac{450}{600{,}000}\right) \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
DPMO = 0.00075 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
DPMO = 750
$$

> **Interpretation:** 750 DPMO corresponds to a yield of 99.925%. This is a strong process — equivalent to a **~4.5σ** capability level.

---

### (b) Process Yield (Y)

> Yield is the *simple complement* of the defect rate. A higher yield implies a healthier process.

$$
Y = \left(1 - \frac{D}{U}\right) \times 100
$$

Substitute the values:
$$
Y = \left(1 - \frac{450}{150{,}000}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
Y = (1 - 0.003) \times 100
$$

$$
Y = 0.997 \times 100 = 99.7\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** The process is defect-free 99.7% of the time. This is the **3σ benchmark** — a respectable but improvable level.

---

### (c) Approximate Sigma Level

> Six Sigma conversion uses the inverse standard normal CDF. For yield $Y$ = 0.997, the area under the curve to the *left* of $+3\sigma$ is ~99.865%, and to *both* sides is ~99.73% — placing the process near the **3σ** mark.

$$
\sigma_{level} \approx \Phi^{-1}(Y) + 1.5
$$

> (The "+1.5σ shift" is Motorola's empirical correction for long-term drift.)

$$
\sigma_{level} \approx \Phi^{-1}(0.997) + 1.5
$$

From the standard normal table:
$$
\Phi^{-1}(0.997) \approx 2.75
$$

$$
\sigma_{level} \approx 2.75 + 1.5 = 4.25\sigma
$$

> **Interpretation:** The invoice process operates at approximately **4.25σ** — better than average (3σ) but short of world-class (6σ). TQM would prescribe a DMAIC project to push this to 5σ.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

**Problem Statement:**
A software release pipeline has 5 sequential stages. The first-pass yields are: 95%, 92%, 98%, 96%, 99%. Calculate:
(a) Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)
(b) Defect Leakage
(c) Cost of Rework if each rework costs ₹5,000 and 10,000 units are processed

**Given Data:**
- $Y_1$ = 0.95, $Y_2$ = 0.92, $Y_3$ = 0.98, $Y_4$ = 0.96, $Y_5$ = 0.99
- Units = 10,000
- Cost per rework = ₹5,000

---

### (a) Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

> RTY is the *product* of individual stage yields. It is always less than or equal to the *minimum* stage yield — illustrating the multiplicative cost of sequential defects.

$$
RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{5} Y_i
$$

Step-by-step multiplication:
$$
RTY = 0.95 \times 0.92
$$

$$
= 0.874
$$

$$
\times\ 0.98 = 0.85652
$$

$$
\times\ 0.96 = 0.82226
$$

$$
\times\ 0.99 = 0.81404
$$

$$
\boxed{RTY \approx 0.8141 \text{ or } 81.41\%}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Although the *best* stage is 99% and the *worst* is 92%, the cumulative end-to-end yield is only ~81%. This is the **hidden factory** problem — small leaks compound into large losses.

---

### (b) Defect Leakage

> Defect leakage is the inverse of RTY — the proportion of units that *require rework* somewhere in the pipeline.

$$
Defect\ Leakage = 1 - RTY
$$

$$
Defect\ Leakage = 1 - 0.8141
$$

$$
Defect\ Leakage = 0.1859 \text{ or } 18.59\%
$$

Number of units requiring rework:
$$
U_{rework} = 10{,}000 \times 0.1859 = 1{,}859\ \text{units}
$$

---

### (c) Total Cost of Rework

> Each defective unit incurs rework cost. This is an *Internal Failure* cost in COQ terminology.

$$
Total\ Rework\ Cost = U_{rework} \times Cost_{per\ rework}
$$

$$
= 1{,}859 \times 5{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{Total\ Rework\ Cost = \text{₹} 92{,}95{,}000}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This is a *pure waste* cost. TQM would target the *lowest-yield* stages (Stage 2: 92%) with process improvement to lift the overall RTY.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Problem 4 — Cost of Quality (COQ) Optimization

**Problem Statement:**
A company's annual COQ data is:
- Prevention = ₹8 lakhs
- Appraisal = ₹12 lakhs
- Internal Failure = ₹18 lakhs
- External Failure = ₹22 lakhs
- Revenue = ₹500 lakhs

Calculate:
(a) Total COQ
(b) COQ as % of Revenue
(c) Cost of Conformance (CoC) and Cost of Non-Conformance (CoNC)
(d) Interpret the distribution

**Given Data:**
- $P = 8$, $A = 12$, $IF = 18$, $EF = 22$ (all in lakhs ₹)
- $Revenue = 500$ lakhs ₹

---

### (a) Total COQ

$$
COQ_{total} = P + A + IF + EF
$$

$$
COQ_{total} = 8 + 12 + 18 + 22
$$

$$
\boxed{COST_{total} = 60\ \text{lakhs (₹} 60{,}00{,}000\text{)}}
$$

---

### (b) COQ as % of Revenue

> COQ-to-Revenue ratio is a *governance-level* KPI. Best-in-class software companies maintain COQ < 5% of revenue.

$$
COQ\% = \left(\frac{COST_{total}}{Revenue}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
COQ\% = \left(\frac{60}{500}\right) \times 100 = 12\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** 12% is *high* — industry leaders operate at 5–7%. Significant TQM opportunity exists.

---

### (c) Cost of Conformance (CoC) vs. Non-Conformance (CoNC)

> CoC captures *doing things right the first time* (prevention + appraisal). CoNC captures the *price of failure* (internal + external failure). Mature TQM organizations minimize CoNC and accept higher CoC as a *strategic investment*.

$$
CoC = P + A
$$

$$
CoC = 8 + 12 = 20\ \text{lakhs}
$$

$$
CoNC = IF + EF
$$

$$
CoNC = 18 + 22 = 40\ \text{lakhs}
$$

$$
CoC : CoNC = 20 : 40 = 1 : 2
$$

> **Interpretation:** The company is spending *twice* as much on failure as on prevention. This is the classic *firefighting* pattern. Increasing prevention spend (e.g., training, design reviews, static analysis) will *collapse* the CoNC side, yielding net savings.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Problem 5 — Process Capability (Cp, Cpk)

**Problem Statement:**
A software build process has:
- USL = 100, LSL = 80
- Process mean $\mu$ = 92
- Standard deviation $\sigma$ = 3

Calculate Cp and Cpk. Is the process capable?

---

### (a) Process Capability Index (Cp)

> Cp measures the *spread* of the process relative to the specification limits, *ignoring centering*. A Cp ≥ 1.33 is considered capable; ≥ 1.67 is excellent.

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}
$$

$$
C_p = \frac{100 - 80}{6 \times 3} = \frac{20}{18}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_p = 1.111}
$$

---

### (b) Process Capability Index (Cpk)

> Cpk accounts for *centering*. If the process is off-center, Cpk < Cp. A Cpk < 1.0 means the process is producing defects.

$$
C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)
$$

Compute the upper term:
$$
C_{pk,\ upper} = \frac{100 - 92}{3 \times 3} = \frac{8}{9} = 0.889
$$

Compute the lower term:
$$
C_{pk,\ lower} = \frac{92 - 80}{3 \times 3} = \frac{12}{9} = 1.333
$$

Take the minimum:
$$
\boxed{C_{pk} = 0.889}
$$

---

### (c) Interpretation

> Cp says the process *could* be capable (1.11) if perfectly centered, but Cpk (0.889) reveals it is *off-center* and producing defects. The corrective action is to *shift the mean* from 92 toward the target of $\frac{100+80}{2} = 90$ — actually closer to 90 than 92, so rebalancing calibration is required.

> **KTU Takeaway:** Always report BOTH Cp and Cpk. Cp alone hides centering errors; Cpk alone hides spread problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — TQM Framework and PDCA Integration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Focus] --> B[Leadership Commitment]
    B --> C[Strategic Planning]
    C --> D[Process Definition and Documentation]
    D --> E[Process Execution and Measurement]
    E --> F{PDCA Cycle}
    F -->|Plan| G[Identify Goals and Metrics]
    F -->|Do| H[Execute and Collect Data]
    F -->|Check| I[Compare Actual vs Target]
    F -->|Act| J[Standardize or Iterate]
    J --> F
    I --> K[Defect Analysis and RCA]
    K --> L[Kaizen Continuous Improvement]
    L --> D
    D --> M[ISO 9001 / CMMi Compliance]
    M --> N[Employee Training and Quality Circles]
    N --> E
    E --> O[Customer Satisfaction and Loyalty]
    O --> A
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The outer loop (A → O) represents the *macro* TQM philosophy. The inner loop (F → J) represents the *micro* PDCA operational cycle. The diagram illustrates that TQM is *recursive* — improvement feeds back into the process definition.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Cost of Quality (COQ) Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Prevention Cost P] --> COQ[Total COQ]
    A[Appraisal Cost A] --> COQ
    IF[Internal Failure Cost IF] --> COQ
    EF[External Failure Cost EF] --> COQ
    P -.->|Invest more| DecreaseIF
    A -.->|Detect earlier| DecreaseIF
    IF -.->|Rework| COQ
    EF -.->|Warranty| COQ
    DecreaseIF[Reduce Internal Failure]
    DecreaseEF[Reduce External Failure]
    P --> DecreaseIF
    A --> DecreaseIF
    DecreaseIF --> DecreaseEF
    COQ --> Optimum[Optimum COQ Minimum]
    style P fill:#90EE90
    style A fill:#FFD700
    style IF fill:#FFA07A
    style EF fill:#FF6347
    style Optimum fill:#87CEEB
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Green (Prevention) and Yellow (Appraisal) are *value-adding* investments. Red shades (Internal and External Failure) are *waste*. The total COQ curve is *U-shaped* — too little prevention/appraisal raises failure costs; too much over-investment in prevention also wastes resources. The optimal point minimizes total COQ.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Six Sigma DMAIC Roadmap

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Define: Identify Problem, Customer, Goal] --> M1[Measure: Capture Baseline Data, DPMO, Yield]
    M1 --> A1[Analyze: Root Cause Analysis, Fishbone, Pareto]
    A1 --> I1[Improve: Pilot Solutions, Validate Improvement]
    I1 --> C1[Control: Standardize, Monitor with Control Charts]
    C1 -->|Sustain| D1
    style D1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style M1 fill:#FFD700
    style A1 fill:#98FB98
    style I1 fill:#87CEFA
    style C1 fill:#DDA0DD
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** DMAIC is the *operational embodiment* of TQM at project level. Each phase has specific tools (Define: VOC, SIPOC; Measure: Gage R&R, DPMO; Analyze: Hypothesis testing, Regression; Improve: DOE, Pilot; Control: SPC, FMEA).

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Quality Guru Contribution Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Deming[Edwards Deming] --> DemingP[14 Points, PDCA, System Profound Knowledge]
    Juran[Joseph Juran] --> JuranP[Quality Trilogy, Pareto 80/20, Fitness for Use]
    Crosby[Philip Crosby] --> CrosbyP[Zero Defects, DIRFT, Quality is Free, 5 Absolutes]
    Ishikawa[Kaoru Ishikawa] --> IshikawaP[Quality Circles, Fishbone Diagram, 7 QC Tools]
    Taguchi[Genichi Taguchi] --> TaguchiP[Loss Function, Robust Design, Quality Engineering]
    Shewhart[Walter Shewhart] --> ShewhartP[Statistical Process Control, Control Charts]
    DemingP --> TQM[Modern TQM]
    JuranP --> TQM
    CrosbyP --> TQM
    IshikawaP --> TQM
    TaguchiP --> TQM
    ShewhartP --> TQM
    style TQM fill:#FFE4B5
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Modern TQM is the *synthesis* of six foundational thinkers. KTU often asks "Which guru is associated with which concept?" — this diagram serves as a single-glance revision map.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECNT747]**
> **Define Total Quality Management (TQM). List any FOUR core principles of TQM.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** TQM is a customer-focused, process-based management philosophy that involves all members of an organization in continuously improving products, services, and processes to achieve long-term customer satisfaction and organizational success.

**Four Core Principles:**

1. **Customer Focus** — The primary goal is to understand and meet customer needs and expectations. Quality is defined by the customer.
2. **Process Approach** — Desired results are achieved more efficiently when activities are managed as interrelated processes rather than isolated tasks.
3. **Continual Improvement (Kaizen)** — A permanent, ongoing objective of the organization; incremental and breakthrough improvements.
4. **Factual Approach to Decision Making** — Decisions are based on the analysis of data, metrics, and statistical evidence rather than intuition.

*(Alternative principles: Leadership, Involvement of People, System Approach, Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships — any four earn full marks.)*

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECNT747]**
> **Explain the PDCA cycle. How is it applied in software testing?**

**Model Answer:**

The **PDCA cycle (Deming Wheel)** is a four-stage iterative management method for continuous improvement:

- **P (Plan):** Identify the problem, set objectives, and plan a course of action. *Software testing application:* design the test plan, define test cases, set coverage targets and KPIs.
- **D (Do):** Execute the plan on a small scale and collect data. *Software testing application:* execute test cases, log defects, capture metrics.
- **C (Check):** Compare actual outcomes against expected outcomes. *Software testing application:* analyze defect reports, compare actual vs. planned test coverage, perform root-cause analysis.
- **A (Act):** Standardize the improvement if successful, or revise the plan and re-iterate. *Software testing application:* update test procedures, refine automated scripts, integrate lessons learned into the next sprint.

**Key Point:** PDCA is *recursive* — after one cycle, the improved standard becomes the new baseline.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 — TQM Concepts)

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, PECNT747]**
> **(a)** Explain the Cost of Quality (COQ) concept in detail. Describe the four categories of COQ with suitable examples for a software project. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A software project of 40 KLOC was tested. 320 defects were found during testing, and 80 more defects were reported by customers after release. Calculate: (i) Defect Density, (ii) Defect Removal Efficiency, and (iii) interpret the results. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Cost of Quality (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Cost of Quality is the total cost incurred by an organization to ensure product quality, comprising both the cost of *achieving* quality and the cost of *failing* to achieve it.

**The Four Categories (5 marks):**

| # | Category | Definition | Software Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Prevention Cost (P)** | Cost of preventing defects from occurring | Training programs, process definitions, design reviews, static analysis tool licenses, coding standards |
| 2 | **Appraisal Cost (A)** | Cost of detecting defects through inspection and testing | Test execution, code reviews, integration testing, acceptance testing, automated test framework |
| 3 | **Internal Failure Cost (IF)** | Cost of defects found *before* delivery | Rework, retest, bug triage, regression fixes, scope replanning |
| 4 | **External Failure Cost (EF)** | Cost of defects found *after* delivery | Hotfixes, patches, customer support, warranty claims, refunds, lawsuits, brand damage |

**Total COQ Formula:**
$$
COQ = P + A + IF + EF
$$

**Strategic Insight (1 mark):** TQM prescribes that increasing P and A investment *reduces* IF and EF, lowering the total COQ over time. This is the *prevention–failure trade-off* and is the foundation of Crosby's "Quality is Free" thesis.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $KLOC = 40$
- $D_{test} = 320$
- $D_{post} = 80$

**Step 1 — Total Defects (1 mark):**
$$
D_{total} = 320 + 80 = 400
$$

**Step 2 — Defect Density (2 marks):**
$$
DD = \frac{D_{total}}{KLOC} = \frac{400}{40} = 10\ \text{defects per KLOC}
$$

**Step 3 — DRE (2 marks):**
$$
DRE = \left(\frac{D_{test}}{D_{total}}\right) \times 100 = \left(\frac{320}{400}\right) \times 100 = 80\%
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (2 marks):**
- **Defect Density = 10/KLOC** is *high* — well above the industry target of ≤ 1/KLOC. The project has serious quality concerns.
- **DRE = 80%** is *below* the world-class benchmark of ≥ 95%. Roughly 1 in 5 defects slipped through testing to customers, indicating insufficient pre-release validation (reviews, static analysis, regression testing).
- **TQM Prescription:** Invest in prevention (training, design reviews) and appraisal (more robust test automation, code review checklists) to push DRE above 95% and reduce post-release defects.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

> **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, PECNT747]**
> **(a)** Discuss the contributions of any FIVE quality gurus to the evolution of TQM. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A manufacturing line processes 8,000 units daily. Over 25 working days, 600 units were defective. Each unit has 3 defect opportunities. Calculate: (i) DPMO, (ii) Process Yield, and (iii) Approximate Sigma Level (assume 1.5σ shift). **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Five Quality Gurus (7 Marks)

| # | Guru | Contribution | Famous Concept |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **W. Edwards Deming** | Introduced the 14 Points for Management; created the PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle. Emphasized that quality originates from management systems, not workers. | *"In God we trust, all others bring data."* |
| 2 | **Joseph M. Juran** | Developed the **Juran Trilogy** (Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement) and applied the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)** to quality problems. | *"Fitness for Use"* — quality is judged by the user. |
| 3 | **Philip B. Crosby** | Championed the **Zero Defects** philosophy. Authored *Quality is Free*. Defined quality as *conformance to requirements*, not elegance. | "Do It Right First Time" (DIRFT) and the **4 Absolutes of Quality**. |
| 4 | **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Pioneered **Quality Circles** (worker-led problem-solving groups) and the **Fishbone (Cause-and-Effect) Diagram** for root-cause analysis. | *"Quality begins and ends with education."* |
| 5 | **Genichi Taguchi** | Developed the **Quality Loss Function** — quality loss is proportional to the *square* of deviation from target. Advocated **Robust Design** to make products insensitive to noise. | Loss is incurred even within specification limits, not just outside. |

*(Each guru = 1.4 marks ≈ 1 mark for name + contribution, 0.4 marks for famous concept.)*

**Synthesis (1 mark):** These five thinkers collectively form the philosophical bedrock of modern TQM — Deming provided the *system*, Juran the *structure*, Crosby the *ethic*, Ishikawa the *engagement model*, and Taguchi the *engineering rigor*.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Units per day = 8,000, Days = 25, Defective units ($D$) = 600
- Opportunities per unit ($O$) = 3
- 1.5σ shift assumed

**Step 1 — Total Units (1 mark):**
$$
U = 8{,}000 \times 25 = 200{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — DPMO (2 marks):**
$$
DPMO = \left(\frac{D}{U \times O}\right) \times 10^{6} = \left(\frac{600}{200{,}000 \times 3}\right) \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
DPMO = \left(\frac{600}{600{,}000}\right) \times 10^{6} = 1{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Process Yield (2 marks):**
$$
Y = \left(1 - \frac{D}{U}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{600}{200{,}000}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
Y = (1 - 0.003) \times 100 = 99.7\%
$$

**Step 4 — Sigma Level (2 marks):**
Using the standard normal table, $\Phi^{-1}(0.997) \approx 2.75$.

$$
\sigma_{level} = \Phi^{-1}(Y) + 1.5 = 2.75 + 1.5 = 4.25\sigma
$$

**Interpretation:** A DPMO of 1,000 and yield of 99.7% places the process at approximately **4.25σ** — a strong process, but with significant room to reach the world-class **6σ** (3.4 DPMO) benchmark through DMAIC methodology.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in TQM questions — and where marks are lost:**

1. **Confusing DRE with Test Coverage** — DRE measures *defects caught*, not *code covered*. Always use the formula:
$$
DRE = \left(\frac{D_{found\ by\ testing}}{D_{total\ present}}\right) \times 100
$$
Using only testing defects without adding post-release defects is the *single most common error*. (Penalty: -2 to -3 marks)

2. **Forgetting the 1.5σ shift in Six Sigma conversions** — Motorola's empirical correction is *always* required in academic Six Sigma problems. Skipping it leads to a wrong sigma level. (Penalty: -1 to -2 marks)

3. **Reporting only Cp OR only Cpk** — KTU expects *both* indices with interpretation. Reporting only Cp (ignoring centering) loses 2 marks. The phrase "**always report both Cp and Cpk**" is itself a board-favorite answer line.

4. **Mixing up Quality Gurus** — Deming is *not* the Pareto Principle (that's Juran). Crosby is *not* Quality Circles (that's Ishikawa). A wrong guru–concept mapping costs full marks for that entry. (Penalty: -1 mark per wrong mapping)

5. **Writing PDCA without software context** — For PECNT747, examiners expect *software-specific* examples. Generic manufacturing examples lose 1–2 marks.

6. **RTY as arithmetic mean instead of geometric product** — RTY is the *product* of stage yields, not the average. (Penalty: -2 marks)

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: TQM Concepts**

- **TQM** is a *management philosophy*, not a technique. It is *customer-centric*, *process-driven*, *data-based*, and *people-empowering*.
- **Eight Core Principles** (ISO 9000): Customer Focus, Leadership, People Involvement, Process Approach, System Management, Continual Improvement, Factual Decisions, Supplier Relationships.
- **PDCA Cycle** (Deming): Plan → Do → Check → Act. It is *recursive* and the engine of continuous improvement.
- **COQ = P + A + IF + EF**. Total COQ is *U-shaped* in prevention investment — optimal point minimizes the sum.
- **DPMO** is the universal Six Sigma metric. Six Sigma target = **3.4 DPMO** ≈ 99.99966% yield.
- **DMAIC** = Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control — the project-level TQM methodology.
- **Quality Gurus Map** (must memorize):
  - Deming → 14 Points, PDCA
  - Juran → Quality Trilogy, Pareto, Fitness for Use
  - Crosby → Zero Defects, DIRFT, 4 Absolutes
  - Ishikawa → Quality Circles, Fishbone, 7 QC Tools
  - Taguchi → Loss Function, Robust Design
  - Shewhart → SPC, Control Charts
- **DRE Formula** = (Defects caught in testing / Total defects) × 100 — **always include post-release defects in the denominator**.
- **Defect Density** = Defects / KLOC — high-quality software target: < 1 defect/KLOC.
- **RTY** = *Product* (not average) of stage yields. The "hidden factory" — even small leaks compound.
- **Cp** measures *spread*; **Cpk** measures *spread + centering*. Report both. Cpk < 1 = process produces defects.
- **Taguchi's Loss Function**: $L = k(y - m)^{2}$ — deviation from target *always* causes loss, even within specs.
- **Kaizen** = small, continuous, employee-driven improvements. **Kaikaku** = radical, transformational change.
- **Jidoka** (autonomation) and **Poka-Yoke** (mistake-proofing) are Japanese TQM principles — bonus marks if mentioned.
- **The prevention–appraisal vs. failure cost trade-off** is the single most-tested COQ concept.
- In **PECNT747**, always frame TQM concepts in **software/SQA context** — generic manufacturing examples lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation of Quality Models and Standards (ISO, Six Sigma)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT747 - SOFTWARE TESTING AND QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evaluation of Quality Models and Standards (ISO, Six Sigma)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Evaluation of Quality Models and Standards?

**Evaluation of Quality Models and Standards** is the systematic, structured process of measuring, assessing, and benchmarking software artifacts (processes, products, and services) against internationally accepted frameworks to ensure conformance, identify gaps, and drive continuous improvement. In the KTU 2024 scheme context, this evaluation bridges the gap between *theoretical quality attributes* (defined by models) and *measurable engineering outcomes* (assessed via standards).

The two dominant pillars in the KTU PECNT747 syllabus are:
- **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)** — a family of standards (notably **ISO 9001** for QMS and **ISO/IEC 25010** for software product quality).
- **Six Sigma** — a data-driven methodology and statistical philosophy aimed at near-perfect process performance (target of **3.4 defects per million opportunities, DPMO**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact):**
> *Quality evaluation* is the systematic examination of the extent to which a software product or process satisfies its specified requirements, based on **ISO/IEC 25010 quality model characteristics** and **Six Sigma statistical principles** (DMAIC / DMADV). It is one of the core activities within the *Software Quality Assurance* (SQA) process.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Inspection"

Imagine you are a food critic evaluating a restaurant chain:

- **ISO 9001** is like a **government health and safety certification**. The restaurant doesn't *prove* every dish is delicious; instead, it proves that it has *documented, repeatable processes* for sourcing ingredients, training chefs, and handling complaints. The certificate says: "Trust us — we have a system."
- **ISO/IEC 25010** is like a **detailed food-quality rubric** that breaks "delicious" into 8 measurable sub-properties: freshness, presentation, taste, nutritional value, consistency, hygiene, speed of service, and ambiance. Each dish is scored on these.
- **Six Sigma** is like a **statistical obsession with the number of wrong orders**. The chain tracks *how many burgers come out wrong per million orders* and aggressively drives that number down to **3.4 DPMO** (the "Six Sigma" standard), meaning almost every customer gets exactly what they ordered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** ISO tells you *what* quality is and *how to manage* it. Six Sigma tells you *how to measure defects statistically* and *eliminate variation* process by process. Together, they form a complete evaluation ecosystem.

### 1.3 Key Metrics at a Glance

| Term | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **DPMO** | Defects Per Million Opportunities | **3.4** (at 6σ) |
| **Yield** | % of outputs free of defects | **99.99966 %** (at 6σ) |
| **RFT** | Right First Time | 95 – 99.99966 % |
| **COPQ** | Cost of Poor Quality | $$COPQ = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\_failure} + C_{external\_failure}$$ |
| **RTY** | Rolled Throughput Yield | $$\prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$$ |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ISO 9001 — Quality Management System (QMS)

**ISO 9001:2015** is a *process-based* standard (not a product standard). It specifies requirements for a **Quality Management System (QMS)**. The current revision (2015) introduces the **High-Level Structure (HLS)** — 10 clauses aligned with other ISO management standards.

#### 2.1.1 The 7 Quality Management Principles (ISO 9001:2015)

1. **Customer Focus** — understanding and meeting customer needs.
2. **Leadership** — unified direction and purpose.
3. **Engagement of People** — competent, empowered workforce.
4. **Process Approach** — manage activities as interrelated processes.
5. **Improvement** — ongoing reactive + proactive enhancement.
6. **Evidence-Based Decision Making** — facts, data, analysis.
7. **Relationship Management** — optimize relationships with interested parties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks the *Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)* cycle in conjunction with ISO 9001. PDCA is the **operating engine** of every QMS process.

#### 2.1.2 PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel)

- **Plan** — establish objectives and processes.
- **Do** — execute the plan.
- **Check** — monitor and measure performance against objectives.
- **Act** — take corrective actions to improve.

### 2.2 ISO/IEC 25010 — Software Product Quality Model

This is the **KTU-favorite** standard for software quality evaluation. It superseded the older **ISO/IEC 9126** standard.

**ISO/IEC 25010 defines 8 quality characteristics** for a software product, each with multiple sub-characteristics:

| # | Characteristic | Meaning | Sub-Characteristics (Highlights) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Functional Suitability** | Does it do what is needed? | Functional completeness, correctness, appropriateness |
| 2 | **Performance Efficiency** | How fast under load? | Time behaviour, resource utilisation, capacity |
| 3 | **Compatibility** | Coexists with other systems? | Co-existence, interoperability |
| 4 | **Usability** | Easy to learn and use? | Appropriateness recognisability, learnability, operability, user error protection, UI aesthetics, accessibility |
| 5 | **Reliability** | Performs without failure? | Maturity, availability, fault tolerance, recoverability |
| 6 | **Security** | Protects information? | Confidentiality, integrity, non-repudiation, accountability, authenticity, resistance |
| 7 | **Maintainability** | Easy to modify? | Modularity, reusability, analysability, modifiability, testability |
| 8 | **Portability** | Easy to transfer? | Adaptability, installability, replaceability |

> [!NOTE]
> A 9th characteristic, **Safety**, was added in the 2011 revision. KTU questions often ask you to *list 8* or *explain any 4 with examples*.

### 2.3 Six Sigma — A Statistical Quality Philosophy

**Six Sigma (6σ)** was developed at **Motorola** in 1986 (engineer **Bill Smith**) and popularized by **General Electric (Jack Welch)** in 1995. It is *not* just a metric — it is a *philosophy, a set of methods, and a metric*.

- **σ (sigma)** = standard deviation
- A process operating at **6σ** allows only **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**
- That equals a yield of **99.99966 %** (often quoted as "the *Six Sigma quality* level")

#### 2.3.1 Six Sigma Levels (Sigma to DPMO Conversion)

| Sigma Level (σ) | DPMO | Yield (%) | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1σ | 691,462 | 30.85 % | Inept, chaotic |
| 2σ | 308,537 | 69.15 % | Poor |
| 3σ | 66,807 | 93.32 % | Average (typical industry) |
| 4σ | 6,210 | 99.38 % | Good |
| 5σ | 233 | 99.977 % | Excellent |
| **6σ** | **3.4** | **99.99966 %** | **World-class (Six Sigma target)** |

#### 2.3.2 DMAIC Methodology (For existing processes — Improve)

- **D — Define** the problem, customer (CTQ = Critical-to-Quality), and project goals.
- **M — Measure** current performance; collect baseline data.
- **A — Analyse** root causes of variation using statistical tools.
- **I — Improve** the process by implementing solutions; verify statistically.
- **C — Control** the improved process using control charts, SOPs, and monitoring.

#### 2.3.3 DMADV Methodology (For new processes — Design)

- **D — Define** design goals aligned with customer needs.
- **M — Measure** CTQs, capabilities, risks.
- **A — Analyse** alternatives; create high-level design.
- **D — Design** detailed design; optimise.
- **V — Verify** through pilot runs, testing, and ramp-up.

#### 2.3.4 Roles in Six Sigma (Belts)

- **Champion** — senior executive sponsor.
- **Master Black Belt** — expert, mentors Black Belts.
- **Black Belt** — full-time project leader.
- **Green Belt** — part-time practitioner.
- **Yellow Belt** — basic awareness.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use **`\vert`** instead of `|` in all math expressions to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| Symbol | Formula | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|
| **DPMO** | $$DPMO = \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{N \times O}$$ | D = defects; N = units; O = opportunities per unit |
| **DPO** (Defects per Opportunity) | $$DPO = \frac{D}{N \times O}$$ | Defect density per opportunity |
| **Yield (Y)** | $$Y = 1 - DPO = 1 - \frac{D}{N \times O}$$ | Fraction of defect-free units |
| **Process Yield (%)** | $$Y_{\%} = Y \times 100$$ | Expressed as a percentage |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $$RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$$ | Product of yields across *n* process steps |
| **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $$C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$ | Centring of process between specification limits |
| **Process Capability (Cp)** | $$C_{p} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$ | Spread of process to specification width |
| **Z-score (for σ level)** | $$Z = \frac{\vert X - \mu \vert}{\sigma}$$ | Distance in standard deviations |
| **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality)** | $$COPQ = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$$ | P = Prevention, A = Appraisal, IF = Internal Failure, EF = External Failure |
| **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $$DRE = \frac{D_{found\ in\ test}}{D_{found\ in\ test} + D_{found\ post-release}} \times 100$$ | Effectiveness of testing phase (%) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Medical Devices** — ISO 9001 + Six Sigma 6σ is mandatory (think Boeing, GE Aviation, Medtronic). Any defect can be fatal.
- **Banking & FinTech** — ISO 25010's *Security* and *Reliability* characteristics are evaluated for compliance with PCI-DSS.
- **Telecommunications** — Six Sigma DMAIC used to reduce dropped-call rates from 6σ equivalents of 4σ to 5σ.
- **Agile/DevOps** — Six Sigma's *Measure → Analyse* aligns with observability and telemetry; *Improve → Control* aligns with CI/CD guardrails.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Six Sigma DPMO to Process Yield (Closed-Form)

**Given:** A process with $N$ units, $O$ opportunities per unit, and $D$ total defects.

**Step 1 — Compute the Defect Rate per Opportunity (DPO).**
The number of defect *opportunities* across the entire sample is the product of units and opportunities per unit:

$$N \times O = \text{Total defect opportunities in the sample}$$

The defects per opportunity are therefore:

$$DPO = \frac{D}{N \times O}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Process Yield as a Fraction.**
Yield is the fraction of opportunities that did *not* result in a defect:

$$Y = 1 - DPO = 1 - \frac{D}{N \times O}$$

**Step 3 — Scale to Per-Million Basis (DPMO).**
Since Six Sigma expresses defects per **one million** opportunities, we multiply DPO by $10^6$:

$$DPMO = DPO \times 10^{6} = \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{N \times O}$$

**Step 4 — Convert DPMO to a Sigma Level (Inverse Normal).**
For a 1.5σ process-mean shift (the long-term Six Sigma assumption), the *upper-tail probability* $p$ of a defect is:

$$p = \frac{DPMO}{10^{6}} = \frac{D}{N \times O}$$

The corresponding short-term $Z_{st}$ is obtained from the standard normal inverse CDF:

$$Z_{st} = \Phi^{-1}\!\left(1 - p\right) + 1.5$$

where $\Phi^{-1}$ is the inverse of the standard normal cumulative distribution function. The added 1.5σ accounts for long-term drift. This $Z_{st}$ value is the **sigma level** of the process.

> [!NOTE]
> For a Six Sigma process, $DPMO = 3.4$, $p = 3.4 \times 10^{-6}$, $1 - p \approx 0.9999966$, $\Phi^{-1}(0.9999966) \approx 4.5$, and adding the 1.5σ shift gives $4.5 + 1.5 = 6.0$ — confirming the 6σ designation.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

> A software team produced $N = 500$ modules in a release. Each module has $O = 20$ defect opportunities (fields, methods, integration points). The QA team found $D = 25$ defects. Compute **DPMO**, **Yield**, and the **sigma level**.

**Step 1 — DPO**

$$DPO = \frac{D}{N \times O} = \frac{25}{500 \times 20} = \frac{25}{10{,}000} = 0.0025$$

**Step 2 — DPMO**

$$DPMO = DPO \times 10^{6} = 0.0025 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 2{,}500$$

**Step 3 — Yield (Fraction)**

$$Y = 1 - DPO = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975$$

**Step 4 — Yield (Percentage)**

$$Y_{\%} = 0.9975 \times 100 = 99.75\ \%$$

**Step 5 — Sigma Level**
$p = 0.0025$, $1 - p = 0.9975$. From standard normal tables, $\Phi^{-1}(0.9975) \approx 2.81$. Adding the long-term 1.5σ shift:

$$\sigma_{level} = 2.81 + 1.5 = 4.31\ \sigma$$

**Interpretation:** The process is operating between 4σ and 4.5σ — *good*, but not world-class Six Sigma (6σ = 3.4 DPMO). The QA team has room for further DMAIC improvement cycles.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Capability Interpretation

| $C_{pk}$ Value | Process Capability | Action Required |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ | **Excellent** (6σ capable) | Continue control, no improvements needed |
| $1.33 \leq C_{pk} < 1.67$ | **Good** | Tighten control limits, monitor |
| $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ | **Marginal** | Process improvement required |
| $C_{pk} < 1.00$ | **Incapable** | Stop production; redesign process |

> **Derivation Note:** The factor **3σ** in the $C_{pk}$ formula comes from the assumption that for a normally distributed process, $\pm 3\sigma$ from the mean captures **99.73 %** of all output. If the specification limits (USL, LSL) fall within this $\pm 3\sigma$ band, the process is barely capable ($C_{pk} = 1.0$).

### 3.4 Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) — Derivation

DRE quantifies the *effectiveness of testing* at catching defects *before* release:

**Given:**
- $D_{test}$ = defects found during testing
- $D_{release}$ = defects reported by users after release

**Step 1 — Total defects reaching customer awareness**

$$D_{total} = D_{test} + D_{release}$$

**Step 2 — DRE formula (as a percentage)**

$$DRE = \frac{D_{test}}{D_{test} + D_{release}} \times 100\ \%$$

**Step 3 — Example**
If QA finds 480 defects in test and 20 escape to production:

$$DRE = \frac{480}{480 + 20} \times 100 = \frac{480}{500} \times 100 = 96\ \%$$

A DRE of 96 % means testing is *good* but a target DRE of ≥ 99 % is typical for Six Sigma mature teams.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Six Sigma. What is the significance of the value **3.4 DPMO** in a Six Sigma process?*

**Model Answer:**

**Six Sigma** is a data-driven quality management philosophy and statistical methodology that aims to reduce process defects to a near-zero level. The term "Sigma" denotes *standard deviation*; achieving "Six Sigma" means the process variation is six standard deviations from the nearest specification limit.

The figure **3.4 DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** is the *benchmark* of a Six Sigma process. It represents the maximum allowable long-term defect rate, corresponding to a process yield of **99.99966 %**. The 3.4 DPMO figure incorporates an empirical **1.5σ process-mean shift** (observed in long-term studies of real processes), which adjusts the short-term theoretical value of 0.002 DPMO to the practical 3.4 DPMO benchmark.

**Significance:**
- Sets a *universal, numerical* quality target.
- Enables comparison across industries and processes.
- Drives continuous DMAIC improvement cycles.
- Reduces Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List and briefly explain any **four quality characteristics** defined by the **ISO/IEC 25010** software product quality model.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Functional Suitability** — The degree to which a product provides functions that meet stated and implied needs. *Example:* An e-commerce checkout module that processes payments, applies discounts, and generates invoices.

2. **Reliability** — The degree to which a product performs specified functions under stated conditions for a specified period. *Sub-characteristics:* maturity, availability, fault tolerance, recoverability. *Example:* A 24×7 trading platform with 99.99 % uptime.

3. **Security** — The degree to which a product protects information and data. *Sub-characteristics:* confidentiality, integrity, non-repudiation, accountability, authenticity. *Example:* A banking app that encrypts transactions and authenticates users via OTP.

4. **Maintainability** — The degree to which a product can be modified to correct defects, improve performance, or adapt to a changed environment. *Sub-characteristics:* modularity, reusability, analysability, modifiability, testability. *Example:* A modular microservice that can be redeployed without affecting other services.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper PECNT747]**
*(a)* Explain the **ISO/IEC 25010** software product quality model in detail. Discuss any **five characteristics** with their sub-characteristics. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* With a suitable example, explain the **DMAIC** methodology of Six Sigma in detail. How is it different from the **DMADV** methodology? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a) — ISO/IEC 25010 (7 marks):**

The **ISO/IEC 25010** standard (published in 2011, replacing ISO/IEC 9126) defines a *quality model* for software products. It decomposes software product quality into **8 main characteristics**, each with several **sub-characteristics**. A software product is evaluated on the extent to which each characteristic is satisfied.

**Five Characteristics (with sub-characteristics and examples):**

1. **Functional Suitability** *(2 sub-characteristics)*
   - *Functional Completeness* — covers all specified tasks. *Example:* HRMS covers attendance, payroll, leave.
   - *Functional Correctness* — produces correct results. *Example:* Tax calculator computes exact TDS.
   - *Functional Appropriateness* — functions facilitate task accomplishment.

2. **Performance Efficiency** *(3 sub-characteristics)*
   - *Time Behaviour* — response and throughput times. *Example:* Search returns in < 200 ms.
   - *Resource Utilisation* — CPU, memory, network usage.
   - *Capacity* — maximum concurrent users / transactions.

3. **Reliability** *(4 sub-characteristics)*
   - *Maturity* — low failure rate under normal operation.
   - *Availability* — system is up and accessible when needed.
   - *Fault Tolerance* — continues operating despite component failures.
   - *Recoverability* — restores service and data after a failure.

4. **Security** *(6 sub-characteristics)*
   - *Confidentiality* — data is accessible only to authorised users.
   - *Integrity* — data is not altered by unauthorised means.
   - *Non-repudiation* — actions cannot be denied by the actor.
   - *Accountability* — actions are traceable to an entity.
   - *Authenticity* — identity of a subject/resource is provable.
   - *Resistance* — withstands malicious attacks (e.g., SQL injection).

5. **Maintainability** *(5 sub-characteristics)*
   - *Modularity* — components are independent and cohesive.
   - *Reusability* — assets can be reused across products.
   - *Analysability* — defects are easy to diagnose.
   - *Modifiability* — easy to change without degrading quality.
   - *Testability* — easy to validate modified software.

> **Conclusion:** ISO/IEC 25010 provides a *standard vocabulary* and *measurable structure* for software quality evaluation. It forms the foundation of Software Quality Requirements and Evaluation (SQuaRE) and is widely used in KTU-aligned SQA modules.

---

**Model Solution to (b) — DMAIC vs DMADV (7 marks):**

**DMAIC** is applied to **existing processes** that are under-performing but fundamentally sound. It is the *Improve* methodology.

**Steps of DMAIC** *(with a banking example — reducing failed ATM transactions):*

- **D — Define:** Identify the problem. The bank's CTQ is *"successful ATM cash withdrawal without card retention or failure"*. Project goal: reduce failure rate from 4 % to 0.1 %.
- **M — Measure:** Collect baseline data. The bank collects 10,000 ATM transactions over 30 days; finds 400 failures (4 %).
- **A — Analyse:** Apply root-cause analysis (5-Whys, Fishbone, Pareto). Top three causes: (1) network timeout (45 %), (2) cash-out jam (30 %), (3) card-reader fault (15 %).
- **I — Improve:** Implement solutions — upgrade ISP link, recalibrate cash dispenser firmware, replace faulty card readers. Pilot at 5 ATMs for 2 weeks.
- **C — Control:** Install SPC (Statistical Process Control) charts on ATM logs; set alerts when failure rate exceeds 0.2 %; update SOPs for daily maintenance.

**Difference between DMAIC and DMADV:**

| Aspect | DMAIC | DMADV |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Improve an existing process | Design a new process / product |
| **Final D** | Define the problem | Define design goals |
| **Final V** | — (Control) | Verify the design |
| **When used** | Process is *broken* | Process does *not exist* |
| **Output** | Sustained improvement | A new, capable process |
| **Example** | Reduce ATM failure rate | Design a new mobile banking app from scratch |

> **Conclusion:** DMAIC is the *statistical, project-driven improvement* loop, while DMADV is the *design-for-Six-Sigma* loop. They are complementary, not interchangeable.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*(a)* Discuss the **ISO 9001:2015** Quality Management System standard. Explain its **seven quality management principles** and the **PDCA cycle** with a software-industry example. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A software organisation produces 2,000 builds per month. Each build has 25 defect opportunities. The QA team records 30 defects per month. Compute the **DPMO**, **Yield**, and determine the **sigma level** of the process. What is the **DRE** if 8 defects are reported by customers post-release? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a) — ISO 9001:2015 and PDCA (7 marks):**

**ISO 9001:2015** is the internationally recognised standard for a **Quality Management System (QMS)**. It is *generic* (applies to any organisation) and *process-based*. It requires the organisation to:
- Determine customer needs and expectations.
- Establish a quality policy and objectives.
- Define processes to deliver the QMS outcomes.
- Monitor, measure, and continually improve the QMS.

**The 7 Quality Management Principles:**

1. **Customer Focus** — primary focus on meeting customer requirements and exceeding expectations.
2. **Leadership** — leaders establish unity of purpose and direction.
3. **Engagement of People** — competent, empowered people at all levels.
4. **Process Approach** — consistent, predictable results through managed processes.
5. **Improvement** — continual improvement is a permanent objective.
6. **Evidence-Based Decision Making** — decisions based on the analysis of data and information.
7. **Relationship Management** — manage relationships with interested parties (suppliers, partners, regulators).

**PDCA Cycle — Software Industry Example (Defect Reduction in a Banking App):**

- **Plan:** Set the goal — reduce production defects from 50 to ≤ 10 per release. Plan additional unit tests and code reviews.
- **Do:** Execute the plan in the next sprint. Add Jest-based unit tests and a peer-review gate in the CI pipeline.
- **Check:** Measure the actual number of defects in production. QA finds only 14 defects — a 72 % reduction.
- **Act:** Standardise the new process — make code review mandatory in CI; train the team; document the SOP; apply to all future modules.

> **Conclusion:** ISO 9001:2015, combined with PDCA, provides a *systematic, auditable* framework for software organisations to deliver consistent quality, satisfy customers, and demonstrate continual improvement — exactly the spirit of KTU's Quality Assurance module.

---

**Model Solution to (b) — Six Sigma Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $N = 2{,}000$ builds/month
- $O = 25$ opportunities/build
- $D_{test} = 30$ defects found by QA
- $D_{release} = 8$ defects reported post-release

**Step 1 — Total Opportunities**

$$N \times O = 2{,}000 \times 25 = 50{,}000$$

**Step 2 — DPO**

$$DPO = \frac{D_{test}}{N \times O} = \frac{30}{50{,}000} = 6 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3 — DPMO**

$$DPMO = DPO \times 10^{6} = 6 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{6} = 600$$

**Step 4 — Yield**

$$Y = 1 - DPO = 1 - 6 \times 10^{-4} = 0.9994$$

$$Y_{\%} = 99.94\ \%$$

**Step 5 — Sigma Level**
$p = DPO = 0.0006$. From the standard normal table, $\Phi^{-1}(1 - 0.0006) = \Phi^{-1}(0.9994) \approx 3.25$.

Applying the 1.5σ long-term shift:

$$\sigma_{level} = 3.25 + 1.5 = 4.75\ \sigma$$

**Step 6 — DRE (Defect Removal Efficiency)**

$$DRE = \frac{D_{test}}{D_{test} + D_{release}} \times 100 = \frac{30}{30 + 8} \times 100 = \frac{30}{38} \times 100 \approx 78.95\ \%$$

**Interpretation:**
- DPMO of 600 and yield 99.94 % place the process at approximately **4.75σ** — solid *good* performance but short of 6σ (3.4 DPMO).
- The DRE of 78.95 % indicates the testing process is *missing ~21 %* of defects that escape to customers. A Six Sigma mature team targets DRE ≥ 99 %.
- Recommended actions: improve test coverage (aim for ≥ 95 % branch coverage), introduce mutation testing, automate regression suite, and tighten code review.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing DPMO with Defect Density.** DPMO uses *opportunities* (denominator $N \times O$); Defect Density uses *KLOC* (thousand lines of code). Do not mix them in the same formula.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1.5σ shift.** When asked to compute the sigma level, students often report the *short-term* Z-score only. KTU expects the *long-term* value (Z + 1.5).
> 3. **Listing ISO/IEC 9126 characteristics in an ISO/IEC 25010 question.** 9126 is *obsolete*; 25010 has 8 (or 9, including Safety) characteristics. The examiner will deduct marks.
> 4. **Writing DMAIC and DMADV as if they are the same.** They are *not* — DMAIC improves existing processes, DMADV designs new ones. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Not stating units.** Always say "DPMO = 600" not just "600". And write "$C_{pk} = 1.33$ (dimensionless)" in capability calculations.
> 6. **Skipping sub-characteristics in 7-mark ISO questions.** A bare list of 8 characteristics without sub-characteristics or examples usually scores 4–5 out of 7, not full marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **ISO 9001:2015** is a *QMS* standard (manages the *processes*); it is **process-based** and based on **7 QMS principles** + **PDCA**.
- ✅ **ISO/IEC 25010** is a *product quality* standard (defines *what* quality means); it has **8 main characteristics** (Functional Suitability, Performance Efficiency, Compatibility, Usability, Reliability, Security, Maintainability, Portability). **Safety** is sometimes added as a 9th.
- ✅ **Six Sigma** target: **3.4 DPMO**, **99.99966 %** yield, **6σ** level with a **1.5σ shift**.
- ✅ **DPMO formula** is the most-asked equation: $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{N \times O}$.
- ✅ **DMAIC** = Define → Measure → Analyse → Improve → Control — for *existing* processes.
- ✅ **DMADV** = Define → Measure → Analyse → Design → Verify — for *new* processes/products.
- ✅ Belts hierarchy: **Champion → Master Black Belt → Black Belt → Green Belt → Yellow Belt**.
- ✅ **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** = process capable; **$C_{pk} < 1.0$** = process incapable.
- ✅ **RTY** (Rolled Throughput Yield) is the *product* of individual step yields — a key metric in multi-stage pipelines.
- ✅ **DRE** measures testing effectiveness; **> 99 %** is the world-class target.
- ✅ Always convert DPO to **DPMO** (multiply by $10^6$) when answering KTU numerical questions.
- ✅ The 1.5σ shift is *empirical* (Motorola's observation of real process drift) — it converts short-term capability to long-term performance.
- ✅ KTU loves mixed questions: *theory (ISO 25010) + numerical (Six Sigma DPMO) + comparison (DMAIC vs DMADV)* — prepare accordingly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Addressing Quality Challenges
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT747 - SOFTWARE TESTING AND QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Addressing Quality Challenges

# Addressing Quality Challenges — Core Foundations

## 1. Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *Software Testing and Quality Assurance (PECNT747)*, **Quality Challenges** refer to the systematic set of obstacles, deficiencies, and process-level impairments that prevent a software product from achieving the desired level of conformance to its specified requirements, user expectations, and recognized industry standards. Addressing these challenges is the discipline of proactively identifying, classifying, analyzing, and mitigating quality-related risks through structured frameworks, process improvement models, root-cause analysis (RCA), and continuous feedback mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECNT747 - Module 3):**
> *Quality challenges* are recurring and systemic defects-in-the-process (as opposed to defects-in-the-product). They manifest due to ambiguous requirements, inadequate testing maturity, poor configuration management, weak traceability, and absence of metrics-driven governance. Addressing them requires a layered approach combining **people, process, product, and project (4Ps)** alignment.

---

## 2. Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Hospital Emergency Room vs. Preventive Health Check-ups"**
> Imagine software quality. Most teams operate like an **Emergency Room (ER)** — they wait for bugs to crash the system, then scramble to fix them. This is **reactive quality control**.
> A mature QA team, however, runs the software development process like a **Preventive Health Clinic** — they monitor vital signs (metrics), prescribe lifestyle changes (process improvements), vaccinate against known diseases (defect prevention), and conduct regular health check-ups (reviews and static analysis). Bugs are caught *before* they become heart attacks.
> **Addressing Quality Challenges** is the transition from ER-mode to Preventive-Medicine-mode.

---

## 3. The 4P Quality Challenge Model (KTU High-Yield Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 4Ps of Quality Challenges — Every KTU examiner expects this!**

| Dimension | Challenge | Real-World Manifestation |
|---|---|---|
| **People** | Skill gaps, lack of QA ownership, poor training | Tester does not know boundary value analysis |
| **Process** | Ad-hoc lifecycle, missing reviews, weak traceability | No formal test plan; requirements are not traceable to test cases |
| **Product** | Complex architecture, third-party APIs, legacy code | Tight coupling makes regression testing difficult |
| **Project** | Schedule pressure, scope creep, budget cuts | Testing phase is compressed to meet deadlines |

---

## 4. Quality Challenge Taxonomy (Classified View)

> [!NOTE]
> Quality challenges can be classified into **seven canonical categories** as recognized by KTU 2024 Scheme Module-3 outcomes:

1. **Requirement Volatility Challenge** — Frequent changes in scope leading to rework.
2. **Communication Gap Challenge** — Misalignment between developers, testers, and stakeholders.
3. **Test Coverage Challenge** — Inability to exercise all paths, branches, or use cases.
4. **Defect Leakage Challenge** — Defects escaping to production.
5. **Process Maturity Challenge** — Absence of a defined, measured, and optimized SDLC.
6. **Tool & Automation Challenge** — Manual-only testing leading to inefficiency and inconsistency.
7. **Cultural & Management Challenge** — Resistance to QA practices, blame culture, no executive buy-in.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When a question asks to *"list the quality challenges"*, the expected answer is the **7-category taxonomy above**, not a generic paragraph. Memorize the categorical headings.

---

## 5. Why Addressing Quality Challenges Matters (Engineering Utility)

- **Cost Reduction:** Studies (including the IBM Systems Sciences Institute) indicate that fixing a defect post-release can cost **30× to 100×** more than fixing it during requirements.
- **Customer Trust:** Quality is the primary driver of Net Promoter Score (NPS) and customer retention in SaaS products.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Domains like medical devices (FDA), aviation (DO-178C), and finance (PCI-DSS) mandate rigorous QA.
- **Predictability:** Mature QA processes enable accurate effort estimation and on-time delivery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Metric to Remember:** The **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** of a high-maturity process should exceed **95%** before release. Below **85%** indicates unaddressed quality challenges.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 1. The Six-Layer Strategy for Addressing Quality Challenges

Quality challenges are not solved by a single technique. The **KTU 2024 scheme** emphasizes a **six-layer integrated strategy**, which must be understood in the order presented below:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six-Layer Addressing Model (SLAM):**
> Layer 1 → **Prevention** (Defect Prevention)
> Layer 2 → **Detection** (Reviews, Inspections, Testing)
> Layer 3 → **Containment** (Configuration Management, Build Verification)
> Layer 4 → **Diagnosis** (Root Cause Analysis — RCA)
> Layer 5 → **Improvement** (Process Maturity Models: CMM/CMMI, TQM, Six Sigma)
> Layer 6 → **Cultural Embedding** (Kaizen, Total Quality Management Mindset)

---

## 2. Layer 1 — Defect Prevention (The Proactive Layer)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defect Prevention** is the practice of identifying the **root causes of defects** and implementing changes to **prevent them from recurring**. It is *cheaper than detection*.

**Key Techniques:**
- **Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Ishikawa / Fishbone Diagram)** — classifies defect causes into 6M categories: *Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment)*.
- **Pareto Analysis (80/20 Rule)** — 80% of defects come from 20% of causes.
- **Formal Technical Reviews (FTR)** — structured walkthroughs that catch defects before testing.
- **Defect Classification & Causal Analysis** — every captured defect is tagged with a *causal category* (e.g., requirements, design, coding, documentation).

---

## 3. Layer 2 — Detection via Reviews & Testing

**Walkthroughs, Inspections, and Reviews:**
- A **Fagan Inspection** is a 7-step formal process: Planning → Overview → Individual Preparation → Inspection Meeting → Rework → Follow-up → Causal Analysis.
- Inspections are proven to remove **60–90%** of defects before test execution.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Formula:** `Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) = Defects Found Before Release / (Defects Found Before Release + Defects Found After Release) × 100`

---

## 4. Layer 4 — Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Framework

RCA answers the question: *"Why did this defect occur, and what process change will prevent it from recurring?"*

### 4.1 The 5-Why Technique (Toyota Production System)

> [!NOTE]
> Repeat "Why?" five times to drill from *symptom* to *root cause*.

**Example:**
- **Defect:** Login page returns 500 error.
- **Why 1?** Database connection failed.
- **Why 2?** Connection pool exhausted.
- **Why 3?** Connections not being released.
- **Why 4?** No `finally` block in code.
- **Why 5?** Coding standard does not mandate resource cleanup.
- **Root Cause:** Missing process enforcement for coding standards.

### 4.2 Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram

Branches into **6M categories** for systematic cause classification.

### 4.3 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)

A top-down deductive analysis using Boolean logic gates (AND/OR) to model how combinations of lower-level faults cause the top-level system failure.

---

## 5. Layer 5 — Process Maturity Models (KTU High-Yield Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Process Models That KTU Repeatedly Asks:**

### 5.1 CMM / CMMI (Capability Maturity Model Integration)

The **CMMI Institute** (now ISACA) defines **5 maturity levels**:

| Level | Name | Description | KTU Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Initial | Chaotic, hero-based, unpredictable | "Ad-hoc" |
| **2** | Managed | Projects are planned and tracked | "Basic discipline" |
| **3** | Defined | Organization-wide standard process | "Proactive" |
| **4** | Quantitatively Managed | Measured and controlled with metrics | "Data-driven" |
| **5** | Optimizing | Continuous process improvement | "Innovation focus" |

### 5.2 TQM (Total Quality Management)

> [!NOTE]
> **TQM Philosophy:** Quality is everyone's responsibility — not just the QA team. Core principles: *Customer focus, continuous improvement (Kaizen), employee involvement, fact-based decision-making, integrated system.*

### 5.3 Six Sigma

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Sigma = 3.4 Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO).**
> It uses the **DMAIC** framework:
> - **D**efine the problem
> - **M**easure current performance
> - **A**nalyze root causes
> - **I**mprove the process
> - **C**ontrol the new state

---

## 6. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Critical for Numerical Problems)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize these formulas — they appear in KTU ESE Part A and Part B.**

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbol / Unit | Definition | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DRE** | $\frac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}} \times 100$ (%) | Defect Removal Efficiency | Quality of process |
| 2 | **DPMO** | $\frac{\text{Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities | Six Sigma maturity |
| 3 | **Sigma Level** | $\sigma$ | Standard deviations from mean | Six Sigma goal: $\sigma \geq 6$ |
| 4 | **Pareto Index** | Top 20% causes → 80% defects | 80/20 rule | Prioritization |
| 5 | **Defect Density** | $\frac{\text{Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$ or $\frac{\text{Defects}}{\text{FP}}$ | Defects per unit size | Product quality |
| 6 | **Mean Time to Failure (MTTF)** | Hours / Cycles | Average uptime | Reliability |
| 7 | **Test Effectiveness** | $\frac{\text{Defects found by test}}{\text{Total defects in system}} \times 100$ (%) | Test process maturity | QA KPI |
| 8 | **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | Prevention + Appraisal + Failure | Total quality spend | Management metric |

---

## 7. Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Quality Challenge Addressing |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace (DO-178C)** | Mandatory CMMI Level 3+ for flight software |
| **Medical Devices (FDA)** | Six Sigma DMAIC for device reliability |
| **Banking & FinTech** | Defect prevention via formal inspections on payment modules |
| **Automotive (ISO 26262)** | Fault Tree Analysis for safety-critical ECUs |
| **Open Source / SaaS** | Kaizen + continuous integration for rapid release cycles |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **Quality Assurance (QA)** with **Quality Control (QC)**. **QA = Process-focused (prevention).** **QC = Product-focused (detection).** This is a **2-mark favorite** in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 1. Derivation: Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) — Boundary Behavior

The DRE is one of the most frequently used metrics in the **Addressing Quality Challenges** module. Below is its complete derivation, including boundary-condition analysis.

### 1.1 Setup and Definition

Let:
- $D_{pre}$ = Number of defects found **before** release
- $D_{post}$ = Number of defects found **after** release (in production or by users)
- $D_{total} = D_{pre} + D_{post}$ = Total defects in the system

The **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** is defined as the ratio of defects removed *before* the product reaches the customer, expressed as a percentage:

$$
DRE = \frac{D_{pre}}{D_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

### 1.2 Step-by-Step Substitution

**Step 1:** Substitute the definition of $D_{total}$.

$$
DRE = \frac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2:** Boundary Case 1 — Perfect Process (No post-release defects).

If $D_{post} = 0$, then:

$$
DRE = \frac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + 0} \times 100\% = \frac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre}} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

This represents an *ideal* process where all defects are removed pre-release.

**Step 3:** Boundary Case 2 — Worst Process (No pre-release detection).

If $D_{pre} = 0$, then:

$$
DRE = \frac{0}{0 + D_{post}} \times 100\% = \frac{0}{D_{post}} \times 100\% = 0\%
$$

This represents a *catastrophic* process where all defects escape to the customer.

### 1.3 Numerical Example (KTU-Style Worked Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** A software project found **180 defects** during testing and **20 defects** were reported by customers in the first month after release. Calculate the DRE.

**Given:**
$D_{pre} = 180$, $D_{post} = 20$

**Step 1:** Compute total defects.

$$
D_{total} = 180 + 20 = 200
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the DRE formula.

$$
DRE = \frac{180}{200} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify the fraction.

$$
DRE = \frac{9}{10} \times 100\% = 0.9 \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4:** Final answer.

$$
\boxed{DRE = 90\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** A DRE of 90% is **acceptable** in many industries, but **best-in-class** organizations (CMMI Level 5) target **> 95%**.

---

## 2. Derivation: Six Sigma DPMO and Sigma Level

### 2.1 DPMO Definition

$$
DPMO = \frac{\text{Number of Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Number of Units Produced} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}}
$$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Conversion to Sigma Level

The conversion from DPMO to **Sigma Level** is based on the *normal distribution* integrated over a 1.5-sigma shift (Six Sigma's defining convention).

**Step 1:** Compute yield.

$$
Y = 1 - \frac{DPMO}{10^{6}}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert yield to a Z-score using the inverse standard normal cumulative distribution function.

$$
Z = \Phi^{-1}(Y)
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **1.5-sigma shift** correction.

$$
\sigma_{level} = Z + 1.5
$$

### 2.3 Numerical Example (Worked)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** A call center processes **50,000 calls per month** with **4 opportunities per call** (greeting, identification, resolution, closure). In one month, **120 defects** were logged. Compute the DPMO and Sigma Level.

**Step 1:** Compute total opportunities.

$$
\text{Opportunities} = 50{,}000 \times 4 = 200{,}000
$$

**Step 2:** Compute DPMO.

$$
DPMO = \frac{120 \times 10^{6}}{200{,}000}
$$

$$
DPMO = \frac{120{,}000{,}000}{200{,}000} = 600
$$

**Step 3:** Compute yield.

$$
Y = 1 - \frac{600}{10^{6}} = 1 - 0.0006 = 0.9994
$$

**Step 4:** Look up $\Phi^{-1}(0.9994) \approx 3.19$ (using standard normal tables).

**Step 5:** Apply the 1.5-sigma shift.

$$
\sigma_{level} = 3.19 + 1.5 = 4.69 \approx 4.7\,\sigma
$$

**Interpretation:** A 4.7σ process is *good* but **not world-class** (Six Sigma = 6σ = 3.4 DPMO).

---

## 3. Derivation: Cost of Quality (CoQ) — Conformance vs. Non-Conformance

The **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** is a financial framework that quantifies the total cost of achieving and failing to achieve product quality.

### 3.1 The Four CoQ Categories

$$
CoQ = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\,failure} + C_{external\,failure}
$$

Where:
- $C_{prevention}$ = Cost of preventing defects (training, process design, tools)
- $C_{appraisal}$ = Cost of detecting defects (testing, inspections, reviews)
- $C_{internal\,failure}$ = Cost of fixing defects *before* release (rework, retest)
- $C_{external\,failure}$ = Cost of fixing defects *after* release (patches, warranty, reputation loss)

### 3.2 The Optimal CoQ Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Juran's Optimal Quality Cost Model** states that total CoQ is minimized at the intersection of rising *failure costs* and rising *prevention + appraisal costs*.

Let the total CoQ be:

$$
CoQ_{total} = (C_p + C_a) + (C_{if} + C_{ef})
$$

- As prevention investment increases, failure costs decrease.
- There exists an **optimal point** $C_{opt}$ where $\frac{d\,CoQ_{total}}{dC_p} = 0$.

### 3.3 Numerical Example (Worked)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** Given: $C_p = \$10{,}000$, $C_a = \$15{,}000$, $C_{if} = \$25{,}000$, $C_{ef} = \$50{,}000$. Compute the total CoQ.

**Step 1:** Sum all categories.

$$
CoQ_{total} = 10{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 25{,}000 + 50{,}000
$$

**Step 2:**

$$
\boxed{CoQ_{total} = \$100{,}000}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the *failure cost ratio*.

$$
\text{Failure Ratio} = \frac{C_{if} + C_{ef}}{CoQ_{total}} = \frac{75{,}000}{100{,}000} = 75\%
$$

**Interpretation:** **75%** of quality cost is from *failures*. A healthy organization should target **< 50%** by investing in prevention and appraisal.

---

## 4. Analytical Matrix: Mapping Challenges to Techniques

| Quality Challenge | Root Cause Category | Recommended Technique | Expected Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequent requirement changes | **Process** | Formal change control + requirements traceability | Reduced scope creep |
| High post-release defects | **Process + Product** | Fagan Inspections + DRE tracking | DRE > 95% |
| Inconsistent test coverage | **Process** | Risk-based testing + coverage metrics | Branch coverage > 90% |
| Tool limitations | **Project** | Tool evaluation framework + pilot | Improved automation ROI |
| Tester skill gaps | **People** | Training matrix + certifications (ISTQB) | Higher test effectiveness |
| Cultural resistance to QA | **People + Project** | TQM adoption + management sponsorship | QA ownership culture |
| Late defect discovery | **Process** | Shift-left testing + static analysis | Defects caught at coding stage |

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is a **2-mark scoring weapon** in KTU Part B answers. Drawing a table of challenges-vs-solutions always scores high.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: The Six-Layer Strategy for Addressing Quality Challenges

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Layer 1: Defect Prevention] --> B[Layer 2: Detection via Reviews and Testing]
    B --> C[Layer 3: Containment via CM and Build Verification]
    C --> D[Layer 4: Root Cause Analysis - 5 Why and Fishbone]
    D --> E[Layer 5: Process Improvement - CMMI, TQM, Six Sigma]
    E --> F[Layer 6: Cultural Embedding - Kaizen and TQM Mindset]
    F --> A
    A1[Inputs: Requirements, Design, Code] --> A
    F1[Feedback Loop: Lessons Learned] --> A
```

## Diagram 2: CMMI 5-Level Maturity Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Level 1: Initial\nChaotic Hero Based Process"]
    L2["Level 2: Managed\nProject Level Discipline"]
    L3["Level 3: Defined\nOrganization Standard Process"]
    L4["Level 4: Quantitatively Managed\nMetrics Driven Control"]
    L5["Level 5: Optimizing\nContinuous Innovation"]
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
    S1["Keyword: Ad Hoc"] -.-> L1
    S2["Keyword: Repeatable"] -.-> L2
    S3["Keyword: Proactive"] -.-> L3
    S4["Keyword: Measured"] -.-> L4
    S5["Keyword: Innovating"] -.-> L5
```

## Diagram 3: Six Sigma DMAIC Cycle (Process Improvement Engine)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D[Define the Problem and Customer CTQ] --> M[Measure Current Baseline Performance]
    M --> A[Analyze Root Causes via Pareto and Fishbone]
    A --> I[Improve the Process via Pilot Solutions]
```
