```mermaid
    I --> C[Control the New State via SOPs and SPC Charts]
    C --> D
    M1[Inputs: Voice of Customer VOC] --> D
    O1[Outputs: Defects Reduced to 3.4 DPMO] --> C
```

## Diagram 4: 5-Why Root Cause Analysis (Worked Example Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Problem: Login Returns HTTP 500"] --> Q1["Why 1: Database Connection Failed"]
    Q1 --> Q2["Why 2: Connection Pool Exhausted"]
    Q2 --> Q3["Why 3: Connections Not Released After Use"]
    Q3 --> Q4["Why 4: Missing Finally Block in Code"]
    Q4 --> Q5["Why 5: Coding Standard Not Enforced by Process"]
    Q5 --> RC["Root Cause: No Automated Lint Rule for Resource Management"]
    RC --> A1["Action 1: Add Lint Rule to CI Pipeline"]
    RC --> A2["Action 2: Update Coding Standard Document"]
    RC --> A3["Action 3: Conduct Training for Developers"]
```

## Diagram 5: Cost of Quality Trade-off Curve (Juran Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Investment in Prevention and Appraisal in USD] --> Y[Total Cost of Quality in USD]
    FC[Failure Costs Decreasing] --> Y
    PC[Prevention Plus Appraisal Costs Increasing] --> Y
    Y --> OPT[Optimal Point: Minimum Total CoQ]
    OPT -.-> L["Left of Optimum: Under Investment in Prevention"]
    OPT -.-> R["Right of Optimum: Over Investment in Appraisal"]
```

## Diagram 6: The 4P Quality Challenge Wheel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PeopleLayer[People Dimension]
        P1[Skill Gaps] --> P2[Lack of QA Ownership]
        P2 --> P3[Poor Training]
    end
    subgraph ProcessLayer[Process Dimension]
        PR1[Ad Hoc Lifecycle] --> PR2[Missing Reviews]
        PR2 --> PR3[Weak Traceability]
    end
    subgraph ProductLayer[Product Dimension]
        PD1[Complex Architecture] --> PD2[Legacy Code]
        PD2 --> PD3[Third Party API Risk]
    end
    subgraph ProjectLayer[Project Dimension]
        PJ1[Schedule Pressure] --> PJ2[Scope Creep]
        PJ2 --> PJ3[Budget Cuts to QA]
    end
    PeopleLayer --> Center[Quality Challenge]
    ProcessLayer --> Center
    ProductLayer --> Center
    ProjectLayer --> Center
    Center --> Solutions[Six Layer SLAM Strategy]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Direct conceptual questions. Answer in 4–6 lines with a crisp definition + example.

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC).**

**Model Answer:**

Quality Assurance (QA) is a *process-oriented*, preventive set of activities that defines and enforces the standards, methods, and procedures used throughout the software development lifecycle. Its goal is to ensure that the *right process is followed* so that defects are prevented from being introduced in the first place. Examples: process audits, training, coding standards, and Fagan inspections.

Quality Control (QC) is a *product-oriented*, detective set of activities that executes the software to identify defects in the *finished product*. Its goal is to ensure that the *right product is built*. Examples: test execution, regression testing, and acceptance testing.

**Key Distinction:**

| Aspect | QA | QC |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Process | Product |
| **Approach** | Preventive | Detective |
| **When** | Throughout SDLC | Mostly during testing phase |
| **Example** | Process audit, FTR | Test case execution |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) with its formula and significance.**

**Model Answer:**

DRE is a software quality metric that measures the *effectiveness of the QA process* in finding and fixing defects *before* the product is released to the customer.

$$
DRE = \frac{D_{pre}}{D_{pre} + D_{post}} \times 100\%
$$

Where $D_{pre}$ = defects found before release and $D_{post}$ = defects found after release.

**Significance:** A high DRE (e.g., > 95%) indicates a mature testing process with strong pre-release defect detection, leading to lower post-release maintenance cost and higher customer satisfaction.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question has 2 sub-parts of 7 marks each. Internal choice means the student can answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1
**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`**

**(a)** Explain the **Capability Maturity Model Integration (CMMI)** framework in detail. Describe all **five maturity levels** with their key process areas (KPAs). [7 Marks]

**(b)** Discuss the **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** techniques used in software quality engineering. Illustrate the **5-Why technique** with a real-time example. [7 Marks]

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A(a): CMMI Framework

**Definition:** The CMMI is a process improvement framework developed by the Software Engineering Institute (SEI) at Carnegie Mellon University, and now governed by ISACA. It provides organizations with a structured roadmap to move from *ad-hoc, chaotic* practices to *optimized, continuously improving* practices.

**The Five Maturity Levels:**

**Level 1 — Initial:**
- The process is *chaotic, unpredictable, and hero-based*.
- Success depends on individual effort, not on organizational process.
- Cost, schedule, and quality outcomes are highly unstable.
- *KTU Keyword:* Ad-hoc, firefighting.

**Level 2 — Managed:**
- Projects are *planned, documented, tracked, and controlled*.
- Key Process Areas (KPAs): Requirements Management, Project Planning, Configuration Management, Process and Product Quality Assurance, Measurement and Analysis, Supplier Agreement Management.
- *KTU Keyword:* Repeatable, basic project management discipline.

**Level 3 — Defined:**
- An *organization-wide standard process* is documented and used across all projects.
- KPAs: Organizational Process Focus, Organizational Process Definition, Integrated Product Development, Organizational Training, Decision Analysis and Resolution.
- *KTU Keyword:* Proactive, standardized, process assets.

**Level 4 — Quantitatively Managed:**
- The organization *collects and analyzes quantitative data* to manage and control processes.
- KPAs: Organizational Process Performance, Quantitative Project Management.
- Uses statistical techniques to predict performance.
- *KTU Keyword:* Measured, data-driven, predictable.

**Level 5 — Optimizing:**
- The organization focuses on *continuous process improvement* using quantitative feedback.
- KPAs: Organizational Innovation and Deployment, Causal Analysis and Resolution.
- *KTU Keyword:* Innovation focus, Kaizen-driven.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KPAs:** "**Q**uality and **C**onfiguration **M**anagement **P**lans **S**upplier **M**easurement" (Level 2).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A(b): Root Cause Analysis

**Definition:** RCA is a systematic method for identifying the *underlying cause(s)* of defects or quality problems, going beyond the visible symptom to the process or system-level root.

**Techniques:**

**(1) 5-Why Technique:**
- Developed by **Sakichi Toyoda** of Toyota.
- Procedure: Ask "Why?" iteratively (typically 5 times) until the root cause is exposed.
- *Limitation:* Linear and best for single-cause problems.

**Real-Time Example:**
- **Problem:** Production server crashes every Monday at 9 AM.
- **Why 1?** Memory utilization hits 100% on Mondays.
- **Why 2?** A scheduled batch job runs heavy queries on Sundays, exhausting temp DB space.
- **Why 3?** The job was not performance-tested.
- **Why 4?** No performance testing in the SDLC for scheduled jobs.
- **Why 5?** Test plan template excludes non-functional testing for batch jobs.
- **Root Cause:** Process gap — performance testing is not mandated for scheduled jobs.

**(2) Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram:**
- Categorizes causes into **6M**: *Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment)*.
- Useful for multi-causal problems.

**(3) Pareto Analysis (80/20):**
- Identifies the *vital few* causes that contribute to *most* defects.
- Plotted as a Pareto chart with cumulative percentage line.

**(4) Fault Tree Analysis (FTA):**
- Top-down deductive analysis using **AND / OR gates**.
- Common in safety-critical systems (aerospace, nuclear, medical devices).

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2
**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`**

**(a)** Discuss the **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** framework. Explain Juran's optimal quality cost model with a labeled diagram. [7 Marks]

**(b)** Describe the **Six Sigma DMAIC** methodology in software quality engineering. Compute the DPMO and Sigma Level for a project with the following data: *Defects = 150, Units = 25,000, Opportunities per unit = 5.* [7 Marks]

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B(a): Cost of Quality Framework

**Definition:** The CoQ is a financial framework that quantifies the total cost of achieving and failing to achieve product quality. It was popularized by **Joseph M. Juran** and **Philip Crosby**.

**Four CoQ Categories:**

1. **Prevention Costs:** Training, process documentation, tool acquisition, design reviews.
2. **Appraisal Costs:** Testing, inspections, audits, calibration.
3. **Internal Failure Costs:** Rework, retest, defect triage (defects caught *before* release).
4. **External Failure Costs:** Patches, warranty claims, customer support, reputation loss, legal liability.

**Mathematical Model:**

$$
CoQ_{total} = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\,failure} + C_{external\,failure}
$$

**Juran's Optimal Quality Cost Model:**
- As prevention investment increases, failure costs decrease.
- The *optimal point* is where the **marginal cost of prevention equals the marginal savings from reduced failures**.
- **Left of optimum:** Under-investment in prevention → high failure costs.
- **Right of optimum:** Over-investment in appraisal → diminishing returns.

**Significance:** A healthy organization typically spends:
- *70%* on failure costs (mostly external) if immature.
- *50–60%* on failure costs and *40–50%* on prevention/appraisal if mature.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B(b): Six Sigma DMAIC + Numerical Computation

**Definition:** Six Sigma is a data-driven methodology that aims to reduce defects to **3.4 per million opportunities (DPMO)**, equivalent to a 6-sigma quality level. It uses the **DMAIC** framework:

- **D — Define:** Identify the problem, the customer (CTQ = Critical-to-Quality), and the project scope.
- **M — Measure:** Quantify the current process performance using baseline data.
- **A — Analyze:** Use statistical and root-cause tools (Pareto, Fishbone, regression) to find the vital few causes.
- **I — Improve:** Implement and pilot solutions to address the root causes.
- **C — Control:** Lock in the gains via SOPs, statistical process control (SPC) charts, and audits.

**Numerical Computation:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** Defects = 150, Units = 25,000, Opportunities per unit = 5.

**Step 1:** Total opportunities.

$$
\text{Opportunities} = 25{,}000 \times 5 = 125{,}000
$$

**Step 2:** DPMO.

$$
DPMO = \frac{150 \times 10^{6}}{125{,}000}
$$

$$
DPMO = \frac{150{,}000{,}000}{125{,}000} = 1{,}200
$$

**Step 3:** Yield.

$$
Y = 1 - \frac{1{,}200}{10^{6}} = 1 - 0.0012 = 0.9988
$$

**Step 4:** Find Z from standard normal table: $\Phi^{-1}(0.9988) \approx 3.01$.

**Step 5:** Apply 1.5-sigma shift.

$$
\sigma_{level} = 3.01 + 1.5 = 4.51 \approx 4.5\,\sigma
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{DPMO = 1{,}200,\quad \sigma \approx 4.5}$

**Interpretation:** A 4.5σ process is *better than average industry* (~4σ) but **does not yet meet the Six Sigma (6σ) standard**. The team must continue DMAIC iterations to eliminate variation and push the sigma level above 6.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing QA and QC.** A common 2-mark loss. Always end with a one-line crisp distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1.5-sigma shift** in DPMO-to-Sigma conversion. The raw Z-score is *not* the sigma level; you **must** add 1.5.
> 3. **Mis-stating the CMMI levels.** Students often say "Level 1 is best" — wrong! Level 5 is the highest maturity.
> 4. **Omitting examples in 5-Why.** A theoretical explanation without a worked example gets only *partial* marks.
> 5. **Mixing TQM and Six Sigma.** TQM is a *philosophy*; Six Sigma is a *methodology with DMAIC*. Examiners expect this distinction.
> 6. **Skipping units and labels** in numerical answers. Always write DRE in **%**, DPMO in **defects/million**, and sigma as **σ**.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save This for Last-Day Revision):**

- ✅ **Quality challenges** are systemic, not product-specific. They live in the **4Ps**: People, Process, Product, Project.
- ✅ The **six-layer SLAM strategy** is the KTU-blessed framework: Prevention → Detection → Containment → Diagnosis → Improvement → Cultural Embedding.
- ✅ **DRE** = (Pre-release defects / Total defects) × 100. Target **> 95%**.
- ✅ **Defect Prevention > Defect Detection** — always cheaper to prevent than to find.
- ✅ **CMMI has 5 levels** — Initial (1), Managed (2), Defined (3), Quantitatively Managed (4), Optimizing (5). Mnemonic: "**I Make Delicious Quality Oats**".
- ✅ **TQM** = philosophy of *everyone owns quality*; **Six Sigma** = data-driven *methodology* with DMAIC.
- ✅ **Six Sigma target = 3.4 DPMO**, equivalent to **6σ** (with the **1.5-sigma shift** convention).
- ✅ **5-Why** = iterative "Why?" questioning to reach the root cause. **Fishbone** = 6M categorization. **Pareto** = 80/20 prioritization.
- ✅ **Cost of Quality** has 4 components: Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure. **External failure is the most expensive.**
- ✅ **Juran's optimum** = the point where prevention/appraisal cost equals the savings from reduced failure cost.
- ✅ **QA is process-focused (prevention)**; **QC is product-focused (detection)**. Memorize this distinction.
- ✅ **Fagan Inspection** is a 7-step formal review: Planning → Overview → Prep → Meeting → Rework → Follow-up → Causal Analysis.
- ✅ **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** uses **AND/OR gates** for top-down safety analysis in safety-critical domains.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Significance of National Quality Awards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT747 - SOFTWARE TESTING AND QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Significance of National Quality Awards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **National Quality Awards (NQAs)** are prestigious honors conferred by national governments, apex industry bodies, or internationally recognized institutions upon organizations that demonstrate exceptional, sustained performance in **Quality Management (QM)**, **Total Quality Management (TQM)** practices, and continuous excellence in their products, services, and operational processes.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *Software Testing and Quality Assurance*, National Quality Awards are formal recognition mechanisms that act as **national benchmarks** for assessing an organization's maturity in implementing world-class quality frameworks. They provide a structured, criteria-based model against which companies (including software and IT firms) can self-assess, identify gaps, and pursue excellence.

The most prominent National Quality Awards studied under this module are:

- **Deming Prize** (Japan, established 1951)
- **Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award (MBNQA)** (USA, established 1987)
- **European Quality Award (EQA) / EFQM Excellence Award** (Europe, established 1992)
- **Rajiv Gandhi National Quality Award (RGNQA)** / **Indian National Quality Award** (India, established by BIS)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "National Quality Oscars":**
> 
> Imagine you are a software company, and the government announces an annual "**National Quality Awards**" — much like the Oscars for the film industry. To win, your company cannot just submit one good product. Instead, you must demonstrate that you excel across **seven (or more) critical dimensions** — leadership, strategy, customer focus, measurement, workforce, operations, and results. 
> 
> The **awards themselves** are the trophies, but the real prize is the **journey of self-evaluation** against a world-class yardstick. Just as athletes train harder when competing for Olympic gold, organizations improve faster when measured against national quality standards. The award, therefore, is both a **destination** and a **roadmap**.

## 1.3 Core Terminology

| **Term** | **Standard Definition (KTU-aligned)** |
|---|---|
| **Quality Award** | Formal recognition given to organizations demonstrating excellence in quality practices. |
| **Self-Assessment** | A comprehensive review by an organization against an established quality model/criteria. |
| **Benchmarking** | The practice of comparing business processes and performance metrics to industry best practices. |
| **TQM** | **Total Quality Management** — a management philosophy focused on customer satisfaction and continuous improvement involving all members of the organization. |
| **Criteria** | The structured categories (e.g., Leadership, Strategy, Customers) used to evaluate applicants. |
| **Site Visit** | On-site verification of an applicant's claims by a panel of examiners. |
| **Feedback Report** | A document provided to applicants highlighting strengths and opportunities for improvement (OFIs). |

## 1.4 The Strategic Role of National Quality Awards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):**
> National Quality Awards are NOT merely trophies. They serve as:
> 1. **Catalysts for TQM adoption** at a national level.
> 2. **Public benchmarks** of quality excellence.
> 3. **Educational tools** that disseminate best practices.
> 4. **Vehicles for national economic competitiveness**.

The significance of these awards extends far beyond the ceremony — they create a **virtuous cycle of improvement** that lifts the entire industry.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do National Quality Awards Exist? — The Underlying Philosophy

National Quality Awards emerged in the post-World War II era when countries like **Japan** needed to rebuild their industrial reputation. The philosophy rests on **three pillars**:

1. **Recognition** of best-in-class organizations.
2. **Replication** of their best practices across the industry.
3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** by every participating organization, regardless of winning status.

## 2.2 Major National Quality Awards — Structured Analysis

### 2.2.1 The Deming Prize (Japan — 1951)

- **Established by:** Union of Japanese Scientists and Engineers (JUSE) in honor of **Dr. W. Edwards Deming**.
- **Purpose:** To honor companies that have achieved distinctive results through the application of **Company-Wide Quality Control (CWQC)**.
- **Focus Areas:** Statistical Quality Control, TQM, and employee involvement.
- **Categories:** Deming Prize (individuals/companies), Deming Application Prize, Deming Grand Prize.
- **Significance:** The **oldest and most respected** quality award globally; it single-handedly revived Japanese industry post-WWII.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Deming Prize evaluation is based on the *Deming Prize Examination Standards*, which include categories such as: Policy and Planning, Organization, Education and Dissemination, Information, Analysis, Standardization, Control, Quality Assurance, Effects, and Future Plans.

### 2.2.2 The Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award (MBNQA — USA, 1987)

- **Established by:** U.S. Congress, named after former Secretary of Commerce **Malcolm Baldrige**.
- **Purpose:** To recognize U.S. companies for outstanding quality and performance excellence.
- **Seven Evaluation Categories:**
   1. **Leadership** (120 points)
   2. **Strategic Planning** (85 points)
   3. **Customer Focus** (85 points)
   4. **Measurement, Analysis, and Knowledge Management** (90 points)
   5. **Workforce Focus** (85 points)
   6. **Operations Focus** (85 points)
   7. **Results** (450 points)
- **Total:** 1000 points.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance for Software Companies:** The Baldrige framework has a dedicated **Information & Communications (I/C) sector** category, making it highly relevant to IT and software firms.

### 2.2.3 European Quality Award (EQA) / EFQM Excellence Model (Europe — 1992)

- **Established by:** **European Foundation for Quality Management (EFQM)**.
- **Purpose:** To drive sustainable excellence in European organizations.
- **Model Structure:** **9 Criteria** divided into **5 Enablers** and **4 Results**.
   - **Enablers:** Leadership, People, Strategy, Partnerships & Resources, Processes, Products & Services.
   - **Results:** People Results, Customer Results, Society Results, Business Results.
- **Significance:** Introduced the **RADAR Logic** (Results, Approach, Deploy, Assess, Refine) for assessment.

### 2.2.4 Rajiv Gandhi National Quality Award (RGNQA — India)

- **Established by:** **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** under the Ministry of Consumer Affairs.
- **Purpose:** To create awareness of quality in industry and encourage Indian companies to benchmark against global standards.
- **Categories:** Large Scale Manufacturing, Small Scale Manufacturing, Service Sector (including IT/Software), Education, Healthcare, and Government.
- **Criteria:** Adopted from the **Baldrige framework** with India-specific adaptations (e.g., emphasis on Make in India, Atmanirbhar Bharat).
- **Significance:** The **highest national quality award in India**, providing global recognition to Indian organizations.

## 2.3 The Common Significance Framework

All major National Quality Awards share **seven universal significance dimensions**:

| **#** | **Significance Dimension** | **Explanation** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **National Productivity Boost** | Awards create a measurable rise in GDP-defining productivity across industries. |
| 2 | **Best Practice Dissemination** | Winning practices are published as case studies, propagating excellence. |
| 3 | **Benchmarking Tool** | Provides a structured yardstick for self-assessment. |
| 4 | **TQM Adoption Driver** | Encourages firms to formally implement TQM, Six Sigma, and CMMI practices. |
| 5 | **Customer Trust** | Winning companies enjoy higher customer trust and brand equity. |
| 6 | **Employee Motivation** | Recognition boosts morale, retention, and engagement. |
| 7 | **Global Competitiveness** | Aligns national industries with international excellence standards. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For numerical analysis of award scoring (e.g., Baldrige), the following simple weighted-score model is used:

$$
S_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

Where:
- $S_{\text{total}}$ is the total score (out of 1000 for Baldrige).
- $w_i$ is the weight of category $i$ (e.g., 450 for Results in MBNQA).
- $s_i$ is the score achieved in category $i$ (on a scale of 0 to 100\%).
- $n$ is the number of evaluation categories.

**Example Computation:**

$$
S_{\text{total}} = (120 \cdot 0.80) + (85 \cdot 0.75) + (85 \cdot 0.70) + (90 \cdot 0.85) + (85 \cdot 0.78) + (85 \cdot 0.82) + (450 \cdot 0.90)
$$

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 96 + 63.75 + 59.5 + 76.5 + 66.3 + 69.7 + 405 = 836.75 \text{ points}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Scoring Threshold (MBNQA):** Generally, recipients score in the **top decile** of applicants, typically above **650–700 points** out of 1000.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **For IT/Software firms:** Baldrige I/C sector helps align with CMMI Level 5 and ISO 9001.
- **For manufacturing firms:** Deming Prize and RGNQA drive Statistical Process Control (SPC) adoption.
- **For service industries:** EFQM Excellence Model is widely used in BPOs, banks, and ITES companies in India.
- **For government bodies:** RGNQA evaluates PSUs and public service organizations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Significance Score Model

The Significance Score $S_{\text{sig}}$ of a National Quality Award to a country's industrial ecosystem can be expressed as a **weighted multi-factor index**. Let us derive it step-by-step.

### Step 1: Identify the Key Significance Factors

From the seven universal significance dimensions identified in Section 2.3, we consolidate them into **four measurable factors** for analytical purposes:

- $F_1$ = **National Productivity Boost**
- $F_2$ = **Best Practice Dissemination**
- $F_3$ = **TQM Adoption Rate**
- $F_4$ = **Global Competitiveness Index**

### Step 2: Assign Normalized Weights

By convention (and aligned with the Baldrige weighting philosophy), we assign:

- $w_1 = 0.30$ (Productivity)
- $w_2 = 0.20$ (Dissemination)
- $w_3 = 0.25$ (TQM Adoption)
- $w_4 = 0.25$ (Global Competitiveness)

Verification of weight normalization:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i = 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.25 + 0.25 = 1.00
$$

### Step 3: Define Individual Factor Scores

Each factor is scored on a normalized scale from 0 to 1. For a hypothetical country *X*:

- $f_1 = 0.85$ (Productivity)
- $f_2 = 0.70$ (Dissemination)
- $f_3 = 0.80$ (TQM Adoption)
- $f_4 = 0.75$ (Global Competitiveness)

### Step 4: Compute the Significance Score

$$
S_{\text{sig}} = w_1 \cdot f_1 + w_2 \cdot f_2 + w_3 \cdot f_3 + w_4 \cdot f_4
$$

Substituting values:

$$
S_{\text{sig}} = (0.30 \cdot 0.85) + (0.20 \cdot 0.70) + (0.25 \cdot 0.80) + (0.25 \cdot 0.75)
$$

$$
S_{\text{sig}} = 0.255 + 0.140 + 0.200 + 0.1875
$$

$$
S_{\text{sig}} = 0.7825
$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$
S_{\text{sig}} \cdot 100 = 78.25\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A Significance Score above **75%** indicates that the National Quality Award program is **highly effective** in driving industrial excellence.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Award Comparison Table

| **Criterion** | **Deming Prize** | **Baldrige Award (MBNQA)** | **EFQM / EQA** | **RGNQA (India)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Country** | Japan | USA | Europe (EU) | India |
| **Year Established** | 1951 | 1987 | 1992 | 1991 |
| **Awarding Body** | JUSE | NIST (US Dept. of Commerce) | EFQM | BIS (Govt. of India) |
| **Number of Criteria** | 10 (Examination Standards) | 7 Categories | 9 Criteria (5 Enablers + 4 Results) | Adapted from Baldrige |
| **Max Score** | Not strictly numerical | 1000 points | 1000 points | 1000 points |
| **Software/IT Sector Coverage** | Indirect (TQM-based) | Yes (Dedicated I/C Category) | Yes (Generic, sector-neutral) | Yes (Dedicated Service Sector Category) |
| **Site Visit Required** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Feedback Report** | Yes | Yes (Detailed OFIs) | Yes (RADAR-based) | Yes |
| **Primary Focus** | Statistical QC, CWQC, Kaizen | Results-driven performance excellence | Sustainable excellence via Enablers & Results | Indian industry alignment with global standards |
| **Eligibility for Foreign Firms** | Yes (since 1984) | No (US firms only) | Yes (any EFQM member country) | No (Indian organizations only) |

## 3.3 Detailed Significance Flow — Analytical Steps

Let us walk through **how a National Quality Award creates significance** in a step-by-step logical flow:

**Step 1: Awareness Creation**
- Government announces the award → industry becomes aware of quality criteria.

**Step 2: Self-Assessment**
- Companies download the criteria → conduct internal gap analysis.

**Step 3: Process Improvement**
- Gaps identified → TQM, Six Sigma, CMMI, ISO implementation initiated.

**Step 4: Application & Documentation**
- Company submits a structured application (often 50+ pages for Baldrige).

**Step 5: Independent Evaluation**
- Panel of experts scores the application → site visit is conducted.

**Step 6: Recognition & Dissemination**
- Winners are celebrated → case studies are published for industry learning.

**Step 7: National Productivity Impact**
- Cumulative improvements → measurable national-level productivity gains.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **Deming Prize** is awarded annually to the **same company for at most two consecutive years** in certain categories — this forces the organization to continuously innovate rather than rely on past glory.

## 3.4 Quantitative Example — Award Eligibility Threshold

A company applies for the **Baldrige Award**. The minimum threshold score to enter the **Site Visit** stage is generally **~450 points**. Let us compute how much each category must score (assuming proportional performance):

$$
\text{Avg. Category Score} = \frac{\text{Threshold}}{\text{Total Points}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Avg. Category Score} = \frac{450}{1000} \times 100 = 45\%
$$

So on average, each of the 7 categories must score at least **45%** for the application to advance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Architecture of a National Quality Award Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Government Apex Body e.g. BIS, JUSE, NIST] --> nodeB[Award Announcement and Criteria Publication]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Industry Awareness and Self Assessment]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Gap Analysis Against Criteria]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Process Improvement TQM Six Sigma CMMI]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Application Submission and Documentation]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Independent Examiner Panel Review]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Site Visit Required}
    nodeH -->|Yes| nodeI[On Site Verification]
    nodeH -->|No| nodeJ[Final Scoring]
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK{Award Threshold Met}
    nodeK -->|Yes| nodeL[Award Conferred Recognition Ceremony]
    nodeK -->|No| nodeM[Feedback Report with OFIs]
    nodeL --> nodeN[Case Study Publication National Dissemination]
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO[Continuous Improvement Cycle]
    nodeO --> nodeC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This flowchart illustrates the closed-loop nature of National Quality Award programs. The **Feedback Report (OFIs)** is a critical instrument — even non-winners receive actionable insights, ensuring **continuous improvement** at the industry level.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Comparative Mapping of Major Quality Awards

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Japan
        JP[Deming Prize 1951 JUSE Statistical QC]
    end
    subgraph USA
        USA1[Baldrige Award 1987 NIST 7 Categories 1000 Points]
    end
    subgraph Europe
        EU[EFQM EQA 1992 9 Criteria RADAR Logic]
    end
    subgraph India
        IN[RGNQA BIS Adapted from Baldrige Service Sector Included]
    end
    JP --> COMMON{Common Significance Drivers}
    USA1 --> COMMON
    EU --> COMMON
    IN --> COMMON
    COMMON --> SIG1[National Productivity Boost]
    COMMON --> SIG2[Best Practice Dissemination]
    COMMON --> SIG3[TQM Adoption Catalyst]
    COMMON --> SIG4[Global Competitiveness]
    COMMON --> SIG5[Customer Trust]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Significance Impact Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Significance of National Quality Awards] --> L1[Organizational Level Impact]
    ROOT --> L2[Industrial Level Impact]
    ROOT --> L3[National Level Impact]
    L1 --> L1a[Improved Quality Management Systems]
    L1 --> L1b[Higher Employee Morale and Retention]
    L1 --> L1c[Enhanced Customer Trust]
    L2 --> L2a[Standardized Best Practices Across Sector]
    L2 --> L2b[Rise in TQM and Six Sigma Adoption]
    L2 --> L2c[Benchmarking Culture]
    L3 --> L3a[National Productivity GDP Growth]
    L3 --> L3b[Global Trade Competitiveness]
    L3 --> L3c[Innovation and R&D Acceleration]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Functional Insight:** The above diagram maps a **three-tier impact hierarchy** — moving from **micro (organization)** → **meso (industry)** → **macro (nation)**. This is a frequently tested concept in KTU Module 3 examinations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define National Quality Award. Mention any two examples. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **National Quality Award** is a prestigious recognition conferred by a national government or apex industry body upon organizations demonstrating excellence in quality management practices, customer satisfaction, and continuous improvement. 
**Examples:** (1) **Deming Prize** (Japan, 1951), (2) **Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award** (USA, 1987), (3) **Rajiv Gandhi National Quality Award** (India). *(Any two with year and country = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2.** List the seven evaluation categories of the Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The seven MBNQA categories are: (1) Leadership, (2) Strategic Planning, (3) Customer Focus, (4) Measurement, Analysis, and Knowledge Management, (5) Workforce Focus, (6) Operations Focus, (7) Results. *(1/2 mark each, 7 × 0.5 = 3.5, round to 3)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** Discuss in detail the significance of National Quality Awards with special reference to the Deming Prize and the Baldrige Award. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the Deming Prize — its origin, criteria, and significance.

**Model Solution:**

The **Deming Prize** was established in **1951** by the **Union of Japanese Scientists and Engineers (JUSE)** to honor **Dr. W. Edwards Deming** for his contributions to Japanese industrial revival post-WWII. It is the **oldest** national quality award in the world.

**Criteria (Examination Standards):**
1. Policy and Planning
2. Organization and its Operation
3. Education and Dissemination of Quality Concepts
4. Information Collection, Analysis, and Use
5. Standardization
6. Control
7. Quality Assurance
8. Effects (Results)
9. Future Plans
10. **Company-Wide Quality Control (CWQC)** implementation

**Significance:**
- Revitalized Japanese industry post-WWII.
- Promoted **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** practices.
- Encouraged **employee participation** at all levels.
- Inspired the creation of similar awards globally (Baldrige, EFQM, RGNQA).

*(7 marks: 2 marks origin, 3 marks criteria, 2 marks significance)*

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the Baldrige Award criteria and its significance for the software industry.

**Model Solution:**

The **Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award (MBNQA)** was established in **1987** by the U.S. Congress and is administered by **NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology)**. It has **seven categories** totalling **1000 points**:

| **Category** | **Points** |
|---|---|
| Leadership | 120 |
| Strategic Planning | 85 |
| Customer Focus | 85 |
| Measurement, Analysis, Knowledge Management | 90 |
| Workforce Focus | 85 |
| Operations Focus | 85 |
| Results | 450 |
| **Total** | **1000** |

**Significance for the Software Industry:**
1. A dedicated **Information & Communications (I/C) sector** category exists.
2. Aligns with **CMMI, ISO 9001, and ITIL** frameworks.
3. Encourages **data-driven decision-making** in software firms.
4. Provides a structured path to **performance excellence** beyond mere defect metrics.
5. Boosts **customer trust** in B2B/B2C software products.

*(7 marks: 2 marks introduction, 3 marks criteria with table, 2 marks software industry significance)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** Compare the Deming Prize, Baldrige Award, and the Rajiv Gandhi National Quality Award (RGNQA) on the basis of origin, criteria, and national significance. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compare the origin and criteria of the three awards.

**Model Solution:**

| **Aspect** | **Deming Prize** | **Baldrige Award** | **RGNQA (India)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Year** | 1951 | 1987 | 1991 |
| **Awarding Body** | JUSE (Japan) | NIST (USA) | BIS (India) |
| **Named After** | Dr. W. Edwards Deming | Malcolm Baldrige (US Sec. of Commerce) | Rajiv Gandhi (Former PM of India) |
| **Number of Criteria** | 10 (Examination Standards) | 7 Categories | Adapted from Baldrige (Indian context) |
| **Scoring System** | Qualitative (Pass/Fail grades) | Quantitative (1000 points) | Quantitative (1000 points) |
| **Sector Focus** | Generic (Manufacturing-led) | Sector-specific (I/C, Manufacturing, Service, etc.) | Service Sector (incl. IT/Software) explicitly included |

*(7 marks: 1 mark tabular structure, 4 marks for populated cells, 2 marks for analysis)*

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the national significance of these awards.

**Model Solution:**

**Universal Significance Dimensions:**
1. **National Productivity Boost** — Recognized companies often show 20–30% productivity gains.
2. **Best Practice Dissemination** — Case studies fuel industry-wide learning.
3. **TQM Adoption Catalyst** — Accelerates adoption of TQM, Six Sigma, Kaizen.
4. **Global Competitiveness** — Aligns national firms with international standards.
5. **Customer Trust & Brand Equity** — Enhances market reputation.

**Country-Specific Significance:**
- **Deming Prize:** Revitalized post-war Japan; established Japan as a global quality benchmark.
- **Baldrige Award:** Propelled the U.S. quality movement; expanded to healthcare, education, and non-profits in 1999.
- **RGNQA:** Recognizes Indian organizations, encouraging Make-in-India excellence and Atmanirbhar Bharat by aligning Indian firms with global benchmarks.

**Conclusion:** National Quality Awards serve as **strategic instruments of industrial policy**, not merely recognition programs. They are **vital catalysts** for sustainable national growth.

*(7 marks: 3 marks universal dimensions, 2 marks country-specific, 2 marks conclusion)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Naming the award without the year/country** — Always mention the year of establishment and awarding body. Partial credit is given only when the award name is correctly tied to its origin.
> 2. **Confusing Deming Prize with Deming Cycle (PDCA)** — They are different. The **Prize** is an award; the **Cycle** is a methodology.
> 3. **Listing categories without their points (for Baldrige)** — Examiners award partial credit only if the **7 categories with their point allocations** are both stated.
> 4. **Ignoring software/IT context** — Since this course is **Software Testing and QA**, examiners expect at least **one explicit connection** to the IT/software industry in your answer.
> 5. **Writing generic answers** — Avoid generic statements like "Awards motivate employees." Use **domain-specific terms** like "TQM adoption," "RADAR logic," "OFI (Opportunities for Improvement)," and "statistical quality control."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Significance of National Quality Awards:**
> 
> - **Definition:** National Quality Awards = national-level recognitions for organizational quality excellence.
> - **Deming Prize (Japan, 1951):** Oldest award; emphasizes Statistical QC and CWQC; 10 examination standards.
> - **Malcolm Baldrige (USA, 1987):** 7 categories, 1000 points; dedicated I/C sector for software firms.
> - **EFQM / EQA (Europe, 1992):** 9 criteria (5 Enablers + 4 Results); uses RADAR logic.
> - **RGNQA (India, 1991):** India's highest quality award; awarded by BIS; adopted from Baldrige.
> - **Seven Universal Significance Dimensions:** Productivity, Dissemination, TQM Adoption, Benchmarking, Customer Trust, Employee Motivation, Global Competitiveness.
> - **Key Formula:** $S_{\text{total}} = \sum w_i \cdot s_i$ (for weighted scoring).
> - **Software Relevance:** Baldrige I/C sector + RGNQA Service Sector category are the most relevant to IT firms.
> - **Baldrige Threshold:** Site Visit eligibility requires ~450/1000 points.
> - **Examiners Reward:** Linking answers to **TQM, Six Sigma, CMMI, ISO 9001, Kaizen, RADAR, OFI** terminology.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Significance of National Quality Awards**  
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regression Testing and Test Management
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT747 - SOFTWARE TESTING AND QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Regression Testing and Test Management

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Regression Testing

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Regression Testing** is a type of software testing that verifies whether a software application still functions correctly after any code change, update, enhancement, bug fix, or configuration change. The primary objective is to ensure that the existing, previously validated functionality has *not regressed* (i.e., broken or degraded) due to the introduction of new changes.

According to the **ISTQB (International Software Testing Qualifications Board) Certified Tester Foundation Level Syllabus v4.0**, regression testing is defined as:

> *"Testing of a previously tested program following modification to ensure that defects have not been introduced or uncovered in unchanged areas of the software, as a result of the changes made."*

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Renovation Test**
>
> Imagine you live in a three-story house. You renovate the kitchen on the ground floor (a "code change"). After the renovation, the lights in the upstairs bedroom stop working because the electrician accidentally cut a shared circuit (a "regression"). 
>
> **Regression testing** is the act of going back to check *every* room, switch, and appliance after the renovation to confirm that nothing else broke. You don't re-renovate; you simply *verify the old stuff still works* with the new stuff.
>
> In software, when a developer fixes a bug in the login module, a careless change might break the payment module. Regression testing re-runs old, known-good test cases to catch these collateral damages.

### Key Characteristics

- **Selective Re-execution**: It does not re-run the *entire* test suite blindly; it intelligently selects impacted test cases.
- **Iterative**: Performed in every iteration of an Agile/DevOps lifecycle.
- **Automation-Friendly**: Heavily reliant on automation frameworks (Selenium, JUnit, TestNG, etc.) to reduce manual effort.
- **Risk-Mitigation**: Acts as a safety net against side-effects of change.

### Why Regression Testing is Required

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why do we need it? (KTU Board Favourite Question)**
> 1. **Changes are inevitable**: Requirements evolve, defects get fixed, environments get upgraded.
> 2. **Unintended side effects**: A fix in one module can ripple to another (the *ripple effect*).
> 3. **Confidence**: It builds confidence that the system remains production-ready after each release.
> 4. **Maintenance cost reduction**: Detecting defects early in CI/CD pipelines is far cheaper than post-release.

---

## 1.2 Test Management

### Formal Definition

**Test Management** is the process of organizing, controlling, and ensuring visibility of the testing activities required to deliver a quality software product. It encompasses the planning, estimation, monitoring, and reporting of testing efforts across the entire Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC).

**KTU 2024 Definition (per ISTQB-aligned syllabus)**:
> *"Test management involves the activities and responsibilities required to plan, schedule, organize, control, and report on testing activities, ensuring alignment with project objectives and stakeholder requirements."*

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Movie Director**
>
> A movie director does not act in every scene but *manages* the actors, script, budget, schedule, and quality. If the script changes mid-shoot (regression!), the director must re-shoot impacted scenes (regression testing), track daily progress (test monitoring), manage the budget (test estimation), and report to the producer (test reporting).
>
> **Test Manager** is the "director" of testing. They don't execute all test cases but orchestrate the entire testing process to ensure the final "movie" (the software) meets quality standards.

### Core Activities of Test Management

| # | Activity | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Test Planning** | Defining the test strategy, scope, resources, schedule |
| 2 | **Test Organization** | Structuring the test team (roles, responsibilities) |
| 3 | **Test Estimation** | Calculating effort, time, cost required for testing |
| 4 | **Test Monitoring & Control** | Tracking progress vs. plan; taking corrective action |
| 5 | **Test Reporting** | Communicating test status to stakeholders |
| 6 | **Configuration Management** | Managing test artifacts (test cases, scripts, data) |
| 7 | **Risk & Issue Management** | Identifying and mitigating testing risks |
| 8 | **Defect Management** | Tracking defects from discovery to closure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight**: Test Management is governed by the **ISTQB Test Management Process**, which is divided into three core groups: **Test Organization, Test Planning & Estimation, and Test Monitoring & Control**.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Regression Testing — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 When is Regression Testing Performed?

Regression testing is triggered by:

1. **Bug Fixes**: A defect is resolved in the source code.
2. **New Features**: Addition of new functional modules.
3. **Performance Optimization**: Code refactoring to improve speed.
4. **Environment Changes**: OS upgrade, database migration, library update.
5. **Configuration Changes**: Updates to environment variables, API endpoints.
6. **Integration with New Components**: Plugging in a new third-party service.

### 2.1.2 Types of Regression Testing

> [!NOTE]
> This is a high-yield KTU topic. Memorize each type with one-line definition.

| Type | Description | When Used |
|------|-------------|-----------|
| **Unit Regression** | Testing a single, isolated unit after a code change | During developer-level unit testing |
| **Partial Regression** | Testing a subset of the application impacted by the change | When a new module integrates with an existing one |
| **Complete (Full) Regression** | Testing the *entire* application from scratch | Before major releases, after major refactoring |
| **Progressive Regression** | Testing new test cases alongside existing ones; new test cases are designed for new functionality | In iterative Agile sprints |
| **Corrective Regression** | Re-running existing test cases when no new changes are introduced; to verify pre-existing functionality | When only minor bug fixes are made |
| **Selective Regression** | Selecting a subset of test cases from the test suite to run, based on impact analysis | To save time and resources in CI/CD |

### 2.1.3 Regression Test Selection Techniques

The crux of regression testing is *selecting which tests to re-run*. Five major techniques exist:

#### A. Retest All
- Re-execute the **complete** test suite.
- **Pros**: Maximum coverage, highest confidence.
- **Cons**: Time-consuming, expensive, redundant.

#### B. Regression Test Selection (RTS)
- Select a **subset** of test cases relevant to the changed code.
- Uses **code coverage**, **dependency analysis**, or **fault-exposure** metrics.
- **KTU Formula**: 

$$
T_{selected} = \{ t_i \in T \mid C(t_i) \cap C(\Delta) \neq \emptyset \}
$$

where $T$ is the total test suite, $C(t_i)$ is the code covered by test $t_i$, and $C(\Delta)$ is the changed code.

#### C. Test Case Prioritization
- **Reorder** test cases so that high-priority / high-fault-detecting tests run first.
- Does not discard tests, only sequences them optimally.
- **KTU Formula** (Average Percentage of Faults Detected — APFD):

$$
APFD = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (n - i) \cdot f_i}{n \cdot m} + \frac{1}{2n}
$$

where $n$ = number of test cases, $m$ = total faults, $f_i$ = faults found by test $i$.

#### D. Hybrid Approach
- Combines **RTS + Prioritization**.
- First select relevant tests, then prioritize them.

#### E. Safe Regression
- Re-runs only those tests that are *guaranteed* to detect any regression.
- Mathematically backed; very expensive to compute but highly reliable.

### 2.1.4 Regression Testing Tools (KTU Expectation)

| Tool | Type | Best For |
|------|------|----------|
| **Selenium** | Open-source | Web UI regression |
| **QTP/UFT (Micro Focus)** | Commercial | Cross-platform functional |
| **TestComplete** | Commercial | Desktop, web, mobile |
| **JUnit / TestNG** | Open-source | Java unit regression |
| **PyTest / Robot Framework** | Open-source | Python-based regression |
| **Microsoft Coded UI** | Commercial | .NET applications |
| **Watir** | Open-source | Ruby-based web regression |

### 2.1.5 Regression Testing in Agile / DevOps

In modern pipelines, regression testing is automated and integrated into **CI/CD**:

```
Developer commits code → CI tool (Jenkins) triggers build → Automated regression suite runs → Pass/Fail reported → Deploy to staging
```

This is called **Continuous Testing**.

---

## 2.2 Test Management — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Test Organization

The test team can be organized in three ways:

| Structure | Description | Pros | Cons |
|-----------|-------------|------|------|
| **Independent Test Team** | A separate QA team, no developers | Objective, unbiased | Communication gaps |
| **Developer-Tester Combined** | Developers also test | Better understanding | Conflict of interest |
| **Outsourced Testing** | External vendor performs testing | Specialized skills, cost-effective | Confidentiality, control issues |

### 2.2.2 Test Planning

The **Master Test Plan (MTP)** and **Level Test Plans (LTP)** are the key deliverables. Per **ISTQB**, a test plan includes:

- **Test Items** (what to test)
- **Test Objectives** (what to achieve)
- **Test Strategy** (how to test)
- **Entry & Exit Criteria** (when to start, when to stop)
- **Resources** (who, what tools)
- **Schedule** (timeline)
- **Risks & Contingencies**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Entry Criteria vs Exit Criteria (KTU Hot Question)**
> - **Entry Criteria**: Conditions that *must* be satisfied *before* testing begins (e.g., code compiled, test environment ready, test cases reviewed).
> - **Exit Criteria**: Conditions that *must* be satisfied *before* testing is declared complete (e.g., 95% test cases executed, all critical defects fixed, no Sev-1 bugs open).

### 2.2.3 Test Estimation Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU ESE.

| Technique | Full Name | Concept |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| **PERT** | Program Evaluation & Review Technique | Weighted average: $\frac{O + 4M + P}{6}$ |
| **UCP** | Use Case Points | Estimates from use case complexity |
| **WBS** | Work Breakdown Structure | Decompose tasks, estimate each |
| **Expert Judgment** | Delphi Technique | Consensus from experts |
| **AD hoc / Analogy** | Past project comparison | Compare with similar prior projects |

#### PERT Formula (Detailed)

$$
E = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}
$$

where $O$ = Optimistic, $M$ = Most Likely, $P$ = Pessimistic estimate.

**Standard Deviation**:

$$
\sigma = \frac{P - O}{6}
$$

### 2.2.4 Test Monitoring & Control

**Metrics used in monitoring**:

| Metric | Formula | Purpose |
|--------|---------|---------|
| **Test Case Execution Rate** | $\frac{\text{Test Cases Executed}}{\text{Total Test Cases}} \times 100$ | Progress tracking |
| **Defect Density** | $\frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{KLOC}}$ | Code quality indicator |
| **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | $\frac{\text{Defects found before release}}{\text{Defects found before release + Defects after release}} \times 100$ | Testing effectiveness |
| **Test Coverage** | $\frac{\text{Requirements covered by tests}}{\text{Total requirements}} \times 100$ | Completeness |
| **Defect Leakage** | $\frac{\text{Defects found in UAT}}{\text{Defects found in testing + UAT}} \times 100$ | Quality of testing |
| **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | Prevention + Appraisal + Failure costs | Financial impact |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Students often confuse **Defect Density** (per KLOC) with **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)**. Remember: **Density measures quality of code; DRE measures quality of testing process.**

### 2.2.5 Configuration Management in Testing

Configuration management ensures that **test artifacts** (test plans, test cases, test scripts, test data, test results) are properly versioned, controlled, and traceable. Tools include **Git, SVN, Rational ClearCase**.

### 2.2.6 Risk-Based Testing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU high-priority topic. **Risk = Probability × Impact**.

$$
Risk = P(\text{failure}) \times I(\text{impact})
$$

Test effort is prioritized based on risk. Higher risk → more thorough testing.

### 2.2.7 Defect Management Process

The standard defect lifecycle:

```
New → Assigned → Open → Fixed → Verified → Closed
            ↓
         Rejected / Deferred / Duplicate
```

**Defect Severity vs Priority**:

- **Severity**: Technical impact on the system (Critical, Major, Minor, Trivial).
- **Priority**: Business urgency to fix (High, Medium, Low).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite**: A high-severity defect can have low priority (e.g., a typo in a rarely-used help menu) and vice-versa.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: APFD Metric for Test Case Prioritization

The **Average Percentage of Faults Detected (APFD)** metric quantifies how quickly a prioritized test suite detects faults. Higher APFD = better prioritization.

### Step 1: Define the Setup

Let there be $n$ test cases in the prioritized order $T = \{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n\}$.
Let there be $m$ total faults in the system.
Let $TF_i$ = position of the test case in the prioritized order $T$ that detects the $i$-th fault (for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$).

### Step 2: Construct the Fault Detection Curve

Plot the cumulative number of faults detected as tests are run. The **area under this curve** represents the effectiveness of the prioritized suite.

### Step 3: Express the Normalized Area

The APFD is defined as the area under the fault-detection curve, normalized to the range $[0, 1]$:

$$
APFD = 1 - \frac{TF_1 + TF_2 + \ldots + TF_m}{n \cdot m} + \frac{1}{2n}
$$

### Step 4: Algebraic Expansion

Let $S = \sum_{i=1}^{m} TF_i$. Then:

$$
APFD = 1 - \frac{S}{n \cdot m} + \frac{1}{2n}
$$

### Step 5: Combine Fractions

Find a common denominator $n \cdot m$ for the last two terms:

$$
APFD = \frac{n \cdot m}{n \cdot m} - \frac{S}{n \cdot m} + \frac{m}{2 \cdot n \cdot m}
$$

$$
APFD = \frac{n \cdot m - S + \frac{m}{2}}{n \cdot m}
$$

### Step 6: Final Compact Form

$$
APFD = \frac{n \cdot m - S + \frac{m}{2}}{n \cdot m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation**: $APFD = 1$ means all faults are detected by the very first test case (perfect prioritization). $APFD = 0$ means no faults are detected (poor prioritization). Realistic values lie between **0.6 and 0.95**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: APFD Calculation

**Problem**: A test suite has $n = 10$ test cases, and there are $m = 5$ faults in the system. After running the prioritized tests, the faults are detected at test positions: $TF_1 = 1, TF_2 = 3, TF_3 = 5, TF_4 = 7, TF_5 = 10$.

### Step 1: Compute the Sum $S$

$$
S = TF_1 + TF_2 + TF_3 + TF_4 + TF_5
$$

$$
S = 1 + 3 + 5 + 7 + 10 = 26
$$

### Step 2: Substitute into APFD Formula

$$
APFD = 1 - \frac{S}{n \cdot m} + \frac{1}{2n}
$$

$$
APFD = 1 - \frac{26}{10 \times 5} + \frac{1}{2 \times 10}
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate Each Term

$$
APFD = 1 - \frac{26}{50} + \frac{1}{20}
$$

$$
APFD = 1 - 0.52 + 0.05
$$

$$
APFD = 0.53
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

An **APFD of 0.53** indicates a *moderate* prioritization effectiveness. Ideally, the test team should re-prioritize to achieve APFD > 0.8.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: PERT Estimation

**Problem**: For a test plan, the optimistic estimate is 8 days, the most likely is 14 days, and the pessimistic is 24 days. Calculate the expected duration and standard deviation.

### Step 1: Apply PERT Formula

$$
E = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}
$$

$$
E = \frac{8 + 4(14) + 24}{6}
$$

$$
E = \frac{8 + 56 + 24}{6} = \frac{88}{6} \approx 14.67 \text{ days}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Standard Deviation

$$
\sigma = \frac{P - O}{6} = \frac{24 - 8}{6} = \frac{16}{6} \approx 2.67 \text{ days}
$$

### Step 3: 95% Confidence Interval

The 95% CI is $E \pm 2\sigma$:

$$
CI_{95\%} = 14.67 \pm 2(2.67) = 14.67 \pm 5.34
$$

$$
CI_{95\%} = [9.33, \; 20.01] \text{ days}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip**: Always show all three values $O, M, P$ explicitly. KTU evaluators look for clear substitution and final answers with units.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Defect Density & DRE

**Problem**: A 50 KLOC module had 120 defects found during testing, and 20 more were found by users post-release. Calculate Defect Density and DRE.

### Step 1: Defect Density

$$
DD = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{KLOC} = \frac{120}{50} = 2.4 \text{ defects/KLOC}
$$

### Step 2: Defect Removal Efficiency

$$
DRE = \frac{\text{Defects found before release}}{\text{Defects found before release} + \text{Defects after release}} \times 100
$$

$$
DRE = \frac{120}{120 + 20} \times 100 = \frac{120}{140} \times 100 \approx 85.71\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Industry benchmark for DRE is **> 90%**. The example above indicates the testing process needs improvement.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Regression Testing | Test Management |
|--------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Primary Goal** | Detect side-effects of change | Orchestrate the entire testing process |
| **When Performed** | After every change/fix | Throughout the project lifecycle |
| **Key Output** | Re-executed test report | Master Test Plan, status reports |
| **Tool Used** | Selenium, JUnit, QTP | JIRA, TestRail, Quality Center |
| **Metric** | APFD, Re-execution rate | DRE, Test Coverage, Defect Density |
| **Owner** | Test execution team | Test Manager / Lead |
| **Automation Role** | Critical | Helpful but secondary |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Regression Test Selection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Code Change Identified] --> B[Impact Analysis]
    B --> C{Change Scope?}
    C -->|Minor Fix| D[Corrective Regression]
    C -->|New Module| E[Partial + Progressive Regression]
    C -->|Major Release| F[Full Regression]
    D --> G[Select Relevant Test Cases]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Prioritize Test Cases via APFD]
    H --> I[Execute Selected Tests]
    I --> J{Tests Pass?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Mark Build Stable]
    J -->|No| L[Log Defect and Send to Dev]
    L --> A
    K --> M[Generate Regression Report]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation**: The flowchart shows the *closed-loop* nature of regression testing — every failed test re-triggers a code change, which re-triggers regression selection, creating an iterative feedback cycle.

---

## 4.2 Test Management Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PLANNING[Phase 1 - Test Planning]
    A1[Define Test Policy] --> A2[Create Master Test Plan]
    A2 --> A3[Define Entry/Exit Criteria]
    A3 --> A4[Resource & Schedule Planning]
    end
    
    subgraph ORGANIZATION[Phase 2 - Test Organization]
    B1[Form Test Team] --> B2[Assign Roles]
    B2 --> B3[Set Up Test Environment]
    B3 --> B4[Train Testers]
    end
    
    subgraph EXECUTION[Phase 3 - Test Execution and Control]
    C1[Execute Test Cases] --> C2[Log Defects]
    C2 --> C3[Monitor Progress via Metrics]
    C3 --> C4[Compare Actual vs Planned]
    C4 --> C5{On Track?}
    C5 -->|No| C6[Corrective Action]
    C6 --> C1
    C5 -->|Yes| C7[Generate Status Report]
    end
    
    subgraph CLOSURE[Phase 4 - Test Closure]
    D1[Test Summary Report] --> D2[Lessons Learned]
    D2 --> D3[Archive Test Artifacts]
    D3 --> D4[Release Sign-off]
    end
    
    A4 --> B1
    B4 --> C1
    C7 --> D1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note**: This 4-phase architecture maps directly to the **ISTQB Test Management process** referenced in the syllabus. Note the **control loop** in Phase 3 — monitoring continuously compares actual progress against plan.

---

## 4.3 Defect Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New
    New --> Assigned: Dev Lead reviews
    Assigned --> Open: Developer accepts
    Assigned --> Rejected: Not a valid defect
    Assigned --> Deferred: Postpone to next release
    Assigned --> Duplicate: Already reported
    Rejected --> Closed
    Duplicate --> Closed
    Deferred --> Closed
    Open --> Fixed: Developer fixes code
    Fixed --> Verified: Tester re-tests
    Verified --> Closed: Test passes
    Verified --> Reopen: Test fails
    Reopen --> Open
    Closed --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip**: Each transition in this state machine represents a status update in a defect-tracking tool (JIRA, Bugzilla). The **Reopen → Open** loop ensures that re-tested-and-still-failing defects do not get falsely marked as fixed.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Regression Testing. Why is it necessary? (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer**:
Regression testing is the type of software testing that re-executes existing test cases after a code change, bug fix, or enhancement to ensure that the previously working functionality has not been broken (i.e., has not *regressed*).

**Necessity**:
1. To detect *side-effects* caused by new changes (the ripple effect).
2. To maintain *confidence* in the software's stability across releases.
3. To verify that *bug fixes* did not introduce new defects elsewhere.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Severity and Priority of a Defect. (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Sample]**

**Model Answer**:

| Aspect | Severity | Priority |
|--------|----------|----------|
| **Definition** | Impact of the defect on the system's functionality | Urgency to fix the defect |
| **Determined by** | Tester / QA | Product Manager / Customer |
| **Scale** | Critical, Major, Minor, Trivial | High, Medium, Low |
| **Focus** | Technical | Business |
| **Example** | App crash on login (Critical severity) | Typo in footer (Trivial severity, Low priority) |

> [!NOTE]
> Key insight: A defect can have *high severity but low priority* (e.g., a crash in a feature used once a year) or *low severity but high priority* (e.g., a typo on the homepage).

---

## Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

### Question A (a): Explain the different types of regression testing in detail. (7 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 3 ESE Pattern]**

**Model Answer**:

**1. Unit Regression (1 Mark)**
- Tests a single, isolated unit (function or class) after a code change.
- Performed by the developer at the unit testing level.
- **Example**: After modifying a sorting function, re-run all its unit tests.

**2. Partial Regression (1.5 Marks)**
- Tests only the *impacted* modules after integrating a new module with the existing codebase.
- Useful when new code is added to a stable system.
- **Example**: Adding a new payment gateway — test the payment module plus the order-confirmation module that calls it.

**3. Complete (Full) Regression (1.5 Marks)**
- Re-executes the *entire* test suite from scratch.
- Performed before major releases or after major refactoring.
- Time-consuming but provides maximum confidence.

**4. Progressive Regression (1 Mark)**
- New test cases are designed for new functionality and combined with the existing regression suite.
- The suite *evolves* with the product.
- Common in Agile sprints.

**5. Corrective Regression (1 Mark)**
- Re-runs existing test cases when no new functionality is added — only minor bug fixes.
- Most cost-effective type.

**6. Selective Regression (1 Mark)**
- Selects a *subset* of the test suite based on impact analysis.
- Balances coverage and cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Students often *omit Corrective and Selective regression*. Both are mandatory types per ISTQB. Avoid losing 2 marks here.

---

### Question A (b): Describe Test Estimation techniques. Compute the expected duration and standard deviation using PERT for the following test activities: Optimistic = 12 days, Most Likely = 18 days, Pessimistic = 30 days. (7 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Pattern 2024]**

**Model Answer**:

**Test Estimation Techniques (4 Marks)**:

1. **Expert Judgment (Delphi Technique)**: A panel of testing experts independently estimates effort, then converges via facilitated discussion. Useful when historical data is unavailable.
2. **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)**: Decomposes the testing project into smaller tasks (test planning, test case design, execution, reporting), estimates each, and sums them up.
3. **Use Case Points (UCP)**: Estimates based on the number and complexity of use cases. Each use case is classified as Simple, Average, or Complex.
4. **PERT (Program Evaluation & Review Technique)**: Uses a weighted average of Optimistic, Most Likely, and Pessimistic estimates.
5. **Analogy-Based Estimation**: Compares with similar past projects and adjusts.
6. **Function Point / Test Point Analysis**: Estimates based on software size (function points) and test coverage.

**PERT Calculation (3 Marks)**:

Given: $O = 12$ days, $M = 18$ days, $P = 30$ days.

**Step 1: Expected Duration**

$$
E = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}
$$

$$
E = \frac{12 + 4(18) + 30}{6} = \frac{12 + 72 + 30}{6} = \frac{114}{6} = 19 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 2: Standard Deviation**

$$
\sigma = \frac{P - O}{6} = \frac{30 - 12}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 3: 95% Confidence Interval (Bonus Point Earner)**

$$
CI_{95\%} = E \pm 2\sigma = 19 \pm 6 = [13, \; 25] \text{ days}
$$

**Final Answer**: Expected duration = **19 days**, Standard deviation = **3 days**, 95% CI = **[13, 25] days**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: Students forget to state the *units* (days). Always write "**19 days**", not just "19". Also, do not confuse the formula: it is $\frac{O + 4M + P}{6}$, *not* $\frac{O + M + P}{3}$.

---

## Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks)

### Question B (a): Explain the Test Management process with a neat diagram. List the key metrics used for Test Monitoring and Control. (7 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Alternate Module 3 Pattern]**

**Model Answer**:

**Test Management Process (4 Marks)**:

The ISTQB-aligned Test Management process consists of four phases:

1. **Test Organization**: Forming the test team, assigning roles (Test Manager, Test Lead, Test Engineer), and choosing the team structure (independent, combined, or outsourced).
2. **Test Planning and Estimation**: Creating the **Master Test Plan (MTP)**, defining test strategy (analytical, model-based, methodical, etc.), setting entry and exit criteria, and estimating effort via PERT, WBS, or Expert Judgment.
3. **Test Monitoring and Control**: Continuously tracking the actual test progress against the plan, using metrics like Defect Density, Test Coverage, and DRE. If deviations occur, corrective actions are initiated.
4. **Test Closure**: Generating the **Test Summary Report**, documenting lessons learned, archiving test artifacts, and obtaining release sign-off from stakeholders.

**Diagram (Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the full flowchart)**.

**Key Test Monitoring Metrics (3 Marks)**:

| Metric | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| **Test Case Execution Rate** | % of test cases executed vs planned |
| **Defect Density** | Defects per KLOC; code quality indicator |
| **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** | % of defects caught before release; testing effectiveness |
| **Test Coverage** | % of requirements covered by tests; completeness |
| **Defect Leakage** | % of defects escaping to UAT/production |
| **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | Total cost of prevention, appraisal, and failure |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: Students often *miss the Test Closure phase*. It is mandatory per ISTQB and worth at least 1 mark.

---

### Question B (b): What is the APFD metric? Compute the APFD for a test suite of 12 test cases that detects 6 faults at positions: T1=2, T2=4, T3=6, T4=8, T5=10, T6=12. (7 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Alternate ESE Pattern]**

**Model Answer**:

**APFD Definition (2 Marks)**:

The **Average Percentage of Faults Detected (APFD)** is a metric used to evaluate the effectiveness of test case prioritization. It measures the weighted average of the percentage of faults detected during the execution of a prioritized test suite. The APFD value ranges from 0 to 1, with higher values indicating better prioritization (faults detected earlier in the run).

**Formula (1 Mark)**:

$$
APFD = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} TF_i}{n \cdot m} + \frac{1}{2n}
$$

where:
- $n$ = total number of test cases
- $m$ = total number of faults
- $TF_i$ = position of the test case that detects the $i$-th fault

**Calculation (3 Marks)**:

**Given**: $n = 12$, $m = 6$, $TF_1=2, TF_2=4, TF_3=6, TF_4=8, TF_5=10, TF_6=12$.

**Step 1: Compute the Sum $S$**

$$
S = 2 + 4 + 6 + 8 + 10 + 12 = 42
$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the APFD Formula**

$$
APFD = 1 - \frac{42}{12 \times 6} + \frac{1}{2 \times 12}
$$

$$
APFD = 1 - \frac{42}{72} + \frac{1}{24}
$$

$$
APFD = 1 - 0.5833 + 0.0417
$$

$$
APFD = 0.4583 \approx 0.46
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation (1 Mark)**

An APFD of **0.46** indicates **poor prioritization**. The faults are being detected only at the *end* of the test execution. The test team should re-prioritize to detect faults earlier, targeting an APFD > **0.80** for good effectiveness.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: Students often write $APFD = 0.46$ and stop. Always provide a *one-line interpretation* of the value. KTU evaluators award at least 1 mark for interpretation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

### Regression Testing
- **Definition**: Re-running existing tests after change to ensure no new defects.
- **Triggers**: Bug fixes, new features, refactoring, environment changes.
- **Types**: Unit, Partial, Full, Progressive, Corrective, Selective — *all six must be memorised*.
- **Selection Techniques**: Retest All, RTS, Prioritization, Hybrid, Safe Regression.
- **APFD Formula**: $APFD = 1 - \frac{\sum TF_i}{n \cdot m} + \frac{1}{2n}$.
- **Tools**: Selenium (web), JUnit/TestNG (Java), QTP/UFT (commercial), PyTest (Python).
- **CI/CD Integration**: Automated regression is critical for DevOps pipelines.

### Test Management
- **4 Phases (ISTQB)**: Organization → Planning/Estimation → Monitoring/Control → Closure.
- **PERT Formula**: $E = \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}$; $\sigma = \frac{P - O}{6}$.
- **Estimation Techniques**: Expert Judgment, WBS, UCP, PERT, Analogy, Function Points.
- **Key Metrics**: Test Coverage, Defect Density, DRE, Defect Leakage, CoQ.
- **Entry vs Exit Criteria**: Entry = *prerequisites to start*; Exit = *conditions to stop*.
- **Severity vs Priority**: Severity = technical impact; Priority = business urgency.
- **Defect Lifecycle States**: New → Assigned → Open → Fixed → Verified → Closed (with Rejected, Deferred, Duplicate, Reopen branches).
- **Risk Formula**: $Risk = P \times I$ (Probability × Impact).

### Exam Strategy
- For **3-mark questions**: 1-line definition + 2-3 bullet points.
- For **14-mark questions**: Sub-parts of 7 marks each; always include a diagram where asked; show *all formula substitution steps*; provide *interpretation* of computed values.
- Always mention **ISTQB terminology** in definitions for full marks.
- Use **tables and bullet points** liberally — KTU answer sheets reward structured answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of Regression Testing in Software Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT747 - SOFTWARE TESTING AND QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Importance of Regression Testing in Software Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regression Testing** is a systematic, selective re-execution of one or more already-executed test cases to verify that modifications, enhancements, corrective actions, or configuration changes to a software system have not introduced new defects and have not broken any previously working functionality.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, regression testing is positioned as a **non-negotiable quality gate** within the **Software Maintenance Life Cycle (SMLC)**. The IEEE Standard 829-2008 and IEEE 1012-2016 both classify it under the **validation activity class** of software testing.

### Key Technical Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Test Suite** | A collection of test cases used in regression testing. |
| **Retest-All Strategy** | Re-executing the entire test suite after a change. |
| **Selective Re-testing** | Executing only those tests affected by recent modifications. |
| **Test Selection Criteria** | Logical rules (e.g., code coverage, dependency mapping) for choosing test cases. |
| **Regression Fault** | A defect introduced into previously validated code due to a recent change. |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 syllabus highlight: Regression testing is a **continuous activity**, not a one-time event. It is performed during **corrective, adaptive, perfective, and preventive maintenance** of software.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you have built a **complex LEGO city** — with houses, roads, bridges, and a hospital. You decide to **add a new floor to the hospital**. Once the new floor is built, would you only check the new floor? **No!** You would walk through the **entire city** to ensure:

- The hospital's old floors still work (elevator, stairs).
- The road connecting the hospital hasn't cracked.
- The bridge that supports the new hospital wing can still hold weight.
- The electricity and water supply to nearby buildings are unaffected.

**This is exactly what regression testing does for software.** When a developer modifies one module, we don't just test the new code — we re-test the **entire interconnected system** to ensure nothing else has broken.

---

## 1.3 The "Why" Behind Regression Testing in Maintenance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Software Maintenance (per ISO/IEC 14764)**
> 1. **Corrective Maintenance (≈ 20%)** — fixing bugs
> 2. **Adaptive Maintenance (≈ 25%)** — adapting to new environments
> 3. **Perfective Maintenance (≈ 50%)** — adding new features
> 4. **Preventive Maintenance (≈ 5%)** — reducing future failures

Every maintenance activity introduces **change**, and **every change is a potential source of regression faults**. The **importance of regression testing grows exponentially** as the software ages and its codebase grows.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Phases of Regression Testing

The regression testing process can be broken down into the following structured phases:

### Phase 1: Change Identification
- Identify the **modules, components, and interfaces** that have been modified.
- Use **version control diff tools** (e.g., `git diff`, `svn diff`).
- Document the **scope and impact** of the change.

### Phase 2: Impact Analysis
- Trace dependencies using **call graphs, control flow graphs, and data flow diagrams**.
- Classify the impact as: **Direct (Type 1)**, **Indirect (Type 2)**, or **Minimal (Type 3)**.

### Phase 3: Test Case Selection
Apply one of three strategies (the **KTU-mandated core decision point**):

1. **Retest-All (Re-test Everything)**
   - Re-run the **complete test suite**.
   - **Pros:** Maximum coverage. **Cons:** Expensive, time-consuming.
2. **Regression Test Selection (RTS)**
   - Select only the **affected test cases**.
   - **Pros:** Cost-effective. **Cons:** Risk of missing indirect impact.
3. **Test Case Prioritization (TCP)**
   - Re-order test cases so that **high-priority tests run first**.
   - **Pros:** Early defect detection. **Cons:** Requires sound prioritization metrics.

### Phase 4: Test Execution
- Execute the selected tests in a **controlled test environment**.
- Capture logs, screenshots, and metrics.
- Use **automation frameworks** (Selenium, JUnit, TestNG, QTP/UFT).

### Phase 5: Result Analysis & Reporting
- Compare actual vs. expected outputs.
- Classify deviations as **new faults** or **pre-existing faults**.
- Generate **regression test reports** with metrics.

### Phase 6: Closure
- Update the **regression test suite** with new test cases.
- Archive artifacts for **future maintenance cycles**.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" — Justification for Regression Testing

| Justification | Engineering Rationale |
|---------------|------------------------|
| **Side Effect Mitigation** | Modifications in one module can ripple through dependent modules. |
| **Continuous Confidence** | Provides confidence that the system remains stable after each change. |
| **Cost Reduction** | Catching regression faults early is **10x–100x cheaper** than catching them post-release. |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | Industries like **aerospace, medical, banking** require documented regression evidence. |
| **Agile/DevOps Enablement** | Regression suites form the backbone of **CI/CD pipelines**. |
| **Customer Trust** | Reduces post-deployment hotfixes and negative user experience. |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all KTU-relevant formulas and metrics** for the topic. Memorize them for Part A and Part B questions.

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbol / Expression | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|------------------|---------------------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Test Selection Ratio (TSR)** | $TSR = \dfrac{\vert T_{selected} \vert}{\vert T_{total} \vert}$ | Fraction of test cases selected for regression | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 2 | **Defect Detection Efficiency (DDE)** | $DDE = \dfrac{\vert D_{reg} \vert}{\vert D_{reg} \vert + \vert D_{missed} \vert}$ | Ability of regression suite to catch faults | Percentage (%) |
| 3 | **Regression Test Cost (RTC)** | $RTC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{exec,i} + C_{setup} + C_{analysis}$ | Total cost of running regression | Currency units |
| 4 | **Cost per Fault (CPF)** | $CPF = \dfrac{Total\,Regression\,Cost}{\vert D_{found} \vert}$ | Economic efficiency metric | Currency per fault |
| 5 | **Code Coverage (CC)** | $CC = \dfrac{\vert L_{executed} \vert}{\vert L_{total} \vert} \times 100\%$ | Lines/branches exercised by regression tests | Percentage (%) |
| 6 | **Mean Time To Detect (MTTD)** | $MTTD = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_{detect,i} - T_{inject,i})}{n}$ | Average time to detect a regression fault | Hours / days |
