| 7 | **APFD (Average Percentage of Faults Detected)** | $APFD = 1 - \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (T_{i} - 1) \cdot f_{i}}{n \cdot m} + \dfrac{1}{2n}$ | Prioritization effectiveness metric | Range 0 to 1 |
| 8 | **Regression Suite Growth Rate (RSGR)** | $RSGR = \dfrac{\vert TS_{new} \vert - \vert TS_{old} \vert}{\vert TS_{old} \vert} \times 100\%$ | Growth of test suite over time | Percentage (%) |
| 9 | **Fault Injection Ratio (FIR)** | $FIR = \dfrac{\vert F_{injected} \vert}{\vert LOC \vert}$ | Density of seeded faults in code | Faults per KLOC |
| 10 | **Maintenance Effort Distribution (MED)** | $MED_{i} = \dfrac{E_{i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{4} E_{j}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage effort for maintenance type $i$ | Percentage (%) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:**
> - $T_{selected}$ = test cases selected, $T_{total}$ = total test cases
> - $D_{reg}$ = defects caught by regression, $D_{missed}$ = defects missed
> - $T_i$ = position of test case $i$ in prioritized order, $f_i$ = faults it detects
> - $n$ = total test cases, $m$ = total faults in system
> - $LOC$ = Lines of Code

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Regression Testing |
|--------|-----------------------------------|
| **Banking & FinTech** | Verifying that a new payment gateway does not break existing fund transfer flows. |
| **Healthcare (FDA)** | Mandatory regression evidence for medical device software updates. |
| **Aviation (DO-178C)** | Every code change requires documented re-verification of safety-critical functions. |
| **E-Commerce** | Ensuring that adding a new product filter does not break the checkout pipeline. |
| **Telecommunications** | Validating that new 5G features do not disrupt legacy 4G/3G handover. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Regression Testing Strategies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **comparative matrix** is a high-frequency KTU exam topic. Memorize the trade-offs.

| Dimension | Retest-All | Regression Test Selection (RTS) | Test Case Prioritization (TCP) |
|-----------|------------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Coverage** | **100%** (theoretical) | Subset (50–80% typically) | Same as Retest-All, but reordered |
| **Cost** | **Highest** | Moderate (30–60% of Retest-All) | Low (since runs are stopped early) |
| **Time** | **Longest** | Moderate | Shortest for early fault detection |
| **Risk of Missed Faults** | **Lowest** | Moderate–High | Moderate |
| **Automation Suitability** | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Tool Support** | Any test runner | Requires dependency analyzer | Requires prioritization algorithm |
| **Best For** | Safety-critical, monthly cycles | Frequent changes, agile sprints | Tight deadlines, daily builds |
| **KTU Recommendation** | "Gold standard" but rarely practical | **Industry default** | **Complement to RTS** |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Regression Test Selection Cost Minimization

We want to derive the **minimum cost configuration** for a regression testing strategy.

### Given:
- $T = \{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n\}$ = set of all test cases
- $C(t_i)$ = cost of executing test case $t_i$
- $D(t_i)$ = number of defects detected by $t_i$
- $S \subseteq T$ = subset selected for regression

### Objective Function:
We want to **minimize total regression cost** while **maximizing defect coverage**.

$$\min_{S \subseteq T} \quad C_{total}(S) = \sum_{t_i \in S} C(t_i)$$

### Constraint:
A minimum number of defects must be covered:

$$\sum_{t_i \in S} D(t_i) \geq D_{min}$$

### Solution Strategy (Lagrangian Relaxation):
We introduce a Lagrangian multiplier $\lambda$:

$$L(S, \lambda) = \sum_{t_i \in S} C(t_i) + \lambda \cdot \left(D_{min} - \sum_{t_i \in S} D(t_i)\right)$$

$$L(S, \lambda) = \sum_{t_i \in S} \left(C(t_i) - \lambda \cdot D(t_i)\right) + \lambda \cdot D_{min}$$

### Decision Rule:
Select test case $t_i$ **if and only if**:

$$C(t_i) - \lambda \cdot D(t_i) < 0$$

$$\boxed{\quad \lambda > \dfrac{C(t_i)}{D(t_i)} \quad \forall t_i \in S \quad}$$

### Interpretation:
- $\lambda$ = **shadow price of a defect** (i.e., the marginal cost we are willing to pay for one additional defect detected).
- The rule says: **Select a test case only if its defect-detection value exceeds its execution cost.**

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

### Problem:
A software module has **6 test cases** with the following cost and defect-detection data. Using the Lagrangian rule with $\lambda = 50$, identify the optimal regression subset $S$ such that $D_{min} = 7$.

| Test Case | $C(t_i)$ (₹) | $D(t_i)$ | $C(t_i)/D(t_i)$ |
|-----------|--------------|----------|------------------|
| $t_1$ | 100 | 2 | 50.0 |
| $t_2$ | 80 | 3 | 26.67 |
| $t_3$ | 150 | 4 | 37.50 |
| $t_4$ | 60 | 1 | 60.00 |
| $t_5$ | 90 | 3 | 30.00 |
| $t_6$ | 120 | 2 | 60.00 |

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute $C(t_i)/D(t_i)$ for all test cases (shown in table above).

**Step 2:** Apply the rule: Select $t_i$ if $C(t_i)/D(t_i) < \lambda = 50$.

**Step 3:** Selection results:
- $t_1$: $50.0 \not< 50$ → **Exclude** (boundary case, depends on strict inequality)
- $t_2$: $26.67 < 50$ → **Include** ✓
- $t_3$: $37.50 < 50$ → **Include** ✓
- $t_4$: $60.00 \not< 50$ → **Exclude** ✗
- $t_5$: $30.00 < 50$ → **Include** ✓
- $t_6$: $60.00 \not< 50$ → **Exclude** ✗

**Step 4:** Verify the constraint:

$$\sum_{t_i \in S} D(t_i) = D(t_2) + D(t_3) + D(t_5) = 3 + 4 + 3 = 10 \geq D_{min} = 7 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5:** Compute total regression cost:

$$C_{total}(S) = C(t_2) + C(t_3) + C(t_5) = 80 + 150 + 90 = \text{₹}320$$

**Step 6:** Compare with Retest-All cost:

$$C_{total}(T) = 100 + 80 + 150 + 60 + 90 + 120 = \text{₹}600$$

**Step 7:** Calculate savings:

$$Savings = \dfrac{600 - 320}{600} \times 100\% = 46.67\%$$

### Final Answer:
> **Optimal regression subset $S = \{t_2, t_3, t_5\}$**, with a total cost of **₹320** and **46.67% cost savings** over the Retest-All strategy, while detecting **10 defects** (exceeding the minimum requirement of 7).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: APFD Metric for Prioritization

The **Average Percentage of Faults Detected (APFD)** measures how early in the prioritized test sequence faults are caught.

$$APFD = 1 - \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} (T_i - 1) \cdot f_i}{n \cdot m} + \dfrac{1}{2n}$$

Where:
- $n$ = total number of test cases
- $m$ = total number of faults
- $T_i$ = position of the $i^{th}$ test case in the prioritized order
- $f_i$ = number of faults detected by the $i^{th}$ test case

### Boundary Conditions:
- $APFD = 1$ → All faults detected by the first test case (ideal prioritization).
- $APFD = 0.5$ → Average case (random order for uniformly distributed faults).
- $APFD \to 0$ → Faults detected only at the very end (worst case).

### Worked Mini-Example:
Given $n = 4$ test cases, $m = 3$ faults, and prioritized order with positions and fault counts:

| Position $i$ | Test Case | $T_i$ | $f_i$ |
|--------------|-----------|-------|-------|
| 1 | $t_A$ | 1 | 2 |
| 2 | $t_B$ | 2 | 1 |
| 3 | $t_C$ | 3 | 0 |
| 4 | $t_D$ | 4 | 0 |

### Step-by-Step Calculation:

**Step 1:** Compute $\sum_{i=1}^{m} (T_i - 1) \cdot f_i$:

$$\sum = (1-1)\cdot 2 + (2-1)\cdot 1 + (3-1)\cdot 0 = 0 + 1 + 0 = 1$$

**Step 2:** Apply the APFD formula:

$$APFD = 1 - \dfrac{1}{4 \cdot 3} + \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot 4} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{12} + \dfrac{1}{8}$$

$$APFD = 1 - 0.0833 + 0.125 = 1.0417 \text{ (clipped to } 1.0 \text{)}$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation: The prioritization is near-optimal (close to 1.0), meaning faults are detected very early in the sequence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Higher APFD values mean the regression suite is more efficient at detecting faults early, which is critical in **time-boxed CI/CD pipelines**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Regression Testing Process Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Software Change Detected] --> B[Identify Modified Modules]
    B --> C[Perform Impact Analysis]
    C --> D{Choose Regression Strategy}
    D -->|Option 1| E1[Retest-All Strategy]
    D -->|Option 2| E2[Regression Test Selection]
    D -->|Option 3| E3[Test Case Prioritization]
    E1 --> F[Execute Test Suite]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Capture Results and Logs]
    G --> H{All Tests Pass?}
    H -->|No| I[Log Regression Fault]
    I --> J[Assign to Developer]
    J --> K[Fix and Re-Verify]
    K --> F
    H -->|Yes| L[Update Regression Suite]
    L --> M[Archive Artifacts]
    M --> N[Process Complete]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Maintenance Workflow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MAINT[Software Maintenance Phase]
        CM[Corrective Maintenance] --> REG[Regression Test Engine]
        AM[Adaptive Maintenance] --> REG
        PM[Perfective Maintenance] --> REG
        PRM[Preventive Maintenance] --> REG
    end
    
    REG --> TC[Test Case Repository]
    TC --> SEL[Test Selector Module]
    SEL --> EXE[Test Execution Engine]
    EXE --> RES[Result Comparator]
    RES --> REP[Test Report Generator]
    REP --> DB[(Defect Database)]
    REP --> CI[CI/CD Pipeline Trigger]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Hierarchy of Regression Testing

```mermaid
graph TD
    RT[Regression Testing] --> UT[Unit Regression]
    RT --> INT[Integration Regression]
    RT --> SYS[System Regression]
    RT --> ACC[Acceptance Regression]
    
    UT --> UJ[Unit Test Suite]
    INT --> IJ[Integration Test Suite]
    SYS --> SJ[System Test Suite]
    ACC --> AJ[UAT Suite]
    
    UJ --> AUTO[Automated Runners]
    IJ --> AUTO
    SJ --> AUTO
    AJ --> MANUAL[Manual Testers]
```

## 4.4 Decision Matrix: When to Apply Which Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[New Code Change] --> Q1{Criticality of Change?}
    Q1 -->|Safety-Critical| SAFE[Retest-All Strategy]
    Q1 -->|Standard Change| Q2{Frequency of Changes?}
    Q2 -->|Daily/Sprint| AGILE[RTS with Prioritization]
    Q2 -->|Weekly| Q3{Budget Available?}
    Q3 -->|Low Budget| RTS[Selective RTS]
    Q3 -->|Adequate| HYBRID[Hybrid: RTS + TCP]
    
    SAFE --> REPORT[Generate Full Report]
    AGILE --> REPORT
    RTS --> REPORT
    HYBRID --> REPORT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Interpretation Tip:** When asked to "draw the regression testing process" in an exam, the **flowchart in Section 4.1** is the model answer. Always include the **feedback loop** (re-execution after defect fix).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define regression testing. Why is it important in software maintenance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Regression testing is a type of software testing that ensures that recent code changes (bug fixes, enhancements, configuration changes, or new feature additions) have not adversely affected existing functionality of the software system.

> **Importance in Maintenance (1.5 marks):** It is critical in software maintenance because every maintenance activity (corrective, adaptive, perfective, preventive) introduces changes. These changes can cause "side effects" or "regression faults" in previously working code. Regression testing re-validates existing functionality to catch these faults early, ensuring system stability, regulatory compliance, and customer trust. Studies show that catching regression faults during maintenance is **10x to 100x cheaper** than catching them post-release.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Retest-All strategy and Regression Test Selection (RTS).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Retest-All | Regression Test Selection |
|--------|------------|---------------------------|
| **Scope (1 mark)** | Re-executes the **entire** test suite. | Re-executes only a **subset** of affected test cases. |
| **Cost (1 mark)** | **High** — full execution cost. | **Moderate** — typically 30–60% of Retest-All cost. |
| **Risk (1 mark)** | **Lowest** missed-fault risk. | **Moderate** — may miss indirect-impact faults. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **"Retest-All" with "Re-testing"**. Re-testing is testing a specific bug fix; Retest-All is a regression strategy. **Do not use these terms interchangeably** — you will lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Pattern: Each Module carries **one 14-mark question with internal choice** (either Question A or Question B). Sub-parts are typically 7 + 7.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Explain the importance of regression testing in software maintenance with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Regression testing is the **re-execution of existing test cases** after a software change to ensure that previously verified functionality still works correctly. In the software maintenance phase, it serves as a **safety net** that validates the integrity of legacy code against new modifications.

**Key Reasons for Importance (4 marks):**

1. **Mitigates Side Effects (1 mark):**  
   Maintenance changes often ripple through dependent modules. Example: Fixing a memory leak in a banking application's transaction module may unintentionally slow down the interest calculation module. Regression testing catches such side effects.

2. **Supports All Maintenance Types (1 mark):**  
   - **Corrective:** Re-test after bug fix to ensure the fix works and nothing else broke.  
   - **Adaptive:** Re-test when migrating to a new OS (e.g., Windows 10 → Windows 11).  
   - **Perfective:** Re-test after adding a new "UPI payment" feature to ensure old "NEFT/RTGS" still works.  
   - **Preventive:** Re-test after refactoring legacy code to improve maintainability.

3. **Cost-Effective Defect Detection (1 mark):**  
   The cost of fixing a regression fault found during maintenance is roughly **10x cheaper** than one found in production. For safety-critical systems (aviation, medical), the cost factor can reach **100x or more**.

4. **Enables CI/CD and DevOps (1 mark):**  
   In modern pipelines, regression suites run automatically on every commit. Without regression testing, Agile and DevOps workflows are impossible.

**Real-World Example (2 marks):**

Consider an e-commerce platform like **Amazon** that pushes code changes multiple times per day. Without regression testing, a small change to the "Add to Cart" button could break the "Checkout" flow, leading to millions in lost revenue. A comprehensive regression suite ensures that the entire purchase pipeline — from search → cart → payment → invoice — remains functional after every change.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Regression testing is the **backbone of software maintenance quality assurance**. It provides confidence, reduces risk, and ensures that the software remains fit-for-purpose throughout its operational lifetime.

---

#### (b) Describe the various regression testing techniques. Compare Regression Test Selection (RTS) and Test Case Prioritization (TCP). **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Techniques Overview (2 marks):**

1. **Retest-All Technique:** Re-runs every test case in the suite. Maximum coverage, but expensive.
2. **Regression Test Selection (RTS):** Selects only the test cases that exercise the changed code or its dependencies. Uses code coverage, call graphs, and fault history.
3. **Test Case Prioritization (TCP):** Reorders test cases so that high-value (frequently fault-detecting) tests run first. APFD metric quantifies effectiveness.
4. **Hybrid Technique:** Combines RTS and TCP for optimal results.
5. **Safe Regression Testing:** Includes all test cases that touch modified components plus their immediate dependencies.

**Detailed Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Dimension | RTS | TCP |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Goal** | Minimize test set size | Maximize early fault detection |
| **Selection Criterion** | Dependency/covariance analysis | Fault history, code coverage, requirements priority |
| **Output** | Smaller subset | Full set, reordered |
| **Cost Reduction** | Yes (fewer tests) | No (same number, but stopped early) |
| **Risk** | May miss indirect faults | May delay detection of low-priority faults |
| **Best Use** | Large, mature test suites | Time-boxed CI/CD cycles |
| **Example Tool** | JReFrameworker, DejaGNU | Selenium + TestNG priority annotations |

**Worked Example (1 mark):**
For a 1000-test-case suite where a 5% code change affects ~200 test cases:
- **RTS:** Selects and runs 200 tests → cost reduction of 80%.
- **TCP:** Runs all 1000 tests but in fault-detection-optimized order → early stoppage possible after 200 tests if 90% of high-priority faults are caught.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
RTS and TCP are **complementary, not competing** strategies. In practice, mature organizations use **RTS to filter** the suite and **TCP to order** the filtered subset, achieving the best cost-quality trade-off.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE**

#### (a) Discuss the challenges of regression testing and explain the best practices to overcome them. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Challenges (4 marks):**

1. **Test Suite Bloat (1 mark):**  
   As the software grows, the test suite expands. Running thousands of tests every cycle becomes infeasible. Studies show test suites can grow by **20–40% annually**.

2. **Inadequate Test Selection (1 mark):**  
   Poor impact analysis leads to **under-selection** (missed faults) or **over-selection** (wasted resources). This is the **#1 cause of regression budget overruns**.

3. **Flaky Tests (1 mark):**  
   Tests that pass/fail inconsistently due to timing, network, or environment issues erode trust in the regression suite. Industry surveys show **~50% of teams** report flakiness as a major pain point.

4. **Environment & Data Dependency (1 mark):**  
   Regression tests require a stable, production-like environment with realistic test data. Setting this up is costly and time-consuming.

**Best Practices (3 marks):**

1. **Test Suite Optimization (1 mark):**  
   Apply **RTS** + **TCP** to keep suite size manageable. Remove obsolete tests, merge duplicate tests, and use **mutation testing** to assess test effectiveness.

2. **Continuous Automation (1 mark):**  
   Use **Selenium, Cypress, JUnit, TestNG, PyTest, Postman** (for APIs) to automate regression. Integrate with **Jenkins, GitLab CI, GitHub Actions** for continuous execution.

3. **Service Virtualization & Test Data Management (1 mark):**  
   Use tools like **Hoverfly, WireMock** to mock unavailable services. Use **synthetic data generators** to ensure data realism without compromising privacy.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
A well-engineered regression testing strategy is a **living system** that evolves with the software. The combination of **smart selection, ruthless automation, and robust data management** is the hallmark of mature QA organizations.

---

#### (b) Explain the role of regression testing in Agile and DevOps environments. Discuss the impact of automation on regression testing effectiveness. **(6 marks)**

*(Note: This sub-part is 6 marks since the full question is 14 marks split as 7+6+1, or the 1 remaining mark is for a labeled diagram — KTU accepts flexible splits.)*

**Model Answer:**

**Role in Agile (2 marks):**
In Agile sprints, regression testing ensures that the **increment delivered at the end of each sprint** does not break previously accepted user stories. The regression suite is built incrementally — at every sprint boundary, the new acceptance tests are added to the master regression suite. This "**growing suite**" approach is central to Scrum and SAFe methodologies.

**Role in DevOps (2 marks):**
In DevOps CI/CD pipelines, regression testing is a **mandatory gate** before deployment. Typical pipeline stages are:
1. **Commit** → Unit tests
2. **Build** → Static analysis + Unit regression
3. **Test** → Integration + System regression
4. **Stage** → Acceptance regression on a staging environment
5. **Deploy to Production** → Smoke regression (subset)

Tools like **Jenkins, GitLab CI, CircleCI** orchestrate this flow. Without regression automation, **continuous delivery is impossible**.

**Impact of Automation (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Manual Regression | Automated Regression |
|--------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Execution Time** | Days to weeks | Minutes to hours |
| **Repeatability** | Error-prone | Deterministic |
| **Coverage** | Limited by human bandwidth | Scalable to thousands of tests |
| **Cost per Run** | High (linear) | Low (amortized over runs) |
| **Feedback Loop** | Slow (delays decisions) | Fast (enables continuous quality) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not write generic statements** like "regression testing is important." Always **back claims with numbers** (e.g., "10x cost saving," "50% of teams face flakiness").
> - **Draw at least one diagram** in your 14-mark answer. A process flow or comparison table earns **+1 to +2 marks** as visual aid.
> - For sub-parts, **use sub-headings** (e.g., "Challenges," "Best Practices") to structure your answer. Unstructured prose loses clarity marks.
> - **Always mention a real tool** (Selenium, Jenkins, JUnit) — vague answers score lower.
> - **Do not skip the conclusion** in long answers (14-mark questions). A 2-line conclusion earns a free half-mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- [ ] Regression testing = re-executing existing tests after a change to catch side effects.
- [ ] It is a **continuous**, **mandatory** activity in **all four maintenance types**.
- [ ] KTU 2024 syllabus position: **Module 3 — Software Maintenance**.

### ✅ Three Main Strategies
- [ ] **Retest-All** — 100% coverage, highest cost.
- [ ] **Regression Test Selection (RTS)** — selective, cost-effective.
- [ ] **Test Case Prioritization (TCP)** — reorders for early fault detection.

### ✅ Key Formulas to Memorize
- [ ] **TSR** = selected/total test cases
- [ ] **DDE** = detected / (detected + missed) defects
- [ ] **APFD** = 1 − Σ(Tᵢ−1)·fᵢ/(n·m) + 1/(2n)
- [ ] **CPF** = total cost / number of defects found

### ✅ Lifecycle Phases
- [ ] Change Identification → Impact Analysis → Test Selection → Execution → Result Analysis → Closure.

### ✅ Real-World Tools
- [ ] **Automation:** Selenium, Cypress, JUnit, TestNG, Postman.
- [ ] **CI/CD:** Jenkins, GitLab CI, GitHub Actions, CircleCI.
- [ ] **Service Virtualization:** WireMock, Hoverfly, Mountebank.

### ✅ Common Exam Traps
- [ ] Confusing **re-testing** (specific bug) with **retest-all** (regression strategy).
- [ ] Forgetting to mention **Lagrangian rule** in cost-minimization questions.
- [ ] Ignoring the **feedback loop** in process diagrams.
- [ ] Not citing **metrics** (APFD, DDE) in 14-mark answers.

### ✅ One-Line Takeaway
> **"Regression testing is not a luxury — it is the insurance policy of software maintenance."** — Always lead your exam answer with this mindset.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Regression Test Planning and Case Selection

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned)**
> **Regression Testing** is a type of software testing executed to verify that a software system still performs correctly after modifications, enhancements, or bug fixes, and that no new defects have been introduced into previously validated code. **Regression Test Planning** is the systematic process of defining the strategy, scope, schedule, resources, and exit criteria for executing regression tests, while **Test Case Selection** is the methodical identification of an optimal subset of test cases from the existing test suite that provides maximum defect-detection capability with minimum execution cost.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have built a beautiful **multi-storey house** 🏠. After construction, you decide to renovate the kitchen — you shift the sink, replace tiles, and add new wiring. Once the kitchen work is done, you must re-check the entire house:
- Does the **bathroom plumbing** still work? (It shares a water line with the kitchen!)
- Are the **upper floor walls** still crack-free? (Drilling for new wiring may have weakened them.)
- Does the **main electrical panel** handle the new load safely?

You don't demolish the house to re-test it. Instead, you re-visit **only the rooms that could be affected**, plus a few **critical rooms** you always check (bedrooms, main door). This is exactly what regression testing does to software — re-runs tests on code paths that may be impacted by the recent change, plus a baseline set of critical tests.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (Must-Memorize Terms)**
> - **Test Suite** — The complete collection of test cases available for execution.
> - **Retest-All Strategy** — Re-executing every test case in the suite.
> - **Regression Test Selection (RTS)** — Choosing only the relevant test cases.
> - **Test Case Prioritization** — Ordering selected tests to maximize early fault detection.
> - **Safe Reduction** — Removing tests that cannot reveal new faults in the modified program.

### Why is Regression Test Planning Critical?

| Reason | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| **Cost Reduction** | Running the entire test suite after every change is **prohibitively expensive** — often 60-80% of the testing budget. |
| **Faster Feedback** | Selective re-testing gives developers quicker turnaround during continuous integration. |
| **Defect Localization** | A well-planned regression suite pinpoints whether the recent change caused the failure. |
| **Risk Mitigation** | Identifies hidden side-effects in modules that are *seemingly unrelated* to the change. |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard KTU Metric to Remember:** The **Retest-All cost** $C_{RA} = n \times t$, where $n$ is the total number of test cases and $t$ is the average execution time per test. Selective regression cost $C_{SR} = m \times t$ where $m \le n$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Regression Testing Process Model

Regression test planning follows a **systematic life-cycle** embedded within the broader software maintenance cycle. Below is the operational breakdown of each phase.

### Phase 1: Test Suite Baseline Creation
- When the software is first validated (typically at the end of the first integration cycle), the entire executed test suite is **frozen** as a baseline.
- This baseline acts as the **golden reference** against which future regressions are compared.

### Phase 2: Change Identification
- A change request (CR) or bug fix enters the system.
- The **impact analysis** identifies:
  - The **modified modules** $M_{mod}$
  - The **directly dependent modules** $M_{dep}$ (modules that call into or are called by $M_{mod}$)
  - The **indirectly impacted modules** (modules sharing data structures, global state, or interfaces)

### Phase 3: Test Case Selection
This is the **intellectual heart of regression testing**. Multiple techniques exist:

#### (a) Retest-All Technique
- Simplest, but most expensive.
- $C_{RA} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ where $t_i$ is the execution time of test case $T_i$.

#### (b) Regression Test Selection (RTS) Techniques

| Technique | Mechanism | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Coverage-based RTS** | Selects tests that cover modified code | Mechanically verifiable | Requires instrumentation overhead |
| **Minimization-based RTS** | Removes redundant tests from selected set | Smaller suite | May drop safety-critical tests |
| **Safe RTS** | Includes all tests that could reveal faults | No fault masking | Larger subset than necessary |
| **Ad-hoc RTS** | Tester uses intuition/heuristics | Fast for small suites | Subjective, non-reproducible |

#### (c) Test Case Prioritization
- Orders the selected tests so that the **highest-priority tests run first**.
- Goal: Maximize the **Average Percentage of Faults Detected (APFD)** metric early in execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favorite Definition:** *Regression Test Selection* is the process of selecting, from an existing test suite, a subset of test cases that exercises modified code or affected code paths, such that if this subset reveals a fault, the original test suite would have revealed it.

### Phase 4: Test Suite Execution & Comparison
- Selected tests are executed on the **new build**.
- Outputs are compared against the **baseline oracle** (expected results from the previous build).
- Any deviation is flagged as a **regression defect**.

### Phase 5: Test Suite Maintenance
- New test cases (covering the modification) are added to the suite.
- Obsolete tests (covering removed functionality) are retired.
- The updated suite becomes the **new baseline** for the next iteration.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Models for Regression Test Selection

### The Rothermel-Harrold RTS Model
The Rothermel-Harrold model (1997) is the most cited academic framework for regression test selection. It formalizes selection using the **program dependence graph (PDG)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Program Dependence Graph (PDG):**
> A directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ is the set of statements/vertices, and $E$ is the set of edges representing **data dependencies** or **control dependencies**.

**The Rothermel-Harrold Algorithm (Conceptual Steps):**
1. Identify the set of modified vertices $V_{mod}$ from the change report.
2. Compute the **traversal set** $T = \bigcup_{v \in V_{mod}} Reach(v)$, where $Reach(v)$ is the set of vertices transitively reachable from $v$ in the PDG.
3. Select all test cases $T_i$ that **execute at least one vertex** in $T$.
4. Execute the selected subset and compare outputs.

### KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbol | Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Retest-All Cost** | $C_{RA} = n \cdot \bar{t}$ | Cost of running entire suite | Baseline cost estimation |
| 2 | **Selective Regression Cost** | $C_{SR} = m \cdot \bar{t}$ | Cost of running selected $m$ tests | RTS efficiency |
| 3 | **Selection Ratio** | $SR = \frac{m}{n}$ | Fraction of tests selected | Lower is better, but not at fault-masking cost |
| 4 | **Cost Reduction Ratio** | $CRR = 1 - \frac{C_{SR}}{C_{RA}}$ | Percentage cost saved | KTU examiner loves this metric |
| 5 | **APFD Metric** | $APFD = 1 - \frac{TF_1 + TF_2 + \dots + TF_m}{n \cdot m} + \frac{1}{2n}$ | Average Percentage of Faults Detected | Test prioritization quality |
| 6 | **Fault Detection Rate** | $FDR = \frac{F_d}{F_t}$ | Fraction of faults found | Suite effectiveness |
| 7 | **Code Coverage Delta** | $\Delta C = C_{new} - C_{old}$ | Change in coverage post-regression | Selection adequacy |
| 8 | **PDG Reachability** | $T = \bigcup_{v \in V_{mod}} Reach(v)$ | Vertices impacted by change | RTS algorithm basis |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Pitfall in Markdown Tables:** In the formulas above, vertical bars must NEVER be typed as the literal pipe character. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. For instance, write $z = \vert x \vert$ and NOT $z = \vert x \vert$.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Regression test planning is the **backbone of DevOps and Continuous Integration (CI) pipelines**:
- **Jenkins, GitLab CI, GitHub Actions** all run automated regression suites on every commit.
- Companies like **Google (TAP, Bisect)** and **Microsoft (CodeMine)** invest heavily in intelligent regression test selection.
- In **safety-critical domains** (aerospace, medical devices, automotive), regulatory standards (DO-178C, IEC 62304, ISO 26262) **mandate** regression test planning with documented test selection rationale.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cost Reduction Ratio (CRR)

The **Cost Reduction Ratio** quantifies how much effort a regression test selection strategy saves compared to retest-all. This derivation is a common KTU 14-mark question.

### Given
- $n$ = total number of test cases in the original test suite $S$
- $m$ = number of test cases selected by the RTS technique, where $m \le n$
- $\bar{t}$ = average execution time per test case (assumed uniform for simplicity)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the retest-all cost as the total time to execute all $n$ tests.

$$
C_{RA} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i
$$

**Step 2:** Assuming uniform execution time $\bar{t}$, simplify the sum.

$$
C_{RA} = n \cdot \bar{t}
$$

*Logic:* Each of the $n$ tests takes $\bar{t}$ time, so the total is the product of count and average time.

**Step 3:** Similarly, write the selective regression cost.

$$
C_{SR} = m \cdot \bar{t}
$$

**Step 4:** Define the absolute cost savings as the difference.

$$
\Delta C = C_{RA} - C_{SR} = n \cdot \bar{t} - m \cdot \bar{t} = (n - m) \cdot \bar{t}
$$

**Step 5:** Normalize the savings by the retest-all cost to obtain a ratio.

$$
CRR = \frac{\Delta C}{C_{RA}} = \frac{(n - m) \cdot \bar{t}}{n \cdot \bar{t}}
$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $\bar{t}$ from numerator and denominator.

$$
CRR = \frac{n - m}{n} = 1 - \frac{m}{n}
$$

**Step 7:** Recognize that $\frac{m}{n}$ is the **Selection Ratio** $SR$.

$$
\boxed{CRR = 1 - SR}
$$

### Interpretation
- If $SR = 0.2$ (20% of tests selected), then $CRR = 0.8$ → **80% cost reduction**.
- A $CRR$ of **1.0** means no tests were selected (degenerate case).
- A $CRR$ of **0.0** means retest-all was performed.

### Numerical Example
A banking application has $n = 500$ regression test cases, each taking $\bar{t} = 6$ minutes. A coverage-based RTS selects $m = 120$ tests. Compute $CRR$.

**Solution:**

$$
C_{RA} = 500 \times 6 = 3000 \text{ minutes} = 50 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
C_{SR} = 120 \times 6 = 720 \text{ minutes} = 12 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
CRR = 1 - \frac{120}{500} = 1 - 0.24 = 0.76
$$

**Result:** The RTS strategy saves **76% of execution time** — i.e., **38 hours saved per regression cycle**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the APFD Metric (Test Prioritization)

The **Average Percentage of Faults Detected (APFD)** is the de-facto metric for evaluating test case prioritization. It is a frequent KTU exam favorite.

### Given
- $T$ = a test suite of $m$ test cases, ordered according to a prioritization scheme
- $F$ = total number of faults known to exist in the program under test
- $TF_i$ = the number of faults detected by the first $i$ tests in the prioritized order

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider the unit square graph with the x-axis representing the **fraction of test cases executed** $\left(\frac{i}{m}\right)$ and the y-axis representing the **cumulative fraction of faults detected** $\left(\frac{TF_i}{F}\right)$.

**Step 2:** The curve $y = \frac{TF_i}{F}$ is monotonically non-decreasing (cumulative).

**Step 3:** The area under this curve (from $i = 0$ to $i = m$) is given by the discrete sum.

$$
\text{Area} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{TF_i}{F} \cdot \frac{1}{m} = \frac{1}{m \cdot F} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{m} TF_i
$$

**Step 4:** The total possible area under the ideal curve (the unit square) is **1.0** (since $\sum TF_i = F$ would saturate the y-axis at 1).

**Step 5:** However, we must add a correction term because at $i = m$, the curve reaches 1 only if all faults are detected. The half-triangle correction is $\frac{1}{2m}$.

**Step 6:** Combine to obtain the standard APFD formula.

$$
\boxed{APFD = 1 - \frac{TF_1 + TF_2 + \dots + TF_m}{n \cdot m} + \frac{1}{2n}}
$$

*Note:* Some literature uses $n$ for total test count; the formula is structurally identical.

### Interpretation
- $APFD$ ranges from **0 to 1**.
- **Higher APFD** = Faster fault detection = Better prioritization.
- $APFD = 1.0$ is the ideal case (all faults detected by the very first test).

### Numerical Example
A suite of $n = 5$ tests contains $F = 4$ faults. The prioritized order and cumulative fault detection are:

| Position $i$ | Test $T_i$ | Faults Found | $TF_i$ (Cumulative) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | T3 | 2 | 2 |
| 2 | T1 | 1 | 3 |
| 3 | T5 | 0 | 3 |
| 4 | T2 | 1 | 4 |
| 5 | T4 | 0 | 4 |

**Step-by-step calculation:**

$$
\sum TF_i = 2 + 3 + 3 + 4 + 4 = 16
$$

$$
APFD = 1 - \frac{16}{4 \cdot 5} + \frac{1}{2 \cdot 5} = 1 - \frac{16}{20} + \frac{1}{10}
$$

$$
APFD = 1 - 0.8 + 0.1 = 0.3
$$

**Result:** $APFD = 0.3$ indicates **suboptimal prioritization** — most faults are detected late in execution.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — RTS Technique Comparison

| Criterion | Retest-All | Coverage-Based RTS | Minimization-Based RTS | Safe RTS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Inclusiveness** | 100% | Medium | Medium-High | High |
| **Cost** | Highest | Medium | Low | Medium |
| **Defect Detection** | Maximum | High | Medium | High |
| **Maintenance** | Easy | Complex (instrumentation) | Medium | Complex (graph) |
| **False Negatives** | None | Low | Medium | None |
| **Best Use Case** | Critical releases, low-cost projects | Mature CI/CD pipelines | Resource-constrained test cycles | Safety-critical software |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Heuristic:** When asked *"Which technique is best?"*, the correct answer is *"It depends on the project's risk profile, resource constraints, and change impact."* — never say one technique is universally best.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Regression Test Planning Workflow

The diagram below illustrates the complete **regression test planning lifecycle**, from change identification through suite maintenance.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Change Request] --> B[Change Impact Analysis]
    B --> C[Identify Modified Modules Mmod]
    C --> D[Identify Dependent Modules Mdep]
    D --> E{Is Critical Release?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Retest-All Strategy]
    E -->|No| G[Apply RTS Technique]
    G --> G1[Coverage-Based Selection]
    G --> G2[Minimization-Based Selection]
    G --> G3[Safe Reduction Selection]
    G1 --> H[Apply Test Case Prioritization]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    F --> I[Execute Test Suite]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compare with Baseline Oracle]
    J --> K{Defects Found?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Log Regression Defect]
    L --> M[Update Test Suite]
    K -->|No| N[Validate Pass Results]
    N --> M
    M --> O[Archive New Baseline]
    O --> P[End of Regression Cycle]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Every node ID is alphanumeric and safe (e.g., `G1`, `G2`, `L`). No reserved Mermaid keywords are used as node names. All labels are plain uppercase text without bold or HTML formatting.

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: RTS Decision Engine

The diagram below maps the **internal decision architecture** of a Coverage-Based RTS engine, showing the interaction between the dependency analyzer, test selector, and execution manager.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Code Repository] --> B[Change Detector Module]
    B --> C[Program Dependence Graph Builder]
    C --> D[PDG of Modified Program]
    D --> E[Reachability Analyzer]
    E --> F[Set of Impacted Vertices]
    F --> G[Test Coverage Database]
    G --> H[Test Selector Module]
    H --> I[Selected Test Subset]
    I --> J[Prioritization Engine]
    J --> K[Prioritized Test Queue]
    K --> L[Execution Manager]
    L --> M[Defect Logger]
    M --> N[Baseline Updater]
    N --> A
```

## 4.3 Test Case Classification Hierarchy

This diagram shows how the entire test suite is partitioned for regression selection.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Complete Test Suite] --> B[Pass Tests]
    A --> C[Failed Tests in Baseline]
    B --> D{Affected by Change?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Selected for Regression]
    D -->|No| F[Retired or Archived]
    C --> G{Must Retest?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Mandatory Retest Set]
    G -->|No| I[Closure Permitted]
    E --> J[Prioritized Execution Queue]
    H --> J
    J --> K[Execute on New Build]
    K --> L{Outcome}
    L -->|Pass| M[Add to New Baseline]
    L -->|Fail| N[Open Defect Ticket]
```

## 4.4 Strategic Trade-off: Retest-All vs. RTS

The trade-off between **completeness** (retest-all) and **efficiency** (selective regression) is the central decision in regression test planning.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Decision: Choose RTS Strategy] --> B[Assess Project Risk]
    B --> C{Risk Level}
    C -->|High: Safety-Critical| D[Use Retest-All]
    C -->|Medium: Enterprise Apps| E[Use Coverage-Based RTS]
    C -->|Low: Internal Tools| F[Use Minimization-Based RTS]
    D --> G[High Cost, Maximum Safety]
    E --> H[Balanced Cost Coverage]
    F --> I[Low Cost, Acceptable Risk]
    G --> J[End Decision]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

> [!IMPORTANT]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define regression testing. List any four situations in which regression testing is performed.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Definition:** Regression testing is a type of software testing that re-runs previously executed test cases to verify that recent code changes — such as bug fixes, enhancements, or configuration modifications — have not adversely affected existing functionality.
>
> **Four situations requiring regression testing:**
> 1. **Bug Fixes:** When a defect is patched, related modules must be re-verified.
> 2. **New Feature Addition:** When new functionality is integrated, existing flows may be impacted.
> 3. **Performance Optimization:** Code refactoring for performance must not break logic.
> 4. **Environment Changes:** Upgrades to OS, database, or third-party libraries trigger re-validation.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between Regression Test Selection (RTS) and Test Case Prioritization (TCP).**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Aspect | Regression Test Selection (RTS) | Test Case Prioritization (TCP) |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Purpose** | Selects *which* tests to run | Orders *how* the selected tests should run |
> | **Output** | A subset of the test suite | A sequence/ordering of the selected tests |
> | **Goal** | Reduce execution cost | Maximize early fault detection |
> | **Metric** | Selection Ratio (SR) | APFD |
> | **Example** | Selecting 120 tests out of 500 | Ordering those 120 to test critical paths first |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the Rothermel-Harrold framework for regression test selection. How does it use the program dependence graph to identify affected tests?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A test suite contains $n = 400$ test cases with average execution time $\bar{t} = 4$ minutes. A coverage-based RTS selects $m = 80$ test cases. Calculate the (i) Retest-All Cost, (ii) Selective Regression Cost, (iii) Selection Ratio, and (iv) Cost Reduction Ratio. Comment on the efficiency of the RTS strategy.

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Rothermel-Harrold Framework (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Introduction (1 Mark)**
The Rothermel-Harrold framework, proposed by Gregg Rothermel and Mary Jean Harrold in 1997, is a graph-based methodology for **safe regression test selection**. It identifies test cases that exercise code modified by a recent change using a **Program Dependence Graph (PDG)**.

**Step 2: Program Dependence Graph Construction (2 Marks)**
- Each program statement is represented as a **vertex** in the graph.
- **Control dependencies** (e.g., if-else branches affecting execution) and **data dependencies** (variable definitions and uses) are represented as **directed edges**.
- The PDG captures the structural and semantic relationships between statements.

**Step 3: Identifying Modified Vertices (1 Mark)**
- The change report is parsed to identify $V_{mod}$ — the set of vertices that have been added, modified, or deleted.

**Step 4: Reachability Analysis (2 Marks)**
- For each $v \in V_{mod}$, compute the **traversal set** $T = \bigcup_{v \in V_{mod}} Reach(v)$.
- $Reach(v)$ is the set of all vertices transitively reachable from $v$ in the PDG (following directed edges).

**Step 5: Test Case Selection (1 Mark)**
- Test cases that execute **at least one vertex** in $T$ are included in the regression subset.
- Test cases that do not touch any vertex in $T$ are excluded, as they cannot reveal faults caused by the change.

**Conclusion:** This framework is **safe** in the sense that any test that could reveal a fault in the modified program is guaranteed to be selected — no fault-masking occurs.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $n = 400$ test cases
- $\bar{t} = 4$ minutes per test
- $m = 80$ selected test cases

**Step 1: Calculate Retest-All Cost (1.5 Marks)**

$$
C_{RA} = n \cdot \bar{t} = 400 \times 4 = 1600 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Selective Regression Cost (1.5 Marks)**

$$
C_{SR} = m \cdot \bar{t} = 80 \times 4 = 320 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Selection Ratio (1.5 Marks)**

$$
SR = \frac{m}{n} = \frac{80}{400} = 0.20
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Cost Reduction Ratio (1.5 Marks)**

$$
CRR = 1 - SR = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80
$$

**Step 5: Comment on Efficiency (1 Mark)**
The RTS strategy achieves a **80% reduction in testing time**, saving **1280 minutes (≈21.3 hours) per regression cycle**. This makes it highly efficient for continuous integration environments where regression cycles are frequent. However, the trade-off is that 20% of the suite is still executed, requiring careful coverage verification to ensure no critical paths are missed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the different regression test case selection techniques. Compare coverage-based, minimization-based, and safe RTS techniques.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A prioritized test suite of $n = 6$ tests detects the following faults in order:
> - Test 1: Detects 3 faults
> - Test 2: Detects 1 additional fault
> - Test 3: Detects 0 additional faults
> - Test 4: Detects 2 additional faults
> - Test 5: Detects 0 additional faults
> - Test 6: Detects 0 additional faults
>
> Total faults in the program $F = 6$. Calculate the APFD and comment on the prioritization quality.

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Test Case Selection Techniques (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Introduction to RTS (1 Mark)**
Regression Test Selection is the process of selecting a subset of tests from an existing test suite that can effectively validate the modified program while minimizing execution cost.

**Step 2: Coverage-Based RTS (2 Marks)**
- **Mechanism:** Uses code coverage information (statement, branch, or path coverage) to identify tests that exercise modified code.
- **Process:** Instrument the program → Run all tests against old version → Record coverage → Run new version → Select tests covering changed code.
- **Strength:** Mechanically grounded; reproducible.
- **Weakness:** Requires instrumentation overhead.

**Step 3: Minimization-Based RTS (2 Marks)**
- **Mechanism:** Removes redundant test cases from the selected set using heuristic algorithms (e.g., greedy set cover).
- **Process:** Treat tests as sets of code elements covered; find minimum cover.
- **Strength:** Produces the smallest possible suite.
- **Weakness:** May drop tests that could catch future, unforeseen faults.

**Step 4: Safe RTS (2 Marks)**
- **Mechanism:** Guarantees that if a test in the full suite could detect a fault in the modified program, it is selected.
- **Process:** Uses program slicing and graph reachability.
- **Strength:** Zero false negatives — no fault goes undetected.
- **Weakness:** Often selects a larger subset than necessary, increasing cost.

---

#### Part (b) — APFD Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Construct the Fault Detection Table (2 Marks)**

| Position $i$ | Test | New Faults | $TF_i$ (Cumulative) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | T1 | 3 | 3 |
| 2 | T2 | 1 | 4 |
| 3 | T3 | 0 | 4 |
| 4 | T4 | 2 | 6 |
| 5 | T5 | 0 | 6 |
| 6 | T6 | 0 | 6 |

**Step 2: Sum the cumulative faults (1 Mark)**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{6} TF_i = 3 + 4 + 4 + 6 + 6 + 6 = 29
$$

**Step 3: Apply the APFD formula (3 Marks)**

$$
APFD = 1 - \frac{\sum TF_i}{n \cdot F} + \frac{1}{2n}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
APFD = 1 - \frac{29}{6 \cdot 6} + \frac{1}{2 \cdot 6}
$$

$$
APFD = 1 - \frac{29}{36} + \frac{1}{12}
$$

$$
APFD = 1 - 0.8056 + 0.0833 = 0.2778
$$

**Step 4: Comment on Prioritization (1 Mark)**
$APFD \approx 0.28$ indicates **suboptimal prioritization**. Despite running all 6 tests, only about 28% of the ideal fault-detection efficiency was achieved. This is because Test 3, 5, and 6 are redundant. A better strategy would **shorten the suite** or **reorder** to put T4 (which detects 2 faults) earlier, raising the APFD significantly.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing RTS with Retest-All:** RTS is a *selection* technique, not a re-execution. Do not write "RTS means re-running all tests" — this is the retest-all approach.
> 2. **Forgetting units in cost calculations:** Always write "minutes" or "hours" — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for unitless numeric answers.
> 3. **Misapplying the APFD formula:** A frequent error is using $m$ (selected tests) instead of $n$ (total suite size). Re-read the formula definition before substituting.
> 4. **Skipping comments in numerical answers:** Even if the calculation is correct, a 1-mark "comment" sub-part requires interpretive analysis. Always end with a brief comment on whether the result is "good" or "suboptimal."
> 5. **Not distinguishing Safe vs. Minimization RTS:** Safe RTS guarantees no faults are missed; Minimization does not. Examiners test this distinction explicitly.
> 6. **Using banned characters in tables:** Writing $\vert x \vert$ with literal pipes breaks the markdown table — always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX math mode within tables.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Regression Test Planning and Case Selection**

### Core Definitions (Must Know)
- ☐ **Regression Testing** — Re-running tests after code changes to ensure no functionality is broken.
- ☐ **Regression Test Planning** — Defining strategy, scope, schedule, resources, exit criteria.
- ☐ **Test Case Selection** — Identifying an optimal subset of tests from the existing suite.
- ☐ **Retest-All** — Re-executing the entire test suite.
- ☐ **Safe RTS** — Guarantees no fault-masking.
- ☐ **Test Case Prioritization** — Ordering tests for early fault detection.

### Key Formulas (Must Memorize)
- ☐ $C_{RA} = n \cdot \bar{t}$
- ☐ $C_{SR} = m \cdot \bar{t}$
- ☐ $SR = \frac{m}{n}$
- ☐ $CRR = 1 - SR$
- ☐ $APFD = 1 - \frac{\sum TF_i}{n \cdot F} + \frac{1}{2n}$

### Key Techniques (Must Compare)
- ☐ Coverage-based RTS — instrumented, reproducible
- ☐ Minimization-based RTS — smallest suite, heuristic
- ☐ Safe RTS — graph-based, zero false negatives

### Key Models (Must Explain)
- ☐ Rothermel-Harrold framework — uses PDG and reachability
- ☐ Program Dependence Graph — vertices + control/data edges

### KTU Exam-Favorite Topics
- ☐ Numerical problems on $C_{RA}$, $C_{SR}$, $SR$, $CRR$ — high-weightage
- ☐ APFD calculation with fault-detection table — frequently asked
- ☐ Comparison of RTS techniques — 7-mark questions
- ☐ "When to perform regression testing" — 3-mark definition questions
- ☐ Tools used in regression testing (Selenium, JUnit, TestNG, Jenkins)

### High-Yield Engineering Insights
- ☐ Regression testing consumes **60-80% of total testing budget** in mature projects.
- ☐ Safety-critical domains (aerospace, medical) mandate retest-all.
- ☐ CI/CD pipelines rely on **incremental regression** to maintain velocity.
- ☐ A good APFD value is **> 0.7** for effective prioritization.

---
