Functional, performance, load, and acceptance tests are run. **Differences from Conventional Testing:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Conventional:} \quad & \text{Unit} \rightarrow \text{Integration} \rightarrow \text{System} \rightarrow \text{Acceptance} \\ \text{OO Strategy:} \quad & \text{Method} \rightarrow \text{Class} \rightarrow \text{Cluster} \rightarrow \text{System} \end{aligned} $$ | Conventional | Object-Oriented | |---|---| | Unit = function/module | Unit = **class** | | Integration = call-stack linkage | Integration = **message-passing** between classes | | Top-down / bottom-up integration | **Cluster / use-case driven** integration | | Re-test only modified module | Re-test **all derived classes** after base-class change | **(b) Solution — Fault-Based Class Testing (7 Marks)** **Definition (1 Mark):** Fault-based testing designs test cases by **hypothesising plausible faults** that may exist in the class, and then constructing tests that would expose those faults. **Example (3 Marks):** Consider a `Stack` class with `push(item)`, `pop()`, and `isEmpty()` methods. | # | Heuristic Fault | Test Case Designed | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Null-object fault** | Call `pop()` on a freshly created `Stack` object before any `push()`. Expected: `EmptyStackException`. | | 2 | **Boundary fault** | Call `push(item)` 101 times on a stack of size 100. Expected: overflow exception. | | 3 | **Uninitialised-state fault** | Call `isEmpty()` immediately after `new Stack()` — should return `true`, not crash. | | 4 | **State-transition fault** | After `pop()` on a single-item stack, call `isEmpty()` — must return `true`. | **Four Fault-Based Heuristics (3 Marks — 0.75 each):** 1. **Constructor / uninitialised-state fault:** Test methods on a freshly constructed object with default state. 2. **Null-object fault:** Test every method with a `null` argument or against a `null` object reference. 3. **Boundary-condition fault:** Test with minimum, maximum, just-below-min, and just-above-max values. 4. **Polymorphism-resolution fault:** For each polymorphic call, instantiate the base reference with every concrete subclass and execute the test. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`** **(a)** Explain the **state-based class testing approach** in detail. How is the state vector of a class identified? How is state coverage measured? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain **scenario-based (interaction) testing of classes** with a suitable banking example. Why is it also called "thread testing"? **(7 Marks)** ##### Model Solution **(a) Solution — State-Based Class Testing (7 Marks)** **Concept (2 Marks):** In state-based testing, the class is modelled as a **finite state machine (FSM)**. The *state* of a class instance is the vector of values of all its attributes at a given moment. A method invocation **transitions** the object from one state to another. The test goal is to cover all reachable states and all valid transitions. **Identifying the State Vector (2 Marks):** For a class with $n$ attributes $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$: $$ \text{State Vector } S = (a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n) $$ The tester then **partitions** each attribute's domain into equivalence classes (e.g., for a numeric attribute: negative, zero, positive). The cross-product of equivalence classes yields the **set of testable states**. **Example:** A `BankAccount` class with `balance` and `isActive` yields 6 states: $S_1 = (\text{Negative}, \text{false}), S_2 = (\text{Negative}, \text{true}), S_3 = (\text{Zero}, \text{false}), S_4 = (\text{Zero}, \text{true}), S_5 = (\text{Positive}, \text{false}), S_6 = (\text{Positive}, \text{true})$. **State Transition Function (2 Marks):** $$ S_{\text{new}} = \delta(S_{\text{old}}, m) $$ where $m$ is the method invoked and $\delta$ defines the new state. **Measuring Coverage (1 Mark):** - **State Coverage (SC):** $$ \text{SC} = \frac{\text{Number of states visited}}{\text{Total number of reachable states}} \times 100\% $$ - **Transition Coverage (TC):** $$ \text{TC} = \frac{\text{Number of transitions exercised}}{\text{Total number of valid transitions}} \times 100\% $$ The KTU 2024 module mandates that **both metrics must be 100%** for complete class testing. --- **(b) Solution — Scenario-Based / Thread Testing (7 Marks)** **Definition (2 Marks):** Scenario-based (or interaction-based) testing of classes focuses on validating the **collaboration between two or more classes** when they exchange messages to fulfil a use-case. The KTU 2024 syllabus calls this **"thread testing"** because each test follows a single thread of execution that weaves through multiple classes — analogous to a single thread of activity in a real-world business process. **Banking Example (4 Marks):** Consider three collaborating classes: `Customer`, `Account`, and `TransactionProcessor`. **Scenario:** *"A customer withdraws ₹5,000 from their savings account and the transaction is logged."* The thread of execution weaves through the classes as: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Customer} &\xrightarrow{\text{requestWithdraw(5000)}} \text{Account} \\ \text{Account} &\xrightarrow{\text{verifyBalance()}} \text{Account (self)} \\ \text{Account} &\xrightarrow{\text{debit(5000)}} \text{Account (state change)} \\ \text{Account} &\xrightarrow{\text{logTransaction()}} \text{TransactionProcessor} \\ \text{TransactionProcessor} &\xrightarrow{\text{writeToDB()}} \text{Database} \end{aligned} $$ A single scenario-based test case validates: - The **message contract** between `Customer` and `Account`. - The **state transition** of `Account` (balance reduced by 5000). - The **side effect** on `TransactionProcessor` (a new log entry). **Why "Thread Testing"? (1 Mark):** Because the test follows a **single thread of activity** (the use-case narrative) through multiple classes, validating the end-to-end collaboration in a single test case — much like a thread of execution in a real-world business workflow. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Confusing "unit" in OO with "unit" in procedural testing.** Many students write "the unit in OO is the function" — this costs full marks on the definition question. The KTU 2024 answer key explicitly states: *"The smallest testable unit in OO is the **class**."* > 2. **Omitting the re-test cascade from inheritance answers.** When asked about inheritance testing, students often write about "testing the derived class" but forget that a **base-class change forces re-testing of all derived classes**. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this omission. > 3. **Mixing up "integration testing" with "cluster testing".** Cluster testing is **not** the same as conventional integration testing. In conventional testing, integration is about linking call-stacks; in OO, it is about validating **message contracts**. > 4. **Forgetting the formula for polymorphic test amplification.** When asked "how does polymorphism affect test effort?", always cite the equation: > $$\text{Tests}_{\text{poly}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Tests}(C_i)$$ > Writing only the qualitative answer ("polymorphism increases tests") without the formula loses 1–2 marks. > 5. **Confusing "state coverage" with "code coverage".** State coverage is measured over the **state space of the class**; code coverage is measured over **lines/branches of the source code**. They are not interchangeable. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ The **smallest testable unit** in OO is the **class**, not the function. - ✅ The 4-level OO testing model is **Method → Class → Cluster → System**. - ✅ Conventional unit ↔ Method; Conventional integration ↔ Cluster. - ✅ **Encapsulation** restricts white-box testing; only black-box interface testing is feasible. - ✅ **Inheritance** mandates a **re-test cascade** — every base-class change forces re-testing of all derived classes. - ✅ **Polymorphism multiplies test effort**: $\text{Tests} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Tests}(C_i)$. - ✅ **Dynamic binding** makes statically written test plans unreliable — runtime behaviour must be observed. - ✅ The three KTU-prescribed class-level test design techniques are: **Fault-based, State-based, Scenario/Interaction-based**. - ✅ State vector $S = (a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n)$; transition $S_{\text{new}} = \delta(S_{\text{old}}, m)$. - ✅ **State coverage** = states visited / total reachable states; **Transition coverage** = transitions exercised / total valid transitions. KTU expects both at 100%. - ✅ **Cluster testing** is also called **thread testing** or **use-case-driven integration testing**. - ✅ **Fault-based heuristics**: uninitialised state, null-object, boundary, polymorphism-resolution, message-ordering. - ✅ When asked to "compare with conventional testing", always present a **2-column table** — KTU examiners reward structured answers.

topics.title: Analysis of Software Quality Metrics and Continuous Improvement Initiatives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT747 - SOFTWARE TESTING AND QUALITY ASSURANCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Software Quality Metrics and Continuous Improvement Initiatives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Software Quality Metrics** are **quantitative measures** that provide a standardized, repeatable, and objective basis for assessing, controlling, and predicting the quality attributes of a software product, the maturity of the software process, and the performance of a software project. They transform subjective quality characteristics into measurable numerical indicators that can be tracked, benchmarked, and used as decision-support evidence across the software lifecycle.

Under the KTU 2024 scheme (aligned with ISO/IEC 25010:2011 and the IEEE 1061 Standard for Software Quality Metrics), quality metrics are categorized into three primary classes:
1. **Product Metrics** — measure the properties of the software artifact (e.g., defect density, cyclomatic complexity, reliability).
2. **Process Metrics** — measure the efficacy of the development and testing activities (e.g., defect removal efficiency, test case productivity).
3. **Project Metrics** — measure the project execution and delivery performance (e.g., schedule variance, effort variance).

A **Continuous Improvement Initiative (CII)** is a structured, long-term, organization-wide program of incremental process enhancements — typically anchored on frameworks such as the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle, CMMI, Six Sigma, and Kaizen** — that uses measured quality data to iteratively reduce variation, eliminate defect root causes, and raise the maturity level of the software engineering process.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Hospital Patient Vitals Monitor":**
> Imagine a hospital Intensive Care Unit (ICU). Doctors do not simply "guess" whether a patient is healthy — they attach a monitor that continuously displays vital signs: heart rate (BPM), blood pressure (mmHg), oxygen saturation (SpO₂), and body temperature (°C). These are **metrics** — numerical values that summarize the state of a complex biological system.
> - If heart rate crosses **120 bpm**, an alarm rings → the metric is **out of threshold**.
> - Nurses respond with treatment → this is a **continuous improvement action**.
> - After treatment, the metrics are checked again → **feedback loop**.
> - The whole protocol (measure → detect anomaly → intervene → re-measure) is exactly how software teams use **quality metrics + continuous improvement**.

In the same way, software teams use metrics to know the "vital signs" of the code and the process, and improvement initiatives to keep the software "healthy."

### 1.3 Key Industry-Standard Metrics in Bold

> [!TIP]
> **The five mandatory metrics every KTU student must memorize:**
> - **Defect Density (DD)** — defects per KLOC or per Function Point.
> - **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** — % of defects removed before delivery.
> - **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** — average uptime between failures.
> - **Cyclomatic Complexity (V(G))** — independent paths through source code.
> - **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** — measured via end-user surveys (CSAT/NPS).

### 1.4 Why Metrics Matter in Industry

| Use-Case | How Metrics Help |
|---|---|
| **Release Go/No-Go Decisions** | Reliability growth curves indicate whether the product is stable enough to ship. |
| **Vendor / Outsourcing Evaluation** | Defect density and MTTF benchmarks help compare supplier quality. |
| **Process Audit Compliance** | CMMI and ISO 9001 demand quantitative evidence — metrics provide it. |
| **Test Process Optimization** | DRE and test effectiveness metrics identify weak test phases. |
| **Predictive Cost Modelling** | COQUALMO and similar models use size + complexity metrics to forecast defect counts. |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Measurement Framework — GQM (Goal-Question-Metric)

The **Goal-Question-Metric (GQM)** paradigm, originally proposed by Basili and Weiss, is the de-facto top-down approach to define meaningful metrics:

- **Step 1 — Goal:** Define a measurable goal (e.g., *"Reduce post-release defects to less than 5 per KLOC"*).
- **Step 2 — Question:** Derive questions that operationalize the goal (e.g., *"How many defects are found in system testing?"*, *"What is the customer-reported defect rate?"*).
- **Step 3 — Metric:** Define the exact data to be collected (e.g., *"Defect Density = Total Defects ÷ Size in KLOC"*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why GQM?** It prevents the common pitfall of "measuring for the sake of measuring." Every metric is justified by tracing back to a stakeholder goal.

### 2.2 Hierarchy of Software Quality Metrics

The metrics are organized in a **three-tier hierarchy**:

**Tier 1 — Product (Internal Quality)**
- Size metrics: **Lines of Code (LOC)**, **Function Points (FP)**.
- Complexity metrics: **Cyclomatic Complexity (V(G))**, **Halstead Measures**.
- Quality metrics: **Defect Density, Code Coverage, Maintainability Index**.

**Tier 2 — Process (Engineering Quality)**
- **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** per phase.
- **Test Case Effectiveness (TCE)**, **Test Coverage**.
- **Code Review Effectiveness (CRE)**.
- **Phase Containment Effectiveness (PCE)**.

**Tier 3 — Project (Delivery Quality)**
- **Schedule Variance (SV)**, **Effort Variance (EV)**.
- **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** — prevention + appraisal + failure costs.
- **Earned Value Management (EVM)** indices.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU exam question on this module.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Defect Density (DD)} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Size in KLOC}} \quad \text{(unit: defects/KLOC)}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{DRE} = \frac{\text{Defects found before delivery}}{\text{Total defects (before + after delivery)}} \times 100\%}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{\text{Total operating time}}{\text{Number of failures observed}}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%}
$$

$$
\boxed{V(G) = E - N + 2P} \quad \text{(Cyclomatic Complexity)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Program Volume } V = N \times \log_2(n)} \quad \text{(Halstead Volume)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Halstead Difficulty } D = \frac{n_1}{2} \times \frac{N_2}{n_2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Halstead Effort } E = V \times D}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Phase Containment Effectiveness (PCE)} = \frac{\text{Defects found in phase}}{\text{Defects found in that phase + later phases}} \times 100\%}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Test Effectiveness} = \frac{\text{Defects detected by tests}}{\text{Total known defects in system}} \times 100\%}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)} = \frac{\sum (\text{Response Score})}{\text{Max Possible Score} \times \text{Number of Responses}} \times 100\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Units Pitfall:** Always normalize defect density by **KLOC** (or **FP**), never by raw LOC. KLU valuation strictly checks units. Also remember that $E$, $N$, $P$ in cyclomatic complexity stand for **Edges, Nodes, Connected Components** — not Effort, Number, or Population.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Domain | Application of Quality Metrics |
|---|---|
| **Avionics (DO-178C)** | Failure rate, MTTF, and code coverage metrics are legally mandated for flight software certification. |
| **Medical Devices (FDA/IEC 62304)** | Defect density, MTBF, and reliability growth curves are evidence in submission dossiers. |
| **Banking & FinTech** | Availability (target 99.999%), MTTR, and mean-time-between-critical-failures drive SLA penalties. |
| **Automotive (ISO 26262)** | Residual defect density per KLOC must be below the ASIL-defined threshold. |
| **E-Commerce (RazorPay, Amazon)** | Latency, error rate per million requests, and NPS (Net Promoter Score) are tracked daily. |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Defect Density and DRE

**Problem:** A 50 KLOC Java module was developed. During unit testing 30 defects were found and fixed. In integration testing 12 more defects surfaced. After release, customers reported 8 additional defects. Compute **(a)** Defect Density and **(b)** DRE.

> [!NOTE]
> **Solution Reasoning:** We separate "defects found before delivery" (in-house test phases) from "defects found after delivery" (customer-reported). The sum gives the total.

**Step 1 — Identify defect counts per phase.**

$$
\text{Unit test defects} = 30, \quad \text{Integration test defects} = 12, \quad \text{Customer defects} = 8
$$

**Step 2 — Total defects and defects before delivery.**

$$
\text{Total defects} = 30 + 12 + 8 = 50
$$

$$
\text{Defects before delivery} = 30 + 12 = 42
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Defect Density (DD).**

$$
\text{DD} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Size in KLOC}} = \frac{50}{50} = 1.0 \;\; \text{defects/KLOC}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE).**

$$
\text{DRE} = \frac{42}{50} \times 100\% = 84\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** An industry-average DRE for high-mature organizations is 95%+. 84% indicates the testing process still leaks 16% of defects to the customer — there is room for continuous improvement in early-phase reviews (e.g., formal inspections of design docs).

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Reliability & Availability

**Problem:** A web server is observed for 1000 hours. In that interval it failed 4 times. Each failure took an average of 1.5 hours to repair. Compute **MTTF, MTBF, and Availability**.

**Step 1 — Compute MTTF (Mean Time To Failure).**

$$
\text{MTTF} = \frac{\text{Total operating time}}{\text{Number of failures}} = \frac{1000}{4} = 250 \;\; \text{hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute MTTR (Mean Time To Repair).** (Given directly in problem)

$$
\text{MTTR} = 1.5 \;\; \text{hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures).**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = 250 + 1.5 = 251.5 \;\; \text{hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Availability.**

$$
\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\% = \frac{250}{251.5} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Availability} \approx 99.4036\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:** A "five-nines" (99.999%) availability target — common in telecom and banking — implies downtime of only ~5.26 minutes per year. This is achievable only with geographically redundant clusters, automated failover, and tight MTTR controls (often under 30 seconds).

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe)

**Problem:** A function contains the control-flow graph with **E = 14 edges, N = 11 nodes, and P = 1 connected component.** Compute the cyclomatic complexity and interpret it.

**Step 1 — Apply the formula.**

$$
V(G) = E - N + 2P = 14 - 11 + 2(1) = 5
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation.**

$$
V(G) = 5 \Rightarrow \text{At least 5 independent test cases are required for full branch coverage.}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Industry Heuristic (McCabe's original guideline):** Any module with **V(G) > 10** is considered high-risk and should be refactored. KTU examiners love this rule — quote it.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Halstead Metrics

**Problem:** A small program has the following counts:

- Number of distinct operators: $n_1 = 8$
- Number of distinct operands: $n_2 = 12$
- Total occurrences of operators: $N_1 = 30$
- Total occurrences of operands: $N_2 = 45$

Compute **Vocabulary (n), Program Length (N), Volume (V), Difficulty (D), and Effort (E).**

**Step 1 — Vocabulary and Length.**

$$
n = n_1 + n_2 = 8 + 12 = 20
$$

$$
N = N_1 + N_2 = 30 + 45 = 75
$$

**Step 2 — Volume (in bits).**

$$
V = N \times \log_2(n) = 75 \times \log_2(20)
$$

$$
\log_2(20) = \frac{\ln 20}{\ln 2} \approx 4.3219
$$

$$
V = 75 \times 4.3219 \approx 324.14 \;\; \text{bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Difficulty.**

$$
D = \frac{n_1}{2} \times \frac{N_2}{n_2} = \frac{8}{2} \times \frac{45}{12} = 4 \times 3.75 = 15
$$

**Step 4 — Effort (in elementary mental discriminations).**

$$
E = V \times D = 324.14 \times 15 \approx 4862.1
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** Higher effort $\Rightarrow$ harder-to-maintain module. Effort values exceeding empirical thresholds signal refactoring candidates.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Metric Selection Guide

| Engineering Question | Recommended Metric | Why This Metric |
|---|---|---|
| *"How big is the project?"* | Function Points (FP), KLOC | Size is the base for almost all other metric normalizations. |
| *"How many bugs does the code have?"* | Defect Density | Normalized for fair comparison across modules. |
| *"Are we testing well enough?"* | DRE, Test Coverage | Quantifies the leakage of defects past each phase. |
| *"Is the code too complex to maintain?"* | Cyclomatic Complexity, Halstead Volume | Predicts maintainability, testability, and bug-proneness. |
| *"Can we trust the system in production?"* | MTTF, MTBF, Availability | Operational reliability measures. |
| *"Are customers happy?"* | CSI, NPS | End-user perception of delivered quality. |
| *"How mature is our process?"* | CMMI Level, PCE | Process capability assessment. |

### 4.5 Block-Level Architecture of a Quality Measurement System

```mermaid
graph LR
    src1["Source Code Repository"] --> coll["Metric Collection Engine"]
    tc["Test Case Execution Logs"] --> coll
    cr["Customer Complaint Portal"] --> coll
    coll --> store["Quality Data Warehouse"]
    store --> dash["Executive Dashboard"]
    store --> ana["Statistical Analysis Module"]
    ana --> root["Root Cause Analysis Ishikawa Pareto"]
    root --> act["Improvement Action Plan"]
    act --> pilot["Pilot Implementation in Next Sprint"]
    pilot --> coll
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Quality data is collected from three independent sources — code, test logs, and customer complaints — aggregated in a warehouse, analysed statistically, root-caused using Ishikawa/Pareto tools, and finally looped back as a pilot improvement. The pilot's impact is re-measured and re-fed into the collection engine, closing the **closed-loop measurement architecture** that all CII frameworks require.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE). A software product had 120 defects detected during testing and 30 more defects reported by customers in the first month after release. Calculate the DRE of the project.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (1 mark):** Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE) is a process quality metric that measures the percentage of defects found and removed *before* software is delivered to the customer, relative to the total number of defects known to exist (both those found pre-delivery and those discovered post-delivery).
> **Calculation (2 marks):**
>
> $$\text{Defects before delivery} = 120, \quad \text{Defects after delivery} = 30$$
>
> $$\text{Total defects} = 120 + 30 = 150$$
>
> $$\text{DRE} = \frac{120}{150} \times 100\% = 80\%$$
>
> **Conclusion:** The team removed 80% of the defects before release; the remaining 20% leaked to customers — scope exists for improvement in early test phases (e.g., requirements reviews).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*List any **three** differences between product metrics and process metrics with one example each.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per correct pair):**
>
> | S.No | Aspect | Product Metric | Process Metric |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | **What it measures** | Attributes of the software artifact (code, design) | Attributes of the development and testing activities |
> | 2 | **Purpose** | Evaluate the quality of the *output* | Evaluate the *maturity* of the method used |
> | 3 | **Example** | Cyclomatic Complexity, Defect Density, Halstead Volume | DRE, PCE, Test Case Effectiveness |
> | 4 | **Data source** | Source code, build artifact | Test logs, defect logs, review records |
> | 5 | **Owner** | Developer / QA Lead | Process Engineer / QA Manager |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks divided into sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. You are expected to write the formula, substitute values, and show the interpretation.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, End-Semester Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **Goal-Question-Metric (GQM)** approach in detail. Illustrate with an example of "improving the reliability of an ATM transaction module." *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What is **Cyclomatic Complexity**? For the following control flow graph of a function, **E = 18 edges, N = 13 nodes, P = 1**, compute the cyclomatic complexity, state McCabe's threshold for high-risk modules, and explain how V(G) drives the test-case design. *(7 marks)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer — (a) GQM Approach [7 marks]:**
> The GQM (Goal-Question-Metric) paradigm, defined by Basili and Weiss, is a top-down measurement framework that ensures every metric collected is tied to a *measurable* business or engineering goal. It has three layers:
> 1. **Conceptual Level — Goal:** A goal is defined for an object (process, product, resource), with a quality perspective (e.g., reliability, usability) and a stakeholder viewpoint. *Example:* *"Improve the reliability of the ATM cash-dispensation module from the customer's perspective."*
> 2. **Operational Level — Question:** A set of questions refines the goal into operational terms. For the ATM goal: Q1 — *What is the mean time to failure of the cash-dispensation module?* Q2 — *How many transaction failures occur per 1000 sessions?* Q3 — *What is the customer-reported failure rate?*
> 3. **Quantitative Level — Metric:** Each question is associated with one or more objectively measurable data items. Q1 → MTTF, Q2 → Defect Density, Q3 → Customer-Reported Failure Index.
> **Advantages:** Avoids collection of useless data, provides traceability from business goal to data point, supports Basili's *goal-driven* improvement. **Marks split:** GQM definition 3, ATM example 3, advantages 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer — (b) Cyclomatic Complexity [7 marks]:**
> **Definition (2 marks):** Cyclomatic complexity V(G), introduced by Thomas McCabe in 1976, is a structural metric that quantifies the number of linearly independent paths through a program's source code. It is derived from the control flow graph (CFG) of the program.
> **Formula (1 mark):**
>
> $$V(G) = E - N + 2P$$
>
> **Substitution (2 marks):**
>
> $$V(G) = 18 - 13 + 2(1) = 7$$
>
> **McCabe's Threshold (1 mark):** Any module with V(G) > 10 is classified as high-risk, untestable, and should be refactored. The industry-recommended upper limit is 10, with a per-function target of V(G) ≤ 10.
> **Test-Case Design (1 mark):** V(G) gives the *minimum* number of independent test paths (and therefore test cases) required to exercise every branch at least once. Hence, for V(G) = 7, at least **7 independent test cases** are needed for complete branch coverage.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-A):** Many students write V(G) = E − N + 2 but **forget to multiply by P** when there are multiple connected components (e.g., a function with multiple `return` statements and helper closures can split into 2 components). Also, do **not** confuse "P" with the number of predicate nodes — the formula is strictly based on graph edges/nodes. KTU valuation deducts 1 mark if the V(G) calculation is correct but the *test-case link* is missing.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, End-Semester]**

**(a)** Describe the **PDCA (Deming Cycle)** with a neat diagram. Explain how it is applied for continuous software quality improvement with a real example. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A payroll system is observed for 2000 hours. During this period, it failed **5 times**. The cumulative repair time was **4 hours**. Compute **MTTF, MTBF, MTTR, and Availability**. Comment on whether the system meets a 99.9% service-level agreement. *(7 marks)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer — (a) PDCA Cycle [7 marks]:**
> The PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle, also called the *Deming Wheel* or *Shewhart cycle*, is an iterative four-phase management method for the continuous improvement of processes and products.
> 1. **Plan:** Identify the problem or improvement opportunity, analyse current data (using quality metrics), and design a change. *Example:* The team observes that DRE has dropped from 90% to 80%. They hypothesise that integrating pair-programming into unit testing will improve early defect detection.
> 2. **Do:** Implement the change on a small pilot scale, collect data, and document observations. *Example:* Pair programming is rolled out in 2 of the 8 development teams for one sprint; defect counts and review times are logged.
> 3. **Check:** Evaluate the pilot results against the metrics defined in the Plan phase. *Example:* The pilot teams recorded a DRE of 92% (up from 80%) with no statistically significant increase in coding time. A paired t-test confirms the improvement.
> 4. **Act:** If results are favourable, standardise the change across the whole organisation; if not, revise the plan and re-enter the cycle. *Example:* Pair programming is institutionalised as a standard practice, and the team re-enters Plan to target the next weak process area (e.g., integration test coverage).
> **Marks split:** Four-phase description 2 marks, diagram 1 mark, real example 3 marks, conclusion 1 mark.

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer — (b) Reliability Computation [7 marks]:**
> **Given:** Total operating time = 2000 hours; Number of failures = 5; Cumulative repair time = 4 hours.
> **Step 1 — MTTR (1 mark):**
>
> $$\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total repair time}}{\text{Number of failures}} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8 \;\; \text{hours}$$
>
> **Step 2 — MTTF (1 mark):**
>
> $$\text{MTTF} = \frac{\text{Total operating time}}{\text{Number of failures}} = \frac{2000}{5} = 400 \;\; \text{hours}$$
>
> **Step 3 — MTBF (1 mark):**
>
> $$\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = 400 + 0.8 = 400.8 \;\; \text{hours}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Availability (2 marks):**
>
> $$\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\% = \frac{400}{400.8} \times 100\% \approx 99.8004\%$$
>
> **Step 5 — SLA Comparison (2 marks):**
> Required SLA ≥ 99.9%. Computed availability = 99.80% < 99.9%. **Conclusion:** The system **fails** to meet the SLA. To achieve 99.9%, either reduce MTTR (e.g., automated failover) or increase MTTF (e.g., better code reviews and reliability testing). One must bring MTTR down to approximately 0.4 hours (≈ 24 minutes) at the same MTTF to cross the 99.9% threshold.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-B):** Three common mistakes cost marks here:
> 1. Confusing **MTTF** (Mean Time *To* Failure) with **MTBF** (Mean Time *Between* Failures). The relation is MTBF = MTTF + MTTR — write it on the answer sheet even if you compute only one.
> 2. Expressing availability as a **decimal (0.998)** instead of a **percentage (99.8%)** — KTU evaluators mark it wrong.
> 3. Forgetting to **comment** on the SLA. Computation alone earns ~5 of 7 marks; the comparative interpretation is the differentiator for the remaining 2 marks.

---

### 📘 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin this before the exam):**
>
> - [x] **Defect Density** = Defects ÷ KLOC (or FP). Normalise — never quote raw counts.
> - [x] **DRE** = (Defects in-house ÷ Total defects) × 100. Industry target ≥ 95%.
> - [x] **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR**; **Availability = MTTF ÷ (MTTF + MTTR)**.
> - [x] **Cyclomatic Complexity V(G) = E − N + 2P**; McCabe's risk threshold = **10**.
> - [x] **Halstead Volume V = N × log₂(n)**; **Effort E = V × D**; **D = (n₁/2)(N₂/n₂)**.
> - [x] **GQM (Goal-Question-Metric)** is the *top-down* metric derivation method.
> - [x] **PDCA = Plan → Do → Check → Act** — every CII is built around this loop.
> - [x] **CMMI** has **5 maturity levels** (Initial → Managed → Defined → Quantitatively Managed → Optimizing).
> - [x] **Six Sigma** uses **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control); target ≤ 3.4 DPMO.
> - [x] **Kaizen** = small, daily, employee-driven improvements.
> - [x] **Ishikawa (fishbone) + Pareto (80/20)** are the standard root-cause-analysis tools used inside the "Check" step of PDCA.
> - [x] **Three tiers of metrics:** Product, Process, Project. Each addresses a *different* stakeholder question.
> - [x] **PCE (Phase Containment Effectiveness)** = defects found in a phase ÷ (defects found in that phase + later phases). High PCE in early phases = mature process.
> - [x] **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure costs.
> - [x] **Always show units** in metric answers (defects/KLOC, hours, %, etc.) — KTU valuation marks it.
> - [x] **McCabe's 10-rule** and **Industry DRE of 95%** are the most-quoted "rule-of-thumb" numbers in viva and exams.
